bio.road:
Gregory Michael Newton was born September 7, 1974, in Niagara Falls, Ontario — on the Canadian side of the falls, in a country where hockey was king and basketball was an afterthought. His father had pitched in the Milwaukee Braves organization. His mother still played recreational basketball. An older brother played college basketball in Canada. The Newtons were athletic, but Niagara Falls didn’t produce basketball recruits.

At A.N. Myer Secondary School, Newton grew to 6’10” and dominated: 28 points, 15 rebounds, 4 blocks per game as a senior. He was a force at the OFSAA provincial tournament. But Canadian high school basketball existed in obscurity — American coaches rarely crossed the border.

So Newton’s father built it himself. He started an AAU team, traveled south of the border, entered Greg in elite American tournaments. Newton played in the Beach Ball Classic in Myrtle Beach — unheard-of for a Canadian kid — and made the Canadian National Team while still in high school. Duke recruited him. He was expected to be the next great Duke big man, heir to a lineage running from Alarie through Ferry, Laettner, and Cherokee Parks.
bio.duke:
Greg Newton played four years at Duke, 1993–97, and his career was defined by promise, turbulence, and one unforgettable rivalry.

Freshman year: backup to Cherokee Parks and Erik Meek, 25 games, 4.0 PPG, 3.4 RPG. Learning from the bench.

Then everything went sideways. In 1995, Newton was suspended for two semesters after being found guilty of cheating on a computer science exam. He denied the charge, but the suspension held. During this period, his father died. A smoking habit he’d picked up at fifteen worsened. He drank. Both habits, he admitted, were things Coach K hadn’t known about. The death of the father who had built the AAU team, who had driven his son across the border, who had pitched in the Braves organization — that loss hollowed something out.

Newton returned for 1995–96 — the year after the 4–15 disaster. With Coach K back and the program rebuilding, Newton had his best year: 12.2 PPG, 8.2 RPG. Aggressive, physical, invaluable on a thin frontcourt. Dave Odom said he’d gotten better every single game. But the defining narrative was his rivalry with Tim Duncan.

Newton told reporters he knew how to shut down Duncan: "You’ve got to be physical with him. He can’t play physical." Duncan’s response: "Peace be to Greg Newton." When pressed: "I think he’s the best player I’ve ever seen." The sarcasm was surgical. Duncan demolished Newton on national television. The quote became legendary — not for what Newton said, but for how completely Duncan dismantled him.

Senior year: team captain. But playing time shrank. Twenty-one total points over the final 11 games. Six minutes in the last regular-season game against Wake. And in his final college game — Duke’s loss to Providence — Newton did not play. DNP–Coach’s Decision. A team captain, on the bench, in his last game.
bio.after:
Newton went undrafted. No team expressed interest. He started with the USBL’s Raleigh Cougars, who promoted him by noting he was one away from Duke’s top ten in career blocks.

What followed was one of the most far-flung careers in Duke history. He played professionally in Belgium, Israel, Brazil, Argentina, Slovenia, Italy, Bulgaria, Serbia, Russia, Bosnia and Herzegovina, and Spain. Eleven countries across three continents over nearly a decade. A basketball journeyman — a 6’10” Canadian who had played at Duke, been suspended, lost his father, talked trash to Tim Duncan, and spent his twenties and thirties in gymnasiums most Americans would never see.

Through it all, he represented Canada. At the 1998 FIBA World Championship, he led the Canadian team in scoring (11.5 PPG) and rebounding (10.1 RPG). At the 2000 Sydney Olympics, he was a member of the team that finished seventh — the best Olympic finish for Canadian basketball in decades. On the international stage, the kid from Niagara Falls was his country’s best big man.
bio.now:
Newton’s professional career ended around 2005. In 2011, he joined the coaching staff at Brock University in St. Catharines, Ontario — a short drive from Niagara Falls. He served as an assistant until 2013. He had come home, back to the Canadian side of the falls, back to where his father had built an AAU team from nothing.

Greg Newton was born in Niagara Falls, Ontario, in a country where basketball was an afterthought. His father pitched in the Braves organization, started an AAU team, and drove his son to American tournaments. Newton was recruited to Duke as the next great big man. He was suspended for cheating. His father died and his habits got worse. He came back and had his best season. He told the world he could shut down Tim Duncan, and Duncan replied with the most politely devastating put-down in ACC history. He was named team captain and didn’t play in his final game. He went undrafted and played in eleven countries. He led Canada in scoring and rebounding at the World Championship. He played in the Olympics. He came home to coach near where he was born. His career was messy, controversial, and human — full of the mistakes and stubbornness and grief that make a life real. He was not the next Laettner. He was Greg Newton. And the Brotherhood includes him, too.

players.name: Carmen Wallace
players.tagline: Delaware’s Player of the Year. 2,004 career points. Survived the 4–15 disaster. Captain of the 1997 ACC championship team. Then built one of the most powerful sports agencies in the world.
players.now: NFL/NBA Certified Contract Advisor, Athletes First (Laguna Hills, CA); founding member; negotiated contracts for Rodgers, Palmer, Lewis, Matthews
players.drafted: Did not play professionally — went directly into sports management
players.stat: 4 Duke seasons • 82 games • 3.4 PPG • 43.3% 3PT • Team Captain 1997 • ACC Champions

sources.label: FOX Sports: NFL Agent Carmen Wallace Draft Blog (2014)
Duke Alumni Magazine: Hoop Profiles — Carmen Wallace
Tower Hill School: Forum Speaker Series (2014)
Duke Basketball Report: Turning Points — Duke 1997 Part I
Sports Reference: Carmen Wallace college stats
NBPA Agent Directory: Carmen Wallace

nba.postCareer: Founding member and NFL/NBA Certified Contract Advisor at Athletes First; previously Marketing Specialist at Steinberg Moorad & Dunn (1997-2001); clients include Carson Palmer, Aaron Rodgers, Ray Lewis, Clay Matthews, Miles Austin

bio.road:
Carmen Wallace grew up in Wilmington, Delaware, and attended Tower Hill School, a small private school where basketball was not supposed to produce Division I talent. Wallace made it produce something extraordinary. Over four years at Tower Hill, he scored 2,004 career points — the third-highest total in Delaware state history at the time, trailing only Brandywine’s Dexter Boney and Wilmington’s Erik Edwards. As a senior in 1993, he averaged 25.5 points per game, shot 58.2 percent from the field, 43.8 percent from three, and added 190 rebounds and 96 blocked shots at 6’5. He led Tower Hill to a 40–8 record over his final two seasons. The Delaware Sportswriters and Broadcasters Association voted him the 1993 Boys Basketball Player of the Year. He was first-team All-State for the second time.

Duke offered Wallace a basketball scholarship, and he took it. A kid from a small Delaware prep school was headed to Cameron Indoor Stadium to play for Coach K. It was an unlikely leap — from Tower Hill’s gymnasium to the ACC — but Wallace had earned it with 2,004 points and a state Player of the Year trophy.
bio.duke:
Carmen Wallace’s four years at Duke, from 1993–94 through 1996–97, were the most turbulent stretch in the modern history of the program. He arrived the year after the back-to-back championships and left the year the dynasty began rebuilding. He was there for all of it — the decline, the disaster, the comeback, and the first signs of what would become the second golden era.

Freshman year, 1993–94, Wallace barely played — 37 minutes in 28 games, never more than six minutes in a single contest. He appeared alongside Grant Hill, Antonio Lang, and Cherokee Parks on the team that lost to Arkansas in the national championship game. He saw the end of the first dynasty from the far end of the bench.

Sophomore year, 1994–95, was the catastrophe. Coach K underwent back surgery and missed most of the season, leaving assistant Pete Gaudet in charge. Duke went 13–18, including 2–14 in ACC play — the 4–15 stretch that remains the worst in program history. Wallace was there for every minute of it. He played through the losses, the empty seats in Cameron, the national embarrassment.

Junior year, 1995–96, Coach K returned and began the rebuild. When the roster thinned out, Krzyzewski went with the 6’5 Wallace at power forward out of sheer desperation. Wallace responded — in a key win over Florida State, he had 13 points, 11 rebounds, and 2 blocks, playing center against bigger men because Coach K believed he would compete. But he injured his knee in February and was lost for the rest of the season. Duke finished 18–13, barely making the NCAA Tournament on the strength of a Ricky Price buzzer-beater against Maryland.

Senior year, 1996–97, was the payoff. Wallace was named team captain — the leader of a squad that now included Trajan Langdon, Roshown McLeod, Chris Carrawell, and a young Steve Wojciechowski. Duke won the ACC regular-season championship. The rebuild was complete. Carmen Wallace, the kid from Tower Hill who had endured the worst of it, was captain of the team that brought Duke back. Over 82 career games, he averaged 3.4 points and 1.7 rebounds while shooting 43.3 percent from three. The numbers don’t tell the story. The captaincy does.
bio.after:
Wallace graduated from Duke in 1997 with degrees in history and sociology. He did not pursue professional basketball. Instead, he went directly into the sports industry, joining Steinberg Moorad & Dunn — one of the most powerful sports agencies in America — as a marketing specialist. His first major client connection was Drew Bledsoe, the New England Patriots quarterback and first overall pick of the 1993 NFL Draft. Wallace watched Bledsoe not as a fan but as an agent, analyzing plays and injuries with the eye of someone whose livelihood depended on the quarterback’s health.

In February 2001, Wallace became a founding member of Athletes First, which would grow into one of the largest and most influential sports agencies in the world. He had been there from the very first draft class — the 2001 draft that included first-round picks Reggie Wayne and Jamal Reynolds. Over more than two decades at Athletes First, Wallace has assisted with contract negotiations for some of the biggest names in professional sports: Carson Palmer, Aaron Rodgers, Ray Lewis, Clay Matthews, and Miles Austin among them. He has been present at more than seventeen NFL Drafts from the inside, shepherding first-round picks from the green room at Radio City Music Hall to their first professional contracts.
bio.now:
Carmen Wallace is now an NFL and NBA Certified Contract Advisor at Athletes First, based in Laguna Hills, California. His role encompasses salary cap strategy, marketing, and full-service client representation. He became NBA-certified in 2024, expanding his reach from the football field to the basketball court — a full-circle return to the sport where his career began.

In 2024, his alma mater Tower Hill School honored him with its Distinguished Alumni Award, recognizing a career that began with 2,004 points in a Delaware prep school gymnasium and led to negotiating contracts worth hundreds of millions of dollars. He has spoken at Tower Hill’s Forum speaker series about recruiting, training, and placing athletes in professional sports — and about the importance of giving back.

When he blogs from the NFL Draft for FOX Sports, Wallace still thinks about Coach K. It makes me think back to something Coach K used to say to us before games, he wrote. I was captain of the Duke basketball team way back in 1997. That sentence — casual, almost throwaway — contains the entire arc. He was captain of the Duke basketball team. Not in a championship year, not in a season anyone remembers. He was captain in the year Duke came back.

Carmen Wallace scored 2,004 points in a Delaware prep school. He went to Duke on a scholarship and endured the worst four-year stretch in program history. He played power forward at 6’5 because Coach K needed him to. He tore a knee and missed the end of one season. He came back and was named captain of the team that won the 1997 ACC regular-season championship. He graduated, walked into the sports agency business, and helped build Athletes First from its founding into one of the most powerful agencies in the world. He has negotiated contracts for Aaron Rodgers, Carson Palmer, and Ray Lewis. He has been in the green room at the NFL Draft seventeen times. The kid from Tower Hill who averaged 3.4 points at Duke now represents athletes whose contracts are measured in the hundreds of millions. The Brotherhood is not just about the players who scored the most points. It is about the ones who stayed when staying was hardest. Carmen Wallace stayed. And then he built an empire.

players.name: Steve Wojciechowski
players.tagline: Heart and hustle in human form.
players.now: Assistant Coach, Utah Jazz
players.stat: 128 games • 505 ast • 203 stl • NABC DPOY 1998 • McDonald’s All-American

bio.road:
Steven Michael Wojciechowski — pronounced woe-ju-HOW-skee, and known to everyone as Wojo — was born August 11, 1976, in Severna Park, Maryland. He was a first-generation college student. He attended Cardinal Gibbons School in Baltimore, where he played under Baltimore Catholic League head coach Ray Mullis and became one of the top high school point guards in the country. He was named to the 1994 McDonald’s All-American Team, playing against future Duke teammates Trajan Langdon and Ricky Price in the game.

He chose Duke. At 5-foot-11, he was never the most physically gifted player on the floor. But he played with a ferocity that bordered on possession. Dean Smith once expressed his admiration and reminded UNC fans — who had mocked Wojciechowski — that there was a reason he had wanted Wojo in Carolina Blue.
bio.duke:
Wojciechowski spent four seasons at Duke from 1994 to 1998, appearing in 128 games and starting 88. He was the engine of the Blue Devils’ backcourt: relentless on defense, an orchestrator on offense, and the emotional heartbeat of every team he played on. He ranks eighth in Duke history in both career assists (505) and career steals (203). His 82 steals in the 1997 season is the second-highest single-season total in program history.

His senior season in 1997–98 was the culmination. He was team captain, leading Duke to a 32–4 record and a No. 1 ranking in the final AP regular season poll. The Blue Devils reached the South Regional final, where they lost to eventual national champion Kentucky 86–84 — a heartbreaker that came down to the final possessions. Wojciechowski was named the 1998 NABC Defensive Player of the Year, the highest individual honor of his career. He earned Second-Team All-ACC as a junior and Third-Team All-ACC as a senior, and was an Honorable Mention All-American.

He graduated with a bachelor’s degree in sociology in 1998. After graduation, he played professional basketball in Poland for one year before realizing his future was on the sideline, not the court.
bio.after:
Wojciechowski returned to Duke in 1999, initially working in a business role and helping with Duke basketball radio broadcasts before Krzyzewski offered him a coaching position. He became an assistant coach and was promoted to associate head coach in 2008. Over 14 seasons on the Duke bench, his record as part of the coaching staff was 441–92. He primarily coached the frontcourt, and two of his players — Shane Battier and Shelden Williams — won the NABC National Defensive Player of the Year. Others who developed under his guidance include Carlos Boozer, Josh McRoberts, Jabari Parker, Mason Plumlee, Miles Plumlee, Rodney Hood, and Lance Thomas. The Blue Devils won NCAA championships in 2001 and 2010 during his tenure.

He also served as a court coach and scout for USA Basketball from 2006 to 2014, helping lead the U.S. to Olympic gold medals in Beijing (2008) and London (2012), working alongside LeBron James, Kobe Bryant, and Marquette alumnus Dwyane Wade.

On April 1, 2014, Marquette hired Wojciechowski as its 17th head coach, replacing Buzz Williams. He established his ‘Win Every Day’ philosophy and assembled nationally ranked recruiting classes. His best season was 2018–19, when Marquette went 24–10 and qualified for the NCAA Tournament. But in seven seasons, he compiled a 124–93 record with two NCAA Tournament appearances and zero tournament victories. With no Big East titles and an 0–2 NCAA Tournament record, Marquette fired him at the conclusion of the 2020–21 season.

A former Duke assistant, Chris Carrawell, who coached alongside Wojo at Marquette, once asked him whether he wanted to win Friday’s practice or Saturday’s game — a comment about the intensity Wojo brought every single day. It was both his greatest strength and, perhaps, his limitation as a head coach.
bio.now:
After being fired from Marquette, Wojciechowski and his wife Lindsay — a Utah native — moved to Park City, Utah. He took time away from basketball. He has joked that he briefly considered teaching hot yoga. But Wojo is a lifer. In June 2023, he was named head coach of the Salt Lake City Stars, the Utah Jazz’s G League affiliate. He compiled a 41–17 record over two seasons, led the Stars to consecutive playoff appearances, and secured the franchise’s first-ever playoff victory on April 1, 2025, over the Rio Grande Valley Vipers.

On June 6, 2025, Jazz head coach Will Hardy announced that Wojciechowski would join the Jazz coaching staff as a full-time assistant for the 2025–26 NBA season. Hardy called him ‘an incredible teacher.’ Wojo is now an NBA assistant coach at age 49, following the same G-League-to-NBA pipeline that Quin Snyder — another Duke point guard — traveled before him.

Wojciechowski lives in Park City with Lindsay and their two sons, Jack and Charlie. His coaching record spans Duke, Marquette, the G League, and now the NBA — a career built on the same relentless intensity he brought to every Duke game as a 5-foot-11 point guard who Dean Smith wanted at North Carolina. His story is the Brotherhood’s purest coaching arc: the player who never had the body but always had the fire, who spent 20 years under Krzyzewski and is now building his own legacy, one level at a time.

Duke’s Brotherhood: Where Are They Now? • Profile • March 2026

nba.postCareer: Assistant Coach, Utah Jazz

players.name: Trajan Langdon
players.tagline: The Alaskan Assassin.
players.now: President of Basketball Operations, Detroit Pistons
players.stat: Duke all-time 3PT leader • 2x EuroLeague champ • Final Four MVP • also drafted by MLB Padres

bio.road:
During winter in Anchorage, Alaska, the sun can set by 3:45 in the afternoon. In high school, Trajan Langdon would go outside three hours before a 7 p.m. tipoff. In the dark, he would think about that night’s game. Who he was playing. How they would guard him. He tried to visualize how it would unfold — a technique his father had taught him. ‘Once you get in the game and it’s happening, it will feel like you’ve already played before and you won’t be as stressed, and you will react a lot quicker,’ Langdon said. ‘So why wouldn’t you do that to the mind as well?’

Trajan Shaka Langdon was born May 13, 1976, in Palo Alto, California, and moved to Anchorage as a young child. His mother, Gladys, is a social worker. His father, Dr. Steve Langdon, is a professor of anthropology at the University of Alaska Anchorage who earned his Ph.D. from Stanford in 1977. His life’s work has been the study of the Tlingit people of southeastern Alaska. He authored The Native People of Alaska. Trajan traveled with his father on anthropological trips through southeastern Alaska, into villages where basketball heritages go back to the early days of the sport. His father told Sports Illustrated: ‘The Tlingits are a historically aggressive people. They had armor and everything. Their villages have basketball heritages that go back to the early days of the sport.’ The anthropologist’s view was also the basketball player’s view: Steve Langdon had played among the Tlingits on the Craig town team in the 1970s.

His first name comes from a Roman emperor. His middle name, Shaka, from a Zulu king. His father entered him into track and field and free-throw shooting competitions. Steve prepared pregame meals — grilled chicken, mac and cheese, peas — served four hours before game time. ‘He had done his research,’ Langdon said.

At East Anchorage High School, Langdon set the state 4A record with 2,200 career points, was a three-time Alaska State Player of the Year (the first to win it more than twice), led East to three consecutive state championships, and earned McDonald’s All-American honors (winning the three-point contest). He received the Dial Award as the nation’s top male student-athlete. The Alaska Sports Hall of Fame: he ‘single-handedly raised the profile of Alaska high school basketball in the Lower 48 states.’

Before Langdon, no Alaskan male had ever played for a Top 20 college program. Sports Illustrated sent a reporter in January 1994 and captured the absurdity: an assistant coach from the Midwest had flown 4,000 miles to watch practice in silence. Bobby Knight, Krzyzewski, and Dean Smith were all spotted in Anchorage — SI reported top coaches had been found ‘in library carrels poring over the November 1991 issue of National Geographic.’

He was also a gifted baseball player. A scout discovered Langdon playing catch with his father in a parking lot during his Duke recruiting visit. The San Diego Padres drafted him in the sixth round of the 1994 MLB Draft — ahead of future All-Stars Carl Pavano and Plácido Polanco. He signed for $230,000 and spent three summers in the Padres’ minor league system while at Duke. In 12 high school baseball games as a senior, he hit .333 with four home runs and struck out eleven batters in the league championship.
bio.duke:
Langdon redshirted the disastrous 1994–95 season (Coach K’s back surgery, Duke’s 13–18 collapse) due to a major knee injury. He then played four seasons, becoming one of the purest shooters in program history. He left Duke as the school’s all-time leader in three-point field goals (broken by J.J. Redick in 2006). He shot 90% from the free-throw line. Two-time First-Team All-ACC (1998, 1999), consensus Second-Team All-American in 1999. Dual degrees in mathematics and history.

The 1998–99 season was the peak. Duke went 37–2, tying the school record. They swept the ACC at 16–0 — the first undefeated ACC campaign in league history. Alongside Elton Brand, William Avery, Chris Carrawell, and Shane Battier, Langdon led one of the most talented teams college basketball had ever seen to the national championship game.

Then the final two minutes against Connecticut. Langdon hit a long three to cut it to 73–72 with 1:40 left. He had already carried Duke with consecutive threes and a four-point play. UConn answered. It was 75–74. Coach K, who had a timeout to spend, declined. He put the ball in Langdon’s hands. ‘Absolutely. Positively. I want Trajan Langdon to take that shot,’ Krzyzewski said. ‘Win or lose with Trajan Langdon. I’ll walk down any road with Trajan Langdon, and I’m proud of Trajan Langdon.’

But Ricky Moore, the tournament’s best defender, was waiting. Moore funneled Langdon into three defenders. Langdon tried to drive — driving was never his forte. He traveled. 5.4 seconds left. ‘I knew it was him against me and my will to win was going to take over,’ Moore said. On the final possession, three UConn defenders converged. Langdon tried to get off a 17-footer, got tripped up, and Moore stripped the ball cleanly. Langdon fell as the buzzer sounded. UConn 77, Duke 74.

In the locker room, Langdon’s response was a masterclass in grace: ‘I brought the ball up and three people were there. I tried to get off a shot but I got tripped up a little. It was a clean strip. Everything about the game was clean. We got beat.’ No excuses. The kid who visualized games in the dark carried that loss for the rest of his career — and turned it into fuel.
bio.after:
Cleveland selected Langdon 11th overall in 1999 — the first Alaskan in the NBA. Three seasons, 119 games, 5.4 ppg. A pure shooter in a league that demanded more. Casual fans remembered him as another Duke lottery bust.

Then he went to Europe and became a star. Benetton Treviso in Italy (15.2 ppg his first year overseas), Efes Pilsen in Turkey, Dynamo Moscow, and then CSKA Moscow. With CSKA: two EuroLeague championships (2006, 2008), Final Four MVP 2008, three All-EuroLeague selections, All-Decade Team (2001–10). He shot 51% from three in his final season. A European scouting report: ‘Trajan Langdon proved again to be one of the premier shooters on the Old Continent. His stroke is a beauty.’ In a 2006 exhibition game, he scored 17 against former Duke teammate Elton Brand’s Clippers. He retired June 2011, two days after CSKA’s ninth consecutive Russian championship. He also won bronze with the U.S. at the 1998 FIBA World Championship.
bio.now:
After retiring, Langdon scouted for the San Antonio Spurs (2012–15), became assistant GM of the Brooklyn Nets (March 2016), was named GM of the New Orleans Pelicans (May 2019), and on May 31, 2024, the Detroit Pistons hired him as President of Basketball Operations.

At 49, Langdon runs the Pistons. He is married to Tatiana. Duke Athletics Hall of Fame (2015). Alaska Sports Hall of Fame. His father still teaches anthropology in Anchorage, still studies the Tlingit.

Langdon’s story is the most geographically improbable in the entire Brotherhood. Born in Palo Alto, raised in Anchorage, drafted by a baseball team, named after a Roman emperor by a Stanford anthropologist who studied indigenous Alaskan tribes. He played catch with his father in a parking lot and a baseball scout changed his life. He flew 4,000 miles from home to play for Coach K. He set Duke’s three-point record and then, in the biggest moment of his career, couldn’t get off a three-point shot. He was labeled an NBA bust and became a European champion. Every chapter has been a reinvention, and every reinvention built on the same foundation: the kid in the Anchorage darkness, three hours before tipoff, visualizing the game. His father taught him that. The Tlingit villages taught his father. See it first, then do it.

nba.postCareer: President of Basketball Operations, Detroit Pistons

players.name: Ricky Price
players.tagline: His jumper saved the 1996 season.
players.now: Founder, Game Ready Skills & Development; speaker; author
players.stat: McDonald’s AA dunk champ 1994 • 3rd-Team All-ACC • 1,000+ pts • 10 yrs pro overseas

bio.road:
When people who grew up playing basketball in Los Angeles in the early 1990s talk about the best hoopers to come out of the area, one name always surfaces: Ricky Price. Not sometimes. Always. The Not In My House podcast called him ‘without question one of the greatest high school basketball players to ever come out of the Los Angeles area.’ His mid-range game was automatic. At 6-foot-6, with explosive athleticism and a scorer’s instinct, he was the complete package.

Price was born February 4, 1976, in Carson, California — a working-class city in the South Bay, fifteen miles south of downtown LA. At Carson High School, he averaged 28 points and 11 rebounds and was unanimously named a USA Today/AP/Parade Top 5 player nationally. Gold medal representing the U.S. in the Junior Olympics in Argentina. And at the 1994 McDonald’s All-American Game, he won the slam dunk contest — the first Duke player ever to do so, a lineage that would later include Gerald Henderson (2006), Grayson Allen (2014), and Zion Williamson (2018). At that same McDonald’s Game, he played alongside future Duke teammate Trajan Langdon and against future Duke teammate Steve Wojciechowski.

His recruitment came down to Duke and Kansas. Roy Williams was his first choice for a while. But as Price spent more time with Coach K, the decision clarified. When he broke the news to Williams, something remarkable happened: the relationship didn’t end. ‘It’s amazing because, of course, I hate Carolina,’ Price said. ‘I think if you’re a Duke guy, naturally, that’s embedded in your DNA. But I love Roy Williams. He’s one of the few honest guys in the game. We still talk frequently. After every Duke-Carolina game, I always make it a point to go see him and say hello. He still asks about my mom. Here we are 20 years later and he’s still asking about my mom and dad. That just shows you the character of the type of guy he is.’
bio.duke:
What happened to Ricky Price at Duke cannot be separated from what happened to Duke in 1994–95. He arrived as one of three prized freshmen — Price, Langdon, and Wojciechowski — joining a program expected to contend. Price had never had consistent coaching in high school, and Duke was supposed to change that. Instead, it got worse.

Coach K had undergone back surgery and rushed back. Twelve games in, with Duke 9–3, the pain became unbearable. Krzyzewski left on medical leave. Pete Gaudet, making just over $300 a week as the ‘restricted earnings’ coach, took over. Duke went 4–15 under Gaudet, finishing 13–18 and 2–14 in the ACC — dead last. Tommy Amaker, who had helped build the program as a player a decade earlier, saw the confidence draining: ‘Once we lost a few games, we lost our confidence. That’s where these guys miss Coach K.’

For Price, 3,000 miles from home: ‘It was very difficult as a freshman. You are still trying to find your way. As a freshman you want to lean on your head coach.’ He didn’t have that opportunity. ‘It was embarrassing. You don’t want to be part of a team that goes down as one of the worst in recent memory. It was a very difficult year and shows how important leadership is.’

But from that disaster, Price took a lesson that shaped the rest of his life. When Krzyzewski returned for 1995–96, the coach was different. ‘Prior to going out he did everything,’ Price recalled. ‘He was taking on too much of a burden. Post back surgery he trusted other guys to do other things. He let other guys step up and do their part.’ The principle of delegation — that leadership means trusting others — became the foundation of Price’s speaking career decades later.

With Krzyzewski back, Price’s sophomore season was his best at Duke: 14.2 ppg, career-high 28 against Virginia, a buzzer-beater to beat Maryland that Duke Basketball Report later credited with putting Duke back in the NCAAs. Third-Team All-ACC. He reached 1,000 career points. Duke won the ACC regular-season title — ‘only five short years after Ricky Price put Duke in the NCAAs.’

As a junior, a broken finger wrecked his jump shot. For a player whose mid-range game was his signature, the injury was devastating. He slumped to just over 9 ppg. Then senior year: academic probation after submitting a paper that wasn’t his. Suspended for the first semester, lost his starting role. His own accountability advice years later: ‘Take accountability for what you did, correct it, apologize to the people you need to apologize to, and then you’ve got to do some self-improvement and make sure that never happens again.’ He didn’t quit. He came back, played off the bench, helped Duke win its second straight ACC regular-season title. He graduated with a degree in sociology.
bio.after:
Price went undrafted in 1998. Not long after, he was playing 5,000 miles from the NBA — in Slovenia. Also the Netherlands, Germany, France, Finland, Asia. NBA preseason stints with the Miami Heat, Detroit Pistons, Orlando Magic, and Charlotte Bobcats — close enough to taste it, never close enough to stay. For about a decade, he saw different arenas across Europe and Asia and played in American pro leagues like the IBL and the CBA.

As a player, he was known for his above-the-rim game. But of all his accomplishments, he has said that learning to become a solid defensive player was his biggest achievement — the thing that kept him employed for a decade overseas when the dunk contest highlights faded. After retiring, he worked as VP of Operations for Worldwide Hoops Sports Marketing (representing overseas and NBA players) and as a Raycom Sports correspondent covering ACC basketball.
bio.now:
In 2010, Price founded Game Ready Skills and Development LLC in North Carolina. His first summer camp in Charlotte drew 150 kids. Former Duke players Ryan Kelly, Seth Curry, Rodney Hood, Andre Dawkins, and Tyler Thornton have appeared. But Price’s focus is development, not exposure: ‘My focus is 100% skills development. My job is to get them ready to get to that level. Unfortunately, a lot of our kids don’t have the necessary essentials, the basics, the fundamentals on how to play the game. How to pivot off your left and right foot, how to pump fake and one dribble, how to shoot a one dribble pull up. You’d be amazed how many kids can’t do that.’

He commands thousands of dollars per speaking engagement, drawing parallels between basketball teamwork and corporate environments, leaning heavily on the lessons from that devastating freshman year. His book, Celebrate Your Wins, has a foreword by Coach K. He still volunteers coaching middle schoolers — spending back-to-back five-hour Sunday sessions with varsity and JV players at schools that might otherwise get overlooked.

Ricky Price’s story is the most candid in the Transition Era. The five-star recruit from the LA basketball machine. First Duke player to win the McDonald’s dunk contest. Chose Duke over Kansas and kept Roy Williams’ friendship for life. Arrived at Duke and immediately endured the worst season in Krzyzewski history. Peaked as a sophomore, lost his jumper to a broken finger, lost his starting job to academic probation, and never quit. Played ten years on three continents. Turned the worst season of his life into the foundation of a speaking career.

Duke Basketball Report observed: ‘If he had the choice to do things over again, he’d still choose Duke.’ That’s the line that matters. Not the dunk contest trophy. Not the All-ACC season. Not the broken finger or the probation. The choice. He’d make it again.

nba.postCareer: Founder, Game Ready Skills & Development; speaker; author

players.name: Chris Carrawell
players.tagline: From freshman unknown to ACC POY.
players.now: Associate Head Coach, Duke Blue Devils
players.stat: 136 games • 1,455 pts • ACC POY 2000 • consensus All-American • 66 ACC wins (2nd all-time Duke)

bio.road:
Chris Carrawell was born November 25, 1977, and grew up in a rough inner-city neighborhood in north St. Louis. He was the oldest of his mother Joanne’s four children. He never knew his father — he would not meet him until 1999, his senior year at Duke. Joanne raised all four kids alone. When Chris was thirteen, a mentor named Vollomar entered his life and became the father figure he’d never had. ‘I’ve called him my father ever since I was 13,’ Carrawell later said.

Basketball was the way out, but the choice of where to play mattered. If he stayed in the public school system, he was ticketed for Beaumont High School, plagued by inner-city problems. Cardinal Ritter College Prep, a Catholic school just blocks from his mother’s home, offered an alternative. ‘There was no way I was going to Beaumont,’ Carrawell said. At Cardinal Ritter, he found coach Preston Thomas, who had built the program from 8–18 into a powerhouse. Thomas would say: ‘Chris is a survivor. He’s always been willing to do what had to be done.’

What Carrawell did was become one of the most dominant high school players in Missouri history. In four varsity years, the team went 80–13 with a three-year undefeated home streak. He won two state Class AA championships. He set school career records for points (2,087), rebounds (1,076), and assists (555). Conference Player of the Year twice, Gatorade Central Region Player of the Year, fourth-team Parade All-American. Cardinal Ritter made USA Today’s top-25 teams list twice. His teammates included future NBA players Loren Woods and Jahidi White.

Then the shoulders betrayed him. During senior year, Carrawell suffered chronic dislocations of both shoulders. Coach Thomas: ‘He was trying to play with one arm strapped to his body. But he was willing to sacrifice some of his scoring to help bolster Loren’s fragile confidence so that the team could be successful.’ By the time he arrived at Duke, he’d had three shoulder surgeries. He was nineteen years old.

Many recruiters backed off. Krzyzewski never wavered. ‘The first time I saw Chris, I thought he could be a great player. After he started having his shoulder problems, I still thought he could be really good.’ His choice came down to Duke or Saint Louis University. Friends told him he was ‘damaged goods’ and couldn’t make it at Duke — in basketball or academically. ‘He’ll be on the first plane ride back. He’ll be back home soon enough.’ Carrawell said the naysayers ‘actually kind of helped.’

‘For whatever reason, I don’t know what reason, Coach K stuck with me and believed in me in high school,’ Carrawell said. ‘So from there, I thought whenever my time started with Duke, I was going to give 100,000 percent for him.’
bio.duke:
To understand Carrawell at Duke, start with a single image: February 5, 1997, at Wake Forest. Duke’s freshman forward, six-foot-six, lined up to guard Wake’s Tim Duncan — six-foot-eleven, the best center in the country, the eventual No. 1 overall pick. Dick Vitale, calling the game on ESPN, marveled: ‘Look at Carrawell, you’ve got to give that kid a lot of credit! There he is, a freshman inside, playing against Duncan, head-to-head.’ The St. Louis Post-Dispatch’s Benjamin Hochman later noted: ‘Except it wasn’t head-to-head. Head-to-neck, maybe.’

Krzyzewski decided the key to beating Wake was to smother Duncan and dare someone else to beat them. Duncan still went 11-of-13 for 26 points — unstoppable when he got the ball — but managed only 7 rebounds (he averaged 14.7 for the season). Then, with Duke up three and 1:18 left, the play that defined Carrawell’s career: he drifted from Duncan, helped on a driving guard, and pinned the layup to the backboard. ‘I just got over a second late. I just waited, came over and blocked it.’ Duke won 73–68, snapping an eight-game losing streak to Wake. Duncan won ACC Player of the Year that season. Three years later, Carrawell would win the same award.

That freshman year, on a perimeter-shooting team, Carrawell led Duke in dunks with 22. He shot 57.6% from the field, ranked third in offensive rebounds, and played with a controlled fury that belied his numbers. Anthony Bonner, an NBA player from SLU, offered the perfect scouting report: ‘He kills you with his unselfishness.’

Shoulder surgery before his sophomore year. Four games missed with a strained back. He still scored in double figures 22 times, including 19 and 18 in two UNC games, as Duke went 32–4 and reached the Elite Eight (falling to Kentucky 86–84). Junior year: started every game of the 37–2 season — Duke’s best record ever, first 16–0 ACC season, the Elton Brand/Trajan Langdon team that reached the national championship game. Carrawell contributed 9.9 points and 130 assists (second on the team). Third-Team All-ACC.

Then the senior season. Brand (No. 1 pick), Langdon (11th), Avery, Burgess, Maggette — all gone. Krzyzewski called on Carrawell to be leader, go-to scorer, and defensive stopper, all at once. After losing their first two games, Duke won 16 straight and climbed to No. 3. Carrawell: 16.9 points, 6.1 rebounds, 3.2 assists, 35.6 minutes per game. ACC Player of the Year. Consensus First-Team All-American. ACC All-Defense. Senior captain. 29–5, ACC Tournament championship, Sweet Sixteen.

He is one of only two players in ACC history to play on four consecutive regular-season championship teams (alongside Nate James). He was the only player in each of Duke’s ACC-record 30 straight league wins. His 66 career ACC victories are the second-most by any Duke player. Four-year record: 133–24 overall, 58–6 ACC. Career totals: 1,455 points, 608 rebounds, 309 assists, 114 blocks.
bio.after:
The San Antonio Spurs drafted Carrawell 41st overall in 2000. He was waived before the season began and never played an NBA minute. With his retirement in 2007, he became the first player of the new millennium drafted into the NBA who never appeared in a regular-season game.

Instead, Carrawell spent seven years chasing basketball around the world. Italy for a year. The inaugural NBA D-League season with the Asheville Altitude (56 games, 7.3 ppg). Lithuania with the storied Žalgiris Kaunas. Germany. Australia, where he played for the Sydney Kings and averaged 13.1 points and 7.1 rebounds in 18 NBL games, scoring 15 in his debut. The Philippines with the Alaska Aces. The ABA, where he won the 2006 league MVP, the championship, and the championship MVP — a triple crown in a league almost nobody had heard of. The Netherlands in 2007, where his team lost in the finals. He retired at 29, having played professional basketball on four continents without ever playing an NBA game.

Then the long road back to Duke. Not as a coach — he crawled. 2007–08: Athletics Outreach Coordinator. 2008–10: graduate assistant and head team manager. 2010–11: assistant video coordinator and assistant strength and conditioning coach. He briefly worked with the Duke women’s team. ‘That wasn’t in my dreams,’ Carrawell said. ‘I never dreamed of being a coach. But the love for the game, the passion for the game and wanting to pay it forward has led to me coaching at Duke?’

The Springfield Armor (Nets D-League affiliate, three years). Then Marquette under his Duke teammate Steve Wojciechowski (four years) — where Carrawell reportedly asked Wojo whether he wanted to ‘win Friday’s practice or Saturday’s game,’ a pointed question about calibrating the Krzyzewski intensity for a different program.
bio.now:
In March 2018, Carrawell returned to Duke as an assistant under Krzyzewski. He helped develop Zion Williamson and RJ Barrett — the first freshman teammates in NCAA history to each average 20+ points — and Tre Jones (ACC Player of the Year and Defensive Player of the Year, 2020). In 2021, Krzyzewski promoted him to associate head coach for Coach K’s final season. ‘This is incredible,’ Carrawell said. ‘For Coach to have that trust in me, especially in his last year, is an honor and privilege. It is the equivalent of being named a captain my senior year because Coach doesn’t just hand these things out — I’ve had to earn it and work for it.’

He now holds the Morris Williams Associate Head Coach endowed chair under Jon Scheyer. In January 2025, when Scheyer was ill, Carrawell served as acting head coach at SMU, guiding the No. 4 Blue Devils to victory. He came out talkative and active — exactly how the 2000 ACC Player of the Year played.

At 47, Carrawell lives in Mebane, North Carolina, with his wife Keisha. Their older son Caleb is a sixth-year forward at Loyola Marymount. Their younger son is Christian. His mother, sister, and brother still live in St. Louis. He returns to recruit, and he’ll argue with anyone that the early-to-mid-’90s were the best years for St. Louis basketball — in one phone call with the Post-Dispatch, he rattled off 19 names of local hoopers without pausing for breath.

The arc of Chris Carrawell’s life is the Brotherhood’s purest meritocracy story. He grew up without a father in north St. Louis. Three shoulder surgeries by age 19. Called ‘damaged goods.’ Chose Duke anyway and gave 100,000 percent. Defended Tim Duncan as a freshman. Won the same award Duncan won. Drafted by an NBA team, never played a second. Seven years on four continents. Came back to Duke and took every unglamorous job they offered — outreach coordinator, team manager, video assistant, strength coach — because being in the building was enough. Worked his way from the Asheville Altitude to the Duke bench. Promoted to associate head coach for the most legendary final season in college basketball history.

As Hochman wrote: ‘It’s an amazing and inspiring story of a St. Louis kid who found a coach who believed in him as much as the kid believed in himself.’ Coach K stuck with damaged goods. And damaged goods became indestructible.

nba.postCareer: Associate Head Coach, Duke Blue Devils

players.name: Roshown McLeod
players.tagline: Coach K’s first transfer. Jersey City to St. John’s to Duke. First Team All-ACC. First-round pick. Then the year everything fell apart — and the long road back.
players.now: Vice President / Home Lending Manager, JPMorgan Chase (Georgia); completed Duke degree 2006
players.stat: 2 Duke seasons • 15.3 PPG/5.6 RPG (senior) • 1st Team All-ACC • 32-4 record • 20th pick • 113 NBA games

sources.label: Duke Basketball Report: Roshown McLeod’s Life Fell Apart at 25 (2020)
Duke Basketball Report: Jim’s Look at Duke Transfers, Part II (2021)
Indiana Athletics: Roshown McLeod coaching bio
Basketball Reference: Roshown McLeod career stats
Wikipedia: Roshown McLeod

teams.note: Signed but permanently injured; never played

highlights.note: Career-high 24 pts vs Orlando Magic (Nov 14, 2000)

coaching.team: Woodward Academy (Atlanta) + Next Level Players Development

nba.postCareer: VP / Home Lending Manager, JPMorgan Chase (Georgia); completed Duke degree 2006; AAU coach and basketball trainer

bio.road:
Roshown McLeod was born November 17, 1975, in Jersey City, New Jersey. He was raised primarily by his mother, Diane McLeod — known to everyone as Ms. Diane — in one of the toughest cities in the New York metro area. Jersey City basketball was a proving ground where talent was forged on outdoor courts and in cramped gymnasiums.

McLeod attended St. Anthony High School, the legendary program coached by Bob Hurley Sr. — the same Bob Hurley whose son Bobby had led Duke to back-to-back championships in 1991 and 1992. Under Hurley Sr., McLeod learned defense, discipline, toughness, and the understanding that basketball was a privilege earned through effort. He was tournament MVP as a senior — 18 points, 9 rebounds, and a key block in the championship game. The connection between St. Anthony’s and Duke ran through Bobby Hurley’s bloodline.

McLeod committed to St. John’s, staying close to home. Over two seasons with the Red Storm, he averaged 7.2 PPG and 5.8 RPG in 57 games — struggling to find consistent playing time. He announced he was transferring.

At the time, transfers to Duke were unheard of. Coach K didn’t take them. But the Hurley connection gave Krzyzewski information he wouldn’t have had otherwise. Roshown McLeod became the first transfer Coach K ever accepted. The door he opened would later be walked through by Dahntay Jones, Seth Curry, and Rodney Hood. Each became All-ACC. Each played in the NBA. McLeod was the one who proved a transfer could work at Duke.
bio.duke:
After sitting out 1995–96 under transfer rules, McLeod played two years at Duke, and the transformation was dramatic. The player who averaged 7.2 at St. John’s became one of the best forwards in the ACC.

Junior year, 1996–97: 12.1 PPG, 5.3 RPG, helping Duke win the ACC regular-season title and reach 24–9. He was part of the bridge generation — alongside Langdon, Wojciechowski, and Carrawell — that carried the program from the 4–15 disaster back to national relevance.

Senior year, 1997–98: the masterpiece. 15.3 PPG, 5.6 RPG, 49.4% from the field. Led Duke in scoring. First Team All-ACC. Honorable mention All-American. Duke went 32–4, won the ACC regular-season title. His best game: Senior Day against Vince Carter, Antawn Jamison, and North Carolina. Duke trailed by 17 in the second half. McLeod scored 23, led the comeback. Duke won 77–75. In the 1998 NCAA Tournament, he averaged 17.5 points across four games before Duke fell to Kentucky in the Elite Eight.

The kid from Jersey City who couldn’t crack the lineup at St. John’s had become a first-round pick from Duke. Coach K’s gamble on his first transfer paid off in full.
bio.after:
Atlanta selected McLeod 20th overall in 1998. Three NBA seasons: two with the Hawks, a stint with Philadelphia. 113 games, 7.2 PPG, 2.7 RPG. Career-high 24 against Orlando. In 2001–02, he signed with the Celtics, but a permanent injury ended his career before he played a game. He was twenty-five years old.

Then his mother died. Ms. Diane — the woman who raised him in Jersey City, the center of his world — was gone. In the same year that injury ended his career, McLeod lost the person who mattered most. He fell into a deep depression. He moved out of the house where his children lived. Fans see players for a couple of hours and think we know them. We knew nothing about the dark places McLeod went.

Years later, he published an open letter to his mother — raw, honest, deeply personal. A man in his forties reckoning with the loss that had broken him at twenty-five and the long road back.
bio.now:
McLeod rebuilt through coaching and eventually a career beyond basketball. Assistant at Fairfield under Ed Cooley. Next Level Players Development and AAU coaching in Atlanta. Indiana University staff under Tom Crean (2008–10). He replaced Dan Hurley — Bob Hurley Sr.’s other son — at St. Benedict’s Prep in Newark, closing a circle that connected Jersey City, Duke, and the Hurley family. He coached at Fellowship Christian School in Georgia.

He completed his Duke degree in 2006, returning to Durham to finish what he started. He transitioned to finance: lending manager at JPMorgan Chase, promoted to VP and home lending manager in Georgia. He has a son Anthony and a daughter Anisa.

Roshown McLeod was born in Jersey City and raised by his mother. He played for Bob Hurley Sr. at St. Anthony’s. He went to St. John’s and couldn’t break through. He transferred to Duke — the first transfer Coach K ever accepted — and became First Team All-ACC and a first-round pick. He scored 23 on Senior Day to lead a 17-point comeback against Vince Carter’s North Carolina. He played 113 NBA games. Then his mother died and an injury ended his career in the same year, and he fell into a darkness fans never saw. He published a letter to his mother and found his way back. He coached at Indiana and St. Benedict’s. He finished his Duke degree. He became a VP at JPMorgan Chase. The door he opened — the first transfer door — led to Seth Curry and Rodney Hood and a tradition of second chances. He was the first to prove you don’t have to start at Duke to belong to the Brotherhood. You just have to earn it. And he earned it.

players.name: Nate James
players.tagline: A Marine’s son. McDonald’s All-American. Five consecutive ACC titles — a record no one else holds. Senior captain of the 2001 champions. Three title rings. Twelve countries. Then the Brotherhood brought him home.
players.now: Associate Head Coach, Howard University (reunited with Kenny Blakeney); married to actress Bobbi Baker James
players.drafted: Undrafted — played professionally overseas in 12 countries
players.stat: 5 Duke seasons • 135 games • 1,116 pts • 12.3/5.2 senior • 3rd Team All-ACC • All-Defensive • 3 title rings

SI: Kenny Blakeney Gives New Opportunity to Fellow Duke Champ (2025)

highlights.note: Carolinas Basketball League MVP
Philadelphia 76ers training camp invite

nba.postCareer: Associate Head Coach at Howard University; co-founded summer basketball camp in Durham with Chris Carrawell (12 years); assisted USA Basketball Olympic camps 2008, 2012, 2016

bio.road:
Nate James was born on August 7, 1977, in Washington, D.C. He was the son of a Marine Sergeant Major, and like all military kids, he grew up everywhere and nowhere — Quantico, Virginia; Albany, Georgia; San Diego, California; and back to Washington, D.C. before his father retired. The constant was basketball. The constant was discipline. The constant was the understanding, absorbed from a military household, that you showed up, you did the work, and you didn’t complain.

James attended Saint John’s Catholic Prep in Buckeystown, Maryland, outside Frederick. By his senior year he was one of the best players in the region — a 6’6 swingman who could shoot, defend, and lead. He was named a 1996 McDonald’s All-American, which alone would have been enough to mark him as elite. But at the McDonald’s All-American Game, James did something that would become a quiet signature of his career: he won the Three-Point Shooting Contest. The kid Coach K would later describe as a high school center — meaning he had to reinvent himself to play at Duke — arrived in Durham as the best shooter in his All-American class. He also received the McDonald’s All-American Sportsmanship Award, the kind of honor that doesn’t make SportsCenter but tells you everything about who a player is.
bio.duke:
James enrolled at Duke in 1996. He would spend five seasons in Durham — freshman year, a medical redshirt sophomore season in 1997–98 when he appeared in only six games, and then three full years from 1998–99 through 2000–01. Over that span, he played in 135 games, started 64, scored 1,116 points, made 111 three-pointers, and grabbed 500 rebounds while shooting 47.3 percent from the floor.

The statistic that defines Nate James at Duke is one no other player in Atlantic Coast Conference history can claim: the Blue Devils won outright or shared the ACC regular-season championship in each of the five years James was on the roster. Five for five. No other player in the league’s history has been on that many consecutive regular-season championship teams. It is a record that may never be matched.

As a reserve during the 1998–99 season, James contributed 5.0 points and 2.6 rebounds in 14.7 minutes per game off the bench, helping Duke reach the 1999 Final Four. The following year, 1999–2000, he was named co-captain alongside Chris Carrawell and Shane Battier, moved into the starting lineup, and averaged 11.0 points and 4.5 rebounds. The jump was dramatic — from role player to starter, from contributor to leader.

His senior year, 2000–01, was the culmination. James was again named co-captain and started 29 of 39 games, leading the team with averages of 12.3 points and 5.2 rebounds per game. He was named Third Team All-ACC and selected to the ACC All-Defensive Team. Duke went 35–4 and won the national championship, beating Arizona 82–72 in the title game in Minneapolis. James played 20 minutes in that championship game, contributing on both ends as Duke completed one of the most dominant tournament runs in NCAA history.

Coach K would later say of James: I’ve seen him grow from being a high school center, to becoming one of the most selfless leaders in Duke basketball history. That word — selfless — is the key. James played alongside Shane Battier, Jason Williams, Mike Dunleavy Jr., Carlos Boozer, and Chris Duhon. He was not the most talented player on his team. He was the most important leader. There is a difference, and Coach K knew it.
bio.after:
James graduated from Duke in 2001 with a degree in sociology and a minor in African-American studies. He was not drafted by the NBA. He tried out for the Washington Wizards and Sacramento Kings summer league teams. He was named MVP of the Carolinas Basketball League in 2003. He earned an invitation to the Philadelphia 76ers training camp in 2004. The NBA never quite opened its door.

So Nate James saw the world. Over six years as a professional, he played in twelve countries: Bosnia and Herzegovina, Brazil, France, Germany, Greece, Holland, Hungary, Italy, Japan, the Philippines, Poland, and Russia. In the Netherlands, he won a league championship. In Hungary, he teamed with former Duke teammate Casey Sanders to win another. He was a globe-trotting Blue Devil, carrying the Duke standard across four continents.

And during the offseasons, he always came back to Durham. James co-founded a summer basketball camp with Chris Carrawell that ran for twelve years, teaching kids the fundamentals of the game and of life. Even while playing in Poland or Russia, Durham was home. Duke was home. The Brotherhood was home.
bio.now:
In 2007, James retired from playing and returned to Duke. He started as an assistant strength and conditioning coach — the bottom rung. He was promoted to director of basketball operations in 2009. When Johnny Dawkins left to become head coach at Stanford, James was elevated to a full assistant coach role for the 2009–10 season. Duke won the national championship that year, making Nate James the first person in program history to win titles at Duke as both a player and a coach.

He would stay on Coach K’s staff for thirteen years in total, rising from strength coach to special assistant to assistant coach to associate head coach — the last promotion coming in 2018 alongside Jon Scheyer. During that span, the Blue Devils won two more national championships (2010 and 2015), five ACC Tournament titles, and produced twenty-one first-round NBA Draft picks. James helped recruit and develop Kyrie Irving, Jayson Tatum, Zion Williamson, Brandon Ingram, the Plumlee brothers, Jahlil Okafor, and Marvin Bagley III. He assisted with USA Basketball Olympic training camps in 2008, 2012, and 2016. His three national championship rings — one as a player, two as a coach — are more than anyone in Duke history except Coach K himself.

In April 2021, exactly twenty years to the day after he played 20 minutes in the 2001 championship game, James was named head coach at Austin Peay State University. He went 21–39 in two seasons before being fired in March 2023. The head coaching dream had not worked out. For many former players, that would have been the end.

But in October 2025, the Brotherhood came calling. Kenny Blakeney — himself a Duke champion from the 1992 team, and one of the profiles in this project — hired Nate James as associate head coach at Howard University. Two Duke champions, separated by a decade as players, reunited on the same bench at an HBCU in their shared hometown of Washington, D.C. Howard had already built its staff with Duke DNA: Tyler Thornton had been an assistant before joining Jon Scheyer’s staff in Durham. Now James joined Blakeney, and the Bison — who had reached the NCAA Tournament in two of the previous three seasons — had two Brotherhood coaches on the same sideline.

James is married to actress Bobbi Baker James. They have two sons, Nathanial Drake James III and Michael Dash James. He still visits Cameron Indoor Stadium regularly, checking in on the program that shaped him.

Nate James was a Marine’s son who grew up everywhere. He won the McDonald’s All-American Three-Point Contest and the Sportsmanship Award in the same weekend. He spent five years at Duke and was part of five consecutive ACC championship teams — a record no one else in ACC history can claim. He was the senior captain of the 2001 national champions, playing alongside Battier and Williams and Boozer and Dunleavy while Coach K called him the most selfless leader in Duke basketball history. He played professional basketball in twelve countries on four continents. He came back to Duke and worked his way from strength coach to associate head coach, winning two more national championships along the way. He was hired as a head coach. He was fired as a head coach. And then Kenny Blakeney called, because that is what the Brotherhood does. It catches you. Nate James is coaching basketball in Washington, D.C., the city where a Marine’s son first learned the game, with a fellow Duke champion on the same bench. Three rings. Twelve countries. Five ACC titles as a player. One phone call from a brother. The Brotherhood always brings you home.

players.name: Shane Battier
players.tagline: The No-Stat All-Star. The soul of the 2001 champions.
players.now: Operating Partner, Garnett Station; minority owner, Tampa Bay Rays
players.stat: 146 games • Naismith POY • 3x DPOY • 2x NBA champ • Final Four MOP

bio.road:
Shane Battier grew up in Birmingham, Michigan, the only child of a Black father and a white mother. By seventh grade at Detroit Country Day School, he was already 6-foot-4. A year later, 6-foot-7. He was also the only biracial kid at Country Day, a prestigious private school in the Detroit suburbs. Michael Lewis, who profiled Battier in a landmark 2009 New York Times Magazine piece, captured the tension: the inner-city AAU kids treated Battier as a suburban kid with a white game, while his suburban teammates treated him like a visitor from another planet.

Battier turned that in-between status into fuel. He led Country Day to three consecutive Class B state championships from 1995 to 1997, earned Michigan Mr. Basketball as a senior, and graduated with a 3.96 GPA while being named the school’s outstanding student. He was the Naismith Prep Player of the Year in 1997 — the first signal of the award he’d bookend four years later.

Dean Smith recruited him for North Carolina. Battier chose Duke instead, beginning one of the most decorated four-year careers in the program’s history.
bio.duke:
Battier was the prototype of the modern do-everything forward before the term existed. At 6-foot-8, 220 pounds, he could guard every position on the floor, shoot threes at a 41.6% clip, post up smaller defenders, and anchor a defense. The Cameron Crazies honored his relentless charge-taking with a chant: ‘Who’s your daddy? Battier!’

His freshman year (1997–98) was solid but ended in heartbreak: Duke blew a 17-point lead to Kentucky in the regional finals, a loss to the eventual national champion. As a sophomore, the Blue Devils reached the Final Four before falling to Connecticut. The program was building toward something. It arrived in 2000–01.

That senior season was one of the greatest individual years in Duke history. Battier averaged 19.9 points, 7.3 rebounds, 2.3 blocks, and 2.1 steals while leading the Blue Devils to a 35–4 record, an undefeated ACC regular season (16–0), and the national championship. In the title game against Arizona, he scored 21 points with six rebounds and four assists. He was named Final Four MOP, consensus National Player of the Year (Naismith, Wooden, AP, Rupp), NABC Defensive Player of the Year for the third straight time, and Academic All-American of the Year.

He is the only player in history to win both the Naismith Prep Player of the Year and the Naismith College Player of the Year. Duke retired his No. 31, and he was inducted into the College Basketball Hall of Fame in 2019. Over 146 career games: 13.6 points, 6.1 rebounds, a 50/41/78 shooting line, and a career record of 131–15. He graduated with a degree in religion.
bio.after:
Memphis took Battier sixth overall in the 2001 draft. He spent five seasons with the Grizzlies (2001–06), becoming their defensive anchor and averaging 10.6 points, 4.8 rebounds, and 1.0 blocks. But his value transcended the box score — a theme that would define his entire professional life.

The Houston Rockets acquired Battier in 2006, and it was there that his statistical legend crystallized. The Rockets made him their only player with access to the team’s highly sophisticated analytical data on opposing players. Battier studied film and data obsessively, using it to contest shots from the exact angles where opponents were least comfortable. He earned NBA All-Defensive Second Team honors in both 2008 and 2009. Michael Lewis’ 2009 profile, ‘The No-Stat All-Star,’ demonstrated that Battier’s teams consistently performed better with him on the court despite his unremarkable counting stats. Harvard Business School turned his approach into a 2010 case study.

In 2011, Battier signed with the Miami Heat and stepped into the most important role of his career: the glue guy on a super-team. Playing alongside LeBron James, Dwyane Wade, and Chris Bosh, Battier provided the defense, the corner threes, and the veteran steadiness that championship teams need from their supporting cast. The Heat won back-to-back titles in 2012 and 2013. Battier retired after the 2013–14 season at age 35. In his final season, the NBA named him the inaugural Teammate of the Year. Career totals: 977 games, 8.6 points, 4.2 rebounds, 38.4% from three.
bio.now:
Battier briefly worked for ESPN as an analyst before the Heat hired him as their Vice President of Analytics and Basketball Development in February 2017. He served until 2021, when he transitioned to a consulting role with the organization.

Since then, Battier has built a diversified post-basketball portfolio. He became an operating partner at Garnett Station Partners, a private equity firm, in December 2023. He serves on the board of directors at Yext, a tech company. He speaks regularly at the MIT Sloan Sports Analytics Conference and corporate events. He has advised Intel on their sports technology division.

In October 2025, Battier joined the ownership group of the Tampa Bay Rays as a minority owner, part of the Patrick Zalupski-led consortium that purchased the franchise. It marked his first foray into professional sports ownership.

In 2010, Battier and his wife Heidi — his high school sweetheart from Country Day — founded the Battier Take Charge Foundation. The organization provides scholarships, mentorship, and leadership training for underserved youth in Miami, Houston, Detroit, and Camden, New Jersey. As of 2025, more than 84% of Battier Scholars are first-generation college students, and over 95% have completed a bachelor’s degree or higher. In 2024, the foundation received a $1.4 million grant from the KPMG US Foundation. After relocating to Charlotte, North Carolina, in mid-2024, Battier established a new outpost for the foundation there.

At 47, Battier lives in Charlotte with Heidi. He is, in many ways, the Brotherhood’s most complete post-playing success story: championships at every level, a pioneering role in basketball analytics, a thriving business career, a meaningful philanthropic legacy, and now a stake in a professional sports franchise.

The kid who navigated between two worlds in suburban Detroit — too Black for one, too white for the other — found a way to belong everywhere. His No. 31 hangs in the Cameron rafters, and his influence hangs over every team that values defense, data, and the things that don’t show up in the box score.

highlights.note: NBA All-Rookie First Team
NBA Champion with Miami Heat (started Game 7 of Finals)
2x NBA Champion with Miami Heat

nba.postCareer: Operating Partner, Garnett Station; minority owner, Tampa Bay Rays

players.name: Elton Brand
players.tagline: From Dunbar Heights to the #1 pick. From Peekskill’s favorite son to the 76ers’ front office. The quiet power forward who changed Duke’s recruiting model forever.
players.now: General Manager, Philadelphia 76ers
players.stat: 16.2 ppg • 8.9 rpg • 37–2 • first 16–0 ACC season • Naismith POY

bio.road:
Elton Tyron Brand was born on March 11, 1979, in Cortlandt Manor, New York, and raised by his mother Daisy Brand and his brother Arthur in the Dunbar Heights Housing Complex in Peekskill — a small city in the Hudson Valley region about an hour north of New York City. His mother encouraged him to channel his natural size and athleticism into basketball rather than other pursuits, and he began playing around age 10 at local courts and recreation leagues.

At thirteen, Brand enrolled at Peekskill High School and was immediately placed on the varsity basketball roster. Under coach Lou Panzanaro, he became a dominant force unlike anything the small-town program had ever seen. He averaged 40 points and 20 rebounds per game by some accounts — numbers so absurd they read like a typo. He led the Peekskill Indians to back-to-back New York State Class B championships in 1995 and 1996, becoming a cult hero in a city that had never produced anything like him. When former New York Governor George Pataki — a Peekskill native himself — was once asked about being the town’s favorite son, he reportedly answered that he wasn’t the favorite. Brand was.

As a senior in 1996-97, Brand averaged 25.9 points and 16.2 rebounds while leading Peekskill to its second straight state title. He was named New York State Mr. Basketball, selected as a McDonald’s All-American and First-Team Parade All-American, and was consistently ranked among the top five players in the country. On the AAU circuit, he played alongside future NBA players Lamar Odom and Ron Artest — a level of competition that prepared him for anything college basketball could throw at him.

Brand chose Duke over a flood of offers, arriving alongside Shane Battier, William Avery, and Chris Burgess in the fall of 1997 — a recruiting class that would fundamentally change the trajectory of the program. His reasoning was characteristically practical. “I didn’t want to stay home — I wanted to get away from the area,” he told ESPN years later. “I picked Duke for academics as well as basketball. I knew with a degree from Duke I could get my mom a house if basketball didn’t pan out.” The kid from Dunbar Heights was thinking three moves ahead, even at seventeen.
bio.duke:
Brand’s freshman season in 1997-98 was interrupted by a broken foot in December that cost him about six weeks. He still started 18 of the 21 games he played, averaging 13.4 points and 7.3 rebounds while shooting 59.2% from the field. Duke went 32-4 and won the ACC regular season title before falling in the Elite Eight. The glimpses of Brand’s dominance were tantalizing — a 6-foot-9, 260-pound freight train who moved with unexpected fluidity and had soft hands around the rim. He was named USA Basketball Male Athlete of the Year as a freshman, a remarkable distinction for a player who missed a quarter of the season.

Then came the sophomore year, and Brand became the best player in college basketball.

The 1998-99 Duke Blue Devils went 37-2 and recorded the first perfect 16-0 ACC regular season in conference history. Brand was the engine. He averaged 17.7 points and 9.8 rebounds per game while shooting 61.8% from the field, leading a roster that also featured Langdon, Battier, Avery, Maggette, Carrawell, and James. He was a force of nature in the paint — broad-shouldered, relentless on the glass, with a growing arsenal of post moves that made him virtually unguardable one-on-one. He recorded 15 double-doubles and was the ACC’s most dominant interior presence since Tim Duncan.

Brand swept every major National Player of the Year award: the Naismith, the Wooden, the AP, and the USBWA. He was named ACC Player of the Year and consensus First-Team All-American. When Duke reached the national championship game, Brand was the reason. He had 15 points and 13 rebounds in the Final Four semifinal win over Michigan State, overpowering a frontcourt that included future pros Mateen Cleaves and A.J. Granger.

The title game against UConn was the bitter end. Richard Hamilton scored 27 points, Khalid El-Amin made clutch plays down the stretch, and the Huskies won 77-74 to claim their first national championship. Brand had 15 points and 13 rebounds but struggled against UConn’s length and physicality. Duke’s 32-game winning streak was snapped. The 37-2 season — one of the greatest in college basketball history — ended one game short.

Brand, along with Avery and Maggette, then made the decision that would reshape Duke basketball forever: all three declared for the 1999 NBA Draft, making them the first players under Mike Krzyzewski to leave school early. It was a seismic moment. K had built his program on four-year players and the values of commitment and development. Brand’s departure — along with his classmates — forced Krzyzewski to adapt his recruiting model to the one-and-done era. The modern Duke, with its annual parade of lottery picks and freshman stars, traces directly back to the spring of 1999 and the exodus that Brand led.
bio.after:
The Chicago Bulls selected Brand with the first overall pick in the 1999 NBA Draft. He justified the selection immediately, averaging 20.1 points and 10.0 rebounds as a rookie while shooting 49.4% from the field. He shared the NBA Rookie of the Year award with Houston’s Steve Francis — the first co-winners since Grant Hill and Jason Kidd in 1995. Through much of his second season, Brand was hailed as the linchpin of a possible new Chicago dynasty, averaging 20.1 points and 10.1 rebounds with 3.9 offensive boards per game.

But the Bulls were rebuilding, and in June 2001 they traded Brand to the Los Angeles Clippers for the draft rights to Tyson Chandler. It was one of the most lopsided trades in NBA history. In LA, Brand became the franchise’s best player and its most respected citizen. He made back-to-back All-Star teams in 2002 and 2006, earned All-NBA Second Team honors in 2005-06, and led the Clippers to the second round of the playoffs for the first time in franchise history. He averaged 20+ points per game in four of his seven Clippers seasons and became one of the most efficient power forwards in the league, combining old-school post play with an increasingly refined mid-range game.

In the summer of 2007, Brand ruptured his Achilles tendon — the kind of injury that ended careers in that era. He recovered, but opted out of his contract with the Clippers in a controversial move that shook the franchise. In July 2008, he signed a five-year, $82 million deal with the Philadelphia 76ers, choosing Philly because it was the closest NBA city to his hometown of Peekskill. But the Achilles was never the same. A dislocated shoulder in December 2008 ended his first Sixers season early. Over five seasons in Philadelphia, Brand was productive but diminished — a shell of the All-Star he’d been in LA, playing through injuries on a team that couldn’t get past the second round.

Brand spent his final NBA years as a respected veteran on minimum contracts with the Dallas Mavericks (2012-13) and Atlanta Hawks (2013-15), providing mentorship and professionalism to younger rosters. He retired in August 2015 at age 36 — then came back five months later, with Coach K’s encouragement, to play 19 games for the Sixers during the tanking-era Process years. His final NBA game came in April 2016. The career totals: 1,058 games, 15.9 points, 8.5 rebounds, 2.1 assists, 1.7 blocks, 50.0% shooting. Over $147 million in career earnings.

Brand had always been the smartest player in the room. At Duke, he chose the school partly because a degree could get his mom a house if basketball didn’t work out. In the NBA, he founded Gibraltar Films, producing the Werner Herzog Vietnam war film Rescue Dawn. He created the Elton Brand Foundation, supporting causes in Chicago, Peekskill, and Durham. He completed the High-Potential Leaders program at the Wharton School of the University of Pennsylvania.

In 2016, the Sixers hired Brand as a player development consultant. Within a year he was running their G League affiliate, the Delaware Blue Coats. In September 2018 — just two years after retiring as a player — he was named General Manager of the Philadelphia 76ers, beating out several more experienced candidates. It was a meteoric rise. Brand played a key role in constructing the roster that earned the #1 seed in the Eastern Conference in 2020-21, and the Sixers compiled a 143-84 regular season record during his first three full seasons as GM, including an NBA-best 91-21 home record.

When Philadelphia hired Daryl Morey as President of Basketball Operations in late 2020, Brand’s role shifted from the top decision-maker to the second-highest-ranking executive. He has remained in that position for five seasons, a respected figure across the league who continues to draw consideration for team president roles elsewhere. In May 2025, he was pursued for the Atlanta Hawks’ president of basketball operations vacancy but withdrew to remain in Philadelphia. At 46, he is widely regarded as one of the most promising front-office executives in the NBA, a former #1 pick who is building a second career as methodically as he built the first.
bio.now:
Elton Brand is 47 years old and in his ninth year working in the Philadelphia 76ers front office, currently serving as the team’s General Manager under President of Basketball Operations Daryl Morey. He lives in the Philadelphia area with his wife Shahara Simmons, whom he met at Duke and married in North Carolina in 2006.

His mother Daisy, who raised him in the Dunbar Heights Housing Complex, got the house he promised her. His Elton Brand Foundation continues to support youth programs in Chicago, Peekskill, and Durham. He serves on the boards of both the Sixers Youth Foundation and the Philadelphia Film Society. The kid who picked Duke because a degree could provide a safety net has built something far beyond what any safety net could offer.

Brand’s Brotherhood story is one of quiet transformation. He was the first consensus National Player of the Year to play for Krzyzewski. He was the first #1 overall pick in Duke history. He was, along with Avery and Maggette, the first early departure — the moment that forced K to evolve or be left behind. His NBA career spanned 17 seasons and five franchises, producing over $147 million in earnings, two All-Star selections, and the respect of everyone who played with or against him. And when the playing was done, he didn’t disappear into retirement. He went to Wharton. He learned the front office. He became the GM of an NBA franchise.

Peekskill’s favorite son. Duke’s first #1 pick. The 76ers’ general manager. Elton Brand has been building things his entire life — from a housing complex in the Hudson Valley to the corner office of an NBA franchise. The foundation his mother laid, the education Duke provided, and the work ethic that carried him through 1,058 NBA games are all still paying dividends. He’s just doing it in a suit now instead of a jersey.

highlights.note: NBA Rookie of the Year; 20.1 PPG/10.0 RPG
2x NBA All-Star (2002, 2006)
Led Clippers to first playoff series win in 30 years

nba.postCareer: GM, Philadelphia 76ers
