players.name: William Avery
players.tagline: Augusta, Georgia. High school teammates with Ricky Moore — who beat him in the 1999 title game wearing a UConn jersey. One of the first to leave early under Coach K. 14th pick. Three NBA seasons. Eight countries. Then came back to Duke at age 40, graduated in 2023, and joined Scheyer’s coaching staff.
players.now: Assistant Coach, Duke University (hired 2023 under Jon Scheyer); graduated Duke 2023 in African American Studies
players.stat: 2 Duke seasons • 74 games • 14.9/5.0 soph • 49.5% FG • 2nd Team All-ACC • 37-2 • Championship game

sources.label: GoDuke.com: Will Avery coaching bio
Basketball Reference: William Avery career stats
EuroLeague: William Avery profile
Wikipedia: William Avery (basketball)

coaching.note: First full-time college coaching position; hired by Jon Scheyer

nba.postCareer: Assistant Coach at Duke University; founded Will Avery Basketball Academy in Evans, GA (helped 20+ players earn college scholarships); graduated Duke 2023 (African American Studies)

bio.road:
William Franklin Avery Jr. was born on August 8, 1979, in Augusta, Georgia. He grew up in a modest household — his mother lived just above the poverty line and worked at a power plant. Basketball was the way out, and Avery was gifted enough to make it the way through.

At Westside High School in Augusta, Avery played alongside a teammate named Ricky Moore. Together they led Westside to the 1995 Georgia state championship — the school’s first title in boys’ basketball. Moore would go on to play at the University of Connecticut. The two childhood teammates from Augusta would meet again on the biggest stage in college basketball, but they would be wearing different jerseys.

To bolster his academic standing and national recruiting profile, Avery transferred to Oak Hill Academy in Mouth of Wilson, Virginia, for his senior year. Oak Hill was the factory — the place where elite prospects went to sharpen their games and get their grades in order. Coach K had initial concerns about Avery’s academics during his junior year at Westside, but Oak Hill smoothed the path. By the time he graduated, Avery was a third-team Parade All-American and one of the most highly regarded point guards in the Class of 1997.
bio.duke:
Avery played two seasons at Duke, 1997–98 and 1998–99. As a freshman, he backed up Steve Wojciechowski and averaged 8.7 points and 2.5 assists in 35 games, learning the position behind one of the toughest defenders in Duke history. The 1997–98 team went 32–4 and reached the Elite Eight before falling to Kentucky.

Sophomore year was the masterpiece. With Wojciechowski graduated, Avery stepped into the starting point guard role alongside Elton Brand, Trajan Langdon, Shane Battier, Chris Carrawell, and Corey Maggette. He averaged 14.9 points and 5.0 assists per game, shot 49.5 percent from the field and 41.1 percent from three, and was named Second Team All-ACC. Duke went 37–2 — winning 32 straight games, going 16–0 in ACC play, and sweeping both the ACC regular-season and tournament championships. It was one of the most dominant seasons in college basketball history.

In the NCAA Tournament, Avery quarterbacked Duke through five wins to reach the national championship game against the University of Connecticut. On the other side of the floor was his old Westside High School teammate, Ricky Moore — the kid he had won a state championship with in Augusta. Moore’s defense on the perimeter was a key factor as UConn won 77–74, ending Duke’s dream of a perfect season. Two boys from Augusta, one title game, different jerseys, different outcomes.

Then came the decision that changed everything. Avery, along with Elton Brand (who won National Player of the Year) and Corey Maggette, left Duke for the NBA Draft. They were among the first players to leave early under Mike Krzyzewski. Coach K had badly wanted Avery to return for his junior season to help groom incoming freshman Jason Williams. Avery had reportedly promised he would come back. He didn’t. The departure — and the circumstances around it — created a rift that would take two decades to fully heal.
bio.after:
The Minnesota Timberwolves selected Avery with the 14th overall pick in the 1999 NBA Draft. His three seasons in Minnesota were difficult: he appeared in 142 games but averaged just 2.7 points and 1.4 assists per game, never establishing himself as a rotation player. After his rookie contract expired in 2002, no NBA team signed him.

What followed was a decade-long odyssey through international basketball. Avery played in France for Strasbourg, in Israel for Hapoel Tel Aviv and Hapoel Jerusalem, in Ukraine for Azovmash Mariupol, in Greece for Makedonikos and Panionios Athens, and in Poland for Energa Czarni Slupsk. Eight countries, eight different leagues, eight different languages and cultures. He was a first-round pick playing in gymnasiums where the fans threw things and the paychecks were uncertain. He kept playing because basketball was all he had ever known, the thing that had taken him out of Augusta.

Avery retired from professional basketball in 2012 after his stint in Poland. He returned to Evans, Georgia — a suburb of Augusta — and founded the Will Avery Basketball Academy, a youth training program inspired in part by his daughter Yasmine’s growing interest in the game. The academy helped over twenty young players secure college scholarships in the years after its founding.
bio.now:
In the fall of 2019, twenty years after he had left Duke as a sophomore, William Avery did something extraordinary: he came back. At age forty, he re-enrolled at Duke University to complete his undergraduate degree in African American studies. While earning his degree, he assisted the basketball team in various capacities, reconnecting with the program he had left so abruptly two decades earlier. He graduated in 2023.

Following his graduation, Avery was hired as an assistant coach on Jon Scheyer’s staff at Duke — his first full-time college coaching position. The kid from Augusta who had been one of the first to leave early was now on the bench in Cameron Indoor Stadium, teaching the next generation of Blue Devils. He works alongside Chris Carrawell, his teammate from the 1999 championship game team, completing a Brotherhood circle that stretches across a quarter century.

William Avery was born in Augusta, Georgia, and raised by a mother who worked at a power plant. He played alongside Ricky Moore at Westside High School and won a state championship. He went to Oak Hill Academy to get his grades right and then to Duke to play for Coach K. He backed up Wojciechowski as a freshman and then became the starting point guard on a team that went 37–2 and reached the national championship game — where his old Augusta teammate was guarding him in a UConn jersey. He left Duke early, one of the first to do so under Krzyzewski, and the departure created a wound that took years to close. He was drafted 14th overall and played three seasons in the NBA. He played professional basketball in eight countries across a decade. He retired, went home to Augusta, and started a basketball academy inspired by his daughter. And then, at age forty, he came back to Duke and finished what he had started. He graduated in 2023 with a degree in African American studies. He is now an assistant coach under Jon Scheyer, on the same bench where Coach K once sat, in the same arena where he once played point guard on one of the greatest teams in Duke history. The Brotherhood doesn’t just welcome you back. Sometimes it waits twenty years for you to be ready. William Avery was ready. And Duke was still there.

players.name: Corey Maggette
players.tagline: One of Duke’s first one-and-dones.
players.now: Fox Sports analyst (Clippers/college basketball); Corey Cares Foundation; 2018 BIG3 MVP
players.stat: 1 Duke season • 10.6 PPG • 1999 NCAA Championship Game • 14 NBA seasons • ~$100M career earnings

bio.road:
Corey Antoine Maggette was born November 12, 1979, in Melrose Park, Illinois — a working-class suburb on Chicago’s western edge. His father was Jimmie Maggette. His mother Marguerite was one of five sisters in a family revolving around their father, Reverend Willie Dugan, a Baptist minister. The sisters lived within a short drive of each other — an extended clan bound by church, meals, and proximity.

There was a Christmas when money was tight. Corey’s brother Jimmie declined his own gifts so Corey could have what he needed. A small act of sacrifice in a modest home that crystallized the family’s values: you share what you have, you look out for the people beside you.

Corey found basketball at the Boys & Girls Club at twelve. The dream started there — not on a manicured AAU circuit, but in a community center where kids shot on bent rims. That origin never left him. When he made the NBA, he started Flight 50 Basketball Camp, initially inviting fifty kids.

At Fenwick High School in Oak Park: 23 PPG/10 RPG as a senior. State quarterfinals. Two-time All-State. McDonald’s All-American. Parade All-American. Also competed in the long jump.

The recruiting decision came down to Georgetown and Duke. John Thompson sold him like a father figure. But Coach K had a shared geography. ‘He’s from the South Side of Chicago, so right there you knew it was some type of connection being a fellow Chicagoan.’ The Chicago kid chose the Chicago coach.
bio.duke:
Maggette arrived in 1998 with Elton Brand (#1 recruit in America) and William Avery. Shane Battier was a sophomore. Trajan Langdon a senior. Duke went 37–2 — the best winning percentage in program history — and went undefeated in ACC play.

Maggette was the sixth man, the explosive freshman off the bench. 10.6 PPG, 3.9 RPG. ACC All-Rookie Team. His teammates nicknamed him “Maximus” for his jumping ability. His defining moment: a thunderous follow-up dunk against Temple in the East Regional Final. Julius Erving later compared his explosiveness to a young Vince Carter.

The championship game against UConn: Duke was favored by 9.5 — the biggest title-game spread in twenty years. TV networks showed Duke fans in Durham already celebrating. UConn saw it in their hotel. They won 77–74. Maggette scored 8 in 11 minutes. Langdon traveled in the final seconds. The best Duke team that never won a championship.

Then Maggette, Brand, and Avery made history: the first players to leave Duke early under Coach K. Before 1999, no Blue Devil had ever departed before his eligibility expired. ‘No one in Duke’s history had ever left, especially as a freshman. I felt it was a great opportunity to pursue this dream I started at the Boys & Girls Club.’
bio.after:
A year after leaving, the Myron Piggie shadow fell. Maggette admitted accepting ~$2,000 from his AAU coach while in high school — an NCAA violation. Piggie was convicted. Duke was cleared in 2004 (unaware of payments). But the episode haunted both Maggette and the program.

Drafted 13th by Seattle, traded to Orlando, then to the LA Clippers (June 2000). The Clippers became home for eight years. “Maximus” in Staples Center: a 6-6, 225-pound freight train who lived at the free-throw line, a perennial 15+ PPG scorer. 2001 Slam Dunk Contest. Career-high 39 vs the Lakers. In 2006, the Clippers won their first playoff series since moving from Buffalo.

Golden State: 5-year, $50M. Chris Mullin praised his work ethic as a model for young players. Averaged 19.3 PPG over two seasons as sixth man. Then Milwaukee, Charlotte, Detroit (2013). Fourteen NBA seasons. ~$100M in career earnings.

His family moved to Orlando when he was drafted — Marguerite leaving her sisters behind so her son wouldn’t be alone. Charles Barkley once complimented her parenting. Julius Erving became a mentor. Michael Finley and Juwan Howard, fellow Chicago guys, advised him on saving money.
bio.now:
In 2006, Maggette founded the Corey Cares Foundation — serving, mentoring, and inspiring underprivileged youth through basketball. Partners with Boys & Girls Clubs. Runs the Flight 50 camp (grown from 50 kids to hundreds). Raises scholarship money. Born from the same impulse as that Christmas when his brother gave away his gifts.

In 2017, he joined Ice Cube’s BIG3 league. Opening game: tore his Achilles. Most 37-year-olds would have retired. Maggette came back the next season, was named 2018 BIG3 MVP and Captain of the Year, scored 27 in the championship, and led Power to the title at age 38.

Now a Fox Sports analyst for the LA Clippers and college basketball. Married Milagro Martinez in 2006. Son Sergio is an emerging high school basketball talent in Orange County. Lives in Southern California.

He is 45. Born in Melrose Park. His brother gave up Christmas presents so Corey could have his. Found basketball at the Boys & Girls Club at twelve. Chose Duke because Coach K was from the South Side. Part of the best team that never won — 37-2, 9.5-point favorites, beaten by UConn. One of the first three to ever leave Duke early. The Piggie scandal cast a shadow. Fourteen NBA seasons, $100 million, called Maximus, Charles Barkley complimented his mother’s parenting. Tore his Achilles at 37, won BIG3 MVP at 38. The camp he started with fifty kids is still running. The foundation is called Corey Cares, because that’s the whole point — he does.

charity.label: Support the Corey Cares Foundation
charity.description: The Corey Cares Foundation works with Boys & Girls Clubs to provide youth basketball programs, mentorship, and community support. The Flight 50 camp has grown from 50 kids to hundreds.

highlights.note: Averaged 22.2 PPG with Clippers — career best
First Duke player to leave for NBA after one season

nba.postCareer: Fox Sports analyst (Clippers/college basketball); Corey Cares Foundation; 2018 BIG3 MVP

players.name: Jay Williams
players.tagline: The motorcycle accident that changed everything.
players.now: ESPN NBA analyst; author; College Basketball Hall of Fame (2017); motivational speaker
players.stat: 109 games • 2,079 pts (6th all-time) • 2x NABC POY • Naismith + Wooden • 2001 NCAA Champion • Jersey #22 retired

bio.road:
Jason David Williams was born September 10, 1981, in Plainfield, New Jersey — a city of stark racial and economic divides. His father, David, worked in management information systems for American Express. His mother, Althea, was a guidance counselor who would earn a second master’s degree while working full-time as assistant principal at North Plainfield High. Jay was their only biological child. Strict household. Education first. You never quit something you committed to.

But the Williams family expanded unexpectedly. Althea was counseling a girl named Jackie Taylor, whose mother had died of cancer. David and Althea brought Jackie home. Never officially adopted her, but raised her, put her through school. ‘They’re mom and dad,’ Taylor said. Jay had a sister.

In fourth grade, David and Althea put up a hoop in the backyard. They had to make Jay come inside at night because of the snow. By fifth grade he’d taken the coach’s son’s job at point guard. He tried everything — karate, volleyball, soccer, baseball, tennis, swimming, chess. He was the state volleyball player of the year as a senior. But basketball consumed him.

At St. Joseph High School in Metuchen, he started all four varsity years. School records: 1,977 points, 407 steals. Senior stats: 19 PPG, 7.0 APG, 4.2 RPG, 3.7 SPG. New Jersey Player of the Year. Parade and USA Today All-American. McDonald’s All-American (20 points in the game). Morgan Wootten Award. 3.6 GPA. David and Althea rarely missed a game despite international travel schedules. Althea started the first girls’ cheerleading squad at the all-boys school. His coach, Mark Taylor: ‘They’re a special family.’
bio.duke:
Williams became one of the few Duke freshmen to average double figures in scoring (14.5 PPG, 6.5 APG). ACC Rookie of the Year. National Freshman of the Year.

Sophomore year was the championship. He started all 39 games alongside Shane Battier, the program’s moral compass. Averaged 21.6 PPG — the first Duke player since Danny Ferry (1989) to lead the ACC in scoring. Also 6.1 APG (2nd in ACC). NABC Player of the Year. Led all NCAA Tournament scorers at 25.7 PPG. Duke beat Arizona 82–72 in Minneapolis for Coach K’s third national title. Williams and Battier combined for over 1,500 points — one of only two Duke duos to each score 700+ in a single year.

Then the decision that defined his character. Projected lottery pick. Could have left for millions. He stayed. His father: ‘If you kept the larger picture in mind, the short-term things will work themselves out.’ His mother was simultaneously earning her second master’s. ‘It was quite a joy to be writing a paper and staying up all night while he was writing a paper and staying up all night. The problem came in that I had to go to work.’

Junior year was a coronation. 21.3 PPG, 5.7 APG, 3.5 RPG. His 841 points broke Dick Groat’s 49-year Duke scoring record. Set the NCAA Tournament record for three-pointers attempted. Won both the Naismith and Wooden Awards. Consensus first-team All-American for the second straight year. Two-time NABC POY. Graduated in three years with a sociology degree — the first Duke athlete to do so. Jersey #22 retired on Senior Day. Career: 2,079 points, 6th all-time in Duke history.

Coach K later described him as having ‘as fast a first step as any point guard in the history of basketball.’
bio.after:
The Chicago Bulls took Williams second overall in the 2002 draft, behind only Yao Ming. He was given Michael Jordan’s locker. He had a three-unit apartment on the 40th floor of the Park Hyatt on Michigan Avenue — floor-to-ceiling windows, Lake Michigan stretching out like an ocean. He was 20 years old.

Rookie season: 75 games, 9.5 PPG, 4.7 APG. Inconsistent but promising. Triple-double against the Nets. All-Rookie Second Team. Represented the US at the 2002 FIBA World Championship.

Then June 19, 2003. A perfect Chicago summer day. He took his black Yamaha R6 with red accents instead of the Corvette. No helmet. No motorcycle license. His contract forbade it. ‘I was that prototypical young hotshot who thought he had all the answers. Motorcycle license? What for? Money and arrogance were all I needed.’

He crashed into a streetlight at Belmont and Honore in Roscoe Village. Fractured pelvis. Severed main nerve in his leg. Three torn ligaments including ACL. Life-threatening internal bleeding. Doctors considered amputation. In an instant, the man with the fastest first step in basketball could no longer walk.

The Bulls drafted Kirk Hinrich a week later. They waived Williams but gave him $3 million for rehab despite having no contractual obligation. He became addicted to OxyContin and other painkillers. Depression. ‘For the longest time I felt like being alive was more of a consolation prize.’ The Nets signed him in 2006 — released after three weeks. Austin Toros D-League — three games, waived. Career over at 25.
bio.now:
Jay Williams rebuilt himself from the wreckage. Not quickly. Not cleanly. But completely. Coach K’s “next play” philosophy — don’t dwell on what happened, move forward — became the operating system for his second life. He went from NBA millions to $30,000 at ESPN.

He is now one of ESPN’s most prominent basketball voices: NBA Countdown, studio shows, game telecasts. He co-hosted the “Keyshawn, JWill & Max Show” on ESPN Radio. Launched “The Limits with Jay Williams” podcast on NPR. Motivational speaker. Co-founded Simatree consulting. Author of Life Is Not an Accident: A Memoir of Reinvention (2016). Grant Hill: ‘Jay’s story is about strength, resilience, and redemption.’ Charles Barkley: ‘A lot of athletes have been to the mountaintop and back to the bottom. But not Jay.’

Inducted into the College Basketball Hall of Fame in 2017. Married Nikki Bonacorsi in May 2018. Daughter Amelia Brooklyn-Rose (October 2018). Son Zane David. Lives in Plainfield, New Jersey — the same town where David and Althea put up a hoop in the backyard and had to make their boy come inside because of the snow.

He is 43 years old. He had everything — a national championship, the Wooden Award, the #2 pick, Jordan’s locker, the fastest first step in basketball — and threw it into a streetlight on a perfect June evening because he thought he was invincible. He wasn’t. But the crash that almost killed him at twenty-one was not the end of his story. It was the beginning of the one that mattered. He is convinced the accident was no accident. It was a tragedy that taught him how to live.

charity.name: The Jay Williams Foundation
charity.label: Give Back with Jay Williams
charity.description: Jay Williams advocates for mental health awareness and second chances, drawing on his own experience recovering from a near-fatal motorcycle accident and addiction. His work focuses on youth mentorship and resilience.

highlights.note: Career ended by motorcycle accident June 2003; suffered severed nerve in left leg, fractured pelvis, dislocated knee

nba.postCareer: ESPN analyst and commentator; motivational speaker; author of Life Is Not an Accident; podcast host

players.name: Carlos Boozer
players.tagline: Duke to the Dream Team.
players.now: Utah Jazz front office; ACC Network analyst; author; Naismith HOF (2008 Team USA)
players.stat: 109 college games • 2001 NCAA Champion • 13 NBA seasons • 16.2 PPG/9.5 RPG • 2x All-Star • Olympic Gold 2008

bio.road:
Carlos Austin Boozer Jr. was born November 20, 1981, at a U.S. military base in Aschaffenburg, West Germany. His father, Carlos Sr., was an Army sergeant. His mother Renee raised five children while both parents worked two jobs. The family settled in Washington, D.C., after Carlos Sr.’s Army service ended.

What happened next changed everything. Six-year-old Carlos was playing neighborhood basketball with his friend Chris. Chris made a basket and knocked another player down. An older kid — a gang member — pulled a gun from his backpack and shot Chris. Then rode away on his bicycle. Chris died in Carlos Boozer’s arms.

Carlos Sr. and Renee loaded the car and drove 2,800 miles to Bellingham, Washington. A ferry carried them to Juneau, Alaska. They were looking for the safest place in America.

Juneau was another planet. Population 30,000. Rainforest. Eagles. Glaciers. No roads in or out. ‘I was very different. My swag was different. I’m coming into school in that old ’85 Dodge van, bumping Tupac and Biggie, and nobody had ever heard of those rappers. They embraced me.’ The family lived in an apartment in Douglas, seven people, lower-middle class. No basketball hoop. So Carlos Sr. drove his son thirty minutes to Auke Bay Elementary, where there was a covered outdoor court. Even in snow: homework, wait for Dad, drive to Auke Bay, go at it for two hours, come home for dinner. Every single day for four years.

At Juneau-Douglas High: 95–12 record. Back-to-back state titles (1997, 1998). Three-time Alaska POY. Two-time Parade All-American. Senior: 28 PPG/14 RPG. Gatorade National POY. McDonald’s All-American (22 pts/11 reb). ‘Think about a kid at 12 years old, in Juneau, Alaska, telling his parents, “I want to go to the NBA.”’ His parents let him dream. Coach K came to Juneau. Boozer chose Duke.
bio.duke:
Boozer arrived in 1999 with Jay Williams. ACC All-Freshman Team. Sophomore year was the masterpiece: Williams, Boozer, Shane Battier, Mike Dunleavy Jr., Chris Duhon — one of the most talented Duke rosters ever. Boozer was the 6-9, 260-pound power forward with a soft touch and a competitive fire forged on that outdoor court in Auke Bay.

Duke went 35–4 and won the 2001 NCAA Championship, beating Arizona 82–72 in Minneapolis. Coach K’s third national title. Boozer averaged double figures alongside Williams (ACC scoring leader) and Battier (National POY).

Junior year, with Williams and Battier gone, Boozer stepped into the starring role: 18.2 PPG, 8.7 RPG. He, Williams, and Dunleavy became the only Duke trio to each score 600+ in a season. 2002 ACC Tournament MVP. NABC Third-Team All-American. Duke reached the Sweet Sixteen. After the season, Boozer declared for the draft.
bio.after:
Drafted 35th overall — massively undervalued. Rookie in Cleveland: 10.0 PPG, 7.5 RPG, All-Rookie Second Team. By year two: 15.5 PPG, 11.4 RPG alongside a rookie named LeBron James.

Then the controversy. The Cavaliers released Boozer from his $695K contract expecting him to re-sign for ~$39M. Instead, he signed a 6-year, $70M deal with Utah. Cleveland’s owner: ‘I decided to trust Carlos. He did not show that trust and respect in return.’ Boozer maintained NBA tampering rules forced his hand. The episode tagged him with a reputation he never fully shook.

In Utah he became a star: 2x All-Star (2007, 2008). Averaged 20+ PPG in 2006–07, leading the Jazz to the Western Conference Finals. Scored 41 against Houston in the playoffs. Six seasons in Utah: 19.3 PPG, 10.6 RPG.

Olympics: bronze in Athens 2004, then the legendary “Redeem Team” gold in Beijing 2008 alongside LeBron, Kobe, Wade, CP3, and Carmelo. The 2008 team was inducted into the Naismith HOF in 2025.

Chicago Bulls: 5-year, $80M contract (2010). Helped the Bulls win the East’s top seed in 2010–11 with Derrick Rose. Four seasons. Lakers 2014–15. Guangdong Southern Tigers (China) 2016–17. Retired. 861 NBA games, ~$130M career earnings.
bio.now:
The most important story of Carlos Boozer’s life has nothing to do with basketball.

On May 31, 2006, his firstborn son Carmani was diagnosed with sickle cell disease. Both Carlos and wife CeCe (Cindy Blackwell, whom he’d met when she was at NC State and he was at Duke) carried the gene. ‘The thought of me losing my son, what do I do? Do I put my kid through a life full of torture? Or do I roll the dice?’

They rolled the dice. Doctors at Emory told them about a bone marrow transplant using stem cells from a sibling’s umbilical cord. The Boozers conceived through IVF — screening embryos to be sickle-free and bone marrow matches. 34 eggs harvested. 10 viable embryos. Two perfect matches. Both implanted.

On July 18, 2007, Cameron and Cayden Boozer were born. Three weeks later, doctors transplanted their umbilical cord stem cells into Carmani. He spent 40 nights in Miami Children’s Hospital. It worked. Carmani was cured.

Those twins became the most dominant high school basketball duo in America. Cameron: 6-9, first player since LeBron to win Mr. Basketball USA (2023, 2025), projected top pick 2026 NBA Draft. Cayden: 6-4 guard, five-star recruit, USA Basketball gold medalist. Three straight Florida state championships at Christopher Columbus High. ESPN’s 2024 documentary Blood Brothers told their story.

Both committed to Duke on October 11, 2024. They are on the 2025–26 Blue Devils roster. The Brotherhood is now literal: a father’s legacy continued by sons who began their lives saving someone else’s.

Carlos joined the Utah Jazz front office in May 2025. ACC Network analyst. Published Every Shot Counts (2023). Still runs camps in Juneau every summer; proceeds go to sickle cell research. His parents moved back to Juneau. ‘We missed Juneau. We came back and decided to live here the rest of our lives.’

He is 43. Born on a military base in Germany. His childhood friend died in his arms. His parents drove 2,800 miles to Alaska. He shot baskets in the snow for four years. Won a championship at Duke, made $130 million, won Olympic gold. Then watched his firstborn diagnosed with a disease that could kill him — and conceived twins through IVF whose bone marrow cured their brother. Those twins are now at Duke, chasing the same championship their father won in 2001. And his parents are back in Juneau, where everybody knows their neighbor’s dog’s name.

charity.name: Sickle Cell Disease Association of America
charity.label: Support Sickle Cell Research
charity.description: Carlos Boozer’s son Carmani was diagnosed with sickle cell disease in 2006. The Boozer family’s IVF story, documented in ESPN’s Blood Brothers, raised awareness of sickle cell disease and the importance of bone marrow transplant research.

highlights.note: Averaged 15.5/11.4 alongside LeBron James in Cleveland
2x NBA All-Star (2007, 2008)
Averaged 21.1/10.4 with Utah

nba.postCareer: Utah Jazz front office; ACC Network analyst; author; Naismith HOF (2008 Team USA)

players.name: Mike Dunleavy Jr.
players.tagline: The coach’s son who carved his own path.
players.now: General Manager, Golden State Warriors
players.stat: 3 Duke seasons • 2001 NCAA Champion (team-high 21 in title game) • Consensus All-American • 15 NBA seasons • 986 games

bio.road:
Michael Joseph Dunleavy Jr. was born September 15, 1980, in Fort Worth, Texas. His father, Mike Dunleavy Sr., was an NBA player whose career spanned 11 seasons before chronic back pain forced his retirement. His mother Emily raised three boys — Mike Jr., Baker, and James — in a household that never stayed in one city long. Basketball was the family business. It still is: Baker played at Villanova and coached at Quinnipiac, James played at USC and became an NBA agent.

When Mike Jr. was ten, his father took the head coaching job with the Los Angeles Lakers. The team featured Magic Johnson and James Worthy. The 1990–91 Lakers reached the NBA Finals before losing to Jordan’s Bulls. A ten-year-old boy had a front-row seat to the end of one era and the beginning of another. ‘Magic Johnson is one of the great athletes, leaders, and winners of all time. To be around somebody like that was great.’

The family moved when the jobs moved. Milwaukee (Bucks), where Mike Jr. attended University School of Milwaukee and Homestead High. Then Portland (Trail Blazers), where his father won NBA Coach of the Year in 1999. Mike transferred to Jesuit High School in Beaverton, Oregon — and did something almost no high schooler gets to do: he practiced against his father’s players. Steve Smith. Bonzi Wells. Rasheed Wallace. ‘Hey, I was a 16-year-old kid playing against pros. It definitely made me better.’ He led Jesuit to the 1999 Oregon 4A State Championship, 65–38 over North Salem.

The biggest lesson was what he saw, not what he was told. ‘Preparation was my dad’s biggest thing. I remember him being up late at night, all night, watching film. That’s something I’ve applied to my craft.’ He chose Duke. Coach K saw in him the most versatile forward since Grant Hill.
bio.duke:
Dunleavy arrived in 1999 alongside Jay Williams, Carlos Boozer, and Chris Duhon. All four became NBA players. Three were first-round picks.

Sophomore year was the championship. Duke went 35–4 with Williams leading the ACC in scoring, Battier winning National POY, Boozer providing muscle inside. Dunleavy was the Swiss Army knife — 6-9 with shooting touch, passing vision, and the basketball IQ of a kid raised in film sessions. In the title game against Arizona: team-high 21 points, including key three-pointers that stretched the lead. Duke won 82–72. That game was the announcement: the coach’s kid wasn’t just along for the ride.

Junior year, with Battier gone: 17.3 PPG, 7.2 RPG. He, Williams, and Boozer each scored 600+ points — a feat matched at Duke only by Scheyer/Singler/Nolan Smith. Consensus second-team All-American. First-team NABC All-American. Duke went 31–4, reached the Sweet Sixteen. He declared for the draft.
bio.after:
Golden State selected Dunleavy 3rd overall in 2002 — one spot behind Duke teammate Jay Williams. Five-year, $44M extension in 2005. But early-2000s Golden State was a wasteland. The #3 pick averaged 12 points on a team that couldn’t make the playoffs. He became an unfair symbol of franchise mediocrity. Traded to Indiana in January 2007.

With the Pacers, Dunleavy became the player Duke always knew he could be. First full season: all 82 starts, career-high 19.1 PPG. A legitimate three-level scoring wing. But injuries (wrist, then nagging issues) slowed him. By the time Indiana returned to the postseason in 2010–11, he’d waited 624 games and nine seasons without a playoff appearance — the longest drought in the Brotherhood.

Milwaukee (2011–13). Then Chicago, where everything clicked: alongside Derrick Rose, Joakim Noah, and Jimmy Butler. April 25, 2014: playoff career-high 35 points with 8 three-pointers against Washington — a Bulls franchise playoff record. Cleveland briefly (2016 championship roster). Atlanta (2017, final stop). Retired June 30, 2017.

Fifteen seasons. Six franchises. 986 games — second-most in the 2002 draft class. Career: 11.2 PPG, 4.5 RPG, 37.7% from three. ~$80M in earnings.
bio.now:
The son of a coach became something his father never was: a general manager of an NBA dynasty.

After retiring, Dunleavy joined the Golden State Warriors’ front office. The franchise that drafted him #3 in 2002 — when the Warriors were terrible and Dunleavy was the symbol of their futility — was now the NBA’s preeminent organization, built around Steph Curry, Klay Thompson, and Draymond Green. He rose through the ranks and was named General Manager.

During the 2025–26 trade deadline, he navigated rumors of a Giannis Antetokounmpo pursuit and managed a franchise in transition — aging champions, salary cap constraints, the pressure to compete while planning for the future. The preparation his father modeled at two in the morning is now his daily reality.

Married Sarah, whom he met at Duke. Three children. A residential sports camp in Eagle River, Wisconsin — Camp Menominee — named its basketball courts ‘The Mike Dunleavy Jr. Courts.’ The Dunleavy family has been in professional basketball, without interruption, for nearly fifty years.

He is 45. Born in Fort Worth. Never stayed anywhere long because his father’s career kept the family moving. Watched Magic Johnson at ten. Guarded Rasheed Wallace at sixteen. Scored a team-high 21 in a championship game at twenty. Was the #3 pick who spent nine years without making the playoffs. Hit 8 threes in a playoff game for the Bulls at thirty-three. Played 986 games and retired quietly at thirty-six. Now he runs the Warriors. The franchise that once represented everything that had gone wrong is the franchise he’s charged with keeping great. The coach’s kid became the coach’s boss.

highlights.note: Career-best 17.1 PPG with Golden State
Key 3-and-D role for Chicago Bulls playoff runs

nba.postCareer: General Manager, Golden State Warriors

players.name: Chris Duhon
players.tagline: The four-year floor general.
players.now: Head coach, Gaston Christian School (NC); Greater New Orleans Sports HOF (2024)
players.stat: 133 starts • 123–21 record • Duke all-time: steals (300), minutes (4,813) • 2001 NCAA Champion • 3 ACC titles

bio.road:
Christopher Nicholas Duhon was born August 31, 1982, in Mamou, Louisiana — a Cajun prairie town of about 3,000 people in the heart of Evangeline Parish. He was raised primarily by his mother, Vivian Harper, a former high school point guard who introduced her son to basketball before he could spell the word. He has a younger brother, Thomas.

The family moved from Mamou to New Orleans, then to Slidell — a suburban community on the north shore of Lake Pontchartrain — when Chris was ten. There was a weathered rim in the family driveway. Duhon shot on it every day. His mother’s guidance was constant. She had been a point guard herself. She knew the position. She understood that the game was about making other people better.

At Salmen High School in Slidell, Duhon became the best player in Louisiana. Senior year: Louisiana Mr. Basketball. McDonald’s All-American. Parade All-American. McDonald’s National HS Player of the Year. Won the McDonald’s Three-Point Shootout. USA Basketball Male Athlete of the Year. Ranked #13 nationally by ESPN, #6 by RSCI.

‘Growing up in Louisiana, LSU was always the school you kind of always wanted to go to. I thought that was the only place I would be able to go.’ But when every program in America called, he chose Duke. ‘I thought Duke gave me the best of both worlds.’
bio.duke:
Duhon arrived in 2000 with Jay Williams, Carlos Boozer, and Mike Dunleavy Jr. He was the point guard — the table-setter. Freshman year: started all 39 games, initially backing up Williams, then moving into the starting lineup alongside him when Boozer was injured. Duke went 35–4 and won the 2001 NCAA Championship.

What made Duhon irreplaceable was durability, steadiness, and defense. 133 starts in four years. 4,813 minutes — most in Duke history. 300 steals — most in Duke history. 819 assists — second in Duke history. A 123–21 record — second-winningest player in ACC history behind teammate Shane Battier. Three ACC Championships. The only ACC player to amass 1,000+ points, 400+ rebounds, 700+ assists, and 200+ steals.

Senior year: 10 PPG, 6 APG, 2.2 SPG, 4.1 RPG. Duke reached the Final Four. Duhon was a finalist for the Wooden Award, Naismith Award, and Rupp Trophy. Not flashy enough to win. Too important to ignore.

What did Coach K teach him? ‘You just learned how to be a man. Everything he taught us was about taking responsibility, and going out and working hard for what you want.’ Regrets about staying four years? ‘No, no regrets at all. Being in college for four years helped me play at a high level. It came with maturity and understanding the game; becoming a man.’
bio.after:
Drafted 38th overall by Chicago in 2004. Rookie year: all 82 games, 5.9 PPG, 4.9 APG. Hit 8 of 9 threes in a comeback win over Atlanta — a Bulls franchise record. In 2006–07, the Bulls won their first playoff series since the Jordan era, sweeping Miami. Duhon was the sixth man who kept the machine running. Career-high 34 points with 9 assists against Golden State in February 2008.

New York Knicks: 2-year, $12M deal. November 29, 2008: 22 assists against Golden State — a Knicks franchise single-game record. The kid from Mamou owned the Garden for one night.

But the most important thing Duhon did had nothing to do with basketball. In August 2005, Hurricane Katrina devastated the Gulf Coast. Slidell was hammered. Duhon founded the Stand Tall Foundation and distributed over 3,000 boxes of supplies worth $450,000 to Slidell residents. Thanksgiving dinners. Free youth clinics. The kid who shot on a weathered driveway rim came back to feed the neighbors who watched him grow up.

Orlando (4-year, $15M). Lakers (2012–13, final season). Nine NBA seasons. 606 games. 6.5 PPG, 4.4 APG. ~$33 million in career earnings. Never an All-Star. Always the glue.
bio.now:
The transition wasn’t smooth. In November 2015, while coaching at Marshall, Duhon was arrested for aggravated DUI. In January 2017, arrested again for driving on a revoked license. He resigned from Marshall three days later. A painful public reckoning for a man who’d built his identity on steadiness.

He rebuilt. Illinois State coaching staff as an assistant (2018). In 2023, his wife Andrea — a mathematics professor — accepted a position at Davidson College. The family relocated to Gastonia, North Carolina. Duhon became head coach of the boys’ basketball team at Gaston Christian School. His eldest son Jeremy plays on the team. Five children: Jeremy, Eliana, Christopher, Faith, and Miya. The family chose the move to be together.

Inducted into the Greater New Orleans Sports Hall of Fame in 2024. His cousin is Jarrett Jack, the longtime NBA point guard. Point guard bloodlines run in the family.

He is 42. Born in Mamou, Louisiana, population 3,000. His mother was a point guard. He shot on a weathered rim in the driveway in Slidell. Chose Duke over LSU. Started 133 games, played more minutes and recorded more steals than anyone in Duke history. Won a championship as a freshman, reached the Final Four as a senior. Set the Knicks’ all-time single-game assist record. When Katrina destroyed his hometown, he drove 3,000 boxes of supplies back to Slidell and fed his neighbors. He stumbled after basketball — the DUI, the resignation — but he stood back up, which is what point guards do. Now he coaches his son’s high school team. His wife teaches math at Davidson. The foundation is still called Stand Tall.

charity.name: Stand Tall Foundation
charity.label: Support Chris Duhon’s Stand Tall Foundation
charity.description: Founded after Hurricane Katrina, the Stand Tall Foundation has distributed over $450,000 in supplies to Slidell, Louisiana, and continues to serve communities through youth clinics and outreach.

highlights.note: Set NBA record with 22 assists in a game (since broken)
Started 82 games as Bulls rookie PG

nba.postCareer: Head coach, Gaston Christian School (NC); Greater New Orleans Sports HOF (2024)

players.name: Dahntay Jones
players.tagline: The Rutgers transfer who became Duke’s best player. Defensive stopper. 624 NBA games, nine teams, fourteen seasons. Won a championship in Cleveland. LeBron paid his fines. Married in the Duke Chapel. Now coaching the Clippers.
players.now: Assistant Coach, Los Angeles Clippers (promoted to full assistant 2024-25 under Tyronn Lue)
players.drafted: 1st Rd, 20th — Boston Celtics (traded to Memphis Grizzlies)
players.stat: 2 Duke seasons • 17.7/5.5 senior • All-ACC • 2x ACC All-Defensive • 20th pick • 624 NBA games • 2016 Champion

sources.label: GoDuke.com: Dahntay Jones player bio
Duke Basketball Report: Jim’s Look at Duke Transfers Part II
Basketball Reference: Dahntay Jones career stats
TrentonDaily: In the Paint with Dahntay Jones
NBA Coaches Association: Dahntay Jones bio
Wikipedia: Dahntay Jones

highlights.note: Career-best 10.2 PPG with Indiana Pacers
NBA Champion with Cleveland Cavaliers; key minutes in Game 6 of Finals
LeBron James paid his ejection fine (,000) after trash-talk technicals vs Toronto
G-League career-high 45 points with Grand Rapids Drive at age 35

coaching.note: Promoted under Tyronn Lue; Summer League head coach 2024

nba.postCareer: Assistant Coach, Los Angeles Clippers; father of five; previously married Valeisha Butterfield (Congressman G.K. Butterfield’s daughter) in Duke Chapel (2011)

bio.road:
Dahntay Lavall Jones was born December 27, 1980, in Trenton, New Jersey, and raised in nearby Hamilton Square. Basketball ran in the family: his father Larry played at St. Peter’s College, and his cousin Al Harrington would play sixteen NBA seasons.

At Steinert High School in Hamilton Township, Jones became one of the most decorated players in Mercer County history. He scored a school-record 1,675 career points, averaged 24 points and 9 rebounds as a senior, earned first-team All-State honors, honorable mention McDonald’s All-America, and Prep High School Academic All-American status. He captained his team as both a junior and senior.

Duke recruited Jones out of high school, but Corey Maggette’s commitment closed that door. He enrolled at Rutgers. By his sophomore year, he was the Scarlet Knights’ best player — 16 PPG, 4.6 RPG, Big East All-Rookie honors as a freshman. But Rutgers was an NIT program and Jones said publicly that coach Kevin Bannon wasn’t helping him get better.

When Maggette left Duke after one season — the first Blue Devil to go to the NBA after a single year — the spot reopened. Jason Williams, Jones’s best friend from New Jersey, was already at Duke and helped seal the deal. Jones became Coach K’s second transfer ever, following Roshown McLeod five years earlier. He sat out the 2001 championship season but contributed in practice daily against Battier, Dunleavy, and Boozer.
bio.duke:
Jones played two Duke seasons (2001–03) and the arc tells a story of transformation.

Junior year, 2001–02: stepped into the starting lineup alongside Williams, Dunleavy, and Boozer on a #1-ranked team. Averaged 11.2/4.2/1.1. Honorable mention All-ACC. Named to the ACC All-Defensive Team and the national All-Defensive Team. Duke’s primary defensive stopper, tasked with shutting down the opponent’s most dangerous scorer every night. Duke won the ACC Tournament.

Then the exodus: Williams drafted 2nd overall. Boozer to Cleveland. Dunleavy to Golden State. Three of Duke’s four best players gone in one offseason.

Senior year, 2002–03: Jones emerged as Duke’s best player. He improved his three-point shooting from 23% to 40% in a single offseason. Averaged 17.7/5.5/1.2 steals across 33 games. All-ACC. ACC All-Defensive Team for the second straight year. Against UNC in February 2003: 23 points, 13 rebounds in an 83–74 Duke victory. He had become a complete two-way player — guard the other team’s best scorer, then score 17 of his own.
bio.after:
Boston selected Jones 20th overall in 2003. His rights were traded to Memphis for Kendrick Perkins and Marcus Banks. Four seasons in Memphis as a defensive role player (5.0 PPG).

What followed was one of the great NBA odysseys of his generation. Sacramento Kings. G-League with the Fort Wayne Mad Ants. Denver Nuggets (2009 Western Conference Finals). A four-year deal with Indiana (peaked at 10.2 PPG in 2009–10). Dallas Mavericks. Atlanta Hawks. Los Angeles Clippers on 10-day contracts. Back to the G-League with Grand Rapids Drive, where at age 35 he scored 45 points in a single game — because he refused to stop playing.

On April 13, 2016, Cleveland signed Jones. He scored 13 in his debut. Fifteen games later, he was in the NBA Finals. He played key minutes in Game 6 as Cleveland completed the historic 3–1 comeback to win its first championship. In the 2017 playoffs, he was ejected for trash-talking Toronto’s Norman Powell — fine: ,000 against his ,127 postseason salary. LeBron covered the fine. His final game: 2 minutes in the 2017 Finals. Nine teams. Fourteen seasons. 624 games. One ring.
bio.now:
On July 23, 2011, Jones married Valeisha Butterfield — daughter of Congressman G.K. Butterfield — in the Duke University Chapel. Two hundred guests. People Magazine coverage. They later divorced.

In November 2020, Jones joined the LA Clippers as player development and video coach. By 2024–25, he was promoted to full assistant under Tyronn Lue, with expanded responsibilities in player development, film, and strategy. He coached the Clippers’ 2024 Summer League team. His coaching emphasizes the defensive fundamentals and toughness that defined his playing career. He is the father of five children; his son Dahntay Jr. plays youth basketball as a combo guard.

Dahntay Jones was born in Trenton. His father played at St. Peter’s, his cousin Al Harrington played sixteen NBA seasons. He scored 1,675 points at Steinert and was an Academic All-American. He went to Rutgers and became the best player on the team, then transferred to Duke because Jason Williams — his best friend — told him to come. He was Coach K’s second transfer, following McLeod through the same door. He sat out a championship. He became Duke’s defensive stopper, then Duke’s best player when Williams and Boozer and Dunleavy left. He improved his three-point shooting from 23% to 40% in one summer. He was drafted 20th overall. He played for nine teams across fourteen seasons. He went to the G-League at 35 and scored 45 in a single game. He won a championship in Cleveland and LeBron paid his fines. He got married in the Duke Chapel. He is now an assistant coach for the LA Clippers, teaching defensive fundamentals to a new generation. The kid from Trenton who couldn’t get to Duke the first time found his way there the second time, and the second time changed everything.

players.name: Luol Deng
players.tagline: From Sudanese refugee to NBA All-Star.
players.now: President, South Sudan Basketball Federation; OBE; led South Sudan to 2024 Olympics
players.drafted: 1st Rd, 7th Overall — Phoenix (traded to Chicago Bulls)
players.stat: 1 Duke season • 15.1 PPG/6.9 RPG • Final Four • 15 NBA seasons • 2x All-Star • All-Defensive Team

bio.road:
Luol Ajou Deng was born April 16, 1985, in Wau, in what is now South Sudan. He is a member of the Dinka ethnic group. His father, Aldo Ajou Deng, was a member of the Sudanese parliament. His mother, Martha Leon John, raised nine children. When Luol was three, his father moved the entire family out of Sudan to escape the Second Sudanese Civil War. They settled in a tiny apartment in Cairo. They were refugees. They had nothing.

‘My parents were my greatest role models. What we went through, how they were always positive. They never lost their edge. My mom is very religious and we always just believed it was going to get better.’

In Egypt, the Deng family met Manute Bol — the former NBA center, 7-foot-7, a Dinka from Sudan who had played for the Bullets, Warriors, and 76ers. After his NBA career, Bol ran a basketball school in Cairo for Sudanese refugees. He taught Luol’s older brother Ajou how to play, and Ajou taught Luol. The game that would carry Deng out of refugee life was delivered by a 7-foot-7 legend in an Egyptian apartment. ‘Manute stood for the right things. He was always going back home and giving back. That’s who he was and basketball didn’t change him.’

The family was granted political asylum in the UK. They settled in Brixton, South London. Luol was about ten. He didn’t speak a word of English. Arabic was his first language. But he was good at football — every kid on the playground wanted him on their team. ‘I was able to learn English quickly because of sports.’ Coach Jimmy Rogers at the Brixton Topcats convinced him to play basketball instead of football.

At fourteen, Deng moved to Blair Academy in New Jersey alone. Another culture shock. By senior year: #2 recruit in America. The only player ranked above him was LeBron James.
bio.duke:
One season at Duke, 2003–04, but it was enough. He averaged 15.1 points and 6.9 rebounds as a freshman — the team’s leading scorer on a roster with Chris Duhon, Shelden Williams, Daniel Ewing, and J.J. Redick. Duke went 31–6 and reached the Final Four before falling to UConn.

Deng played with the maturity and composure of someone who had been through things that made college basketball seem manageable. A refugee from a civil war does not panic in a half-court set. He declared for the draft after one season.
bio.after:
Phoenix selected Deng 7th overall and immediately traded him to Chicago. He spent ten seasons with the Bulls (2004–14). Never the face of the franchise — that was Derrick Rose — but Dime magazine called him ‘the most underrated player in the NBA.’ Coach Thibodeau: ‘The glue that holds the Bulls together.’ GM John Paxson: ‘He really does epitomize everything I had hoped for as a person and a basketball player.’

All-Rookie First Team (2005). Two-time All-Star (2012, 2013). All-Defensive Second Team (2012). Career-high 40 points vs Portland. Six-year, $71M extension. Averaged 43 minutes per game in the 2011 playoffs, praised for defending LeBron in the Eastern Conference Finals.

Cleveland (traded 2014). Miami (two seasons). LA Lakers (4-year, $72M — a contract that became infamous when they benched him during their rebuild). Minnesota (final stop, 2018–19). Fifteen seasons. 900+ games. ~$150M in career earnings. NBA Sportsmanship Award. J. Walter Kennedy Citizenship Award. UN Refugee Agency Humanitarian of the Year (2008). ‘I love basketball, but if I don’t share my success, what’s the point.’
bio.now:
In 2005, one year into his NBA career, Deng founded the Luol Deng Foundation. In 2010, he returned to South Sudan for the first time since fleeing as a toddler. He didn’t recognize anything he saw. He built basketball courts, donated computers to refugee camps, opened the Manute Bol Court in Juba — dedicated to the man who taught him the game in Cairo.

In 2012, he captained Great Britain’s basketball team at the London Olympics. Became a British citizen in 2006. Appointed OBE in 2021. ‘A refugee is a survivor. A refugee is someone that has no choice but to make a difficult decision.’

In 2019, Deng became President of the South Sudan Basketball Federation. Using his own money, he built the national team from nothing into a global force. They qualified for the 2023 FIBA World Cup (their first ever) and the 2024 Paris Olympics — the first time South Sudan, a nation that has existed only since 2011, competed in Olympic basketball. Deng served as assistant coach. The team flew 18-hour flights to Rwanda for training camp. They squeezed big men into exit rows. In Paris, South Sudan defeated Puerto Rico in their Olympic debut.

He is 40. Born in Wau during a civil war. His father packed nine children into a Cairo apartment. Manute Bol taught his brother basketball there, and his brother taught him. A ten-year-old who didn’t speak English learned the language playing football in Brixton. Ranked #2 behind only LeBron. One season at Duke, Final Four. Drafted 7th, fifteen NBA years, two-time All-Star, OBE. Then he went home. He opened the Manute Bol Court. He became president of the basketball federation of a country that didn’t exist when he was born. He funded the national team from his own pocket. He took South Sudan to the Olympics. Because Manute Bol gave him hope in an Egyptian apartment, and Luol Deng decided the only way to repay that was to give it to everyone else.

charity.label: Support the Luol Deng Foundation
charity.description: The Luol Deng Foundation builds basketball courts, donates computers to refugee camps, and funds South Sudan’s national basketball program. Luol has used his own money to take South Sudan to the Olympics.

players.name: JJ Redick
players.tagline: The most hated player in America. Now he coaches the Lakers.
players.now: Head Coach, Los Angeles Lakers
players.stat: 2,769 pts — Duke all-time scorer • 457 career 3s • Naismith POY

bio.road:
JJ Redick was born in Cookeville, Tennessee, and raised in Roanoke, Virginia, the second of five children in a family wired for basketball. His father, Ken, had played two seasons at Ohio Wesleyan University. His mother, Jeanie, was a nurse. His twin sisters, Catie and Alyssa, both played at Campbell University. His younger sister Abigail played at Virginia Tech and Drexel. His brother David was a football player at Marshall. In the Redick household, competition was the family language.

His nickname came from the twins. As a toddler, the family called him ‘J.’ The sisters kept repeating it — ‘J, J’ — and it stuck. His middle name, Clay, came from his father’s background as a stoneware potter. Jonathan Clay Redick became JJ to the world before he could walk.

At Cave Spring High School in Roanoke, Redick became the best player in Virginia. He averaged 28.3 points, 5 rebounds, and 4 assists as a senior. In the 2002 VHSL Class AAA state championship game, he scored 43 points — a championship-game record across all classifications that stood for 16 years, until Mac McClung broke it in 2018. He finished as Virginia’s all-time AAA leading scorer with 2,215 career points and shot over 44 percent from three. He played AAU ball for the Boo Williams All-Stars, whose national championship teams faced future NBA stars like Dwyane Wade.

He was the 2002 McDonald’s All-American Game MVP, scoring 26 points on 10-of-15 shooting and winning the three-point competition. Ranked the No. 2 shooting guard and No. 13 overall prospect in the country. Virginia Mr. Basketball. Parade All-American. Every major program wanted him. He chose Duke.
bio.duke:
JJ Redick left Duke as the most prolific scorer in school history and one of the most decorated — and most despised — players in the history of college basketball. He scored 2,769 career points, a Duke record. He made 457 career three-pointers, an NCAA record at the time of his graduation. He set the ACC single-season free throw percentage record at .953 during his sophomore year, including a streak of 54 consecutive free throws. He led Duke to a 116-23 record, three ACC Tournament championships, and a Final Four. He was a two-time National Player of the Year, two-time ACC Player of the Year, two-time ACC Tournament MVP, three-time All-American, and the 2006 Sullivan Award winner as the nation’s top amateur athlete. His jersey No. 4 was retired in February 2007.

Freshman (2002–03): The Arrival. Redick led the team with 30 points in the ACC Tournament championship game against NC State. He averaged 15.0 points per game — the fourth-highest freshman scoring average in Duke history behind only Johnny Dawkins, Gene Banks, and Mike Gminski. He set the ACC and Duke freshman record with a .919 free throw percentage. He made 95 three-pointers.

Sophomore (2003–04): The Final Four — and the Breaking Point. Duke reached the Final Four, where they fell to Connecticut. Redick was named to the All-Final Four team. But the season was defined by something darker: JJ Redick had become the most hated player in college basketball. Students from rival schools discovered his cell phone number; he estimated receiving 50 to 75 hate calls per day. Opposing crowds screamed vulgar things about his 12-year-old sister. Custom t-shirts with his photo that read ‘When I grow up, I want to name my kid JJ Redick’ on the front and ‘and beat him every day’ on the back. An NFL team owner talking trash to him courtside. An entire arena chanting an obscenity. He nearly quit. In December of his sophomore year, he had his twin sisters meet him on campus for dinner. ‘I don’t want to play anymore,’ he told them. ‘This is not for me. It’s not fun.’ He turned to writing poetry as an outlet. CBS Sports would later name him the ‘most hated current athlete in America.’

The Transformation: Summer Before Junior Year. Coach K sat him down in late May and told him the truth: ‘You’ve been good. You were All-ACC and a third-team All-American. We made the Final Four. So you’ve been good, but what you’re doing isn’t good enough.’ Krzyzewski kept him from playing USA Basketball that summer. Redick walked around campus carrying a jug of water. He lost 23 pounds, dropping from over 215 to 192. He won every preseason conditioning test.

Junior (2004–05): The transformed Redick averaged 21.8 points per game and won ACC Player of the Year and consensus National Player of the Year honors. Duke earned a No. 1 seed but lost in the Sweet Sixteen. He was playing over 37 minutes per game now, leaner, faster, more dynamic, able to create his own shot rather than just catch and shoot.

Senior (2005–06): The Record-Breaker. Redick averaged 26.8 points per game — a Duke single-season record. He broke Curtis Staples’ NCAA career three-point record against Wake Forest. He passed Dickie Hemric’s 51-year-old ACC career scoring record with a pair of free throws against Temple. He won every major national player of the year award: Naismith, Wooden, AP, NABC. He was a two-time team captain, a history major with a minor in cultural anthropology, and still the most hated player in America.

The final act was bittersweet. In the 2006 NCAA Tournament, LSU’s Garrett Temple — a 6’5 defensive specialist with a long wingspan — chased Redick relentlessly. The nation’s No. 2 scorer shot 3-for-18 and scored just 11 points. Duke lost.
bio.after:
The Orlando Magic selected Redick with the 11th pick in the 2006 draft. His early years were a test of patience. He competed for minutes, battled back spasms, and spent stretches as a third-string player. In January 2008, he posted on his blog that it had ‘been proven that even if I play well in the limited minutes I get, not much is going to change.’ He asked his agent about a trade. But he kept working. By the 2008-09 season, he was helping the Magic reach the NBA Finals. It was the first of 13 consecutive playoff appearances for Redick.

A three-team sign-and-trade sent Redick to the Clippers on a four-year, $27 million deal. He became a full-time starter for the first time in his career at age 29 — starting 218 of his first 219 games. He scored a career-high 40 points against Houston in January 2016 on 9-of-12 from three. He led the NBA in three-point shooting at 47.5% during the 2015-16 season. He made 200 or more three-pointers in four of five seasons from 2014 to 2019.

Redick signed a one-year, $23 million deal with Philadelphia in 2017 and posted his best overall statistical season in 2018-19: 18.1 points per game on 44% shooting and 39.7% from three. He moved to New Orleans in 2019, was traded to Dallas in 2021, and retired on September 21 of that year. His farewell was quintessentially Redick — announced on his own podcast: ‘I started playing basketball 30 years ago in my backyard on an uneven patch of dirt, gravel, and grass.’ Fifteen seasons. 940 games. 12,028 points. 1,950 career three-pointers — 15th most in NBA history.

In January 2016, while still an active player with the Clippers, Redick launched a podcast on Yahoo! Sports — becoming the first active NBA player to host one. The podcast evolved through multiple platforms. In August 2020, Redick and producer Tommy Alter co-founded ThreeFourTwo Productions — the name a reference to the 342 shots Redick took every Sunday during the offseason as part of his shooting regimen. Their flagship, ‘The Old Man and the Three,’ launched during the NBA’s COVID bubble with Damian Lillard as the first guest. It became one of the most popular sports podcasts in the world, amassing over 320 million YouTube views. The title was a play on Hemingway — Redick saw himself as the aging craftsman, still learning. After retirement, he joined ESPN as an analyst and quickly rose to the lead broadcast team by February 2024. LeBron James shared a clip and captioned it ‘JJ FOR PRESIDENT!!’ In March 2024, the two launched a joint podcast, ‘Mind the Game,’ focused on basketball strategy and analysis.
bio.now:
On June 26, 2024, the Los Angeles Lakers hired JJ Redick as their 29th head coach, signing him to a four-year contract. He had never coached at any level. The hire came after the Lakers’ pursuit of Dan Hurley was rebuffed (Hurley turned down a six-year, $70 million offer to stay at UConn). GM Rob Pelinka was sold on Redick’s basketball IQ, his ability to connect with players, and his relationship with LeBron James.

Redick’s first season exceeded expectations. He coached the Lakers to a 50-32 record, the third-best in the Western Conference, despite navigating a mid-season trade for Luka Doncic and the challenge of integrating ball-dominant superstars. The Lakers made the playoffs but were eliminated by the Minnesota Timberwolves in five games in the first round.

The season was also marked by personal devastation. On January 7, 2025, the Palisades Fire swept through Los Angeles. Redick’s rental home in Pacific Palisades burned to the ground. Chelsea evacuated their sons, Knox and Kai, while the team was on the road in Dallas. Everything they owned from nearly 20 years together was gone: his son’s charcoal painting of a lighthouse, the boys’ basketball card collections saved up from birthday money and chores, Chelsea’s engagement ring. Austin Reaves embraced him at the next practice.

In September 2025, the Lakers extended Redick’s contract through the 2029-30 season. He spent the offseason meeting with Rams coach Sean McVay to pick his brain on offensive concepts. He has set a remarkable NBA record: the Lakers are 57-4 when leading after three quarters under his watch, the best mark in league history.

He lives in Los Angeles with his wife, Chelsea Kilgore Redick, whom he married in 2010. They have two sons, Knox and Kai. His podcast empire continues without him — ‘The Old Man and the Three’ was rebranded as ‘The Young Man and the Three,’ with Cam Johnson of the Nuggets taking the host chair. Redick has sworn off social media to focus on coaching. ‘I am excommunicated from the content space,’ he said with a grin.

He has four tattoos of Bible verses on his body: Isaiah 40:31, Joshua 1:9, Psalm 40:1–3, and Philippians 4:13, plus a sleeve of the Virgin Mary. He graduated from Duke with a degree in history and a minor in cultural anthropology. He still holds Duke’s all-time scoring record. His net worth is estimated at approximately $50 million.

The transformation is the story. A kid from Roanoke who shot on an uneven patch of dirt, gravel, and grass. The most hated player in America who nearly quit because strangers were making signs about beating his future children. A poet who couldn’t find another way to process the pain. A role player who reinvented himself three times — as a starter, as a media mogul, and now as the coach of the Lakers.

highlights.note: Started for Orlando Magic NBA Finals team
Career-high 16.3 PPG with Clippers
Shot .440 from three — career 41.5% 3PT shooter

nba.postCareer: Podcaster (The Old Man and the Three); ESPN/NBA TV analyst; Head Coach, Los Angeles Lakers (2024-present)

players.name: Shelden Williams
players.tagline: The Landlord.
players.now: Coach/mentor; Shelden Williams Foundation; G League asst. coach
players.stat: 139 games • 13.9 ppg • 9.1 rpg • 3.0 bpg • 2x NABC DPOY • Duke career blocks/rebounds leader

bio.road:
Shelden DeMar Williams was born October 21, 1983, in Oklahoma City, Oklahoma, the son of educators Bob and Jeannette Williams. His father played basketball at Oklahoma Christian University and coached high school and junior high school basketball. His brother Quincy played basketball at North Texas. Shelden grew up in the household of teachers, and the value of learning and academic achievement was instilled early. He was a member of the National Honor Society and the Fellowship of Christian Athletes.

At Midwest City High School, Williams became one of the most dominant big men in the country. As a senior, he averaged 20 points, 12 rebounds, three blocked shots, and three assists per game. He was named the Gatorade Oklahoma Player of the Year in both 2001 and 2002, a Parade All-American, and was rated the nation’s fifth-best prep player by Future Stars and BlueChipHoops.com. His nickname — ‘The Landlord’ — stuck from high school, a nod to his ability to own the paint.

He chose Duke, arriving in the fall of 2002 as part of a class that included J.J. Redick and Sean Dockery.
bio.duke:
Williams was the most dominant defensive player in Duke history. Over four seasons, he averaged 13.9 points, 9.1 rebounds, and 3.0 blocked shots per game while shooting 57.2% from the floor. He became the first player in NCAA history to register 1,750 points, 1,250 rebounds, 400 blocked shots, and 150 steals in a career. He holds Duke’s career records in rebounds (1,217) and blocked shots (422), as well as the single-season blocks record (137 as a senior). He is the third player in ACC history to reach 1,500 points, 1,000 rebounds, 350 blocks, and 150 steals — joining Tim Duncan and Ralph Sampson.

He won the NABC Defensive Player of the Year award in both 2005 and 2006, becoming only the fifth player in history to win it in consecutive years. He was a three-time All-ACC selection, three-time All-ACC Tournament selection, three-time ACC All-Defense, and two-time ACC Defensive Player of the Year. As a senior, he was a consensus All-American and a John R. Wooden Award finalist.

His signature game came on January 11, 2006, against Maryland: 19 points, 11 rebounds, and 10 blocked shots — only the third triple-double in Duke basketball history. He was a tri-captain as a senior, helping the Blue Devils to a four-year record of 116–23 with two ACC regular season titles, three ACC Tournament championships, and four Sweet Sixteen appearances, including the 2004 Final Four. Duke retired his No. 23 jersey on January 28, 2007. He was inducted into the Duke Athletics Hall of Fame in 2018. He graduated with a degree in sociology and a certificate in Markets and Management.
bio.after:
The Atlanta Hawks selected Williams with the 5th overall pick in the 2006 NBA Draft. He showed flashes — 21 points and 10 rebounds against Indiana as a rookie, Eastern Conference Rookie of the Month for April 2007 — but his college dominance never fully translated. In February 2008, he was traded to the Sacramento Kings in the Mike Bibby deal. He bounced through seven NBA teams over parts of seven seasons: Atlanta, Sacramento, Minnesota, Boston (where he was part of the 2008 championship roster), Denver, the New York Knicks, and the New Jersey Nets.

His NBA career totals were modest: 4.5 points, 4.3 rebounds, and 0.5 assists per game across 228 games. He earned approximately $12.3 million. After his final NBA season in 2011–12 with the Nets, Williams played overseas: with Élan Chalon in France and Tianjin Ronggang in the Chinese Basketball Association for two seasons. He retired from playing around 2015.

In 2008, Williams married WNBA star Candace Parker. Their daughter, Lailaa Nicole Williams, was born in 2009. The couple divorced in 2016. Despite the separation, both parents have remained committed to co-parenting.
bio.now:
In 2019, Williams founded the Shelden Williams Foundation, dedicated to empowering student-athletes and their families by delivering and supporting initiatives that promote emotional and physical health through sports, education, and community involvement. The foundation operates in Oklahoma, Atlanta, and Durham — the three communities that shaped his life. When he made it to the NBA, he used his resources and platform to help fund the launch of a Life Center with his church.

Williams has transitioned into coaching, serving as an assistant coach for the College Park Skyhawks in the NBA G League — the Atlanta Hawks’ affiliate, bringing his career full circle to the franchise that drafted him. He mentors young players on defense, positioning, and the mental discipline that made him The Landlord at Cameron Indoor.

At 42, Shelden Williams lives as the son of educators who became one himself. His No. 23 hangs in the Cameron rafters alongside the men who preceded and followed him. The records he set in blocks and rebounds may stand for decades. The kid from Oklahoma City who owned every paint he stepped into is still teaching others how to claim theirs.

Duke’s Brotherhood: Where Are They Now? • Profile • March 2026

nba.postCareer: Coach/mentor; Shelden Williams Foundation; G League asst. coach

players.name: DeMarcus Nelson
players.tagline: California’s all-time leading scorer (3,462 points). Pastor’s son from Oakland. Grew up an Arizona fan who didn’t like Duke until they showed him a Jay Williams video. Stayed all four years. ACC Defensive Player of the Year. Sole captain. Undrafted — then became the first undrafted rookie to start on NBA opening night in history. EuroLeague MVP. French Finals MVP. Vallejo retired his jersey — the first in 150 years.
players.now: Retired; lives in Las Vegas, NV; Vallejo Sports Hall of Fame (2024); jersey #21 retired at Vallejo High (Jan 2025, first in school’s 150-year history)
players.drafted: Undrafted — signed by Golden State Warriors
players.stat: 4 Duke seasons • 124 games • 14.5/5.8 senior • ACC DPOY • 1st Team All-ACC • 3rd Team All-American • Sole captain

highlights.note: First undrafted rookie to start on NBA opening night (Golden State)
EuroLeague MVP (Round 4) with Crvena zvezda Belgrade
