overseas.note: EuroLeague MVP Rd 4; ABA League Ideal Team; obtained Serbian citizenship 2014
LNB Pro A Champion 2019; Finals MVP; French Cup 2019

nba.postCareer: Retired (Dec 2020); lives in Las Vegas; Vallejo Sports HOF 2024; jersey #21 retired at Vallejo High Jan 2025 (first in 150-year school history)

bio.road:
DeMarcus De’Juan Nelson was born November 2, 1985, in Oakland, California. His father Ron was a pastor who grew up playing basketball on the streets of Richmond. His mother Denise was a hair stylist. Ron had been offered a chance to play professionally in Switzerland but chose family instead. When DeMarcus was in sixth grade, his father took him from Vallejo’s local tournaments to Oakland’s courts, and the boy dominated everywhere.

At Vallejo High School, Nelson became a phenomenon. Fans lined up outside Bottari Gym to watch him play. He played three years at Vallejo before his family moved to Sacramento, finishing his senior year at Sheldon High. Over four years he scored 3,462 career points — the all-time record in California boys basketball history. California Mr. Basketball 2004. Second-team Parade All-American. McDonald’s All-American. His father said DeMarcus might even have been a better football player, but he gave up football to protect against injury.

At the 2004 McDonald’s All-American Game — Dwight Howard, Rajon Rondo, LaMarcus Aldridge, J.R. Smith — Nelson wasn’t named a starter. He took it personally. Entered midway through, scored 22 — second-highest in the game.

Growing up, he was an Arizona fan who didn’t like Duke — games came on too late on the West Coast. But when he visited Durham, they showed him a video of Jay Williams. “I just felt like I could have that same type of impact, and it would be no better platform.” He committed as a sophomore in 2002, planning to stay two or three years. He stayed all four.
bio.duke:
Nelson’s four years at Duke (2004–08) tested him in ways he never anticipated. Freshman: energy off the bench, 6.2/4.5 in 33 games. Then a torn thumb ligament and chipped bone in preseason. Another injury derailed a Team USA opportunity. The kid who planned to leave after two years kept getting knocked down and kept getting up.

Junior year: emerged as a starter, 14.4/5.1, playing 30+ minutes alongside Paulus and McRoberts. The injuries had sharpened his toughness. “The fear of failure is a good thing. It drove me to try to be as perfect as possible.”

Senior year, 2007–08: the culmination. Named sole team captain — the only captain on the roster. Averaged 14.5/5.8, led the team in scoring and steals. ACC Defensive Player of the Year. First Team All-ACC. ACC All-Tournament. Third Team All-American. The best defender in the conference, the leader of the program, the player who stayed when leaving would have been easier.

He was also the godfather to his Vallejo coach Duke Brown’s son — the infant who died at two months old. When Brown was grieving, Nelson called every morning and every evening. Not because he had to, but because he was a deeply caring person.
bio.after:
Despite ACC DPOY and Third Team All-American honors, Nelson was not drafted. The injuries had shaped how scouts evaluated him. The Golden State Warriors signed him September 9, 2008. On opening night, Nelson started — the first undrafted rookie to start on opening night in NBA history. Playing for his hometown team, friends and family in the stands. He appeared in 13 games (4.1 PPG) before being waived in January 2009.

Brief stints with Chicago and Milwaukee. Then overseas — where he thrived. Israel, Serbia, Spain, Monaco, France, Germany. In November 2013, playing for Crvena zvezda Belgrade alongside Boban Marjanovic, he was named EuroLeague MVP for Round 4. Selected to the ABA League Ideal Team. Obtained Serbian citizenship in 2014.

His finest chapter: ASVEL Lyon-Villeurbanne in France (2016–19). He helped win the LNB Pro A championship in 2019 and was named Finals MVP. Also won the French Cup that year. The undrafted kid from Vallejo had won championships and MVP awards on three continents. He retired in December 2020.
bio.now:
DeMarcus Nelson lives in Las Vegas, Nevada. On January 22, 2025, Vallejo High School retired his #21 jersey — the first jersey retirement in the school’s 150-year history. In 2024, he was inducted into the Vallejo Sports Hall of Fame. “Most of this record is Vallejo’s. It’s still my hometown and the people there can take most of the credit.”

DeMarcus Nelson was born in Oakland, the son of a pastor from Richmond who chose family over playing professionally in Switzerland. He grew up in Vallejo, where fans lined up outside Bottari Gym. He scored 3,462 points — the most in California history. He was the McDonald’s All-American who wasn’t named a starter and scored 22 with a chip on his shoulder. He grew up an Arizona fan who didn’t like Duke until they showed him a Jay Williams video and he saw himself. He planned to stay two years. He stayed four. He tore a ligament as a freshman. He kept getting up. He was ACC Defensive Player of the Year and sole captain. He called his coach every morning and night when the coach’s infant son died. He was undrafted. He started on opening night for the Warriors anyway — first undrafted player in NBA history to do so. He played in five countries. He was EuroLeague MVP. He won the French championship and was named Finals MVP. Vallejo retired his jersey — the first in 150 years. “The fear of failure is a good thing,” DeMarcus Nelson says. “It drove me to try to be as perfect as possible.” Perfectly imperfect, relentlessly tough, and forever Vallejo’s own.

players.name: Josh McRoberts
players.tagline: Flair, flash, and an early exit.
players.now: Assistant coach, Carmel HS; Hamilton County Basketball HOF
players.stat: 69 games • 10.8 PPG • 6.5 RPG • McDonald’s AA Game MVP • ACC All-Freshman • 11 NBA seasons

bio.road:
Josh McRoberts grew up in Carmel, Indiana, a prosperous suburb north of Indianapolis, in a basketball-centric family. His father, Tim McRoberts, was a Carmel High School alumnus who had played on the school’s basketball team. His mother, Jennifer, was a teacher. He had two younger sisters, Elizabeth and Sarah, and a younger brother, Zach. One of his most influential mentors was the late Eric Clark, a beloved Carmel administrator, teacher, and head coach for football, basketball, and track, for whom the school’s athletic facilities were eventually named.

What separates McRoberts from most blue-chip recruits is the modesty of his original ambition. In a 2016 interview with WTHR in Indianapolis, McRoberts stood on the Carmel court where the Greyhounds play and said: ‘I just love playing basketball. I had the opportunity to play from an early age. My family was always involved in it. Honestly my biggest dream was probably to play here, to play at Carmel High School. That was my biggest aspiration. So everything after that was like a bonus to me.’ His picture hangs on the wall outside the gym.

At Carmel, McRoberts became one of the most decorated high school players in Indiana history. As a senior, he averaged 17.9 points, 11.4 rebounds, 4.2 assists, and 3.0 blocks while shooting 59.3% from the field, leading the Greyhounds to a 21–4 record and a sectional championship game. He finished his high school career with 1,264 points, 759 rebounds, 255 assists, and 259 blocks. He was rated the No. 1 power forward in the Class of 2005 by Rivals.com and Scout.com, ranked second overall nationally, and named the top-ranked senior by Bob Gibbons. He was named the Morgan Wootten Award winner as the McDonald’s All-American Player of the Year and won the McDonald’s All-American Game MVP with 17 points on 7-of-8 shooting and a game-high 12 rebounds. He was also a first-team Parade All-American and All-USA Today selection. He was a finalist for Indiana Mr. Basketball (finishing behind Luke Zeller). He was a five-tool athlete who also earned all-conference honors in golf as a scratch golfer and was a two-time league champion in both the high jump and triple jump.
bio.duke:
McRoberts arrived at Duke in the fall of 2005 as the headliner of what was supposed to be the nation’s top recruiting class. The class included McRoberts, point guard Greg Paulus, center Eric Boateng, power forward Jamal Boykin, and guard Marty Pocius. Duke Basketball Report would later call it ‘a major disappointment — at least in Duke terms.’ Boateng and Boykin transferred. Pocius became a European professional but never contributed at Duke. Paulus finished his college career playing football for Syracuse. Of the five, only McRoberts delivered on his recruiting ranking — and even he fell short of the standard that had been set for him.

But before the disappointment, there was the bonding. A Sports Illustrated feature from November 2005 captured a quintessential Duke scene: J.J. Redick, the most famous player in college basketball, driving McRoberts home from practice in his 2001 Toyota Corolla. McRoberts’s 6-foot-10 frame was jammed into the passenger seat, knees near his chin. ‘It seems like J.J.’s always driving me around,’ McRoberts said, ‘and I’m always paying for his gas.’ Redick took McRoberts home to Roanoke over fall break; McRoberts hosted Redick in Carmel. They played video games instead of going to clubs. Redick, when asked what he thought during the NCAA president’s ‘message: graduate’ speech, cracked: ‘That there’s no way this dude’s going to graduate.’ Coach K compared McRoberts’s potential to Grant Hill’s.

As a freshman, McRoberts averaged 8.7 points and 5.3 rebounds while leading Duke in field goal percentage (60.5%). He made the ACC All-Freshman team. Duke went 32–4, won the ACC regular-season and tournament titles, and reached the Sweet Sixteen. He scored 16 points on 5-of-5 shooting in the ACC Tournament championship game against Boston College. His late-season surge — 10 double-figure scoring games in his final 14 — suggested the best was yet to come.

It wasn’t. Before his sophomore year, McRoberts underwent a lumbar discectomy on August 3, 2006, for chronic back problems that had plagued him since high school. He recovered in time for the season opener and was named one of three team captains — he and Paulus became the first two sophomore captains in Duke history. On December 19 against Kent State, McRoberts had the game of his life: 19 points, six blocks, six rebounds, four assists, and four steals — the first Duke player ever to tally at least four in all five statistical categories. He made All-ACC and ACC All-Defense. But Duke lost to VCU in the first round of the 2007 NCAA Tournament, 79–77. McRoberts played all 40 minutes and put up 22 points and 12 rebounds with five blocks and four assists — but missed two of four free throws in the final 1:41. Eric Maynor hit a game-winning jumper with 1.8 seconds left. Krzyzewski said McRoberts and Paulus ‘really carried us.’ It wasn’t enough.

McRoberts declared for the draft after the VCU loss. A year earlier, he had been projected as a mid-to-late first-round pick. His stock had fallen. Portland took him 37th overall — second round. The kid who had been the No. 1 power forward in America and the McDonald’s All-American Game MVP was a second-round pick two years later. It was the sharpest draft-stock decline of any Duke player in the Brotherhood.
bio.after:
McRoberts spent eleven seasons in the NBA playing for seven teams: Portland Trail Blazers (2007–08), Indiana Pacers (2008–11), Los Angeles Lakers (2011–12), Orlando Magic (2012–13), Charlotte Bobcats/Hornets (2013–14), Miami Heat (2014–17), and Dallas Mavericks (2017–18). He was not a star. He was not an All-Star. He was what Pau Gasol, his Lakers teammate, called ‘scrappy and a hustler’ — a screen-setter with a decent jumper, excellent passing skills for a big man, and a relentless motor. His scoring came off fast breaks, rebounds, and hustle plays.

His best NBA season was 2013–14 in Charlotte, where he started all 78 games and scored a career-high 24 points against Orlando. His biggest payday came in July 2014, when the Miami Heat signed him to a four-year, $23 million contract. But he tore his right meniscus 17 games into the first season and never regained his footing in Miami. Hassan Whiteside’s emergence took his starting spot. A bruised knee cost him two months. A stress fracture in his left foot ended his 2016–17 season. He was traded to Dallas in July 2017, played two games for the Mavericks, and was waived in February 2018.

Across eleven seasons: approximately 450 NBA games, career averages of 5.4 points, 4.0 rebounds, and 2.2 assists. Roughly $30 million in career earnings. He was part of the blockbuster trade that sent Dwight Howard from Orlando to the Lakers in 2012, and he was traded to his hometown Indiana Pacers on draft night 2008 — the closest he ever got to fulfilling his childhood dream of playing basketball at home.
bio.now:
Josh McRoberts came home. During the 2011 NBA lockout, when his brother Zach was a sophomore on the Carmel basketball team, head coach Scott Heady let Josh hang around the program and help out for a month. McRoberts loved it. ‘I’ve never been in a coaching position, so I’m learning as I go,’ he said. ‘I’ve been having a great time.’

In the 2020–21 season, during the COVID-19 pandemic, McRoberts officially joined the Carmel High School varsity basketball staff as an assistant coach under Ryan Osborn. He moved back to Carmel to be near his family and reestablish himself. ‘Having been part of a team for as long as I had, I felt the absence of that kind of inclusion and purpose,’ he said. ‘The help from my coaches in high school and having a support system that included my family and the Carmel community as a whole was a big thing for me.’

He was inducted into the Hamilton County Basketball Hall of Fame. He is 38 years old. His picture still hangs on the wall outside the Carmel gym. He watches junior high kids chase the same dream he once chased on the same court.

Josh McRoberts’s Brotherhood story is the cautionary tale of the Between Era — and the most honest about what happens when the hype doesn’t match the outcome. He was the No. 1 power forward in America, the McDonald’s All-American Game MVP, the player Coach K compared to Grant Hill. He arrived at Duke as the headliner of the most disappointing class in program history. He had back surgery before his sophomore year, made the first five-category stat line in Duke history, and then watched Eric Maynor’s jumper end his college career with 1.8 seconds left. His draft stock fell from the lottery to the 37th pick. He spent eleven NBA seasons on seven teams, earning the respect of teammates like Gasol (‘scrappy and a hustler’) but never the stardom his high school ranking promised.

And then he went home. Back to Carmel. Back to the court where his biggest dream was simply to play. He’s coaching the next generation of Greyhounds in the gym named after his mentor, Eric Clark. The kid whose knees were jammed into J.J. Redick’s Toyota Corolla is teaching high schoolers how to set screens and make the extra pass. His career gave him perspective, he said. Everything after Carmel was a bonus. And the bonus, eventually, brought him back to where he started.

Duke’s Brotherhood: Where Are They Now? • Profile • March 2026

highlights.note: Best year: 10.1/5.2/3.8 with Charlotte — key to playoff push

nba.postCareer: Assistant coach, Carmel HS; Hamilton County Basketball HOF

players.name: Greg Paulus
players.tagline: Born in Ohio, raised in Wisconsin, made in Syracuse. The nation’s best QB AND best PG simultaneously. Beat Ray Rice in a state football championship. Gatorade National Athlete of the Year. Chose Duke basketball. Won four ACC titles. Then went back to Syracuse and started at quarterback without playing football in four years. Head coach at Niagara. Two Halls of Fame. There will never be another.
players.now: Head Coach, Niagara University men’s basketball (since 2019); inducted into Greater Syracuse Sports HOF and NYSPHSAA HOF (2024)
players.drafted: Did not play professional basketball or football — went directly into coaching
players.stat: Duke: 4 seasons • 139 games • 1,193 pts • 468 assists • 39.8% 3PT • 112-28 • 4 ACC titles | Syracuse: 12 starts • 2,024 yds • 13 TDs

sources.label: GoDuke.com: Greg Paulus player bio
NYSPHSAA Hall of Fame: Greg Paulus (2024)
Greater Syracuse Sports Hall of Fame: Greg Paulus
GW Athletics: Greg Paulus coaching bio
Sports Reference: Greg Paulus college stats
Wikipedia: Greg Paulus

nba.postCareer: Head Coach at Niagara University; also started all 12 games at QB for Syracuse (2009) after 4 years without football; tried out with New Orleans Saints (2010); master’s degree from Syracuse Newhouse School

bio.road:
Gregory Russell Paulus was born on July 3, 1986, in Medina, Ohio. He grew up in Appleton, Wisconsin, before his family moved to the Syracuse suburb of Manlius, New York. He was one of seven children — six brothers and a sister: David, Matt, Dan, Chris, Mike, and Sarah. The Paulus household was a factory of athletes, and Greg was the most gifted of them all.

At Christian Brothers Academy in Syracuse, Paulus became something that shouldn’t be possible: the best high school quarterback AND the best high school point guard in New York State simultaneously, and arguably in the entire country. On the football field, he broke New York state records for career passing touchdowns (152) and career passing yards (11,760), and set the single-season yardage record with 3,677. He was named Sports Illustrated’s best quarterback in the nation. He was a U.S. Army All-American, a Parade All-American, and a USA Today second-team All-American — all in football. In the 2004 New York State Class AA Championship game, Paulus led CBA to the school’s first-ever state football title, defeating New Rochelle High School and future NFL running back Ray Rice 41–35 in the Carrier Dome in Syracuse. He was named MVP of the championship game.

On the basketball court, he accumulated 2,399 career points — twelfth in New York state history — and averaged 26.8 points, 8.2 assists, 5.1 rebounds, and 3.4 steals as a senior. He was a five-time all-league selection, four-time all-state, New York State Mr. Basketball, a McDonald’s All-American, a Parade All-American, and the top-ranked point guard in the country by both Rivals.com and Scout.com. At the Nike Hoop Summit, he set a record with 10 assists.

He was named Gatorade National High School Football Player of the Year in 2004 and then Gatorade National High School Male Athlete of the Year in 2005 — covering all sports. He received football scholarship offers from Miami and Notre Dame. He received basketball offers from Duke, Syracuse, Georgetown, Florida, North Carolina, and Xavier. He had to choose between being the nation’s best quarterback and the nation’s best point guard. In his first week as a high school junior, Greg Paulus announced he would attend Duke to play basketball. First and foremost, his father David said, Greg has a passion for basketball, and he’d love to be involved in helping Coach Krzyzewski and his team compete for a national championship.
bio.duke:
Paulus played four seasons at Duke, 2005–06 through 2008–09, appearing in 139 games. He was a three-year starter, a two-time team captain, and a three-time Academic All-ACC selection and two-time Academic All-American. He scored 1,193 career points, dished 468 assists, and shot 39.8 percent from three-point range. His teams went a combined 112–28, won four ACC championships, and made four NCAA Tournament appearances including two Sweet Sixteen runs.

As a freshman in 2005–06, Paulus led the ACC in assists at 5.2 per game and was named to the ACC All-Freshman First Team. He set a Duke freshman record with 15 assists in a single game against Valparaiso — one short of the all-time Duke record held by Bobby Hurley. His 187 freshman assists ranked third in Duke history behind only Hurley and Jay Williams. Duke went 32–4 and won the ACC regular-season and tournament titles.

Junior year, 2007–08, was his best statistical season: he led the ACC in assist-to-turnover ratio, shot over 42 percent from three, hit 82 threes, and averaged 10.5 points and 4.3 assists. He led Duke to a 28–6 record and scored 18 points in an 89–78 victory over rival North Carolina. He was named Third Team All-ACC.

His senior season was humbling. The development of sophomore Nolan Smith pushed Paulus out of the starting lineup — he started only five games and averaged 16 minutes. For a player who had been the nation’s number one point guard recruit, coming off the bench as a senior tested everything he was made of. His father’s high school scouting report had said that football made Greg tough — tough enough to handle the disappointment of diminished playing time and still lead from wherever Coach K needed him.
bio.after:
What Greg Paulus did next is one of the most remarkable athletic feats in modern college sports history. After four years of playing only basketball at Duke, Paulus took advantage of an NCAA provision allowing him a graduate transfer year for football. On May 14, 2009, he announced he would play quarterback at Syracuse University — the school in his hometown, in the same Carrier Dome where he had won a state football championship five years earlier. He enrolled in the S.I. Newhouse School of Public Communications for his master’s degree.

Paulus had not taken a competitive snap in four years. He had to compete against redshirt freshman Ryan Nassib and senior Cameron Dantley for the starting job under first-year head coach Doug Marrone. Marrone named Paulus the starter on August 18, 2009, citing his leadership and competitive experience. He started all twelve games that season. He completed 193 of 285 passes for 2,024 yards and 13 touchdowns, setting a Syracuse single-season record for completion percentage at 67.7 percent. The team went 4–8.

After going undrafted in the 2010 NFL Draft, Paulus tried out with the New Orleans Saints at rookie minicamp, competing behind Drew Brees, Chase Daniel, and Sean Canfield. He was not offered a contract. The football dream was over. But the basketball dream had already been fulfilled, and a coaching career was about to begin.
bio.now:
Paulus went into coaching immediately. He spent a season at Navy, then six years at Ohio State — starting as video coordinator and rising to assistant coach, helping the Buckeyes reach the 2012 Final Four and sign top-10 recruiting classes. He coached at Louisville under David Padgett, then at George Washington, before returning to his home state of New York to join the Niagara staff. In October 2019, he was named interim head coach after Patrick Beilein resigned, and was later made the permanent head coach. He is married to Megan.

In 2024, Paulus was inducted into both the Greater Syracuse Sports Hall of Fame and the New York State Public High School Athletic Association Hall of Fame, recognition of a high school career that may never be matched in the state: first-ever athlete to be named the national player of the year in football AND the national athlete of the year across all sports, while also being named the state’s best basketball player.

Greg Paulus was born in Medina, Ohio, raised in Appleton, Wisconsin, and made in Syracuse, New York. He broke New York state records for career passing touchdowns and yards. He beat Ray Rice in a state championship game in the Carrier Dome. He scored 2,399 points on the basketball court. He was the Gatorade National Football Player of the Year and the Gatorade National Athlete of the Year and New York State Mr. Basketball and a McDonald’s All-American and the nation’s number one ranked point guard. He chose Duke basketball over Miami football, and his father said it was about passion. He played 139 games for Coach K across four seasons, won four ACC championships, led the ACC in assists as a freshman, and came within one assist of Bobby Hurley’s all-time Duke record in a single game. He lost his starting job as a senior and handled it with grace. Then he went back to the Carrier Dome — the same building where he had won a state football championship five years earlier — and started all twelve games at quarterback for Syracuse without having played football in four years. He tried out for the Saints. He became a head coach. He was inducted into two Halls of Fame. There has never been another Greg Paulus, and there probably never will be. He was the best at everything, and when he had to choose, he chose basketball. And then, because he was Greg Paulus, he went back and played football anyway.

players.name: Gerald Henderson
players.tagline: The spark that reignited the engine.
players.now: Retired; Charlotte Hornets broadcast analyst
players.stat: 103 games • First-Team All-ACC 2009 • McDonald’s AA dunk champion • 535 NBA games • $37.4M career earnings

bio.road:
Gerald Henderson Jr. was born into basketball royalty and raised in the shadow of one of the most famous steals in NBA history. His father, Gerald Henderson Sr., was a 13-year NBA veteran who won three championship rings — two with the Boston Celtics (1981, 1984) and one with the Detroit Pistons (1990). He played alongside Larry Bird, Robert Parish, Kevin McHale, Patrick Ewing, Charles Barkley, Isiah Thomas, and Joe Dumars. His signature moment: the 1984 NBA Finals against the Lakers, when he stole a James Worthy pass and scored a game-tying layup that forced overtime. The Celtics won the game and the series.

Gerald Jr. was born December 9, 1987, in Caldwell, New Jersey, while his father was playing for the 76ers. Youngest of three children — older brother Jermaine (basketball at Saint Rose) and sister Jade (graduated from Villanova). The father played his son one-on-one in the backyard and never let him win. ‘I beat him that last time when he was in ninth, tenth grade,’ Henderson Sr. said. ‘I played him until I got to the point where I said, I can’t take this punishment no more. He was getting bigger and stronger. That athleticism — that’s something you can’t teach.’

‘I’ve seen that steal hundreds of times,’ Gerald Jr. said of his father’s defining moment. When the 2009 NCAA East Regional was held at Madison Square Garden, the Boston Globe ran a feature: ‘Son is in Forecast.’ Gerald Jr. was asked what he knew about his dad’s era. ‘All he talks about is our shorts and how the guys didn’t used to dunk as much.’

At Episcopal Academy in Merion, Pennsylvania: five-sport phenom. 18.4 ppg, 8.6 rpg as a junior; 25–3 record; state champion. Scratch golfer with all-conference honors. Two-time league champion in high jump and triple jump. His teammate was Wayne Ellington, a future four-year UNC starter. McDonald’s All-American. Won the 2006 McDonald’s dunk contest — the second Duke player to win it (after Ricky Price in 1994). Also won the Naismith Sportsmanship Award. Led the East with 16 points.
bio.duke:
Freshman year (2006–07): 6.8 ppg off the bench. Raw and electric — 45 dunks as a sophomore, the most by a Duke wing since Grant Hill’s 48 in 1991–92. Sophomore year: starting lineup, 12.7 ppg, leading the team in scoring seven times and rebounding eight times. In the nine biggest games — two UNC, two Clemson, Davidson, Wisconsin, two NCAA Tournament games — he averaged 14.2 ppg, 5.7 rpg, 1.6 blocks, above his season averages across the board.

The Belmont game. First round, 2008 NCAA Tournament, Washington’s Verizon Center. No. 15 Belmont led Duke by one with two minutes left. Henderson had kept Duke alive with 21 points on 8-of-14. With 11.9 seconds left, Duke facing elimination, Henderson drove coast-to-coast and tipped in the game-winning shot. DeMarcus Nelson preserved the win with a steal. Duke 71, Belmont 70. DBR described it as one of the closest calls in Duke tournament history.

Junior year (2008–09): unquestioned leader. 16.5 ppg. First-Team All-ACC — first Duke player to earn it since Redick in 2006. ACC Tournament champion. No. 2 seed. He left for the draft after three seasons.
bio.after:
Charlotte Bobcats, 12th overall, 2009. Six seasons in Charlotte — the longest stretch of his career. During the catastrophic 2011–12 season (7–59, worst record in NBA history), Henderson was one of the lone bright spots, averaging a career-high 15.1 points. Traded to Portland (2015). Signed with Philadelphia (2016). Career: 535 games, 333 starts, 11.2 ppg, $37.4 million.

In August 2017, hip surgery. Missed the entire 2017–18 season. Tried to come back in 2018 — worked out for teams, then tore his Achilles in a pickup game. The comeback was over before it started. January 2019: joined the Charlotte Hornets broadcast team. April 30, 2019: officially retired.
bio.now:
Gerald Henderson Jr. is 37 years old. Hornets broadcast analyst in Charlotte, close to the franchise that drafted him. His father and mother run a real estate business in Blue Bell, Pennsylvania.

His Brotherhood story is the quietest of the Between Era — and the most shaped by inheritance. He grew up watching his father’s championship steal on replay. Played one-on-one against a three-time NBA champion and lost every time until high school. Won the McDonald’s dunk contest — the second Henderson to define a moment on national television. Tipped in a shot with 11.9 seconds left to save Duke from a 15-seed upset. Was the best player on the worst team in NBA history. Tried to come back from hip surgery and tore his Achilles in a pickup game.

His father stole the ball from James Worthy and changed a Finals. His son tipped in a shot against Belmont and changed a first-round game. The scale is different. The gene is the same. Gerald Jr. never won a championship. But he carried the name for eight NBA seasons, and when the body said no, he picked up a microphone. The Henderson basketball story spans two generations, three rings (all his father’s), one legendary steal, one legendary tip-in, and a family that understood: you play until it doesn’t let you anymore.

players.name: Jon Scheyer
players.tagline: The Jewish Jordan from Northbrook. Scored 21 points in 75 seconds. Disliked Duke as a kid because all his friends liked Duke. Chose Duke anyway. Played every game for four years. 2010 National Champion. Recruited Zion, Tatum, Barrett, Banchero, and Cooper Flagg. Named the 20th head coach in Duke history. 2025 Final Four. National Coach of the Year. The kid who said “We’ll just do it here” is doing it here. And the Brotherhood continues.
players.now: Head Coach, Duke University men’s basketball (20th in program history, named June 4, 2021); 89 wins in first 3 seasons; 2025 Final Four; McLendon National Coach of the Year
players.drafted: Undrafted — played professionally in G-League, Israel (Maccabi Tel Aviv), and Spain (Gran Canaria)
players.stat: Player: 4 seasons • 138 games • 18.2/4.9 senior • 2nd Team All-American • 2010 Champion | Coach: 89 wins in 3 years • 2025 Final Four

sources.label: GoDuke.com: Jon Scheyer head coach bio
FOX Sports: Duke’s new king — Jon Scheyer’s journey (2023)
Duke Chronicle: Before Scheyer belonged to The Brotherhood, he was the pride of Chicago basketball (2025)
Wikipedia: Jon Scheyer

coaching.note: 2022 Final Four; recruited Zion, Tatum, Barrett, Banchero
89 wins in 3 seasons (tied D-I record); 2025 Final Four; McLendon National Coach of the Year; recruited Cooper Flagg

nba.postCareer: 20th head coach in Duke basketball history; 6-year contract through 2028-29; married to Marcelle; three children: Noa, Jett, James; founded Scheyer Kid Captain program at Duke Children’s Hospital

bio.road:
Jonathan James Scheyer was born August 24, 1987, in Northbrook, Illinois, a suburb of Chicago. Youngest of three children of Jim and Laury Scheyer. Raised Jewish, had a basketball-themed Bar Mitzvah, started dribbling at age three. Played in the Fellowship of Afro-American Men league in Evanston. Tom Crean offered him a Marquette scholarship in eighth grade.

Everyone told his parents to transfer him to a powerhouse. He refused. “We’ll just do it here. We’ll build the success at Glenbrook North.” His father got chills thinking about it.

At Glenbrook North (coached by Dave Weber, Bruce Weber’s brother): 3,034 career points — 4th in Illinois history. Led the Spartans to the 2005 state championship as a junior with 27 in the title game. The team had an all-Jewish starting lineup — the only state championship team in the country known to have accomplished that. Nicknamed the “Jewish Jordan.” Illinois Mr. Basketball 2006, McDonald’s All-American, one of the 100 Legends of Illinois Basketball. His coach compared him to Larry Bird and Pistol Pete.

The legend: at the Proviso West Holiday Tournament as a senior, he scored 21 points in 75 seconds during a one-man comeback. He scored 33 against a Lawrence North team ranked #1 nationally that featured Mike Conley and Greg Oden.

He chose Duke over Arizona, Illinois, and Wisconsin. Irony: in junior high, he disliked Duke because all his friends liked Duke and he wanted to be different. He chose Duke anyway — Chris Collins, a Glenbrook North alum, was an assistant coach. Being different is not the same as being stubborn.
bio.duke:
Scheyer played four seasons (2006–10), starting all 138 games. Freshman: started every game at SG, 11.2 PPG, led ACC freshmen in FT%. Sophomore: came off bench, 11.7 PPG, .889 FT% (2nd in ACC), scored 27 off the bench at Miami (tied Duke record).

The transformation: midway through junior year, Coach K moved him from SG (91 games) to PG. Over the final 9 games and into the postseason: 19.7 PPG, 2.5 APG, 1 turnover per game. ACC Tournament MVP (21.7 PPG). Sports Illustrated: “Scheyer is one of the shyest Duke stars in recent memory. The next time he brags or trash-talks will be the first.”

Senior year: 18.2/4.9/1.6. Started all 40 games. Scored in double figures in all but two. Set the ACC single-season record for minutes played (1,470). First Team All-ACC. Consensus Second Team All-American. Duke went 35–5. On April 5, 2010, in Indianapolis, they beat Butler 61–59 for the national championship. The Big Three — Scheyer, Singler, Nolan Smith — had stayed four years and won the only trophy that mattered.
bio.after:
Undrafted. Training camp with the LA Clippers (waived). Played for Rio Grande Valley Vipers (G-League), Maccabi Tel Aviv in Israel — meaningful for a Jewish player — and Gran Canaria in Spain. An eye injury contributed to his transition to coaching.

In 2013, he returned to Duke as a special assistant. Promoted to assistant coach, then associate head coach alongside Nate James in 2018. Over the next decade, he became college basketball’s most important recruiter: the lead recruiter on Jayson Tatum, Zion Williamson, RJ Barrett, Paolo Banchero, and Cooper Flagg. He told Luke Kennard’s family: “We didn’t bring Luke here to be a basketball player. We brought him here to be a star.” Part of two national championships as coach (2010 player, 2015 coach) and two more Final Fours.
bio.now:
On June 4, 2021, Jon Scheyer was named the 20th head coach in Duke basketball history. He was thirty-five — the youngest at a power conference institution. Coach K’s final season ended with a 2022 Final Four loss to UNC. Then it was Scheyer’s program.

Year 1 (2022–23): ACC Tournament champions. Year 2 (2023–24): Elite Eight. Year 3 (2024–25): ACC regular-season and tournament championships, 32–6, Final Four (fell to Houston in the semifinals). His 89 wins in three seasons tied the D-I record. John McLendon National Coach of the Year. Duke averaged 83.2 PPG while allowing fewer than 63 — the only D-I team to achieve both. In 16 seasons as player and coach: 2 national championships, 4 Final Fours, 6 ACC Tournament crowns, 449–122 (.786).

Six-year extension through 2028–29. Married to Marcelle. Three children: Noa, Jett, and James. He and Marcelle founded the Scheyer Kid Captain program, inviting patients from Duke Children’s Hospital to spend game days with the team.

Jon Scheyer started dribbling at three. He was offered a scholarship in eighth grade. He refused to transfer and said “We’ll just do it here.” He scored 3,034 points at Glenbrook North with an all-Jewish starting lineup. They called him the Jewish Jordan. He scored 21 points in 75 seconds. He disliked Duke as a kid and chose Duke anyway. He played every game for four years. He was moved from SG to PG and became the ACC Tournament MVP. He led Duke to the 2010 championship. He played in Israel and Spain. He came back and became the most important recruiter in college basketball. He was named the 20th head coach at thirty-five. He won 89 games in three years and took Duke to the 2025 Final Four. He was named National Coach of the Year. He started the Kid Captain program at Duke Children’s Hospital. He is the bridge between the Krzyzewski era and whatever comes next — and what comes next is more of what Duke has always been: the standard. The kid from Northbrook who said “We’ll just do it here” is doing it here. And the Brotherhood continues.

players.name: Nolan Smith
players.tagline: His father won the 1980 championship with Louisville, outscored Michael Jordan, played nine NBA years, and died on a cruise ship near Bermuda when Nolan was eight. Uncle Johnny Dawkins raised him in basketball. Michael Beasley moved in as a brother. He looked at the ceiling in every arena for his father. Won the 2010 title in Indianapolis — same city as his father in 1980. ACC Player of the Year. Tattoo: Forever Watching. Now head coach at Tennessee State.
players.now: Head Coach, Tennessee State University (hired July 2025); previously assistant at Duke (2016-22), Louisville (2022-24), Memphis (2024-25)
players.stat: 4 Duke seasons • 143 games • 1,911 pts • 20.6/5.1 senior • ACC POY • Consensus All-American • 2010 Champion

coaching.role: Special Assistant / Dir. of Basketball Ops / Assistant Coach
coaching.note: 159-47 record; 2022 Final Four; coached 25 future NBA players
First head coaching position; HBCU in Nashville

nba.postCareer: Head Coach, Tennessee State University; married Cheyna Elliott (UNC grad); three children: Camryn, Derek “Deuce,” and Kai

bio.road:
Nolan Derek Smith was born July 25, 1988, in Louisville, Kentucky. Named after Nolan Ryan. His mother Monica had a law degree from Louisville. His father was Derek Smith.

Derek arrived at Louisville in 1979 as a sixteen-year-old from Hogansville, Georgia — population barely a thousand — with his wardrobe in a brown paper sack. He was a starting forward on Louisville’s 1980 NCAA championship team. He outscored Michael Jordan 33–20 in a famous 1984 game. He played nine NBA years. Many credit him with helping popularize the high five. He became an assistant coach for the Washington Bullets, and little Nolan set screens on NBA paint as a toddler. “The first person to put a ball in my hands was my dad. I felt like it was at birth.”

On August 9, 1996, while on a family cruise near Bermuda, Derek suffered a fatal heart attack on deck. He was thirty-four. Nolan was eight. Their last conversation was about attitude: “If this is the sport you choose to play the rest of your life, it’s all going to come down to your attitude. Atttiiiituuuude. You hear me?”

After Derek’s death, the Bullets made Nolan a fixture at home games. Derek’s former teammate and Duke alumnus Johnny Dawkins became “Uncle Johnny” — the father figure. At thirteen, Nolan met Michael Beasley on a court. Beasley had his own problems at home. Nolan’s mother and stepfather Curtis Malone took him in. By eighth grade, Beasley had moved in permanently. “It was like the brother he never had.”

At Oak Hill Academy, Nolan played alongside Beasley and Ty Lawson. McDonald’s All-American. He considered Louisville — his father’s school — but knew the weight would be unbearable. Uncle Johnny, now at Duke, arranged a camp visit. Coach K saw the boy’s huge feet. Nolan committed January 12, 2006. He would write his own chapters while still being his father’s little shadow.
bio.duke:
Nolan played four years at Duke (2007–11), appearing in 143 games. His freshman year was emotionally devastating. Taking the floor at Cameron for the first time, he found himself looking at the ceiling for his father. It became his ritual. Every building in the ACC — always the roof. His mother shouted “Go to work!” before every tip-off. Their ode to Derek.

Freshman and sophomore years: 6.1 and 8.5 PPG. Coach K pulled him from the starting lineup midseason as a sophomore. It could have broken him. Instead, it forged him.

Junior year, 2009–10: the explosion. 17.4 PPG. Scored 29 in the Elite Eight vs Baylor. The championship game was in Indianapolis — the same city where his father won in 1980. Duke beat Butler 61–59. Nolan was a champion, thirty years after his father, in the same city. All-Final Four Team.

Senior year: the coronation. 20.6/5.1/4.5 — one of two players nationally averaging 20 and 5. Career-high 34 vs UNC. ACC Player of the Year. Consensus First Team All-American. Finalist for Naismith, Wooden, Robertson, and Cousy Awards. Duke went 32–5. His senior class finished 65–2 at home — .970, the best in Duke history.
bio.after: Portland took Smith 21st overall in 2011. Played 44 rookie games (3.8 PPG). Carried off on a stretcher in 2012 Summer League after an elbow to the head. G-League stint with Idaho Stampede (22.5 PPG). Signed with Cedevita Zagreb in Croatia (2013), where he hit a game-winner with 0.6 seconds left. The NBA career was brief. The overseas career was productive. The coaching career was inevitable.
bio.now:
Smith returned to Duke in 2016: special assistant, director of basketball ops, assistant coach by 2021. Six years on staff: 159-47 record (.772), 2022 Final Four, coached 25 future NBA players including Tatum, Williamson, Barrett, Banchero, and Bagley.

When Coach K retired, Nolan felt Louisville’s pull. Kenny Payne — Derek’s former teammate — hired him. But Louisville won just 12 games in two seasons; the entire staff was let go. Smith briefly joined Memphis before being named head coach at Tennessee State in July 2025 — an HBCU in Nashville, his first head coaching position.

He is married to Cheyna Elliott, a UNC graduate. Three children: Camryn, Derek “Deuce,” and Kai. His father is buried in Louisville’s Cave Hill Cemetery, near Colonel Sanders and a short distance from Muhammad Ali. On his right arm: a tattoo of his father’s face and the words “Forever Watching.”

Nolan Smith was born in Louisville and named after Nolan Ryan. His father arrived at Louisville with his wardrobe in a paper sack, won a championship, outscored Jordan, played nine NBA years, and died on a cruise ship near Bermuda when Nolan was eight. Johnny Dawkins became Uncle Johnny. Michael Beasley became the brother he never had. At Duke, Nolan looked at the ceiling in every arena. His mother shouted “Go to work!” He won the 2010 championship in Indianapolis — same city, thirty years later. ACC Player of the Year. Consensus All-American. Drafted 21st. Went back to Duke to coach. Went to Louisville to honor his father. Now the head coach at Tennessee State. His son is named Deuce. His father is buried near Muhammad Ali. And on his right arm, in permanent ink, Derek Smith is still watching. Forever.

bio.road:
Kyrie Andrew Irving was born March 23, 1992, in Melbourne, Australia. His parents, Drederick and Elizabeth Irving, were American expatriates. Drederick had played college basketball at Boston University, a steady scoring guard who averaged nearly 20 points as a senior and led the Terriers to a conference championship. After college, he moved his young family to Australia to play for the Bulleen Boomers. Kyrie was born in Melbourne and lived in the suburb of Kew until he was two. He holds dual American and Australian citizenship.

His mother, Elizabeth Ann Larson Irving, was African American and a member of the Standing Rock Sioux Tribe, of Lakota heritage. She had played basketball and volleyball at Boston University, where she met Drederick. Elizabeth died of an illness when Kyrie was four. She was twenty-nine. Drederick raised Kyrie and his older sister Asia with the help of Irving’s aunts, brought them back to the United States, and settled in West Orange, New Jersey. Everything he had dreamed for himself, he poured into his son.

Kyrie grew up watching Drederick’s adult-league games. His father parked Asia’s carriage at the end of the bench. Kyrie didn’t play with the other kids — he watched. In fifth grade, he attended a camp at Boston University and the head coach offered him a scholarship on the spot. He was ten years old. But the defining moment came in fourth grade, when his school visited Continental Airlines Arena. Kyrie played on the court during the trip and wrote on a piece of paper: I will play in the NBA, I promise.

He attended Montclair Kimberley Academy, a small private school, and became only the second 1,000-point scorer in school history: 26.5 PPG, 10.3 APG, 4.8 RPG, 3.6 SPG. Spectacular on the floor, invisible to the national rankings. His father believed he wasn’t getting the exposure he deserved.

Before his junior year, Kyrie transferred to St. Patrick High School in Elizabeth — a basketball powerhouse. On his first day, his host spotted him in the hallway: an untucked uniform two sizes too big, a mismatched vest, and two G-Shock watches on the same wrist. Then came the pickup game. Kyrie was shy at first. Then he threw the ball through a defender’s legs, ran after it, pulled back, and shot. The gym went quiet.

At St. Patrick, he played alongside Michael Kidd-Gilchrist. They led the team to a state championship. Senior year, the school was banned from the Tournament of Champions after an illegal preseason practice was caught on tape. Irving cried when he heard the news. That night he scored 28 against Oak Hill. He also performed in the school’s production of High School Musical. McDonald’s All-American. Jordan Brand Classic Co-MVP. FIBA Americas U18 gold. He committed to Duke on October 22, 2009, on live television.

His father told him in eighth grade he’d be the best guard in New Jersey. Coach K saw him in an AAU tournament and said: you are single-handedly killing all these teams. If you are fortunate to have a father like I have, Kyrie would later say, you’re given a foundation. You can be content with that, or take it and run with it, like I did.
bio.duke:
Kyrie Irving played eleven games at Duke. Eleven. It was enough. Through the first eight games of 2010–11, he averaged 17.4 points on 53.2% shooting, 5.1 assists, 3.8 rebounds, and 1.5 steals. He was the best freshman point guard in America. On December 1, he scored 31 against Michigan State — one of the most electrifying individual Duke freshman performances in decades.

Then, in the ninth game, he suffered a severe ligament injury to his right big toe. He missed the next twenty-six games. Duke’s season went on without him, and his absence was felt in every possession.

He returned March 17, the day before Duke’s first-round tournament game against Hampton. He scored 14 in his comeback, then 28 against Michigan in the second round — his final game in a Duke uniform. Duke lost to Arizona in the Sweet Sixteen.

Irving declared for the 2011 draft: "Duke has a special place in my heart. Even though I’m leaving this year, Duke will always be in my mind and my heart. I’m going to miss putting on that number one jersey." He promised his father he would finish his bachelor’s degree within five years.

Eleven games. 17.5 PPG, 4.3 APG, 3.4 RPG. 52.9% from the field, 46.2% from three. The shortest Duke career that ever produced a number-one overall pick. And arguably the most purely talented guard to ever wear Duke blue.
bio.after:
Cleveland selected Irving first overall in 2011. He was nineteen. Rookie of the Year. Rising Stars MVP (8-of-8 from three, 34 points). Youngest player to score 40 at Madison Square Garden — younger than Jordan when he did it. All-Star Game MVP in 2014.

LeBron came home. The Big Three — James, Love, Irving — reached the 2015 Finals, but Irving fractured his kneecap in the opener against Golden State. He watched from the sideline as Cleveland lost. He came back in 2015–16 determined to finish what was taken.

Game 7, 2016 NBA Finals. Cleveland at Golden State. The Warriors had won 73 regular-season games. The Cavaliers trailed 3–1 in the series. With 53 seconds remaining, the cumulative score of the entire Finals — through 335+ minutes — was 699–699. Irving caught the ball on the right wing. Curry switched onto him. Irving jabbed, gave him an in-and-out, created a half-step of space, and rose for a step-back three over the two-time MVP. It went in. Cleveland won 93–89. The franchise’s first championship in fifty-two years. The Wall Street Journal called it the biggest shot in NBA history. Irving hugged LeBron. He hugged his coach. He found his father and sister and gave them his jersey.

Then the wandering. Trade request in 2017 — tired of LeBron’s shadow — dealt to Boston. Knee surgery, turbulent exit. Brooklyn: three seasons with Kevin Durant, derailed by injuries, a vaccination refusal, an antisemitism controversy and suspension. Dallas: a February 2023 trade that rejuvenated his career. In 2023–24, Irving and Luka Dončić led the Mavericks to the Finals, losing to Boston in five.

Fifteen seasons. Nine All-Star selections. Three All-NBA Teams. A championship. An Olympic gold. Career averages of ~23 PPG, 5.7 APG. The Uncle Drew Pepsi character that became a feature film. The best ball-handler many have ever seen. And always the complications: flat-earth comments, controversies, absences, restlessness. Kyrie Irving has never been simple. His talent has never been in question.
bio.now:
In August 2018, Kyrie Irving traveled to the Standing Rock Sioux Reservation in South Dakota. His mother Elizabeth had been born into the Standing Rock Sioux Tribe. In a naming ceremony, Irving received the Lakota name Hĕla — Little Mountain. He donated food and supplies and has returned multiple times. He named his daughter Azurie Elizabeth, after the mother he lost at four.

On March 3, 2025, Irving tore the ACL in his left knee in the first quarter against Sacramento. Surgery on March 26. In February 2026, the Mavericks announced he would miss the entire 2025–26 season — the first full season he’s missed in fifteen years. He’s targeting 2026–27. His stepmother and agent, Shetellia Riley Irving, said the priority is making sure Kyrie is one thousand percent. Irving, now thirty-three, is mentoring Cooper Flagg, the Mavericks’ rookie — the next Duke Brotherhood member to carry the franchise.

He was born in Melbourne and lost his mother at four. His father raised him alone and taught him to want more. He wrote in fourth grade: I will play in the NBA, I promise. He showed up at St. Patrick’s in a uniform two sizes too big and two watches on one wrist. He played eleven games at Duke and was drafted first. He won a championship with a three over Curry when the cumulative Finals score was tied. He went to Standing Rock and received a name in his mother’s language. He has played for five teams, been suspended, celebrated, booed, brilliant, maddening, and undeniably one of the most gifted basketball players who has ever lived. He is recovering from a torn ACL. He is mentoring a nineteen-year-old from Maine. He promised his father he would finish his degree. He named his daughter after the mother he barely knew. Little Mountain is still climbing.

bio.road:
Kyle Edward Singler was born May 4, 1988, in Medford, Oregon — a small city in the Rogue Valley, tucked between the Cascades and the Siskiyous, closer to the California border than to Portland. His father, Ed Singler, had played quarterback at Oregon State. His mother, Kris Brosterhous, had played basketball at the same school. Four of his uncles played Division I football, baseball, or basketball. Athletics weren’t optional in the Singler household. They were genetic.

Kyle grew up playing multiple sports — basketball, hockey, whatever was in season. His younger brother E.J. would star at the University of Oregon. The backyard was the first gymnasium, Ed and Kris the first coaches. Medford was not a basketball factory. Kyle Singler would change that.

At South Medford High School, he became the best player in Oregon history. Four-year starter, 110–10 record. Senior year: 29.3 points, 10.6 rebounds, 3.5 assists, 2.0 steals. Sixth-leading scorer in Oregon high school history with 2,207 career points. The defining moment: the 2007 state championship. South Medford faced defending champion Lake Oswego and national standout Kevin Love. The year before, Love had beaten Singler for the title. This time, Singler won 58–54 — the school’s first-ever state championship.

He was ranked #1 small forward and #4 overall in the class of 2007. McDonald’s All-American. Jordan Classic All-Star. Member of the 2006 U18 USA Junior National Team alongside Michael Beasley and Jerryd Bayless. He was one of eight players featured in Gunnin’ for That #1 Spot, a documentary directed by Adam Yauch of the Beastie Boys about the top high school prospects in America.

He and Kevin Love played on the same Legends AAU team. Rivals and teammates, linked by geography and talent. In October 2006, Singler signed his letter of intent to Duke. Love went to UCLA.

Before leaving for Durham, Kyle created a youth basketball tournament as his senior project, with all proceeds going to Kids Unlimited, a Medford nonprofit serving vulnerable children. The first year: 19 teams, ,000 raised. Nearly two decades later, that tournament — the Southern Oregon Open — is the largest youth basketball event in southern Oregon, generating an estimated  million annually in local economic impact. His family continued to run it after he left.
bio.duke:
Kyle Singler played four years at Duke, 2007–11. In the one-and-done era, this made him an anachronism — a four-year starter who chose to come back, who chose to grow, who chose to finish what he started. By graduation, he held school records for games played (148), consecutive games played (148), games started (147), and minutes played (4,887). He scored 2,392 career points (4th all-time) and grabbed 1,007 rebounds (6th). Two-time All-ACC First Team. Three-time All-ACC Tournament First Team.

Freshman year: 13.3 PPG, 5.9 RPG, ACC Rookie of the Year, Maui Classic MVP. After a second-round tournament loss, he dismissed draft talk: "I didn’t give the NBA any thought. I’m not ready. I need to mature physically and mentally. I’m at a great place for that."

Sophomore year: led the team in scoring at 16.5 PPG. Junior year: career-high 30 against Georgia Tech (8-of-10 from three). He, Jon Scheyer, and Nolan Smith became the second trio in Duke history to each score 600+ points in a single season, joining Jason Williams, Carlos Boozer, and Mike Dunleavy Jr. The Big Three — three upperclassmen who proved the one-and-done model wasn’t the only path.

The 2010 NCAA Tournament: Duke entered as the #1 seed and cut through the bracket. In the Final Four semifinal, they demolished West Virginia 78–57, shredding the 1-3-1 zone. Singler scored 21, Smith 19. In the championship against Butler — the Cinderella from Indianapolis, coached by Brad Stevens, playing six miles from their home court — Singler scored 19 on 7-of-13 with 9 rebounds and 2 blocks. Duke won 61–59 when Gordon Hayward’s half-court heave hit backboard, hit rim, bounced out. Three inches. Singler was named Final Four Most Outstanding Player.

He came back for senior year. Preseason ACC Player of the Year. On November 27, 2010, he scored 30 against Oregon — against his brother E.J. Two Singlers on the same floor, the older in Duke blue, the younger in Oregon green. They hugged at half court. The season ended with a Sweet Sixteen loss to Arizona, but Singler had cemented something larger than any single run: a four-year body of work that stood as a monument to patience and loyalty.
bio.after:
Detroit drafted Singler 33rd overall in 2011. The NBA lockout delayed his debut, so he went to Spain — Lucentum Alicante, then Real Madrid. He helped Real Madrid defeat Barcelona for the 2012 Spanish King’s Cup. The kid from Medford won a championship in Spain before playing an NBA game.

With the Pistons, he earned All-Rookie Second Team in 2012–13: 8.8 PPG, 4.0 RPG, 82 games. A starter for three seasons — reliable, versatile, professional. Career-high 22 against Atlanta. Never flashy.

Traded to Oklahoma City in February 2015. Re-signed that summer, but his role shrank. By 2015–16, he was a seldom-used third-string forward, sometimes sitting out, sometimes playing fewer than sixty seconds. The gap between Final Four MOP and DNP–Coach’s Decision is the cruelest distance in professional basketball. Released in 2018.

He returned to Spain, playing for Obradoiro CAB and CB Canarias. In 2019, he retired from professional basketball, citing personal reasons. Seven professional seasons. 356 NBA games. 6.5 PPG, 2.9 RPG. An All-Rookie honor. A Spanish King’s Cup. An NCAA championship ring.
bio.now:
In 2022, Kyle Singler was inducted into the Medford Sports Hall of Fame. The tournament he created as a senior project — now the Southern Oregon Open — continued to grow, with Ed, Kris, and E.J. ensuring its expansion. Proceeds still benefit Kids Unlimited.

In November 2024, Singler posted videos on Instagram that alarmed the basketball community. He spoke of feeling mistreated and neglected, of fearing for his life. The videos were raw, emotional, and abrupt. The basketball world responded immediately. Kevin Love — the same Kevin Love whom Singler had beaten for a state championship in 2007, the same Kevin Love who had played alongside him on the Legends AAU team — wrote publicly: "To everyone who has spent time with Kyle Singler and whose lives he has touched, please shower him with the love and support he needs and deserves. I would not be who I am today without him." Isaiah Thomas, Andre Drummond, and Chandler Parsons reached out. The NBPA contacted his family. The outpouring revealed something important: Kyle Singler was loved. The Brotherhood — Duke, the NBA, South Medford — closed ranks around him.

Kyle Singler was born in Medford, Oregon, and raised in a family where both parents played college sports at Oregon State. He beat Kevin Love for a state championship. He was filmed by a Beastie Boy. He went to Duke for four years when everyone else was leaving after one. He played 148 games, more than any Blue Devil in history. He won a national championship and was named the best player in the Final Four. He came back for his senior year and scored 30 against his brother. He won a title in Spain before he ever played in the NBA. He created a tournament that grew into the largest youth event in southern Oregon. He has struggled. And when he struggled, the people who knew him did not look away. They reached back. That is what Brotherhood means.

bio.road:
Miles Christian Plumlee was born September 1, 1988 — the firstborn son of Perky and Leslie Plumlee, two college basketball players who met at a Purdue basketball camp in 1979 and found each other again at a tournament in Indianapolis. Perky had played at Tennessee Tech, a 6’7” forward. Leslie had played at Purdue, a 6’1” forward who once grabbed 25 rebounds in a single game — a record that still stands. When Miles was born, at 11 pounds and 23 inches, the nurse found his feet were too long for the ink pad. The Plumlee dynasty was announced at birth.

The family settled in Warsaw, Indiana. Perky put an adjustable hoop in the driveway. Miles, Mason (born 1990), and Marshall (born 1992) grew up fighting over it. Perky called it cheap entertainment. The games between Miles and Mason were brutal. Marshall squeezed in when he could. It wasn’t always literally a fight, Miles admitted, but we never finished a lot of the games.

Miles attended Warsaw Community High School for three years but played only about thirteen minutes per game. Perky made the controversial decision: Miles and Mason would transfer to Christ School, an all-boys boarding school in Arden, North Carolina, 600 miles from home. Miles was the first one across the mountains. He repeated his junior year. The letters to the editor were brutal. Perky and Leslie wiped away tears all the way back across the mountains.

Miles thrived. Christ School: 63–6 record, back-to-back state championships. Senior year: 15.8 PPG, 6.9 RPG, 2.5 BPG. School high jump record at 6’9”. He committed to Stanford. Then coach Trent Johnson left for LSU. Mason had already committed to Duke. Miles called his brother, who didn’t hesitate: come be a Blue Devil. Miles decommitted from Stanford and followed his younger brother to Durham. The first Plumlee to leave Warsaw was now the second Plumlee at Duke.
bio.duke:
Miles Plumlee played four years at Duke, 2008–12. His career was defined not by stardom but by steadiness, growth, and one historic performance.

Freshman year: limited role, 2.2 PPG, 2.3 RPG. Sophomore year: everything changed. He earned the starting center position over senior Brian Zoubek, started 24 of 40 games, averaged 5.2 PPG, 4.9 RPG, shot 56.1% from the field, and led the team with 33 dunks. He and Mason became the sixth pair of brothers to play at Duke at the same time. That team won the 2010 national championship — Butler, Hayward’s near-miss at the buzzer, 61–59. Miles was part of it. Not the headline, but the foundation.

Junior year: 4.8 PPG, 4.9 RPG, consistent minutes. Then came senior year, 2011–12 — the year all three Plumlee brothers wore Duke blue simultaneously. Twenty feet seven inches and 705 pounds of Plumlees. Miles came off the bench but had his best statistical year: 6.6 PPG, 7.1 RPG, team-leading 61% from the field.

The signature moment: against Maryland, Miles grabbed 22 rebounds — the most ever by a player coached by Mike Krzyzewski, ninth-most in Duke history. He finished his career with 650 points and 654 rebounds in 135 games. The numbers were modest. The presence was not.
bio.after:
Indiana drafted Miles 26th overall in 2012 — his hometown team. Rookie year was mostly spent in the D-League with the Fort Wayne Mad Ants: 55 minutes, 14 games with the Pacers. Then he was traded to Phoenix alongside Gerald Green. In Phoenix, everything opened up: career highs of 8.1 PPG, 7.8 RPG, 1.1 BPG. Career-high 20 rebounds against the Lakers. Selected for the 2014 Rising Stars Challenge alongside Mason — the first brothers in the same Rising Stars game. On March 17, 2014, both Plumlee brothers started for their respective teams when the Suns visited Brooklyn.

Milwaukee acquired him in February 2015. In August 2016, he signed a four-year, $52 million contract — the largest deal any Plumlee would receive. But injuries and diminished play followed. Charlotte, then Atlanta, where knee injuries limited him. Seven NBA seasons, 258 games, 4.9 PPG, 4.5 RPG.

In January 2020, Miles signed with the Perth Wildcats in Australia’s NBL. Third game: 23 points and 18 rebounds — the first Wildcat to post 20 and 15 since 2007. Perth won the 2020 NBL Championship, truncated when Sydney withdrew due to COVID. The kid from Warsaw whose feet broke the ink pad won a championship on the other side of the world.
bio.now:
Miles Plumlee retired after the 2020 NBL season. He is thirty-seven. Mason is still playing, at thirty-five, for the San Antonio Spurs. Marshall won the 2015 NCAA title, played briefly with the Knicks, and became a Ranger-qualified Army officer. Sister Madeleine played volleyball at Notre Dame. Their mother’s Purdue rebound record still stands.

Miles was the first. First born too big for the ink pad. First to transfer to Christ School, first to endure the letters to the editor and the tears across the mountains. First to commit to a college — Stanford — and first to change his mind. First Plumlee at Duke, even though his younger brother arrived first. He won a championship as a sophomore. He played four years and grabbed 22 rebounds in a single game — more than any player Coach K had ever coached. He played seven NBA seasons and won a championship in Australia during a pandemic. He earned $52 million. He watched Mason play thirteen NBA seasons and Marshall serve his country as an Army Ranger. Every path the Plumlee brothers walked, Miles walked first. He was the trailblazer. He was the one who went across the mountains so his brothers would know the way.

players.name: Mason Plumlee
players.tagline: Three brothers. Seven seasons. One driveway hoop in Warsaw. All three won championships at Duke. The ink pad was too small for the first son’s feet.
players.now: San Antonio Spurs (rest-of-season contract, Feb 2026); 13th NBA season; age 35; still active
players.stat: 4 Duke seasons • 139 games • 2010 NCAA Champion • All-ACC • ACC DPOY • Academic All-American • 13 NBA seasons • 874+ games

bio.road:
The Plumlee basketball story begins at a Purdue basketball camp in the summer of 1979. Leslie Schultz, a 6’1” forward on the Purdue women’s team, was working as a counselor. Millard "Perky" Plumlee, a 6’7” forward from Tennessee Tech, was there too. They noticed each other but didn’t connect. Years later, at a summer tournament in Indianapolis, Leslie fouled out and sat down on the bench. Perky was sitting right behind her, waiting for his game. This time, the connection held.

Leslie had grabbed 25 rebounds in a single game at Purdue — a record that still stands. Perky was known for his leaping ability at Tennessee Tech. Their genetic match, as one writer put it, was meant to bear future post players. That was confirmed when their first son, Miles, was born at 11 pounds and 23 inches long. The nurse found his feet were too long for the ink pad.

The lineage ran deeper. Leslie’s father, Albert "Bud" Schultz, played at Michigan Tech in 1944 and on the U.S. Air Force Service Team in 1945. Her brother William played at Wisconsin-Eau Claire (1972 NAIA National Championship game). Another brother, Chad, played at Wisconsin-Oshkosh. The Plumlees’ youngest child, Madeleine, would play volleyball at Notre Dame. Every branch of the family tree led back to a gymnasium.

Mason Alexander Plumlee was born March 5, 1990, in Fort Wayne, Indiana — the middle of three brothers. Miles was two years older, Marshall two years younger. They moved to Warsaw, Indiana, population 13,500, into a house with an adjustable hoop in the driveway. That driveway was where it began. Perky called it cheap entertainment. The one-on-one games between Miles and Mason were ferocious. Marshall, smaller and younger, got in when he could. Miles admitted: it wasn’t always literally a fight, but we never finished a lot of the games.

After Mason’s freshman year at Warsaw Community High School, Perky and Leslie made a controversial decision: they sent Miles and Mason to Christ School, an all-boys boarding school in Arden, North Carolina, 600 miles from home. Both were getting D-I attention but playing only 13 minutes per game at Warsaw. The letters to the editor in Warsaw were brutal: What kind of parents are these? Perky and Leslie wiped away tears all the way across the mountains.

Mason adjusted. At Christ School — 200 students, all boys — he won three state championships, compiled a 99–8 record, was named NC Mr. Basketball, and earned McDonald’s All-American honors. He also cleared 6’8” in the high jump. Miles had committed to Stanford, but when the coach left for LSU, Mason convinced him to come to Duke. When Coach K asked if it was okay to recruit Miles, Mason didn’t hesitate: That’d be great. I’d love it if you’d recruit Miles.
bio.duke:
Mason Plumlee played four years at Duke, 2009–13 — the most dramatic growth story in modern Duke history. As a freshman in 2009–10, he was a backup who started one game and averaged 3.7 PPG, 3.1 RPG. He played alongside Miles on the 2010 national championship team — the Hayward-at-the-buzzer game against Butler. Mason was a role player who carried water and cheered and learned.

Sophomore: started 31 games, 7.2 PPG, 8.5 RPG, six double-doubles. Junior: 11.1 PPG, 9.2 RPG, All-ACC Third Team, Academic All-American First Team — only the third Duke player to earn that honor twice, alongside Shane Battier and Greg Paulus. He appeared on NBA mock drafts and could have left. Instead, he came back. The allure of playing with both brothers was a factor — Marshall had arrived as a freshman, and 2011–12 was the only year all three Plumlees wore Duke blue at the same time. They became the third D-I brother trio in history on the same team, joining Villanova’s Brennans and Dayton’s Bockhorns from 1957–58. Twenty feet seven inches and 705 pounds of Plumlees banging the boards.

Senior year was the masterpiece: 17.1 PPG, 10.0 RPG, 1.8 BPG. All-ACC First Team. ACC Defensive Player of the Year. He broke Robert Brickey’s all-time Duke career dunk record of 147 — which had stood since 1990. He told reporters: "Nobody goes from the bench to the NBA. Come on." Then he proved himself wrong. Selected 22nd overall by the Brooklyn Nets.

Seven consecutive seasons of Plumlees in Durham (2008–16). All three won NCAA championships — Miles and Mason in 2010, Marshall in 2015. No family in Duke basketball history can match that.
bio.after:
Brooklyn selected Plumlee 22nd overall in 2013. On November 15, his first game against Miles and the Phoenix Suns, he had 7 points, 3 rebounds, 2 assists in a Nets overtime win. On March 17, 2014, both brothers started for their respective teams. That summer, Mason won a gold medal with Team USA at the 2014 FIBA World Cup.

Thirteen NBA seasons followed: Brooklyn, Portland, Denver, Detroit, Charlotte, LA Clippers, Phoenix, Oklahoma City, San Antonio. Eight teams. 874+ career games. 7.9 PPG, 6.6 RPG, 2.4 APG. Never an All-Star. A center who could pass — consistently averaging over two assists from the five, a rarity — who could screen, defend, run the floor, and be the smartest player on any roster. His nickname, Plumdog Millionaire, captured the joy and the improbability: the kid from Warsaw who went from a freshman bench player to a thirteen-year NBA career.
bio.now:
In February 2026, Mason Plumlee signed with the San Antonio Spurs for the remainder of the season. He turns thirty-six on March 5. He is still playing — conditioning his way back from a December groin surgery, working alongside Victor Wembanyama as the oldest player on the roster.

Miles retired after winning an NBL championship with the Perth Wildcats in 2020. Marshall won the 2015 NCAA title, played briefly with the Knicks, and became a Ranger-qualified officer in the United States Army. Sister Madeleine played volleyball at Notre Dame. Their mother still holds the Purdue single-game rebound record. Their father still quotes scripture about trusting the path.

Mason Plumlee was born in Fort Wayne and raised in Warsaw, Indiana, in a house with an adjustable hoop and two brothers who fought over every game and never finished most of them. His parents met at a basketball camp. His grandfather played on an Air Force team in 1945. His mother’s rebound record at Purdue still stands. His father sent him to boarding school 600 miles away and cried all the way across the mountains. He won three state championships at a school with 200 students. He was a backup on a championship team as a freshman and came back four straight years until he was All-ACC and ACC Defensive Player of the Year. He broke Robert Brickey’s dunk record. He played one season with both brothers — the third time that happened in D-I history since 1958. All three Plumlee brothers won NCAA championships at Duke. He has played thirteen NBA seasons for eight teams. He won a FIBA World Cup gold medal. He is thirty-five and still playing. Seven consecutive seasons of Plumlees in Durham. The driveway hoop. The tears on the mountain road. The ink pad that was too small for the first son’s feet. This is a basketball family, and Duke was their home.

players.name: Seth Curry
players.tagline: The other Curry.
players.now: Golden State Warriors (with brother Steph); 7th all-time NBA career 3PT%
players.stat: 99 Duke games • Sr: 17.5 PPG/43.8% 3PT • All-ACC 1st Team • 12+ NBA seasons • Led NBA 3PT% 2024–25

bio.road:
Seth Adham Curry was born August 23, 1990, in Charlotte, North Carolina. His father, Dell Curry, was in the middle of a 16-year NBA career — most of it with the Charlotte Hornets, where he became the franchise’s all-time leading scorer. His mother, Sonya Adams Curry, had played college volleyball at Virginia Tech. His older brother was Steph. His younger sister was Sydel, who would play volleyball at Elon. This was the first family of Charlotte sports.

As children, Dell took Seth and Steph to Hornets games. They shot around with the team during warm-ups — learning the geometry of the three-point line before they were old enough to reach it. They played on the backyard court constantly, competitive to the point of physical fights their mother had to break up. Both wore #30 in honor of their father. Both inherited the shooting gene.

But Steph was the older one, the one who broke out first, who made Davidson a national story. By the time Seth was a senior at Charlotte Christian (22.3 PPG, 5.0 APG, 105–24 team record, all-state, academic honor roll all four years), his brother was leading Davidson to the Elite Eight. Seth received two- and three-star recruiting ratings. Despite being Dell Curry’s son, the major programs didn’t come calling. He chose Liberty University.

At Liberty, he exploded: 20.2 PPG as a freshman — best among all freshmen nationally. Broke the Big South Conference freshman scoring record with 707 points. Scored 26 against Virginia. Big South Freshman of the Year. Dick Vitale’s ‘Diaper Dandy.’ After one season, he transferred to Duke.
bio.duke:
Curry sat out 2009–10 per transfer rules. He watched from the bench as Duke won the national championship. He wore #30. He waited.

His first playable season, Kyrie Irving — the most talented freshman in college basketball — ran the show. Irving averaged 17.4 PPG through eight games. Then a toe injury knocked him out for 26 games. Curry was named the starter. He never really gave it back. With Irving out, he averaged 9.6 PPG shooting 42.2% from three. Duke went 32–5, reached the Sweet Sixteen. Irving returned for the tournament, scored 28 against Arizona, and was drafted #1 overall. Curry had kept the ship afloat.

Junior year, alongside Austin Rivers: 13.2 PPG, led the ACC in free throw percentage (87.3%). All-ACC Third Team. Duke went 27–7.

Senior year was the coronation. 17.5 PPG, 43.8% from three. Career-high 31 against Santa Clara when Duke was ranked #1. All-ACC First Team. Second-team All-American (Sporting News). NCAA All-Region. Duke went 30–6, reached the Elite Eight.

He went undrafted. Not a single team used a pick on Dell Curry’s son and Steph Curry’s brother. The kid overlooked at every turn — by recruiting services, by major programs, by the draft — would have to find another way.
bio.after:
On his 23rd birthday, Curry signed a non-guaranteed contract with Golden State — Steph’s team. Six preseason games. Waived. Assigned to the Santa Cruz Warriors (D-League), where he played in the backcourt with Mychel Thompson while their brothers formed the ‘Splash Brothers’ upstairs.

The NBA journey was a masterclass in persistence. Memphis (waived after 1 game). Cleveland (10-day contract, 19 games). Phoenix. Erie BayHawks. Sacramento (first real chance: averaged 15.2 PPG over the final 11 games). Dallas (70 games, 12.8 PPG). Portland — where he was selected for the 2019 Three-Point Contest and reached the Western Conference Finals. Portland’s opponent: Steph and Golden State. First time brothers faced each other in an NBA playoff series. Golden State swept 4–0. Seth called it a dream come true.

Dallas again: career-high 37 points on 13-of-15 shooting, 8-of-9 from three against Miami — one of the most efficient scoring games in NBA history. Philadelphia under Doc Rivers (2020–22): career-best 15.0 PPG, 49/42/87 splits. Traded to Brooklyn in the James Harden deal. Dallas again. Charlotte — his hometown, his father’s franchise. Through all of it: career 43.3% from three, seventh all-time in NBA history. Led the entire NBA in 3PT% for 2024–25 at 45.6%.
bio.now:
On October 1, 2025, Seth Curry signed with the Golden State Warriors. His brother’s team. For the first time since those 2013 preseason games that ended with a waiver, the Curry brothers are NBA teammates. Steph is 37, the greatest shooter in basketball history. Seth is 35, seventh all-time in three-point percentage, a man who has played for ten-plus teams and never stopped shooting.

He married Callie Rivers on September 14, 2019. Callie is a former Florida Gators volleyball player — the daughter of Doc Rivers, the sister of Austin Rivers, Seth’s Duke teammate. The Duke-to-in-laws pipeline is real. Three children: Carter Lynn (2018), Cash (2021), Cruz (2023).

In January 2026, the Charlotte Hornets retired Dell Curry’s #30. The ceremony reunited the family in the city where it started — where Dell became a franchise icon, where Steph and Seth shot around during warm-ups as children, where the backyard court produced two NBA shooters. Seth said: ‘The coolest thing for me is that I would be the last person to wear number 30.’

Every family has the golden child and the other one. Seth Curry was always the other one. Two-star recruit. Liberty. Transfer. Sat on the bench while Duke won a championship. Stepped in when Kyrie went down. All-American. Undrafted. D-League. Waived by his brother’s team. Ten franchises. The slowest rise in Brotherhood history. Now he stands seventh all-time in NBA three-point percentage, playing alongside the greatest shooter who ever lived — who also happens to be the kid who used to beat him up on the backyard court in Charlotte. He just kept shooting until the world had no choice but to notice.

nba.postCareer: Golden State Warriors (with brother Steph); 7th all-time NBA career 3PT%
