players.name: Austin Rivers
players.tagline: Doc’s son. The Shot at Carolina. The kid who spent 707 games and eleven years proving he was more than a last name.
players.now: NBA analyst, ESPN and NBC Sports; engaged to Audreyana Michelle; 2 sons
players.stat: 1 Duke season • 34 games • 15.5 PPG/3.4 RPG/2.1 APG • Buzzer-beater at UNC • 11 NBA seasons • 707 games

sources.label: Grantland: Ball in the Family — Austin Rivers (2015)
Basketball Reference: Austin Rivers career stats
Wikipedia: Austin Rivers (biographical details)
GoDuke.com: Austin Rivers player bio
ESPN: Austin Rivers career coverage

bio.road:
Austin James Rivers was born August 1, 1992, in Santa Monica, California. His father, Glenn Anton Rivers — known to the world as Doc — was finishing his thirteenth NBA season as a steady, defensive-minded point guard. His mother, Kristen Campion Rivers, had spent those thirteen years building a family while her husband built a career. Austin was named after Austin Carr, the former Cleveland Cavaliers guard. His middle name, James, honored his great-uncle Jim Brewer, who had played nine NBA seasons. In the Rivers household, basketball was the family business, the dinner-table language, the thing that determined where you lived and how often you saw your father.

Austin was the third of four children. His older brother Jeremiah played basketball at Georgetown and Indiana. His older sister Callie played volleyball at the University of Florida and would marry Seth Curry — Austin’s Duke teammate, Dell Curry’s son, Steph Curry’s brother. His younger brother Spencer played at UC Irvine. Every Rivers child was an athlete.

In 1999, when Austin was seven, Doc was hired as head coach of the Orlando Magic. The family moved to Winter Park, Florida. Austin began playing youth basketball and immediately confronted the weight that would follow him forever: his last name. He regarded himself as one of the worst players on one of his early teams. Outsiders agreed. The taunts came fast: you’re only there because of your dad. Austin would later admit: it was almost true.

Then Doc took the Celtics job. Austin was still in Orlando. His father was in Boston. They connected through phone calls about basketball. Doc didn’t know who Austin was dating or what trouble he got into. The relationship was built almost entirely on the game. Austin reflected on this without bitterness: he loved basketball so much, and so did his father, that it became the bridge between them.

His mother Kris learned to let insults roll off her back at Austin’s games. People said ugly things — nepotism, not that good, only on the court because of his last name. Kris absorbed it. Austin, when young, couldn’t. The taunts tormented him. They also fueled him.

At Winter Park High School, Austin silenced the doubters. Back-to-back Florida 6A state championships in 2010 and 2011 — the school’s first titles. Senior year: 25 points, 11 rebounds, 4 steals in the title game. 2011 Naismith Prep Player of the Year. McDonald’s All-American. Florida Mr. Basketball. Nike Hoop Summit. Rivals.com’s #1 recruit in the 2011 class. He committed to Duke in September 2010. The kid who was only there because of his dad was the best high school player in America.
bio.duke:
Austin Rivers played one season at Duke, 2011–12, and it produced the single most famous shot of the era. He averaged 15.5 points, 3.4 rebounds, and 2.1 assists. All-ACC Freshman Team. 34 games. But one moment defined everything.

February 8, 2012. Duke at Chapel Hill. North Carolina ranked fifth, riding a 31-game home winning streak at the Dean Smith Center. Duke trailed by one with seconds remaining. Rivers caught the ball on the right wing, rose, and launched a contested three as the clock expired. It went in. Duke 85, UNC 84. Rivers sprinted across the court, arms spread, mouth open, consumed by the moment. The shot that every coach’s son dreams of — the one that belongs entirely to you, that nobody can attribute to your father’s name. For one frozen moment, Austin Rivers was not Doc Rivers’ kid. He was the kid who beat Carolina at the buzzer.

The season ended painfully. Duke, a #2 seed, lost to fifteenth-seeded Lehigh in the first round, 75–70 — one of the biggest upsets in tournament history. Rivers scored 21 in the loss. The Lehigh game attached itself to his legacy the way the Carolina shot did — two extremes, one season. He declared for the draft.
bio.after:
New Orleans selected Rivers 10th overall in 2012. He chose jersey #25 — his father’s number. The night before the draft: ‘I want to be like my dad, only better.’

The NBA did not cooperate. Rookie year: 37% shooting, one of the worst box-plus-minus numbers among rookies playing significant minutes. Broken hand in March, season over. The second and third years were marginally better. He watched players he’d competed against getting opportunities while he sat. Rivers called this the darkest time of his life.

He called his father — the NBA head coach, the thirteen-year veteran, the one person who understood survival in this league. Doc told him to get back in the gym. Stay late. Work with the future in mind. The conversations were about basketball, the way they’d always been.

January 2015: traded to Boston, then immediately to the LA Clippers — his father’s team. January 16, 2015: Austin Rivers became the first player in NBA history to play for his father. Nepotism, critics said. Austin heard all of it. He’d been hearing it since youth basketball.

Then he played. And he earned it. 25 points in a playoff game against Houston. Career-high 32 vs Oklahoma City. By the end of three years in LA, the nepotism narrative had softened — not disappeared, but softened.

The journey continued: Washington, Houston (career-high 41 off the bench in the 2020 Bubble), New York, Oklahoma City, Denver, Minnesota. Seven teams. Eleven seasons. 707 games. 8.5 PPG, 2.0 RPG, 2.1 APG. Never an All-Star. Never a franchise player. A rotation guard who could shoot, defend, and handle pressure — a professional who lasted eleven years in a league that discards most players within three.
bio.now:
Austin Rivers retired from playing in 2023 and was hired by ESPN as an NBA analyst. In 2025, he joined NBC Sports for college basketball and NBA coverage. He is engaged to Audreyana Michelle. Sons Reign (born 2021) and Kayden (born 2018). His father Doc coaches the Milwaukee Bucks. His sister Callie is married to Seth Curry. The Rivers and Curry families are intertwined — two Brotherhood dynasties connected by marriage, basketball, and the shared experience of growing up in the shadow of famous fathers.

He was born in Santa Monica and named after Austin Carr. His father played thirteen NBA seasons and has coached for more than two decades. His mother absorbed the taunts at his youth games. He considered himself one of the worst players on his first team. He was told he was only there because of his dad. He led Winter Park to back-to-back state titles and was named the best high school player in America. He hit a buzzer-beating three at Chapel Hill that ended a 31-game home streak. He lost to Lehigh in the first round. He was drafted 10th and wore his father’s number. He called the early years in New Orleans the darkest time of his life. He became the first son to play for his father in NBA history. He scored 41 in the Bubble. He played 707 games for seven teams over eleven seasons. He is now a broadcaster, a father, and a man who spent his entire life answering the same question: are you good enough, or are you just Doc’s kid? The answer took eleven years and 707 games, and it was never going to satisfy everyone. But Austin Rivers was never playing for everyone. He was playing for the kid on the youth team who heard the taunts and couldn’t let them go, and who turned all of that anger into a career.

players.name: Quinn Cook
players.tagline: Four years. One ring. Two more in the NBA.
players.now: Playing overseas; 4th Quarter Sports Bar, Bowie MD
players.stat: 1,571 pts • 143 games • 2015 NCAA champ • 2x NBA champ (Warriors/Lakers)

bio.road:
Quinn Alexander Cook was born March 23, 1993, in Washington, D.C., the son of Ted and Janet Cook, both graduates of Howard University who met while attending the school. Ted Cook was a former star basketball player at Glenville High School in the late 1970s who became an entrepreneur in the restaurant business. Ted and Janet ran 4th and Goal, a sports bar on 11th Street and Pennsylvania Avenue in D.C. Quinn’s earliest childhood memories are of that bar — shooting on a miniature Fisher-Price hoop inside at age four while his parents worked.

Ted was a lifelong Los Angeles Lakers fan who instilled in his son a love for basketball from the beginning. Quinn had his own basketball as a toddler and was playing organized ball by age ten. But tragedy struck early: Ted Cook died of a heart attack in 2008, when Quinn was just fourteen and had only recently begun playing at DeMatha Catholic High School. The loss was devastating. Quinn missed games. It took time to escape the trauma. After Ted’s death, Janet supported the family by working two jobs in security and event staffing.

At DeMatha — the same school that produced Danny Ferry — Cook played three seasons. The Stags went 85–18 and were ranked No. 1 in Maryland during his junior year. He was named the Washington Post All-Met Player of the Year as a junior, the first junior to receive that honor in 30 years. His teammates included future NBA players Jerami Grant, Jerian Grant, and Victor Oladipo.

Cook transferred to Oak Hill Academy for his senior year, leading the Warriors to a 31–4 record while averaging 19.1 points, 10.9 assists, and 2.5 steals. McDonald’s All-American. He chose Duke over Villanova, UCLA, and North Carolina. When asked why: ‘The reason I chose Duke is because of Coach K.’
bio.duke:
Cook played all four years at Duke, amassing 1,571 career points (28th on Duke’s all-time list), becoming one of only seven Blue Devils to record 1,000 points and 500 assists, and finishing fourth in career free-throw percentage at .853.

As a freshman in 2011–12, he backed up Austin Rivers and Seth Curry, averaging 4.4 points in 11.7 minutes. As a sophomore, he moved into the starting lineup and led the team with 5.3 assists per game while averaging 11.7 points. Each year, Cook grew — not in dramatic fashion, but steadily, like a player building a house one brick at a time.

The payoff came as a senior in 2014–15. Cook averaged 15.3 points and 2.6 assists and was named All-ACC Third Team. He was the emotional engine of the team — the four-year player holding together a roster of talented freshmen (Jahlil Okafor, Justise Winslow, Tyus Jones) who would leave after one season. On April 6, 2015, Duke defeated Wisconsin 68–63 to win the national championship. Jones was named Final Four MOP. But Cook was the glue — the senior who knew the system, who had survived four years of development, who gave that team its backbone. He graduated with a degree in sociology.
bio.after:
Cook went undrafted in 2015. What followed was one of the most remarkable persistence stories in recent basketball: a grinding, multi-year odyssey through the G League, 10-day contracts, and overseas stints that produced two NBA championship rings.

He spent the 2015–16 season with the Canton Charge in the D-League, averaging 19.6 points and 5.4 assists to win D-League Rookie of the Year and the All-Star Game MVP. Then came 10-day contracts: Dallas in February 2017, New Orleans in March 2017. Each time he proved he could play; each time the opportunity evaporated.

In October 2017, Golden State signed Cook to a two-way contract. When Stephen Curry went down with an injury, Cook stepped into the starting lineup and delivered: 9.5 points per game as a starter, 44.2% from three. The Warriors won the 2018 championship. Cook had a ring — earned through the G League, through 10-day contracts, through sheer refusal to quit.

In July 2019, Cook signed with the Los Angeles Lakers. He won his second championship when the Lakers defeated the Miami Heat in the 2020 Finals in the COVID bubble. Two rings. Neither handed to him.

After the Lakers, the journey continued: brief stints with the Cavaliers, Portland training camp, Lokomotiv Kuban in Russia, Stockton Kings in the G League (averaging 23.5 ppg), Zhejiang Lions in China (where he scored a career-high 54 points in 29 minutes), time in Puerto Rico, Al-Ula in Saudi Arabia, and as of early 2025, the Fujian Sturgeons in the CBA. Career NBA totals: 188 games, 6.4 ppg — modest numbers that do not capture the journey.
bio.now:
Quinn Cook is 32 years old and still playing professional basketball overseas, chasing the game his father gave him. In 2024, he announced the opening of 4th Quarter Sports Bar in Bowie, Maryland — a tribute to his parents’ sports bar, 4th and Goal, where he first fell in love with sports. His mother Janet and sister Kelsey help run it while his playing career continues.

He has played on three continents, in seven countries, and won championships at every level: NCAA (2015), NBA (2018, 2020). Duke Basketball Report has noted he has an incredibly bright future in coaching if he wants it.

His story is the Brotherhood’s purest underdog narrative: the kid from D.C. who lost his father at fourteen, who played at DeMatha and Oak Hill and Duke, who went undrafted, who ground through the G League and 10-day contracts and overseas leagues, and who somehow ended up with two NBA rings and a sports bar named after the fourth quarter — because that’s when the game is decided, and Quinn Cook has always been a finisher.

nba.postCareer: Playing overseas; 4th Quarter Sports Bar, Bowie MD

players.now: Harvard Business School (2022–); former Blackstone intern; Special Operations Warrior Foundation board; U.S. Army Captain (Ranger-qualified)
players.stat: 5 years at Duke • 124 games • 2015 NCAA Champion • Team Captain • 29 NBA games • U.S. Army Ranger • 2 Afghanistan deployments

highlights.note: NBA debut: cab ride through Manhattan to MSG when Noah got sick
First career double-double: 14 pts, 11 reb vs Philadelphia 76ers
2017 FIBA AmeriCup gold medal (Team USA)

nba.postCareer: U.S. Army Ranger (2019); 3rd Ranger Battalion, 75th Regiment; 2x Afghanistan deployments; Harvard Business School (2022–); Blackstone Tactical Opportunities intern

bio.road:
Marshall Harrison Plumlee was born July 14, 1992, in Warsaw, Indiana — the youngest of three brothers in a family built on basketball. His mother Leslie had set the single-game rebound record at Purdue. His father Perky had played at Tennessee Tech. His oldest brother Miles had been born at eleven pounds. His middle brother Mason would become a McDonald’s All-American. Marshall was the little one. In the driveway one-on-one games, he squeezed in when he could. "Growing up, there was always a bit of a physical difference," he said. "I didn’t have much of a chance."

He followed his brothers to Christ School in Arden, North Carolina. Four consecutive state championships, 139 wins in 150 games. By junior year, with both brothers at Duke, he averaged 8.6 PPG, 8.0 RPG, 2.6 BPG. McDonald’s All-American, top-65 national recruit, third Plumlee committed to Duke.

But something happened at Christ School that separated Marshall from his brothers. At a high school basketball tournament, he met General Robert Brown, a retired Army four-star who had played basketball under Coach K at West Point. Brown was nearly as tall as Marshall and spoke about duty, service, and the possibility of playing college basketball and serving your country at the same time. Marshall’s biggest role models, he later said, had always been servicemembers. Brown gave him a way to pursue both dreams.

At Duke, Marshall became the first Blue Devil basketball player in three decades to participate in ROTC. He did it alongside basketball practice, alongside classes, alongside everything. On January 23, 2015, he participated in an ROTC contracting ceremony on the court at Cameron Indoor Stadium — the same floor where he played basketball, where his brothers had played before him. He stood in uniform and pledged to serve. The ceremony happened in the middle of the season that would end with a national championship.
bio.duke:
Marshall Plumlee spent five years at Duke, 2011–16. He redshirted his first year — the season all three Plumlee brothers were on the roster. For three years, he was a reserve center: behind Mason, then Amile Jefferson, then Jahlil Okafor on the 2014–15 championship team. He appeared in 30 games that title season, averaging 8.5 minutes. Not the star. The foundation beneath the stars.

Senior year: everything changed. He started all 36 games — 8.3 PPG, 8.6 RPG, 1.6 BPG. Team captain alongside Jefferson and Matt Jones. The kid who squeezed in during driveway games was now Duke’s starting center, anchor of the defense, vocal leader directing traffic for younger players.

He wasn’t the most talented Plumlee at Duke. He knew it. Everyone knew it. But Marshall had something the others didn’t: a second mission. The ROTC training. The early mornings. The parallel life of a student-athlete who was also a future officer. He carried it quietly, without fanfare, and the program respected it.
bio.after:
Marshall went undrafted in 2016. He was initially set to join the Army immediately. Then, surprisingly, the NBA called. The Knicks signed him in July 2016. His debut, November 20 against Atlanta, was an adventure: rushed from a G-League assignment when Joakim Noah got sick, Marshall took a cab through Manhattan traffic and sprinted into Madison Square Garden just in time. Five minutes, one rebound, a Knicks win. Best game: 14 points, 11 rebounds against Philadelphia — his first double-double. Twenty-one games with New York. Two-way contract with Milwaukee in 2018. FIBA AmeriCup gold medal. 29 total NBA games. Brief, but enough to say he’d done it.

In 2019, Marshall transitioned to active duty. Ranger School at Fort Benning — the two-month crucible. At seven feet tall, he towered over every soldier in formation. The graduation photo is almost comical: a 7’0” man in camouflage, a full head above everyone. His mother Leslie pinned the Ranger Tab on his uniform. Marshall posted: "No one more deserving of pinning my Ranger Tab. Going from the NBA to the active duty Army and now Ranger School, I have to thank my biggest supporter in the transition, my mom. I love you."

He was assigned to the 3rd Ranger Battalion, 75th Ranger Regiment. He deployed twice to Afghanistan — including during the 2021 evacuation. When soldiers said they looked up to him — literally, because he was seven feet tall, or figuratively, because he’d played in the NBA — Marshall’s answer was always the same: "It’s just so backwards for me, because I look up to them."
bio.now:
In April 2022, Captain Marshall Plumlee transitioned from active duty. That fall, he enrolled at Harvard Business School. Before HBS, he interned with Blackstone’s Tactical Opportunities Group. He returned for a second summer. He serves on the board of the Special Operations Warrior Foundation.

His advice to junior leaders: "Set really high goals for yourself. Do awesome things and bring a soldier with you. It has never taken away from any experience of mine to have another leader there. It has only enriched it."

Marshall Plumlee was the youngest of three brothers, the smallest in the driveway games, the one who didn’t have much of a chance. He followed Miles and Mason to Christ School and won four consecutive state championships. He followed them to Duke and redshirted while they played together. He backed up Jahlil Okafor and won a national championship. He did ROTC on the same court where he played basketball. He was undrafted and took a cab through Manhattan to make his NBA debut at Madison Square Garden. He played 29 games and won a FIBA gold medal. Then he became a Ranger. Seven feet tall in formation, a head above everyone. His mother pinned the tab. He deployed twice to Afghanistan. He went to Harvard Business School and interned at Blackstone. His brother Mason is still playing in the NBA at thirty-five. His brother Miles won a championship in Australia. Every Plumlee brother found his own path — and Marshall’s led farthest from the court and closest to something larger than basketball. He is the youngest brother who became a Ranger. He is the one who looked up to the soldiers and meant it.

players.name: Jabari Parker
players.tagline: The kid from the church gym. Sports Illustrated cover. Four state titles. Two torn ACLs. Tears of gratitude in Barcelona.
players.now: Playing for Joventut Badalona (Spain), on loan from Partizan Belgrade; age 30; still active
players.stat: 1 Duke season • 35 games • 19.1 PPG/8.7 RPG • Consensus All-American • National FOY • 8 NBA seasons • 321 games

Career-high 36 pts vs Houston Rockets

bio.road:
Jabari Ali Parker was born March 15, 1995, on the South Side of Chicago. His father, Sonny Parker, was a Chicago native who had starred at Farragut Career Academy — the same school that would later produce Kevin Garnett — before playing two years at Texas A&M and being drafted 17th overall by the Golden State Warriors in 1976. Sonny played six NBA seasons. His mother, Folola "Lola" Finau-Parker, was a Polynesian native of Tonga who emigrated to Salt Lake City at age three. Her grandfather was the second Tongan baptized by Latter-day Saint missionaries. The family lived in the South Shore neighborhood, bound together by Sonny’s South Side roots and Lola’s Tongan heritage and faith.

Lola’s relatives were embedded in professional sports: NFL players Haloti Ngata, Tony Moeaki, and Harvey Unga, and PGA Tour golfer Tony Finau. Jabari was the youngest of five children. Brothers Darryl (Oregon) and Christian (BYU-Hawaii) both played college basketball. Sisters Iman and Tilah also attended Simeon. By second grade, Lola could see Jabari was different.

The South Side’s outdoor courts weren’t safe for a child. So Jabari and Christian went to the gym at their LDS meetinghouse in Hyde Park. They played for hours, sometimes all night. There were images of Christ on the walls. Christian called it their safe haven. Jabari added: there was a special spirit there, because they were at a church. The game and the faith grew together, inseparable, in a gymnasium where the backboards shared space with scripture.

By seven, Jabari competed against boys two and three years older. His mother brought his birth certificate to games. He stayed up late watching George Gervin and Larry Bird on NBA TV. At night, Lola played reporter, asking him questions about his career, his goals, his faith. Jabari answered as if he were already an NBA veteran. By fifth grade he was six feet tall with five Division I scholarship offers.

In sixth grade, he visited Simeon Career Academy and scrimmaged with Derrick Rose. That settled it. Simeon had produced Rose, Nick Anderson, Bobby Simmons, and the legendary Ben Wilson. Jabari became the first freshman in school history to start on varsity. Coach Robert Smith knew: this kid would have been bored on the sophomore team, because he had always played with older kids.

Four consecutive Class 4A state championships followed. 118 wins, 15 losses. 28 consecutive postseason victories. Two-time Illinois Gatorade Player of the Year. McDonald’s All-American. MaxPreps National Player of the Year. Sports Illustrated cover: the best high school basketball player since LeBron James.

Through all of it, Jabari woke before dawn for seminary. He maintained a 3.7 GPA. He posted the Ten Commandments on his bedroom door alongside his own rules: Don’t be quick to judge. Think positive things. Some friends couldn’t fathom his Mormon faith. Others mocked him for having a father: I would hear things about how because I had a dad, I must be soft. Three pillars — family, basketball, church — never wavering.

In 2013, Sonny developed kidney disease requiring dialysis three times a week. After the fourth championship, Jabari gave his medal to his father. My dad wasn’t able to be a part of my season like previous years. The fact that he made it out was real big for me.

Coach K had been writing handwritten letters for eighteen months. A shoebox in Jabari’s closet held half a dozen. The night before the commitment, Krzyzewski came to the South Side in a dark-blue suit. Duke wasn’t the favorite — Michigan State and BYU were. But when Jabari held up a Blue Devils shirt, it was over. The kid from the church gym was going to Durham.
bio.duke:
Jabari Parker played one season at Duke, 2013–14, and it was the most statistically dominant freshman season in program history. He averaged 19.1 points and 8.7 rebounds, the first Duke freshman to lead the team in both categories. He set or tied freshman records for points (670), rebounds (306), double-doubles (14), free throws made (160), dunks (61), 20-point games (18), and double-figure scoring games (33). Consensus first-team All-American, USBWA National Freshman of the Year, ACC Freshman of the Year, Wooden Award runner-up.

He debuted with 22 against Davidson. At the Champions Classic in his hometown United Center, he posted 27 and 9 against Andrew Wiggins and Kansas. He helped Coach K win his 900th game with 14 points and 14 rebounds against Florida State. He earned ACC Rookie of the Week a record-tying ten times.

His masterpiece: March 8, 2014. Thirty points on 10-of-17 shooting and 11 rebounds in a 93–81 demolition of 14th-ranked North Carolina. The most points by a Duke freshman in the 238-game rivalry history, breaking Austin Rivers’s record. Roy Williams: "Whatever the hell ‘it’ is, Jabari found it." Parker told reporters: "No more freshman. My team needs me more than just an 18-year-old. It’s time to grow up."

Off the court, he was the team’s biggest joker and worst dresser. Roommate Matt Jones doubled over laughing when Parker emerged in rainbow sneakers, bright yellow pants, and a North Korea jersey. He didn’t date or party. Movies were his escape — he loved The Secret Life of Walter Mitty and said he related to a man who goes places in his mind. In a Spike Lee seminar, he was an enthusiastic participant on Malcolm X and Do the Right Thing. Coach K: "He doesn’t drink or party or have a problem with authority. Those things rob you of your love of preparation for the game. Jabari has none of those demons."

The season ended in heartbreak: a first-round loss to 14th-seeded Mercer, 78–71. Like Austin Rivers and Lehigh, like Cooper Flagg and Houston, the early exit attached itself to Parker’s legacy. He declared for the draft. Coach K: "We will be on the Jabari Parker team the rest of our lives."
bio.after:
Milwaukee took Parker second overall in 2014, one pick behind Andrew Wiggins. Jordan Brand endorsement. Summer League. NBA debut October 29 against Charlotte. Double-double in his first home game. Rookie of the Month. Everything on schedule.

Then, December 15, 2014 — twenty-five games in — he tore the ACL in his left knee against Phoenix. Season over. He was twenty years old.

He came back. The 2015–16 season was full: 76 games, steady improvement, career-high 36 against Houston. The 2016–17 season was a revelation: 20.1 PPG, 6.2 RPG, 2.8 APG, career highs in everything. Explosive drives, the old Jabari. His face alongside Giannis Antetokounmpo’s on a poster outside Bucks headquarters. The franchise’s future was Parker and Giannis.

February 8, 2017 — fifty-one games into his best season — he tore the ACL in the same left knee. Non-contact. His knee buckled on a drive against Miami. Teammates reached for him. He stayed down. Paul George, who had shattered his own leg: "He’s on the verge, right there in being a star. But he keeps getting bit by the injury bug."

Twelve months of rehab. The same exercises every day. He drew inspiration from Derrick Rose, his Simeon predecessor, who had fought back from his own ACL tears. He returned in February 2018 but was never the same. Defense struggled. Weight fluctuated. Giannis became the undisputed franchise player. Milwaukee let Parker go.

The wandering years: Chicago (0M contract, traded in half a season), Washington, Atlanta (shoulder injury, COVID shutdown), Sacramento (waived), Boston. Six teams after Milwaukee. The 48 million extension that went to Wiggins — the contract that was supposed to be Jabari’s — belonged to someone else. By summer 2023, he was on Milwaukee’s Summer League roster at age twenty-eight, trying to find his way back.
bio.now:
In August 2023, with NBA doors closed, Jabari Parker signed with FC Barcelona. A new country, a new league, a new life. He averaged 10.3 points in 33 EuroLeague games and 11.5 in 28 Liga ACB games. He helped Barcelona reach the playoff semifinals, scoring 27 in the deciding game against Real Madrid. He signed an extension through 2026.

Then a reporter asked him to reflect on his first season, and Jabari Parker broke down in tears on camera. When he composed himself: "They were tears of gratitude. I wasn’t sure I’d be able to play again. In Barcelona, I’ve recovered my love for basketball."

In his second season, he scored 34 with six three-pointers against Andorra — Barcelona’s highest individual Liga ACB scoring game in eleven years. He was named All-Liga ACB Second Team. And he connected back to Duke: his teammate Dame Sarr chose to attend Duke for 2025–26, saying the decision was heavily influenced by his friendship with Parker. A decade removed from Durham, Jabari was still recruiting for Duke.

In June 2025, he and Barcelona parted ways. He signed with Partizan Belgrade, drawn by legendary coach Željko Obradović. But playing time diminished, and in late February 2026, he was loaned to Joventut Badalona in Spain, returning to Catalonia to play alongside Ricky Rubio and Ante Tomic. He is thirty years old. He is still playing.

He was born on the South Side and raised in a church gym because the outdoor courts weren’t safe. His father played six NBA seasons and then ran youth leagues for thirty years. His mother carried Tongan heritage and Mormon faith across an ocean. He woke before dawn for seminary. He scrimmaged with Derrick Rose in sixth grade. He won four state titles and was called the best since LeBron. Coach K kept handwritten letters in a shoebox. He went to Duke and set every freshman record. He watched Walter Mitty and said he related to a man who goes places in his mind. He was drafted second and tore his ACL in game twenty-five. He came back and tore it again in his best season. He wandered through six NBA teams. He went to Barcelona and cried because he wasn’t sure he’d ever play again. He scored 34 at age thirty. He recruited a teammate to Duke without being asked. He is still playing, still faithful, still the kid from the church gym who never stopped believing that the game and the spirit were the same thing.

players.name: Rodney Hood
players.tagline: Both parents played at Mississippi State. Childhood neighbor: Paramore’s Hayley Williams. Two-time Mississippi Gatorade POY. State champion. Coach K’s fourth-ever transfer. Brought chitterlings to Duke from Thanksgiving in Meridian. Left-handed stroke as smooth as anything in the ACC. 23rd pick. Eight NBA seasons. Ruptured his Achilles chasing a dream. Married a Duke women’s basketball player. Retired November 2024. The Deep South never left him.
players.now: Retired (Nov 2024); hosts annual basketball camp in Meridian, MS; working toward Duke degree; married to Richa Jackson (Duke women’s basketball, Class of 2014)
players.stat: 1 Duke playing season • 16.1/3.9/2.1 • 48.6% FG • 40.8% 3PT • All-ACC • 23rd pick • 8 NBA seasons • 6 teams

teams.note: 2019 Western Conference Finals; ruptured Achilles Dec 2019

highlights.note: Career-best 16.8 PPG with Utah Jazz
2018 NBA Finals with Cleveland Cavaliers
2019 Western Conference Finals with Portland Trail Blazers
Ruptured left Achilles tendon vs Lakers, Dec 6, 2019

nba.postCareer: Retired Nov 2024; annual basketball camp in Meridian, MS; working toward Duke degree; represented Team USA in 2023 FIBA World Cup qualification

bio.road:
Rodney Michael Hood was born on October 20, 1992, in Meridian, Mississippi — a small city in the eastern part of the state, deep in the heart of the Deep South. Basketball was in the blood on both sides. His father, Ricky Hood Sr., was an undersized post player who played at Mississippi State from 1978 to 1980 before transferring to finish at Murray State. His mother, Vicky, also played basketball at Mississippi State. His brother Ricky Jr. and his sister Whitney both played at the University of Tennessee at Chattanooga, where Whitney was an All-Southern Conference performer. His mother later became the principal at Newton High School and also coached a team — three-year-old Rodney would sit in the stands watching her coach. He was a student of the game, his father said, all along.

At Meridian High School, Hood was a two-time Mississippi Gatorade Player of the Year and the Clarion Ledger’s Player of the Year. As a senior, he averaged 24.0 points and 8.0 rebounds, leading Meridian to a 29–2 record and the Class 6A state championship — scoring 24 points in the title game victory over Vicksburg and 27 in the semifinal. He was a five-star recruit ranked 16th nationally by Rivals. In a detail that has nothing to do with basketball and everything to do with Meridian: his childhood neighbor was Hayley Williams, the lead singer of Paramore. Hood didn’t know this until March 2021.

Hood followed his parents to Mississippi State, committing in October 2010. As a freshman in 2011–12, he averaged 10.3 points and 4.8 rebounds and was named to the SEC All-Freshman Team. He was at home in Starkville. Then head coach Rick Stansbury retired. The coaching change unsettled everything. Hood wanted more.

Duke had recruited Hood out of high school but backed off early, assuming the Mississippi kid would never leave the state. Coach K was wrong. When Hood became available as a transfer, Krzyzewski pounced. We recruited him out of high school and followed his progress, Coach K said. Hood became the fourth transfer Mike Krzyzewski ever accepted at Duke, following Roshown McLeod, Dahntay Jones, and Seth Curry. He sat out the 2012–13 season under transfer rules, bringing chitterlings and pickled pigs’ feet back from Thanksgiving in Meridian and introducing his Duke teammates to the Deep South one plate at a time.
bio.duke:
Hood played one season on the court at Duke, 2013–14, and it was brilliant. He averaged 16.1 points, 3.9 rebounds, and 2.1 assists per game while shooting 48.6 percent from the field and 40.8 percent from three. He was a left-handed scorer with a smooth stroke, a 6’8 frame, and the ability to get buckets at every level — at the rim, from mid-range, and from deep. He was named to the All-ACC Second Team.

Hood played alongside Jabari Parker, Quinn Cook, and Rasheed Sulaimon on a Duke team that went 26–9 and reached the Sweet Sixteen. His teammate Amile Jefferson described what Hood brought to the roster: When he came in, it wasn’t, ‘Where’s Rodney going to fit in?’ It was, ‘We know we have Rodney Hood.’ We know what he’s going to give us night in and night out. The quiet consistency, the leadership by example, the Deep South humility — that was Hood’s gift to the program.

After the season, Hood declared for the 2014 NBA Draft as a junior. He was the only member of his immediate family who had not earned a college degree when he left. His wife, Richa Jackson — who played basketball at Duke from 2010 to 2014 and graduated — would later be the driving force behind him going back to finish.
bio.after:
The Utah Jazz selected Hood with the 23rd overall pick in the 2014 NBA Draft. He spent three and a half seasons in Salt Lake City, developing into a reliable starter alongside Gordon Hayward and Rudy Gobert. His best season was 2017–18, when he averaged 16.8 points and 2.8 rebounds — the highest-scoring season of his career. In February 2018, he was traded to the Cleveland Cavaliers as part of a three-team deal, joining LeBron James for the stretch run and the 2018 NBA Finals against Golden State.

In February 2019, Hood was traded to the Portland Trail Blazers, where he found a home alongside Damian Lillard and CJ McCollum. The Trail Blazers reached the 2019 Western Conference Finals, and Hood emerged as a critical third scorer in the playoff run. It was the peak of his professional career — a legitimate contributor on a team that was four wins from the NBA Finals.

Then catastrophe. On December 6, 2019, Hood ruptured his left Achilles tendon against the Los Angeles Lakers. He was thirty-one months into his career’s best stretch. The injury is one of the most devastating in basketball — a sentence that changes a player’s trajectory permanently. Hood fought his way back, playing for Toronto and Milwaukee, but was never the same. He attempted a comeback with the Memphis Hustle in the G-League in 2024 but was sidelined by injury. In November 2024, Rodney Hood officially retired from professional basketball. He had played eight NBA seasons across six teams and appeared in over 400 games.
bio.now:
Hood hosts an annual basketball camp in Meridian that attracts hundreds of kids each year — giving back to the community that produced him. In 2020, during his recovery from the Achilles tear, Hood went back to Duke to work toward finishing his degree — inspired by his wife Richa, who had completed hers. He married Richa Jackson on July 29, 2016, in Oklahoma City. They have a son, born in January 2016. In November 2022, Hood was selected to represent Team USA in the FIBA Basketball World Cup qualification.

Rodney Hood was born in Meridian, Mississippi, the son of two Mississippi State basketball players. His mother was a school principal who also coached, and three-year-old Rodney sat in the stands watching her. His brother and sister both played at Chattanooga. His childhood neighbor was the lead singer of Paramore. He was a two-time Mississippi Gatorade Player of the Year and led Meridian to a state championship with 24 points in the title game. He followed his parents to Mississippi State, then transferred to Duke when the coach retired — becoming Coach K’s fourth-ever transfer. He brought chitterlings and pickled pigs’ feet back from Thanksgiving and taught his roommate what Southern food tasted like. He averaged 16.1 points at Duke with a left-handed stroke as smooth as anything in the ACC. He was drafted 23rd overall, played eight NBA seasons, reached two conference finals, and ruptured his Achilles chasing a dream. He married a Duke women’s basketball player who convinced him to go back and finish his degree. He retired in November 2024 and runs a basketball camp in Meridian every summer. The Deep South gave him everything — the family, the food, the toughness, and the smooth left-handed shot — and he never forgot where any of it came from.

players.name: Grayson Allen
players.tagline: The hero. The villain. The shooter. Four years at Duke, 1,996 points, a national championship, three trips, and a $70 million redemption arc.
players.now: Phoenix Suns starting guard; 17.7 PPG in 2025–26; $70M extension; career-high 42 pts/10 threes (Nov 2025)
players.stat: 4 Duke seasons • 142 games • 1,996 pts • 2015 NCAA Champion • All-American • 8 NBA seasons • Led NBA 3PT% (46.1%, 2024)

bio.road:
Grayson James Allen was born October 8, 1995, in Jacksonville, Florida. His father, William Allen, was a government contractor. His mother, Sherry Allen, worked in human resources. Neither parent coached or played basketball. Grayson Allen’s career was self-made.

He was an only child, but not entirely. When Grayson was young, his parents took in his best friend from school, Tonan Ferrell, whose mother had given him up. Tonan became the closest thing Grayson had to a brother. The two grew up together in the Allen household, and Tonan remains part of the family today.

Grayson attended Providence School in Jacksonville, a small private school where he earned an AP diploma and starred on the basketball team. He led Providence to a 90–10 record, a state championship in 2013, and two state semifinal appearances. Senior year: 29.3 PPG, 10.6 RPG. McDonald’s All-American in 2014. At the All-American game, he won the Slam Dunk Contest — jumping over future Duke teammate Jahlil Okafor, the 6’11”, 270-pound #1 recruit in America.

His parents stressed academics from the beginning. Getting a Duke degree has always been a goal and a dream of mine. He was active in his church, Grace Lutheran, in Jacksonville. Coach K visited the Allen home, and Grayson committed — then waited a few days to announce because he didn’t want attention at a Nike tournament. Those were the longest days of my life.

Some nights during freshman year, after practice, Grayson would wait until the facility emptied. Then he’d sit alone in the stands at Cameron and look around, trying to make sense of the fact that he was actually there. This was where Grant Hill and J.J. Redick had played. He was blessed to be part of that brotherhood.
bio.duke:
Grayson Allen played four years at Duke, 2014–18, and his career contained more extremes than any player in modern Duke history. He was the hero of a national championship. He was the most hated player in college basketball. He was suspended and stripped of his captaincy. He was voted captain the next year. He left with 1,996 points and a Players’ Tribune essay that tried to explain it all.

The hero came first. As a freshman, he averaged 4.4 PPG in 9.2 minutes — the eighth man on a team built around Okafor, Winslow, and Tyus Jones. Coach K told him: Don’t think this isn’t your year. Stay ready. Allen admitted he was falling into spectator mode.

April 6, 2015. Championship game vs. Wisconsin. Duke down nine, second half. Okafor and Winslow in foul trouble. Allen scored eight consecutive points to spark the comeback — a three, a steal, three free throws, a driving and-one over Sam Dekker punctuated by pounding his chest. 16 points, 21 minutes. Duke 68, Wisconsin 63. Scheyer: "I don’t think we win without him." Coach K called him "the hero." At Cameron, a banner: FIFTY SHADES OF GRAYSON. His mother didn’t like it at all.

Sophomore year: 21.6 PPG, 41.7% from three, All-American. Then February 8, 2016: he tripped Louisville’s Ray Spalding. It looked intentional. Later, he tripped Florida State’s Xavier Rathan-Mayes. The internet made him college basketball’s villain.

Junior year opened with preseason ACC POY expectations. December 21: he tripped Elon’s Steven Santa Ana. Coach K suspended him indefinitely, stripped his captaincy. Allen cried on the bench. The tears were broadcast nationally and became a meme. Duke fell to South Carolina in the tournament’s second round. Allen scored 20 in the loss.

He came back for senior year. His teammates voted him sole captain. Coach K wrote: "Grayson was constantly humiliated, scrutinized and dissected by those outside of Duke Basketball. I know the type of person he is. He has learned from previous mistakes." Allen led Duke to the Elite Eight, losing to Kansas in OT. He finished with 1,996 career points — four short of 2,000 — and was one of three Blue Devils to earn All-ACC Academic honors all four years.
bio.after:
Utah drafted Allen 21st overall in 2018. Rookie year: G-League assignment, 4.4 PPG. Ejected from his first Summer League game for back-to-back flagrant fouls. The reputation followed him.

Traded to Memphis in July 2019 (part of the Mike Conley deal). Over two Grizzlies seasons, he found his footing: 10.6 PPG in 2020–21, 39.1% from three. Traded to Milwaukee in August 2021. In January 2022, he fouled Chicago’s Alex Caruso hard enough to fracture Caruso’s wrist. Ejected. Billy Donovan: "could’ve ended his career." One-game NBA suspension. Booed relentlessly everywhere. The tripping narrative had evolved: Grayson Allen was the player people loved to hate.

He stayed. He shot. He earned a $20M extension with Milwaukee. Traded to Phoenix in September 2023 as part of the Damian Lillard blockbuster. First Suns season: 13.1 PPG and a league-leading 46.1% from three — the best in the entire NBA. Four-year, $70 million extension. The kid nobody’s parents coached had earned $70 million to shoot a basketball.
bio.now:
On November 10, 2025, Grayson Allen scored a career-high 42 points against New Orleans, making 10 three-pointers on 15 attempts — both Suns franchise records. He is averaging 17.7 PPG in 2025–26, shooting 41.1% from three, starting alongside Devin Booker and Kevin Durant. He is thirty years old and in the prime of his career.

He married Morgan Reid in July 2022. They met at Duke, where Morgan played women’s soccer before going pro with the North Carolina Courage and Orlando Pride. A hip injury ended her career in 2020. Daughter Emslie May was born October 2024. Allen missed a Suns game to be there.

Grayson Allen was born in Jacksonville to parents who didn’t play basketball. They took in his best friend and raised him as a brother. He won a state championship at a small school and dunked over the #1 recruit to win the McDonald’s Slam Dunk Contest. He sat alone in Cameron’s stands trying to believe he was there. He scored 16 as a freshman to win a championship and was called the hero. He tripped three opponents and was publicly suspended by the same coach who called him the hero. He cried on the bench and the tears became a meme. He came back for a fourth year and was voted captain. He left four points short of 2,000. He was assigned to the G-League, ejected from Summer League, traded twice, booed everywhere. He led the NBA in three-point shooting. He scored 42 with ten threes. He signed a $70 million contract. He married a Duke soccer player and named their daughter Emslie. He has never been easy to root for and never stopped being impossible to ignore. That is the Brotherhood at its most complicated, most human, and most Duke.

players.name: Jahlil Okafor
players.tagline: One year. One ring. Then the NBA broke him.
players.now: Levanga Hokkaido (Japan B.League); represented Nigeria 2020 Olympics
players.stat: 38 games • 17.3 PPG • 66.4% FG • ACC POY (1st freshman) • unanimous All-American • 2015 NCAA champion

bio.road:
There are two Jahlil Okafors. There is the one you see on the court: all power dunks, spin moves, stare-downs, and displays of domination. And there is the one you don’t see. The one who plays chess and the saxophone and the tuba. The one who worked stage crew for a musical in junior high. The one whose morning alarm, for years after his mother died, was a recording of her voice: ‘Jahlil, Jahlil.’

Jahlil Obika Okafor was born December 15, 1995, in Fort Smith, Arkansas. His father, Chukwudi — known as Chuck or Chucky — is a second-generation Nigerian American of Igbo descent. His paternal grandfather immigrated from Nigeria to the United States. His mother, Dacresha Lanett Benton, was an athlete — a 6-foot-2 basketball force at Carl Albert State College before a knee injury ended her playing career. Chuck and Dacresha met there while both had basketball scholarships. As a young child, Jahlil split time between his mother’s home in Moffett, Oklahoma — population 127 — and his father’s home in Chicago.

When Jahlil was nine years old, he was watching music videos on BET with his older sister, Jalen, when they noticed their mother breathing hard. Really hard. She lay on the couch taking awkward, gulping breaths. They thought she was joking — their mom was usually joking. Jahlil teased her. He said he was going to steal her Oreos. Then he realized it wasn’t a joke. He called 911. She had contracted bronchitis two weeks earlier and suffered a collapsed lung. She died. She was gone. He was nine.

Jahlil called his father. Chuck heard it immediately in his son’s voice — somewhere between a cry and a scream. The only words he could make out: ‘Mama. Mama.’ Chuck got on the next plane to Fort Smith. He stood next to his nine-year-old son as Jahlil read a poem he had written to honor his mother. He put his arms around his children and told them their mother would always be with them — and as he said it, the wind lifted, and Chuck spread out his arms and said: ‘Every time you feel the wind, that is your mom with you.’

Chuck’s own story was a mirror of grief. His mother had died from breast cancer when he was eighteen months old. His father, a Nigerian immigrant, raised six children alone, working three jobs, getting home just before midnight. Chuck had been, in his sister Chinyere’s words, a ‘roughneck’ — caught driving a stolen car at thirteen, hustling drugs, bounced through five high schools in three years, expelled repeatedly for fighting, lived in a group home. But when Dacresha died and Jahlil came to Chicago, Chuck changed. ‘Without Jah,’ he said simply, ‘I’d probably be dead or in jail.’ His sister saw the transformation: ‘I saw him try to create a human being better than who he was.’

The adjustment was hard. Jahlil was shy and so tall that classmates at Rosemont Elementary assumed he had been held back. By seventh grade, he matched his father’s height at 6-foot-5. The family moved to Chicago’s North Side so Jahlil could attend Whitney M. Young Magnet High School — the same school Michelle Obama had attended. At Whitney Young, coached by Tyrone Slaughter, Okafor became the most dominant high school center in the country. He won the national player of the year awards from McDonald’s, USA Today, and Parade. He was named Illinois Mr. Basketball. He earned three USA Basketball gold medals (U16 Americas 2011, U17 World Championship 2012, U19 World Championship 2013) and made the U19 All-Tournament team. Sports Illustrated named him a ‘Future Game Changer’ at age sixteen. DePaul offered him a scholarship before he played a high school game.

He committed to Duke in a package deal with his AAU friend Tyus Jones, his longtime teammate from the Mac Irvin Fire. Together, they gave Duke the consensus No. 1 recruiting class in the nation. ‘My deepest fear is losing someone else close to me,’ Okafor told Chicago magazine as a senior. ‘That’s something I think about way more than I should.’
bio.duke:
Okafor’s lone season at Duke was a masterclass in post dominance. He averaged 17.3 points and 8.5 rebounds while shooting 66.4% from the field — second in the nation. He shot 50% or better in all but five of his 38 games, including 70% or better twenty times. He was the first freshman in Duke history to win ACC Player of the Year. He was named USBWA National Freshman of the Year and a unanimous First-Team All-American. He was a finalist for both the Wooden and Naismith awards.

In the 2015 national championship game against Wisconsin, Okafor scored 10 key points as Duke won 68–63. He played alongside Tyus Jones (tournament MOP), Justise Winslow, Quinn Cook, and a freshman bench player named Grayson Allen who scored 16 off the bench. Duke went 35–4. Okafor declared for the draft.
bio.after:
The Philadelphia 76ers selected Okafor third overall in the 2015 Draft, behind Karl-Anthony Towns and D’Angelo Russell. He debuted with 26 points, seven rebounds, and two blocks against the Celtics. He averaged 17.5 points as a rookie — leading all first-year players and the entire Sixers team. He was named to the All-Rookie First Team. But the team was a catastrophe: 10–72, the architecture of Sam Hinkie’s ‘Process.’ Okafor was the best player on the worst team in modern NBA history.

Then the off-court implosion. In October 2015, a heckler pulled a gun on Okafor after a verbal exchange at a Philadelphia club. In November, after the Sixers lost to extend their losing streak to 16 games, Okafor went to a Boston nightclub and was filmed in a street fight, slurring his words. A second fight video from the same night surfaced on TMZ a week later. He was clocked driving 108 mph on a Philadelphia bridge. He had a fake ID. The Sixers suspended him for two games and assigned him a personal security guard. He was nineteen years old. Brett Brown, his coach, said: ‘I’m looking at a 19-year-old kid that all of a sudden has come into money and has been used to winning. I do not feel betrayed.’

Then Joel Embiid arrived, healthy at last, and Okafor became expendable. In December 2017, the Sixers traded him to Brooklyn for Trevor Booker. ‘I wouldn’t say there’s regrets,’ Brown said. ‘I’d say there’s human disappointment.’ Embiid texted Okafor: ‘Love you. New beginnings.’

Okafor reinvented his body in New Orleans. He committed to a semi-vegan diet, resculpted his frame, and averaged 8.2 points in 59 games for the Pelicans in 2018–19 — his best season since his rookie year. He played for Detroit in 2020–21, then went unsigned. In February 2025, the Indiana Pacers signed him to a 10-day contract; he appeared in one game. In July 2025, he signed with Levanga Hokkaido of Japan’s B.League. He also represented Nigeria at the 2020 Tokyo Olympics, honoring his grandfather’s homeland.
bio.now:
Jahlil Okafor is 30 years old and playing professional basketball in Hokkaido, Japan. He is 6,200 miles from Cameron Indoor Stadium and 6,800 miles from the South Side of Chicago where his father reinvented himself. He has an older sister, Jalen, and two younger brothers, Jamonte and Ashondre. His aunt Chinyere Okafor-Conley — his ‘auntie mom’ — helped raise him after his mother died. His father, Chuck, was a mainstay in the stands at Duke, one of the team’s most vocal and entertaining supporters. His Twitter profile still reads: ‘R.I.P. I love you mom.’ A picture of her headstone is the backdrop on his phone.

Okafor’s Brotherhood story is the most heartbreaking in the Superteam Era. He lost his mother at nine and called 911 while she was dying on the couch. His father told him the wind was her. He set his morning alarm to a recording of her voice. He became the No. 1 recruit in the country, won a national championship at Duke, and was drafted third overall. Then he got into street fights and drove 108 mph and was traded for Trevor Booker. He reinvented his body with the Pelicans and played for Nigeria in the Olympics and signed a 10-day contract with Indiana nine years after being a top-three pick.

He is now in Japan, still playing. The kid who played chess and the saxophone and the tuba, who worked stage crew for a musical, whose deepest fear was losing someone else close to him — he is still playing basketball at thirty years old, on a different continent, because the game is the thing that saved him when his mother died and the thing that nearly destroyed him when the money came. Every time he feels the wind, that is his mom with him. The Brotherhood’s saddest story is also its most human.

Duke’s Brotherhood: Where Are They Now? • Profile • March 2026

12.4 PPG off bench for Pelicans

players.name: Tyus Jones
players.tagline: Hit the biggest shot of 2015.
players.now: NBA point guard (Phoenix Suns); 6x NBA assist-to-turnover ratio leader; Write Your Own Story Foundation
players.stat: 1 Duke season • 11.8 PPG/5.6 APG • 2015 NCAA Champion • Final Four MOP • NBA record A/TO ratio (7.35)

bio.road:
The name came from a basketball game his mother was watching on television. In 1995, Debbie Jones watched UCLA’s Tyus Edney race the length of the court and hit a buzzer-beater against Missouri. A year later, she named her second son Tyus. She was a point guard herself — she’d led Devils Lake High School to the North Dakota state championship in 1981. She knew what she was watching. She knew what she wanted her boy to become.

The basketball bloodline runs deep. Father Rob Jones, 6-6, played at Division III Wisconsin-Parkside. Aunt Darcy Cascaes won two state titles at Devils Lake. Uncle Al Nuness captained the Minnesota Gophers in the 1960s. Older half-brother Jadee played at Furman and Mankato State. Younger brother Tre would follow Tyus to Duke and now plays for the Chicago Bulls. ‘It’s kind of in the blood, I guess you could say.’ Grandma eventually got tired of hearing about nothing but basketball at family gatherings.

Rob and Debbie divorced. Debbie raised the boys as a single mother in Apple Valley, Minnesota — a Twin Cities suburb of 50,000 with no basketball tradition. She worked as a paralegal. She used her vacation days to travel with Tyus and Tre to tournaments across the country. ‘All the time we’ve spent, all the money, all the days off work, all the early mornings … it’s just so worth it.’

In eighth grade — not yet enrolled at Apple Valley High — Tyus was named the varsity starting point guard. ‘He was the best point guard at our school, and he wasn’t even at our school.’ Over five years: first-ever state championship, Minnesota Mr. Basketball, 3x AP Player of the Year, 3x Gatorade POY, McDonald’s All-American. Before Tyus, if you typed ‘Apple Valley’ into Google, it showed California. After Tyus, it showed Minnesota.

He’d been friends with Jahlil Okafor since age eight through AAU. They committed to Duke together as a package deal. Something his family told him again and again became his mantra: ‘Tyus, you have to write your own story.’
bio.duke:
The 2014–15 roster was stacked with freshmen: Okafor, Jones, Justise Winslow, Grayson Allen. Senior Quinn Cook ran the offense. Jones averaged 11.8 PPG, 5.6 APG. All-ACC Third Team. All-ACC Freshman First Team (first trio ever unanimously selected, with Okafor and Winslow). AP honorable mention All-American.

Jeff Capel: ‘The one thing the best point guards have in common is that they win. Whatever it takes to win, Tyus does.’

The championship game against Wisconsin proved it. Frank Kaminsky, Sam Dekker, an experienced Badgers team. Jones scored 23 points with 5 rebounds. He hit a deep three in the final minutes that gave Duke a lead it never relinquished. Duke 68–63. Final Four Most Outstanding Player. Nineteen years old. True freshman. The photo of Jones cutting down the net at Lucas Oil Stadium graced the cover of Sports Illustrated.

April 22, 2015: the mayor of Apple Valley proclaimed “Tyus Jones Day.” Key to the city. First pitch at a Twins game. Number retired at Apple Valley High after a parade through town. President Obama referenced his nickname, “Tyus Stones.” He declared for the draft.
bio.after:
Drafted 24th by Cleveland, immediately traded to his hometown Timberwolves. Four seasons as a backup (2015–19) behind Ricky Rubio and Jeff Teague — reliable, steady, always efficient.

Memphis (2019–23) is where the quiet excellence became historic. Jones set the NBA single-season record for assist-to-turnover ratio: 6.96 in 2020–21. Broke his own record: 7.04 in 2021–22. Broke it again: 7.35 in 2022–23. Six consecutive seasons leading the NBA. No player in league history has ever been as precise with the basketball. His mother was a point guard who taught him the position is about making others better and never giving the ball away. He internalized it at a cellular level.

With Ja Morant suspended, Jones stepped into the starting lineup and proved he could run a team at the highest level — just as he had as an eighth-grader, just as he had in the championship game. Traded to Washington (2023–24). Signed with Phoenix (2024). Career: ~8.5 PPG, ~4.5 APG, historically unprecedented A/TO ratio.
bio.now:
In 2019, Debbie was diagnosed with breast cancer. For the first time in over two decades, basketball took a backseat. ‘Without my mom, I wouldn’t be where I am today. This was a wakeup call. She came first. Family was the only thing I focused on.’

In Minnesota, his former teammate Karl-Anthony Towns had custom sneakers created for the entire Timberwolves squad to wear in a game honoring breast cancer awareness. Towns’s own mother had battled COVID-19. The gesture moved the Jones family deeply.

Debbie underwent two rounds of chemotherapy and five weeks of radiation. ‘She’s Superwoman. She’s the toughest, strongest person I know.’ After nine months: cancer-free. The single mother who used her vacation days to drive to AAU tournaments had beaten the disease that tried to take her from the family she’d built.

Tyus is 29. Lives in Minnesota. Dog named Duke Stone Jones. Runs “Write Your Own Story” — refurbishing technology labs for kids, hosting camps, giving speeches. Brother Tre plays for the Bulls. Brother Jadee trains players. The family that got tired of hearing nothing but basketball at holiday dinners produced three college players and two NBA pros from a North Dakota point guard who named her son after a buzzer-beater on television.

He was named after Tyus Edney because his mother saw a point guard do something extraordinary. He started varsity as an eighth-grader. Led Apple Valley to its first state title and got a day named after him. Committed to Duke with a friend he’d known since age eight. Hit the biggest shot of the 2015 tournament. Set the NBA A/TO record six times. And when his mother was diagnosed with cancer, he put the game aside — because she had always come first. The point guard who taught him how to be a point guard. The toughest person he’s ever known. ‘Tyus, you have to write your own story.’ He did. The best chapter is the one where his mom is still here to read it.

Career-high 10.8 PPG with Memphis
NCAA Tournament Most Outstanding Player (2015 championship)

players.name: Justise Winslow
players.tagline: The glue of the 2015 title team.
players.now: Free agent; Robin’s House Family Foundation; pursuing media/entertainment career
players.stat: 1 Duke season • 12.6 PPG/6.5 RPG • NCAA Tourney: 14.3 PPG/9.3 RPG • 2015 NCAA Champion • 8 NBA seasons

bio.road:
Justise Jon Winslow was born March 26, 1996, in Houston, Texas, the youngest of five children. His father, Rickie Winslow, played basketball at Houston from 1983 to 1987 as part of the legendary Phi Slama Jama teams alongside Hakeem Olajuwon and Clyde Drexler. Rickie was drafted 28th by the Bulls in 1987 and played professionally in Europe until 2000. Sister Bianca played basketball at Houston. Brother Josh played football at Dartmouth.

But Justise didn’t cling to basketball because of his father. Rickie and his mother Robin Davis divorced when Justise was in grade school. Robin got full custody and raised all five children with an African proverb as her philosophy: It takes a village to raise a child.

Beverly Mosby was part of that village. Miss Bev was the unofficial team mom for the Houston Jaguars, Justise’s third-grade traveling team. She drove kids to practice, gave money to players who couldn’t afford food on road trips, and told Justise there were no limits. She died in 2012. When Winslow announced his Duke commitment, he choked back tears: ‘She really just found this dream inside of me. I dedicate my life in basketball to her.’

Steve and Leticia Trauber became his godparents in sixth grade. ‘If anything ever happened to me, they would be in control.’ They designated a room in their home for Justise. Steve once drove him to the movies and laughed from the back row when the gangly teenager got rejected trying to put his arm around his date.

John Lucas — former Rockets player, longtime NBA coach, Houston basketball godfather — became another mentor. At St. John’s School: four-year starter. Senior: 27.5 PPG/13.6 RPG/3.5 APG/2.1 BPG. Gatorade Texas POY. McDonald’s All-American. Jersey #42 retired.

The college decision: Robin pulled Justise from class the day before the signing period. They sat at a picnic table for three hours. On his first Durham visit at fifteen, they stopped for potato chips and onion dip at the Washington Duke Inn at 11 p.m. ‘If you want to commit now, you can,’ Robin said. Justise smiled: ‘Mom, we’ve only been here for three hours. The visit hasn’t even started yet.’ He took two more years. Then chose Duke.
bio.duke:
Winslow arrived as the “forgotten freshman.” Okafor was the #1 recruit. Tyus Jones was the #1 point guard. They committed as a package deal. Winslow committed a week later, almost an afterthought. But Coach K compared his game to Tommy Amaker and Grant Hill.

Jeff Capel saw something deeper: ‘He sees the world in a very mature way. He has an old soul. It’s almost like he’s been here before.’ At seventeen, playing for USA Basketball in Prague, Winslow spent his downtime visiting museums and photographing the city. He became interested in art through documentaries.

12.6 PPG, 6.5 RPG, 2.1 APG, 1.3 SPG. The do-everything wing who could guard four positions. Scored in double digits in Duke’s final five tournament games. Against Utah in the Sweet Sixteen: 21 points, 10 rebounds. Played through rib and shoulder injuries. In the championship game vs Wisconsin: 11 points, but his defense and rebounding let Tyus Jones and Okafor do their damage. Duke won 68–63.

During a late-game moment, Jon Scheyer’s mother tapped Robin Davis on the shoulder in the stands: ‘Look. Justise is smiling.’ The afterthought had become the championship’s most indispensable player.
bio.after:
Miami Heat, 10th overall, 2015. Five seasons in Miami. Raw rookie (6.4 PPG, All-Rookie Second Team). Career-high 28 points vs Lakers (December 2018). Reimagined as a 6-6 point guard when Dragić went down — 24 points, 11 rebounds, 7 assists in his best game.

But injuries devastated his trajectory. Torn labrum (2017). Knee strains. Back problems. Only 11 games in 2019–20. Traded to Memphis. 26 games over two seasons. Clippers. Portland — 11 games before another season-ending injury. Eight seasons, six teams, ~$45M career earnings. The body that was so durable in college kept breaking.

After Hurricane Harvey destroyed Houston in 2017, Winslow donated $100,000 through Robin’s House Foundation. $50,000 to Small Steps Nurturing Center for at-risk children. Organized a charity event at Cameron Indoor with Duke. Personally donated cars to two families who lost vehicles. Lanesha Dabney, a 24-year-old single mother of two, received a Chevy Equinox: ‘It feels so good to know I have a safe car to drive to work.’ Winslow: ‘These families were hurt hard. This is just a small token of my appreciation for the city and what it’s done for me.’
bio.now:
Justise Winslow is 29. He last played for the Wisconsin Herd (G League) in 2024, still chasing a return. Has a son, Niko Tosoani Winslow (born 2021, with partner Kristina Escobar). Pursuing media and entertainment — drawn to storytelling and sports documentaries, the same impulse that sent him into Prague’s museums at seventeen. Favorite athletes growing up: Jackie Robinson and Muhammad Ali.

The Robin’s House Foundation continues — free annual basketball camps, the D.R.E.A.M. Showcase talent show for under-resourced children in Miami. Named after his mother.

He was the youngest of five from a broken home in Houston. His father played on Phi Slama Jama but wasn’t around much. His mother raised him with a village — Miss Bev, who paid for his meals and died in 2012; the godparents who designated a room in their home; John Lucas, who opened the door; Coach K, who compared him to Grant Hill. He sat at a picnic table with his mother for three hours and chose Duke. He was the forgotten freshman who became the championship’s most versatile player — the one Scheyer’s mother noticed was smiling during the biggest game. Drafted 10th. Injuries stole his prime. Donated $100,000 to Hurricane Harvey relief because Houston raised him. Visited museums in Prague at seventeen because he has an old soul. Named his foundation after his mother.

It takes a village. Robin Davis built one. And Justise Winslow has spent his whole life trying to build it for everyone else.

charity.description: Named after Justise Winslow’s mother Robin, the foundation runs free annual basketball camps and the D.R.E.A.M. Showcase talent show for under-resourced children in Miami. Justise donated $100,000 after Hurricane Harvey.

players.name: Ryan Kelly
players.tagline: The Ivy League kid who won a national championship, married a Cowher, played for the Lakers, and found his best basketball in Japan.
players.now: Professional basketball, Fukui Blowinds (Japan B.League)
players.stat: 126 games at Duke (7.5 ppg, 37.9% 3PT); 2010 champion; 163 NBA games; 22+ ppg in Japan

bio.road:
Ryan Matthew Kelly was born in Carmel, New York, and raised in Raleigh, North Carolina, in a family where athletics and academics were inseparable. His father Chris played basketball at Yale from 1981 to 1985 and then professionally in France for three years. His mother Doreen played volleyball at the University of Pennsylvania and toured South America with Athletes In Action. When both your parents are Ivy League athletes, the expectation isn’t just that you’ll play sports — it’s that you’ll think while you play them.

At Ravenscroft School in Raleigh — the same private school where his mother would eventually serve as Head of School — Kelly was a four-year star. He set school career records in points (2,065), rebounds (950), blocks (379), field goals (864), and free throws (312). As a senior, he averaged 25.2 points, 10.2 rebounds, and 2.8 blocks, earning North Carolina Gatorade Player of the Year, McDonald’s All-American honors, and Parade All-America First Team selection. He was ranked as high as #12 nationally by Scout.com and was the #4 power forward in the country.

Kelly chose Duke over North Carolina, Wake Forest, Vanderbilt, Notre Dame, and Georgetown. The decision, he later said, came down to a gut feeling. “When I went to Duke and played pickup, it was as if I was already on the team,” he recalled. “I was coming for somebody’s minutes.” It helped that during his recruitment, Krzyzewski was coaching the 2008 Olympic team in Beijing — and the idea of hearing from K while he was coaching the best players in the world carried enormous weight for a high schooler from Raleigh.
bio.duke:
Kelly arrived at Duke in the fall of 2009 and immediately became part of something historic. As a freshman, he appeared in 35 games off the bench for the 2009-10 Blue Devils — the team that won the national championship, beating Butler 61-59 on Gordon Hayward’s near-miss half-court shot. Kelly was a reserve on a roster featuring Kyle Singler, Jon Scheyer, Nolan Smith, and the Plumlee brothers. He was 18 years old with a ring.

By his sophomore year, Kelly had grown into a key rotation player, averaging 6.6 points and 3.7 rebounds with 1.4 blocks per game. His defining moment came at the 2011 Maui Invitational, where he was named tournament MVP — a 6-foot-11 stretch four hitting threes and blocking shots against high-major competition in paradise. The award signaled his arrival as a legitimate ACC player.

His junior season was his best statistically: 11.8 points, 5.4 rebounds, and 1.1 blocks per game. He was named team captain alongside Seth Curry. But a foot injury late in the season sidelined him, and he missed both the ACC Tournament and the NCAA Tournament. Duke lost in the first round to Lehigh without him — one of the most stunning upsets in tournament history. Kelly always wondered what might have been if he’d been healthy.

He returned for his senior year in 2012-13 despite the foot concerns, because staying at Duke for four years was always the plan. The Ivy League kid wanted the degree. He led a team that featured freshman Rasheed Sulaimon, Mason Plumlee, Seth Curry, and Quinn Cook to the ACC regular-season title and an Elite Eight appearance before falling to Louisville. He was named to the ACC All-Academic Team for the fourth consecutive year — an achievement that mattered as much to his family as any basketball statistic.

Over 126 games, Kelly averaged 7.5 points, 3.7 rebounds, and 0.9 assists on 46.0% shooting and 37.9% from three. He was a four-year player in the one-and-done era, a team captain twice over, a national champion, a Maui MVP, and a four-time academic all-conference selection. He bought fully into Krzyzewski’s floor-slapping defensive culture, leading the team in charges drawn in multiple seasons — the ultimate sacrifice-your-body statistic.
bio.after:
The Los Angeles Lakers selected Kelly 48th overall in the 2013 NBA Draft — their only pick that year. He couldn’t work out for teams before the draft because he was recovering from foot surgery, which made the selection something of a blind bet. The Lakers gave him a non-guaranteed deal. “The situation was similar to Duke’s,” Kelly said. “The Lakers are beloved, hated, and worldwide.”

His rookie year under Mike D’Antoni was his best in the NBA. He appeared in 59 games, started 25, and averaged 8.0 points and 3.7 rebounds. On February 5, 2014, he scored a career-high 26 points in a win over the Cleveland Cavaliers. The stretch-four game that K had developed at Duke — the ability to shoot threes at 6-foot-11 while also blocking shots and taking charges — translated, at least in flashes.

Kelly spent three seasons with the Lakers and one with the Atlanta Hawks, appearing in 163 total NBA games and earning approximately $4.3 million. He was never a star, but he was a pro — a second-round pick who stuck in the league for four years, which puts him ahead of the majority of players drafted in his range.

After his NBA run, Kelly played a season with Real Betis in Spain’s Liga ACB, averaging 13.4 points and 4.6 rebounds. Then came the chapter nobody expected.

In 2018, Kelly’s former Lakers teammate Robert Sacre connected him with an opportunity in Japan’s B.League. Kelly signed with the Shibuya Sun Rockers — and everything clicked. In his first season, he averaged a double-double: 21.1 points and 10.2 rebounds per game. In his second, he put up 22.3 points and 7.3 rebounds. The stretch-four who had been a role player in the NBA became a dominant force in Japanese basketball.

He spent six seasons with the Sun Rockers, becoming one of the most consistent performers in the B.League. His wife Lindsay and their three children eventually moved to Japan with him, and the family embraced the culture. For a kid raised in an Ivy League household in North Carolina, the idea of building a life in Tokyo was the kind of adventure his parents would have understood perfectly.

Kelly now plays for the Fukui Blowinds in the B.League, still going strong at 34 years old. His career arc — from a Duke national championship as a teenager to averaging 22 points a night in Japan in his mid-thirties — is one of the most unusual and satisfying in the Brotherhood.
bio.now:
Ryan Kelly is 34 years old and in his seventh season of professional basketball in Japan. He has earned over $4 million in NBA salary alone, plus significant income from Spain and Japan. He is married to Lindsay Cowher — the daughter of NFL legend and CBS broadcaster Bill Cowher, who coached the Pittsburgh Steelers to a Super Bowl championship. They have three children and have built a life that spans continents.

His younger brother Sean was a walk-on at Duke during the 2014-15 season — the year Duke won its fifth national championship — making the Kellys a two-brother Duke basketball family.

Kelly’s Brotherhood story is about the long game. In an era where freshmen leave after one season and the transfer portal churns rosters annually, he stayed at Duke for four years, got his degree, won a national championship, captained two teams, made the NBA, played in Spain, and then found his best professional basketball on the other side of the world. He took charges at Duke. He averaged 22 points in Japan. He married into NFL royalty. And he did it all with the Ivy League values his parents instilled: work hard, think clearly, and never stop competing.

As he told The Next Prospect in 2020: “My rookie year in the NBA, playing for Mike D’Antoni, was my best year in the NBA even though I didn’t start to play closer to Christmas time.” The patience. The willingness to wait. That’s the most Duke thing about Ryan Kelly — the understanding that the best things come to those who show up every day and refuse to quit.

nba.overseas: Real Betis (Spain, 2017-18); Shibuya Sun Rockers (Japan B.League, 2018-24); Fukui Blowinds (Japan B.League, 2024-present)
nba.postCareer: Active — professional basketball in Japan
