players.name: Semi Ojeleye
players.tagline: Parade National Player of the Year. 23 games at Duke. AAC Player of the Year at SMU. NBA playoff warrior. European champion.
players.now: Professional basketball, Crvena zvezda (Serbia)
players.stat: 23 games at Duke (2.0 ppg); AAC POY at SMU (19.0 ppg); NBA: 198 games, guarded Giannis in playoffs

bio.road:
His full name is Jesusemilore Talodabijesu Ojeleye. His mother Joy created it, beginning with the name Jesus, as she did for all three of her children. His parents, Ernest and Joy, emigrated from Nigeria to Ottawa, Kansas — a small town of 12,000 people about 45 minutes south of Kansas City. Ernest is a doctor. Joy poured her faith into her children’s names and their upbringing. Semi and his older brother Victor grew up in a household where education, faith, and discipline were non-negotiable.

At Ottawa High School, Ojeleye was a force unlike anything small-town Kansas had ever produced. He set state records for career points (2,763) and single-season points (952). By his senior year, he was a five-star recruit ranked 26th nationally and #8 among small forwards. In 2013, Parade Magazine named him the National High School Player of the Year — the most prestigious individual award in prep basketball. He was a McDonald’s All-American and the most decorated player in Kansas high school history.

He chose Duke. Of course he chose Duke. When you’re the Parade National Player of the Year and Coach K comes calling, you go to Durham. The 2013-14 Blue Devils were loaded with talent — Jabari Parker, Rodney Hood, Rasheed Sulaimon, Quinn Cook, and Amile Jefferson were all on the roster. Seven players from that team would eventually reach the NBA. It was the kind of class where even elite talent could get buried.
bio.duke:
Ojeleye’s Duke career is the most painful in the Brotherhood. The Parade National Player of the Year played 5.0 minutes per game as a freshman, averaging 1.6 points. He appeared in 19 games and never started. On a roster with Jabari Parker and Rodney Hood, there simply weren’t enough minutes to go around.

His sophomore year started the same way. After appearing in just six games and barely seeing the court, Ojeleye made the agonizing decision to transfer in the middle of the 2014-15 season. The team he left would go on to win the national championship, led by Tyus Jones, Justise Winslow, and Jahlil Okafor. Semi Ojeleye watched from the outside as his former teammates cut down the nets.

The departure was not clean. His mother Joy later described a meeting with Krzyzewski where K initially promised playing time would come, texting her that Semi was “going to break out.” When a second meeting with the coaching staff turned heated, Joy was brought to tears. His father Ernest said an assistant coach called to disparage Semi’s work ethic — an accusation the family found deeply insulting given the state records and national awards their son had accumulated.

Duke issued a statement acknowledging that “transfer situations are sometimes emotional” and wished Ojeleye well. Semi himself was characteristically gracious: “I wasn’t prepared, mentally or physically. I don’t think I ever performed at my best. Being a young kid, I could never get over that hump.” And then, the line that defined his character: “If I could do it over again, I’d still go there. It was a great opportunity playing for Coach K. It definitely made me the person I am today.”

His mother saw it differently: “He would have toiled away in obscurity. He would have slaved away and the dream would have been completely dead.”

The SMU Renaissance

Ojeleye’s former AAU coach, Rodney Perry, connected him with Larry Brown at SMU. Brown walked into the Ojeleye home with his entire staff and told Semi he knew what he had without seeing another minute of game film. The fit was immediate.

But Ojeleye made a remarkable decision: instead of playing immediately after sitting out the NCAA-mandated transfer year, he redshirted the entire 2015-16 season as well. That meant he hadn’t played a meaningful game in nearly three years. He used the time to transform his body into what Draft Express would later call “a heavy, jacked frame” and to hone his three-point shot from good to lethal. He shot 42.4% from three at SMU.

When he finally took the court for the 2016-17 season, Semi Ojeleye was a different player. He averaged 19.0 points and 6.9 rebounds, shot 48.7% from the field and 42.4% from three, and was named AAC Player of the Year, AAC Scholar-Athlete of the Year, and AAC Tournament MVP. He led SMU to a 30-5 record and both the regular-season and tournament championships. His coach Tim Jankovich marveled at the combination: “To have his body, and the kind of shooting ability he has … it certainly doesn’t match that body type. You don’t find many guys with that soft a touch and that strong a body.”
bio.after:
The Boston Celtics selected Ojeleye 37th overall in the 2017 NBA Draft. Brad Stevens saw the defensive versatility immediately: “We knew he could be a versatile defender.” In his rookie season, Ojeleye carved out a playoff role that nobody expected. When injuries depleted the Celtics’ frontcourt, Stevens turned to the rookie and asked him to guard Giannis Antetokounmpo in a first-round series against Milwaukee. In Game 7, Ojeleye formed a wall between the Greek Freak and the basket. Antetokounmpo shot 7-for-17 under his pressure. Boston won the series.

Ojeleye spent four seasons with the Celtics, appearing in 198 regular-season games and 23 playoff games. He was never a star — his NBA averages of 3.4 points and 2.2 rebounds reflected a role player’s life — but he earned $7.8 million and proved he belonged on an NBA roster. He had brief stops with the Milwaukee Bucks and Los Angeles Clippers before moving to Europe in 2022.

At Virtus Bologna in Italy, Ojeleye found a second basketball life. He was named MVP of the Italian Supercup in 2022, helping Virtus win the championship. He competed in the EuroLeague — the highest level of European club basketball. After a season in Bologna, he moved to Valencia in Spain, and then to Crvena zvezda in Serbia, where he continues to play in the ABA League.
bio.now:
Semi Ojeleye is 31 years old and still playing professional basketball, now with Crvena zvezda in Belgrade, Serbia. He has earned over $8 million combined in NBA and European salaries. His nicknames tell you everything about the body he built: “Muscles Jesus,” “The Ox,” “Thor,” “The Man Made of Granite.”

His Brotherhood story is the road not taken — or rather, the road that forked. He was the Parade National Player of the Year who played 23 games at Duke and never started. He transferred, sat out for essentially three years, reinvented himself at SMU, got drafted, guarded Giannis in the NBA playoffs, won a championship in Italy, and is still playing professionally a decade after leaving Durham.

The fact that he remained gracious about Duke — “If I could do it over again, I’d still go there” — while his parents openly described the pain of the experience speaks to the complexity of the Brotherhood. Not every Duke story is a triumph in Durham. Some players need to leave to find themselves. Semi Ojeleye is proof that the name on the front of the jersey isn’t always where the story happens — but it’s always where the story starts.

players.stat: 17.3 ppg at Duke • ACC ROY • MIP 2020 • 2x All-Star • ~$182M career earnings

bio.road:
There are two Jahlil Okafors. There is the one you see on the court: all power dunks, spin moves, stare-downs. And there is the one you don’t see. The one who plays chess and the saxophone and the tuba. Who worked stage crew for a musical in junior high. Whose morning alarm, for years after his mother died, was a recording of her voice: ‘Jahlil, Jahlil.’

He was born December 15, 1995, in Fort Smith, Arkansas. His father, Chukwudi — known as Chuck — is a second-generation Nigerian American of Igbo descent. His mother, Dacresha Lanett Benton, was a 6-foot-2 basketball force at Carl Albert State College before a knee injury ended her career. They met there on basketball scholarships. As a young child, Jahlil split time between his mother’s home in Moffett, Oklahoma — population 127 — and his father’s home in Chicago.

When Jahlil was nine, he was watching music videos on BET with his sister Jalen when their mother started breathing hard. They thought she was joking. Jahlil teased her — said he was going to steal her Oreos. Then he realized it wasn’t a joke. He called 911. She had contracted bronchitis two weeks earlier and suffered a collapsed lung. She died.

Jahlil called his father. Chuck heard it in his son’s voice — somewhere between a cry and a scream. The only words: ‘Mama. Mama.’ Chuck flew to Fort Smith. At the funeral, Jahlil read a poem he’d written. At the gravesite, the wind lifted, and Chuck spread out his arms: ‘Every time you feel the wind, that is your mom with you.’

Chuck’s own story was a mirror of grief. His mother died of breast cancer when he was eighteen months old. His father, a Nigerian immigrant, raised six children alone. Chuck had been a ‘roughneck’ — stolen car at thirteen, drugs, five high schools in three years, a group home. But when Dacresha died: ‘Without Jah, I’d probably be dead or in jail.’

At Whitney Young Magnet High School — Michelle Obama’s alma mater — Okafor became the most dominant high school center in the country. National Player of the Year (McDonald’s, USA Today, Parade). Illinois Mr. Basketball. Three USA Basketball golds (U16, U17, U19). DePaul offered him a scholarship before he played a high school game. He committed to Duke in a package deal with his AAU friend Tyus Jones. ‘My deepest fear is losing someone else close to me,’ he told Chicago magazine. ‘That’s something I think about way more than I should.’
bio.duke:
One season. 38 games. 17.3 ppg, 8.5 rpg, 66.4% FG — second in the nation. Shot 70% or better twenty times. First freshman in Duke history to win ACC Player of the Year. USBWA National Freshman of the Year. Unanimous First-Team All-American.

In the 2015 championship game against Wisconsin, Okafor scored 10 key points as Duke won 68–63. He played alongside Tyus Jones (tournament MOP), Justise Winslow, Quinn Cook, and a freshman bench player named Grayson Allen who scored 16 off the bench. Duke went 35–4.
bio.after:
Philadelphia, 3rd overall, 2015. Debuted with 26 points against the Celtics. Averaged 17.5 as a rookie — leading the Sixers and all first-year players. All-Rookie First Team. But the team was 10–72 — the architecture of The Process.

Then the implosion. A heckler pulled a gun on him at a Philadelphia club. A Boston street fight filmed by TMZ. A second fight the same night. 108 mph on a Philadelphia bridge. A fake ID. Suspended two games. A personal security guard assigned. He was nineteen. Brett Brown: ‘I’m looking at a 19-year-old kid that all of a sudden has come into money and has been used to winning. I do not feel betrayed.’

Joel Embiid arrived and Okafor became expendable. Traded to Brooklyn for Trevor Booker. Brown: ‘I wouldn’t say there’s regrets. I’d say there’s human disappointment.’ Embiid texted him: ‘Love you. New beginnings.’

Okafor reinvented his body in New Orleans — semi-vegan diet, resculpted his frame, averaged 8.2 points in 59 Pelicans games. Detroit in 2020–21. Then unsigned. In February 2025, Indiana signed him to a 10-day contract; he appeared in one game. In July 2025, he signed with Levanga Hokkaido in Japan’s B.League. He represented Nigeria at the 2020 Tokyo Olympics, honoring his grandfather’s homeland.
bio.now:
Jahlil Okafor is 30 years old, playing basketball in Hokkaido, Japan — 6,200 miles from Cameron Indoor and 6,800 from the South Side of Chicago where his father reinvented himself. His Twitter profile still reads: ‘R.I.P. I love you mom.’ A picture of her headstone is the backdrop on his phone.

His Brotherhood story is the most heartbreaking in the Superteam Era. He lost his mother at nine and called 911 while she was dying on the couch. His father told him the wind was her. He set his alarm to a recording of her voice. He became the No. 1 recruit, won a national championship, and was drafted third overall. Then he got into street fights and drove 108 mph and was traded for Trevor Booker. He reinvented his body with the Pelicans and played for Nigeria in the Olympics and signed a 10-day contract nine years after being a top-three pick.

The kid who played chess and saxophone and tuba, whose deepest fear was losing someone else close to him — he is still playing basketball at thirty, on a different continent, because the game is the thing that saved him when his mother died and the thing that nearly destroyed him when the money came. Every time he feels the wind, that is his mom with him.

highlights.note: NBA Most Improved Player; All-Star (24.0/6.1/4.2)
Averaged 23.8 PPG with Pelicans

bio.road:
Luke Douglas Kennard was born June 24, 1996, in Franklin, Ohio, a small city near Dayton. His father Mark played college basketball at Georgetown College in Kentucky and started taking Luke to the gym at age three. His mother Jennifer sometimes got annoyed with Mark for pushing so hard, but Mark had a vision and Luke had the talent.

At Franklin High School, Kennard became a legend. He finished with 2,997 career points — the second-highest total in Ohio history, three spots ahead of LeBron James. Two-time Ohio Mr. Basketball, two-time Gatorade POY, Parade National Player of the Year as a senior. He averaged 38.1/9.7/5.9/2.2 his final season, scoring 50+ on five occasions and setting the school record with 59 in a single game.

He also lettered three years as a quarterback and was named Ohio Division II Offensive Player of the Year in 2013 (2,331 yards, 26 TDs). He shot left-handed in basketball and threw right-handed in football.

The defining story: after scoring 27 in one of his first varsity road games as a freshman, his parents waited in the car. Instead of celebrating, he texted them to come inside — he needed a rebounder. He was so bothered by going 7-for-10 from the free throw line that he practiced after the bus ride home. His coach told him to lock the doors when he was done. “Coach, to go where I want to go, 7-of-10 is not going to get it done.” He made one college visit: Duke. When Coach K visited the Kennard home, it was coach Bales’s wife who cooked the meal. After Luke left for Durham, Franklin posted signs at the town’s entry points: “Welcome to Franklin, Ohio, Home of Luke Kennard.”
bio.duke:
As a freshman in 2015–16, Kennard moved in and out of the starting lineup: 11.8/3.6/1.5. Flashes — 30 against Notre Dame — but not yet the player Coach K recruited. He practiced daily against Grayson Allen.

During the offseason, assistant Jon Scheyer kept repeating a mantra to Luke, his father, and his high school coach: “We didn’t bring Luke here to be a basketball player. We brought him here to be a star.”

Sophomore year was the revelation. 19.5/5.1/2.5. Shot 50% from the floor, 43.8% from three, 85.6% from the line — one of only two players nationally with a 50/40/85 line while averaging 20. First Team All-ACC. Second Team All-American. ACC Tournament MVP after scoring 24 against Louisville, 20 against UNC, and 16 in the championship win over Notre Dame. Coach K: “Luke is one of the hardest-working guys I’ve ever coached.”

The NCAA Tournament ended bitterly — 1-for-6, fouled out in a second-round loss to South Carolina. But the body of work was undeniable. He declared for the draft alongside Jayson Tatum, Harry Giles, and Frank Jackson.
bio.after:
Detroit took Kennard 12th overall in 2017. Three seasons with the Pistons — steady improvement, persistent knee injuries. Bilateral tendinitis limited him to 28 games in 2019–20 before COVID.

Traded to the Clippers in November 2020, he established himself as one of the NBA’s most efficient shooters — over 44% from three on high volume. Cool Hand Luke.

In Memphis (traded Feb 2023), he had the best stretch of his career: 13.9 PPG in his final 15 games, shooting 56.3% overall. On March 24, 2023, he hit 10-of-11 threes for 30 points against Houston — a franchise record, one of the highest single-game 3PT percentages in NBA history for a player making 10+.

Signed with Atlanta for 2024–25, traded to the Lakers in February 2026, joining LeBron, Luka Doncic, and Austin Reaves. As of March 2026, Kennard ranks second in NBA history in career 3PT% at 44.2%, trailing only Hubert Davis. First among active players.
bio.now:
Luke Kennard is currently playing for the Los Angeles Lakers, shooting 49.7% from three through his first stretch in purple and gold. Eight NBA seasons across five teams. His career 44.2% from three is a number that belongs in the Hall of Fame conversation for shooting specialists.

In Franklin, the community has never forgotten. His #10 jersey was retired in 2020. The gymnasium was dedicated in his honor in 2023. He donates shoes each season, bought a shooting machine for the programs, gave scoreboards and weight room equipment. His signed Duke jersey hangs at A&G Pizza, the restaurant that becomes a watch party every time he plays on television. “I will always be just a kid from Franklin, Ohio.”

The Welcome to Franklin, Ohio, Home of Luke Kennard signs still stand at the edge of town.

Luke Kennard scored 2,997 high school points — more than LeBron. He was Ohio’s Mr. Basketball twice and the Parade National POY. He was also the state’s best QB, throwing right-handed while shooting left-handed. He went back to the gym after his first varsity game because 7-for-10 wasn’t good enough. He made one college visit: Duke. Scheyer told his family: “We brought him here to be a star.” He became an All-American and ACC Tournament MVP. He was drafted 12th. He hit 10-of-11 threes in a single game. He has the second-highest career three-point percentage in NBA history. Franklin dedicated a gymnasium in his name and posted signs at the city limits. The kid who practiced free throws at midnight because 70% wasn’t good enough has become the purest shooter in the game. Cool Hand Luke. Just a kid from Franklin.

players.name: Jayson Tatum
players.tagline: One year at Duke. NBA champion at 26.
players.now: Boston Celtics — rehabbing Achilles; Duke Chief Basketball Officer
players.stat: 16.8 ppg at Duke • 6x All-Star • 5x All-NBA • 2024 NBA champ • 2x Olympic gold

bio.road:
Jayson Tatum’s first basketball court was a bathtub. His mother, Brandy Cole, hung a suction-cup hoop on the wall, and her son would shoot for hours. He was born March 3, 1998, in St. Louis, Missouri. Brandy was eighteen, a high school graduate who’d been offered a volleyball scholarship to Tennessee. She passed on it. She enrolled at the University of Missouri–St. Louis, worked gift-wrapping at Cingular Wireless, and raised Jayson alone. Four hours was a quality night’s sleep. She moved out of her mother’s house when Jayson was six months old. She brought her toddler to class. She missed his first crawl because she was at work. ‘I just broke down and cried,’ she said.

When Jayson was eleven, the bank threatened to foreclose. He understood what was happening. Cole saved the house at the last minute. ‘I’m not going to become a statistic.’ She eventually earned degrees in communications and political science, a law degree from Saint Louis University, and an MBA. Every lesson Jayson learned about work ethic, he learned watching her.

His father, Justin Tatum, played at Saint Louis University and in the Netherlands. He came back into Jayson’s life around age seven or eight. Justin’s best friend, NBA player Larry Hughes, is Jayson’s godfather. Through Hughes, eight-year-old Jayson met LeBron James and rebounded for him at practice. He’s also a cousin of NBA head coach Tyronn Lue.

The moment Justin knew: Jayson was eleven, playing in a men’s league. Justin’s team was short a player. He told his son, ‘Just shoot the ball.’ Jayson scored 22 points against grown men. But he also craved his father’s approval in ways that haunted him: ‘I could score 40 and he would never clap. He would just sit there. He would leave. I would go to the gym thinking if I score 50, then maybe that would be good enough.’

At Chaminade Prep, his coach Frank Bennett marveled: ‘I get to school about 6:30 every day, and he was here at 5:45. The only day he took off was the day after we won state. He just worked and worked and worked.’ 250 shots every morning. His mother prepared him for fame by pausing his NBA2K, shoving a hairbrush in his face, and grilling him with postgame questions like Craig Sager.

Senior year: 29.6 points, 9.1 rebounds, six 40-point games. State champion (40 in the final). Beat Malik Monk, Miles Bridges, and Markelle Fultz — three future lottery picks in three showcase games. McDonald’s All-American. Gatorade National Player of the Year. In gym class at Chaminade, he became friends with Matthew Tkachuk. Both would win championships in the same week in 2024.
bio.duke:
Tatum missed the first eight games with a foot injury. He debuted in December 2016 and immediately became Duke’s best player. In the ACC Tournament: 22.0 ppg across four games in four days as Duke won the title. Season: 16.8 ppg, 7.3 rpg in 29 games. In the NCAA Tournament, South Carolina pulled the upset in the second round. One season. One ACC title. Then gone.

Danny Ainge traded the No. 1 overall pick to Philadelphia for No. 3 and a future first. Philly took Markelle Fultz — the same kid Tatum had beaten by 40 at the HoopHall Classic. Ainge took Tatum. Fultz’s shot vanished. Tatum became a superstar. Widely regarded as the steal of the decade.
bio.after:
351 playoff points as a rookie — most in Celtics first-year history. 60-point game in 2021 (tying Larry Bird, 21 in the fourth quarter to erase a 32-point deficit). Six-time All-Star. Five-time All-NBA. Two Olympic golds (2020 Tokyo, 2024 Paris). In the 2024 NBA Finals: Tatum and Jaylen Brown led Boston to its 18th championship, beating Dallas in five games after a 64-win season. Supermax extension July 2024. Career earnings approaching $400 million. Jordan Brand signature shoe (Jordan Tatum 1).

Then May 12, 2025: Achilles rupture, Game 4 vs the Knicks at Madison Square Garden. Surgery. No timeline. Four months later, he posted video of himself shooting again: ‘It’s been a long journey but I’m working my tail off to get back.’ In October 2025, Duke named him Chief Basketball Officer — a first-of-its-kind volunteer role mentoring current players on development, professionalism, career management, and personal branding. Scheyer: ‘Jayson is the ultimate professional.’ Tatum: ‘This program means so much to me.’

By February 2026, full five-on-five scrimmages. Shams Charania reported he was cleared physically but the decision was his. He told ESPN he ‘wants to get it right the first time.’ Bill Simmons reported in early March that all signs pointed to a return against Dallas on March 7 — a rematch of the 2024 Finals.
bio.now:
Jayson Tatum is 27 years old. He runs the Jayson Tatum Foundation, helping low-income St. Louis families build generational wealth (SoFi donated $1M to the foundation in 2024). Son Jayson Jr. (‘Deuce’), born 2017, is a courtside celebrity. In 2024, Tatum and singer Ella Mai had their first child together.

His mother was eighteen when she had him. She missed his first crawl. She saved their house from foreclosure. She became an attorney. His father never clapped when he scored 40. His high school coach watched him arrive at 5:45 every morning — 250 shots before class, only day off the day after winning state. His mother practiced media interviews with a hairbrush.

He went third instead of first, and the team that passed on him got Markelle Fultz. He scored 60 in a game and won an NBA championship and two Olympic golds. Then his Achilles ruptured at Madison Square Garden and he spent the fall back at Duke mentoring the next generation while his tendon healed. The kid who shot hoops in the bathtub — ten months from a ruptured Achilles and scrimmaging five-on-five. The Brotherhood’s most dramatic story isn’t finished yet. It might not even be at halftime.

highlights.note: NBA All-Rookie First Team
First Eastern Conference Finals MVP
NBA Champion; 6x All-Star; led Celtics to league-best 64-18 record
2x Olympic gold medalist (2020, 2024)
Returned from ruptured Achilles (May 2025) on March 6, 2026

Consensus All-American. Drafted #2 behind his own high school teammate. Six teams. Still going. Still rapping.
players.now: Forward/Center, Dallas Mavericks (traded from Washington, Feb 2026); averaging 13.0/8.9 in first 7 games with Dallas
players.stat: 1 Duke season • 21.0/11.1 • ACC POY + ROY • Consensus All-American • 2nd pick • 8 NBA seasons • 6 teams

nba.postCareer: Still active — Dallas Mavericks (2025-26); rapper and hip-hop artist; volunteered with Hoops with Heart in LA

bio.road:
Marvin Bagley III was born March 14, 1999, in Tempe, Arizona. The bloodline ran deep: his grandfather was Joe Caldwell — “Jumpin’ Joe” — the #2 overall pick in the 1964 NBA Draft and an Olympic basketball player. His father, Marvin Jr., grew up in Durham, North Carolina, played football at North Carolina A&T and professionally for the Arizona Rattlers. In Arizona, Marvin Jr. met Tracy Caldwell — Joe’s daughter — and started a family.

Young Marvin was 6’5 in middle school. He briefly played quarterback until a large boy wearing #99 body-slammed him from behind in a game in the rain. “That was the day I was like, ‘I don’t think football is for me.’”

His high school journey covered three schools and two states. Freshman at Corona del Sol (Tempe): state champion, MaxPreps National Freshman of the Year. Sophomore at Hillcrest Prep (Phoenix): teammates with Deandre Ayton. Then Sierra Canyon (Chatsworth, CA): sat out a year, then averaged 24.9/10.1. California Gatorade POY. Consensus #1 recruit in the Class of 2018.

Then the earthquake: in August 2017, Bagley announced on SportsCenter that he’d reclassified from 2018 to 2017, pulling Danny Ferry’s retired #35 Duke jersey from a bag on live television. “That’s what Coach K preached — the brotherhood.” Vegas odds for Duke winning the title shifted from 7-1 to 3-1 instantly. His father, from Durham: “You just got to go with your heart. And that’s where his heart is, at Duke, in the Bull City.” Duke first offered him a scholarship in ninth grade.
bio.duke:
Bagley’s single season at Duke (2017–18) was historic. Debuted with 25/10 vs Elon. 34 points and a Duke freshman-record 15 rebounds vs Texas. 30/11 vs Florida — first Duke player to open with back-to-back 30-point games since the 1960s. 32 points and 21 rebounds vs Florida State (21 boards a Duke freshman record).

Over 33 games: 21.0 PPG, 11.1 RPG, 61.4% FG. Set the ACC record for 30/10 double-doubles in a season. Swept the conference awards: ACC Player of the Year AND Rookie of the Year, First Team All-ACC. Consensus First Team All-American. 3.3 GPA his first semester.

Beyond basketball, Bagley was a rapper who performed for 9th Wonder, a Grammy-winning producer who’d worked with Kendrick Lamar and Jay-Z. He loved the storytellers — Tupac, Biggie, Nas — and wanted people to see another side. His first album was Bow Wow’s The Price of Fame, bought at Christmas because he wanted to be like the kid in Like Mike.

Duke’s season ended in the Elite Eight. Bagley declared for the 2018 Draft.
bio.after:
Sacramento took Bagley #2 overall on June 21, 2018. Drafted one spot behind him: Luka Doncic. Drafted one spot ahead: his Hillcrest Prep teammate Deandre Ayton. The Doncic comparison — a burden no player should carry — would shadow Bagley’s career from that night forward.

Rookie year showed promise: 14.9/7.6 in 62 games, NBA All-Rookie First Team. But injuries — fractured thumb, sprained knee, bone bruise — derailed his trajectory. He missed significant time in each of his first three Sacramento seasons. In February 2022, traded to Detroit — more injuries: knee, back, surgery on two fractured fingers.

The journey: Washington (Jan 2024), Memphis (Feb 2025 trade deadline, 12 regular-season + 2 playoff games), back to Washington (July 2025), then Dallas (Feb 2026, part of the Anthony Davis blockbuster). Six teams in eight seasons for a #2 pick.

When asked about the journey, Bagley answered without bitterness: “I would say it has been a journey, but I’m grateful for every moment. I wouldn’t change a thing.”
bio.now:
Marvin Bagley III is currently with the Dallas Mavericks, averaging 13.0/8.9 in his first seven games. He has earned $73.6 million in his career. He is twenty-six and still fighting for a permanent role.

He still writes his own raps, connecting with people through storytelling about where he grew up and what he’s seen. He has two younger brothers: Marcus (Arizona State) and Martray. His father coaches AAU for the Nike Phamily. His parents married in June 2025.

Marvin Bagley III was born in Tempe, the grandson of Jumpin’ Joe Caldwell — the #2 pick in 1964. His father grew up in Durham. He was 6’5 in middle school and quit football after a body slam in the rain. He attended three high schools, was teammates with the #1 pick at one of them, and reclassified a year early to play for Duke. He pulled Danny Ferry’s #35 out of a bag on SportsCenter and Vegas changed instantly. He averaged 21 and 11 as a freshman, won ACC POY and ROY in the same season, and was a Consensus All-American. He was drafted #2 behind his own high school teammate and ahead of Luka Doncic, and the Doncic comparison has followed him to six cities across eight seasons. He raps about his life because people only see basketball. He was body-slammed at QB as a kid and chose basketball instead, and basketball chose him back — not always gently, not always the way the mock drafts predicted, but always. The journey continues in Dallas, where his coach told him to just go play. And that is what Marvin Bagley III has always done.

players.name: Wendell Carter Jr.
players.tagline: His father was abandoned as an infant and raised in an orphanage. His mother was 6’5 and played at Ole Miss. They met at a dunk contest. Their son was born at 11 lbs 8 oz, earned a 3.8 GPA, acted in the school play, almost went to Harvard, then chose Duke. Drafted 7th. 3,000 rebounds. $50M contract. His mother still has the piece of paper from second grade.
players.now: Center, Orlando Magic ($50M extension through 2025-26); founded A Platform Squared nonprofit; NBA Cares Community Assist Award
players.stat: 1 Duke season • 13.5/9.1/2.1 blk • 56.1% FG • 2nd Team All-ACC • 7th pick • 383 NBA games • $50M deal

sources.label: CBS Sports: Duke’s Wendell Carter Jr. was born into basketball (2018)
Andscape: Duke signee Wendell Carter Jr. and his parents (2017)
Andscape: The NBA journey begins for Wendell Carter Jr. (2018)
NBA.com: Wendell Carter Jr. bio
WendellACJr.com: Official site
Wikipedia: Wendell Carter Jr.

highlights.note: Career-high 28 pts vs Detroit (Nov 30)
Surpassed 4,000 career pts and 3,000 career rebounds; playoff double-double avg (10.2/10.8)
110 career double-doubles; NBA Cares Bob Lanier Community Assist Award

nba.postCareer: Still active — Orlando Magic (2025-26); founded A Platform Squared nonprofit (financial literacy, social justice, mentorship)

bio.road:
Every story is derived from an older story, and Wendell Carter Jr.’s begins before he was born. His father, Wendell Carter Sr., was born to a convicted felon in Atlanta. Having a child out of wedlock was a violation of her parole, so after the baby was born, she abandoned him in an apartment. Police broke in and found an infant alone, his diapers soiled. He was sent to an orphanage. He was there until he was five or six years old, when a woman adopted him and raised him as her own. That woman’s gravestone is in an Atlanta cemetery.

Years later, Wendell Sr. drove his teenage son to that cemetery. They walked up to the gravestone of his adoptive mother. He immediately broke down. So did his son. That was the moment Wendell Carter Jr. learned his own origin story — how his father had been abandoned, found by police, raised in an orphanage, and saved by a woman who chose to love him. It was the day Wendell Jr. understood why his father was so present, so involved, so determined to never let his own child feel alone. I could have easily ran with the wrong crowd, Wendell Sr. said. But I was so focused on basketball. That was the only thing that kept me going.

Basketball saved Wendell Sr. He grew to 6’6 with a 42-inch vertical and played college basketball at Delta State University in Mississippi. After college, he played professionally in the Dominican Republic. One day in Atlanta, Kylia’s sister introduced them as Wendell was leaving for a summer pro-am game — a league where Dominique Wilkins and Spud Webb played. Kylia came to watch. She was 6’5, a basketball player at the University of Mississippi. I thought he was cute, to be honest, Kylia said. But any excuse to go watch basketball. At halftime, there was a slam-dunk contest. Twenty-six years of marriage later, Wendell Sr.’s dunk contest trophy still sits on the piano in their Atlanta home.

When Wendell Carter Jr. came out of the womb — 11 pounds, 8 ounces, 26 inches long — you could say he was born to play basketball. As a toddler, he ran up and down the floor at his father’s church league games, mimicking everything his dad did. At Pace Academy in Atlanta, he became a dominant force: 22.7 points, 15.5 rebounds, and 5.5 blocks as a senior, a Georgia Class AA state championship with 30 points and 20 rebounds in the title game, the Morgan Wootten National Player of the Year award for character and academics, a 3.8 GPA, the Lance and Shield Award as Pace’s top scholar-athlete, a member of the National Honor Society, and an actor in the school play Thoroughly Modern Millie.

He also seriously considered Harvard. His mother, who valued education above everything, pushed hard for the Ivy League. Carter took his first official visit to Cambridge, enjoyed the diversity and academic environment, and called it a great possibility. In a second-grade class, a teacher had asked students what they wanted to be when they grew up. Carter wrote that he wanted to be a professional basketball player. His classmates ridiculed him. His mother still has the piece of paper. He chose Duke — the compromise between Harvard’s academics and championship basketball.
bio.duke:
Carter arrived at Duke for the 2017–18 season alongside Marvin Bagley III, Gary Trent Jr., and one of the most talented freshman classes in the country. On a team where Bagley was the headliner — the reclassified number one recruit, the ACC Player of the Year — Carter was the anchor. He formed a devastating frontcourt tandem with Bagley, providing the defense, rim protection, and efficiency that Bagley’s scoring required.

Over 37 starts, Carter averaged 13.5 points, 9.1 rebounds, 2.0 assists, and 2.1 blocks per game, shooting 56.1 percent from the field and 46.3 percent from three. He finished second all-time among Duke freshmen in rebounds (335), blocked shots (76), and double-doubles (16). He was named Second Team All-ACC and a unanimous selection to the ACC All-Freshman Team. The NBA comparison that made the most sense, CBS Sports wrote at the time, was Al Horford: a lack of flash but an abundance of fundamentals.

Duke reached the Elite Eight before falling, and Carter declared for the 2018 NBA Draft.
bio.after:
The Chicago Bulls selected Carter with the seventh overall pick in the 2018 NBA Draft. His mother, ever protective, rejected the notion that being a first-round pick meant her 19-year-old son was ready to live on his own. Every other 19-year-old kid lives at home with their momma, Kylia said. So why do we become helicopter parents because our kid’s a millionaire and we still want him at home with us?

Carter played two and a half seasons in Chicago, establishing himself as a reliable young center averaging around 11 points and 8 rebounds. In March 2021, he was traded to the Orlando Magic, where his career found its fullest expression. With Orlando, Carter became the starting center of one of the NBA’s most promising young cores. He signed a four-year, $50 million contract extension. In 2024–25, he appeared in 68 games, averaging 9.1 points, 7.2 rebounds, and 2.0 assists while ranking 29th in the NBA in rebounding. He surpassed 4,000 career points and 3,000 career rebounds. He averaged a double-double in the playoffs: 10.2 points and 10.8 rebounds in 32.4 minutes per game.

Through seven NBA seasons, Carter has appeared in 383 regular-season games with 345 starts, averaging 11.9 points, 8.3 rebounds, and 2.0 assists while shooting 50.9 percent from the field. He is what the modern NBA needs from its centers: a rim protector who can stretch the floor with a three-point shot, facilitate from the high post, and anchor a defense without requiring the ball on offense.
bio.now:
Wendell Carter Jr. is currently the starting center for the Orlando Magic, under contract through 2025–26 on his $50 million extension. He and his parents founded A Platform Squared, a nonprofit organization focused on financial literacy, social justice, mentorship, and economic empowerment. He received the NBA Cares Bob Lanier Community Assist Award and the Presidential Scholar Award in 2022 for combining basketball with education advocacy. His mother, Kylia, became a prominent critic of NCAA exploitation and co-founded Elevate, an enterprise designed to empower athletes and their families.

In a league full of flash, Carter operates with fundamentals. In a sport that celebrates scorers, he protects the rim and sets screens. In a culture that measures basketball players by their Instagram following, he earned a 3.8 GPA, acted in his high school play, and almost went to Harvard. His parents call it love. Other people call it spoiled. But they participated in everything he did.

Wendell Carter Jr. was born at 11 pounds, 8 ounces in Atlanta, Georgia. His father was abandoned as an infant, found by police in a soiled diaper, raised in an orphanage, saved by an adoptive mother, and went on to play professional basketball in the Dominican Republic. His mother was a 6’5 basketball player at Ole Miss who valued education above everything else. They met because of a dunk contest. Their son was mimicking his father’s moves on the church basketball court at age three. He earned a 3.8 GPA at Pace Academy, won a state championship with 30 points and 20 rebounds, acted in Thoroughly Modern Millie, and seriously considered going to Harvard. He chose Duke, formed a frontcourt with Marvin Bagley III, averaged a double-double, and was drafted seventh overall. He has played seven NBA seasons, started 345 games, pulled down 3,000 rebounds, and signed a $50 million contract. His mother still has the piece of paper from second grade where he wrote that he wanted to be a professional basketball player. The other kids laughed. Nobody is laughing now.

players.name: Zion Williamson
players.tagline: The shoe exploded. The legend was born.
players.now: New Orleans Pelicans; $197M contract; career-high 35-game streak 2025–26
players.stat: 22.6 ppg/68% FG at Duke • Unanimous POY • 2x NBA All-Star • 259 games/296 missed

bio.road:
Zion Lateef Williamson was born July 6, 2000, in Salisbury, North Carolina. His mother, Sharonda Sampson, ran track at Livingstone College. His stepfather, Lee Anderson, played basketball at Clemson. Starting at the age of nine, Williamson woke up at 5 AM daily for basketball training. He was built differently from the start — not just talented but physically overwhelming, with a combination of size, speed, and explosive leaping ability that bordered on the supernatural.

He attended Spartanburg Day School, a small private school in Spartanburg, South Carolina. He led the team to three consecutive state championships and was named South Carolina Mr. Basketball as a senior. He became a viral sensation before he ever played a college game — his high school dunk highlights accumulated millions of views on YouTube and social media, making him the most famous high school basketball player since LeBron James. He was a consensus five-star recruit, ranked among the top five players in the 2018 class, a McDonald’s All-American, runner-up for Mr. Basketball USA, and a USA Today All-USA first team honoree. He chose Duke.
bio.duke:
Williamson’s single season at Duke was one of the most electrifying individual years in college basketball history. In 33 games, he averaged 22.6 points, 8.9 rebounds, 2.1 assists, 1.8 steals, and 1.8 blocks while shooting 68.0% from the floor — a staggering efficiency mark for a player with that volume of scoring. He was a unanimous consensus National Player of the Year, winning the AP, Naismith, Wooden, and virtually every other major award.

The defining moment came on February 20, 2019: Duke vs. North Carolina, the most-watched regular season college basketball game in years. Thirty-three seconds into the game, Williamson planted his left foot and his Nike PG 2.5 shoe literally exploded — the sole ripping away from the upper. He suffered a Grade 1 knee sprain. Nike’s stock dropped $1.1 billion the next day. The shoe heard round the world became the most viral moment in college basketball history. Williamson returned three weeks later for the ACC Tournament and led Duke to the title.

He played alongside RJ Barrett and Cam Reddish — all three would be selected in the top 10 of the 2019 NBA Draft. Duke was eliminated in the Elite Eight by Michigan State. Williamson declared for the draft, and on June 20, 2019, the Pelicans selected him first overall. The hype was unlike anything the sport had seen since LeBron.

PROFESSIONAL CAREER: BRILLIANCE AND FRAGILITY

Williamson tore his meniscus in the preseason of his rookie campaign, delaying his NBA debut until January 22, 2020. When he finally played, he scored 17 consecutive points in a 3-minute, 8-second span in the fourth quarter against the Spurs. He became the first teenager in NBA history to score 20+ points in 10 consecutive games and finished his abbreviated rookie season averaging 22.5 points on 58.3% shooting — the highest scoring average through 24 career games since Michael Jordan.

In 2021, at age 20, Williamson became the fourth-youngest NBA All-Star ever, averaging 27.0 points, 7.2 rebounds, and 3.7 assists while shooting 61.1% from the field. But then came the injuries. He missed the entire 2021–22 season with a broken right foot. He played 29 games in 2022–23 before a hamstring strain shut him down. His most durable season was 2023–24, when he appeared in a career-high 70 games and averaged 22.9 points — but a hamstring strain in the play-in game against the Lakers ended his season, and the Pelicans were swept by Oklahoma City.

The 2024–25 campaign was another lost year: a November hamstring strain cost him 27 games, a January suspension for being late to a team flight, and a March back contusion ended his season at just 30 games played. Through seven NBA seasons, Williamson has played in 259 games and missed 296 — unavailable more often than available. He signed a five-year, $197.2 million max extension in 2022, but the deal includes non-guaranteed years tied to weight checkpoints and games-played thresholds. His next two seasons’ salaries can be voided by the Pelicans if he fails to meet those benchmarks.
bio.after:
The Pelicans selected Williamson first overall on June 20, 2019. Torn meniscus in preseason delayed his debut until January 22, 2020, when he scored 17 consecutive points in 3:08 vs. the Spurs. He became the first teenager in NBA history to score 20+ in 10 consecutive games. Highest scoring average through 24 career games since Michael Jordan.

In 2021, at age 20, he became the fourth-youngest NBA All-Star: 27.0 points, 7.2 rebounds, 61.1% shooting. Then the injuries: missed all of 2021–22 (broken foot), 29 games in 2022–23 (hamstring), career-high 70 games in 2023–24 (but hamstring strain in play-in game), just 30 games in 2024–25 (hamstring, suspension for late team flight, back contusion).

Through seven NBA seasons: 259 games played, 296 missed — unavailable more often than available. He signed a five-year, $197.2 million max extension in 2022, but the deal includes non-guaranteed years tied to weight checkpoints and games-played thresholds.
bio.now:
The 2025–26 season has offered the most sustained hope of Williamson’s career. He set a new personal record by playing 35 consecutive games before spraining his ankle against the Utah Jazz on March 1, 2026. Through 45 games this season (his third-highest total), he is averaging approximately 21.5 points and 5.8 rebounds. He has publicly committed to New Orleans: ‘If you can win a championship in New Orleans, you’re forever. And who doesn’t want to be forever?’

The Pelicans, at 17–42, are among the worst teams in the NBA and do not control their own 2026 first-round pick. Yet Williamson, now 25, remains under contract through at least 2027–28 (if he meets his guarantees). He is still, when healthy, one of the most physically dominant players in basketball — a 6-foot-6, 284-pound force who shoots over 57% from the field for his career.

Zion Williamson’s Brotherhood story is the most complicated of the modern era. He was the most hyped prospect since LeBron James, the player whose shoe exploded on national television, the No. 1 pick whose body has betrayed his talent more games than it has served it. He is simultaneously a two-time All-Star and a player who has missed more games than he has played. He is a $197 million investment with a clause that says the money disappears if he can’t stay on the court. His ceiling remains absurdly high. His floor remains the injured list. The story is unresolved, and that is the most honest thing anyone can say about Zion Williamson in March 2026.

Duke’s Brotherhood: Where Are They Now? • Profile • March 2026

players.name: RJ Barrett
players.tagline: #3 pick. Canadian. Still proving himself.
players.now: Toronto Raptors; 2023 FIBA World Cup Bronze (Canada’s first in 87 years); $120M contract
players.stat: 1 Duke season • 22.6 PPG/7.6 RPG • ACC Freshman of Year • #1 recruit (1st since LeBron to sweep all HS awards)

bio.road:
Rowan Alexander Barrett Jr. was born June 14, 2000, in Toronto, Ontario. His father, Rowan Sr., was born in Scarborough to Jamaican parents. The elder Rowan hadn’t even played basketball as a child — he ran track, racing friends at recess every day. Basketball came later. He played at St. John’s, turned pro in Europe and South America, and captained the Canadian national team at the 2000 Sydney Olympics. His closest friend on that team was Steve Nash.

When RJ was born, Nash bought him his first crib. He became the baby’s godfather. Two-time NBA MVP. Hall of Famer. ‘Dad, what’s Uncle Steve gonna do against Kobe?’ RJ once asked his father while watching Nash on television.

RJ’s mother, Kesha Duhaney, was a nationally ranked sprinter and long jumper at St. John’s — where she met Rowan. Brooklyn-born, Jamaican descent. Her aunt Dahlia won a 4×100m gold at the 1991 World Championships for Jamaica. ‘My mom is crazy competitive. She always wants to beat my dad. But my dad never wants us to beat him. Ever.’

The family lived in France for five years — Dijon, Chalon, Lyon — where RJ played youth basketball in the JDA Dijon Basket club, wearing the same uniform and number as his father. He attended French schools. His mother taught him English at home. He grew up bilingual.

The family returned to Mississauga in 2008. At twelve, after struggling in a game and coming home crying, RJ decided to focus entirely on basketball. At fifteen, he left Canada alone for Montverde Academy in Florida. ‘The first year was definitely the worst. I was not used to being away from my parents.’ He saw them every couple months for three years.

At Montverde: #1 recruit in America. Undefeated season. National championship. Naismith Prep POY. Gatorade National POY. McDonald’s All-American (26 pts). First since LeBron to sweep all major HS awards and win a national title. Summer 2017: led Canada to FIBA U19 World Cup gold, scoring 38/13/5 vs Team USA to end America’s six-year winning streak. Tournament MVP at seventeen.
bio.duke:
The 2018–19 freshman class was the most hyped in college basketball history: Barrett, Zion Williamson, Cam Reddish, Tre Jones. All top-fifteen recruits. Barrett and Williamson were roommates, friends, the #1 and #2 players in America.

Barrett averaged 22.6 PPG, 7.6 RPG — 860 points, the most by a Duke freshman in history. All-ACC First Team. ACC Freshman of the Year. 33 points against North Carolina. Back-to-back 30-point games. Relentless, physical, a 6-6 wing who attacked the basket with force.

But the season will always be remembered through Zion. Williamson was a phenomenon — the shoe blowout against UNC, dunks that crashed the internet. Barrett was the #1 recruit who became the #2 story on his own team. He handled it with the quiet professionalism of a kid raised in professional locker rooms across three continents. Duke went 32–6, reached the Elite Eight, fell to Michigan State.
bio.after:
New York selected Barrett 3rd overall in 2019, behind Zion (#1) and Ja Morant (#2). Madison Square Garden was supposed to be his stage. Rookie year: 14.3 PPG, youngest player in Knicks history to reach 1,000 points. Improved every year: 17.6 PPG (2020–21), 20.0 PPG (2021–22). Signed a 4-year, $120M extension in 2022.

In January 2024, traded to the Toronto Raptors for OG Anunoby. He was going home. The kid from Mississauga who left Canada at fifteen was now playing professional basketball in the city where his father captained the national team.

But the international stage delivered his most meaningful basketball. Summer 2023: wearing the maple leaf, Barrett helped Canada win bronze at the FIBA World Cup — Canada’s first medal at a major global tournament since the 1936 Olympics. In the bronze medal game against the United States, Barrett scored 23 points, outplaying his Knicks teammates Brunson and Hart on the opposing side. He combined with Shai Gilgeous-Alexander and Dillon Brooks for 93 points. His father was in the stands — the man who captained Canada at the 2000 Olympics and spent two decades building Canadian basketball. Represented Canada again at the 2024 Paris Olympics.
bio.now:
RJ Barrett is 25. He plays for the Toronto Raptors — in the city where his father grew up, where Steve Nash changed the perception of Canadian basketball, where Vince Carter’s dunks in 2000 lit a fire still burning. Barrett is the inheritor of all of it: son of the man who runs Canada Basketball, godson of the man who proved Canadians could be MVPs.

His brother Nathan also attended Montverde. He speaks fluent French. He played piano as a child. He lived in Dijon wearing his father’s number before he was old enough for school. He came home crying at twelve and decided to dedicate his life to basketball. Left his country at fifteen. Became the #1 recruit. Beat Team USA at seventeen. Averaged 22.6 at Duke alongside the most hyped player in history. Drafted third. Signed $120 million. Got traded home. Helped his country win its first basketball medal in 87 years.

Steve Nash bought him his first crib. His mother taught him English in a French apartment. His aunt won gold in the relay for Jamaica. His father captained Canada at the Olympics. And now RJ Barrett plays for the Raptors, carrying the inheritance of a basketball family that stretches from Jamaica to Scarborough to St. John’s to Sydney to Dijon to Mississauga to Durham to Madison Square Garden and finally, home.

highlights.note: Career-high 20.0 PPG with Knicks
21.3/6.2/4.8 with Raptors; blossomed since trade to Toronto

nba.postCareer: Toronto Raptors; 2023 FIBA World Cup Bronze (Canada’s first in 87 years); $120M contract

players.name: Tre Jones
players.tagline: Tyus’s brother. Stayed two years.
players.now: Chicago Bulls; 3-year/$24M contract; Susan G. Komen Foundation advocate
players.stat: 2 Duke seasons • 65 starts • 2020 ACC POY + Defensive POY (first to win both) • Duke career A/TO record (2.88)

bio.road:
Tre Isiah Jones was born January 8, 2000, in Apple Valley, Minnesota — four years after Tyus, into the same basketball family, the same single-mother household. Debbie Jones had won a North Dakota state championship as a point guard. Rob Jones played at Division III Wisconsin-Parkside. Older brother Jadee played at Furman. Uncle Al Nuness captained the Gophers in 1969. Debbie and Rob divorced when Tre was five. Debbie, a paralegal, raised the boys on her own.

In 2010, Uncle Al took both boys to the Final Four in Indianapolis. While visiting Durham, Al told Coach K to remember the name Tyus Jones. Then he mentioned Tre: ‘Tre Jones is a Duke fanatic.’ Al got Krzyzewski to sign memorabilia and gave ten-year-old Tre a Blue Devils watch from Jostens. Tre created a Duke shrine in his bedroom — posters, signed memorabilia, the watch. Years later, when Coach K visited the Jones home on a recruiting trip and saw Tre’s room, the decision was already made. ‘It was pretty obvious where Tre had his mind set. He always had an infatuation with Duke and Coach K.’

But Tre had to earn it on his own. In Apple Valley, Tyus was a legend — a day named after him, a parade through the streets. Every time Tre played: ‘How does your game compare to your brother’s?’ He answered by being different. Tyus was the scorer. Tre was the defender.

Varsity as an eighth-grader. Two state championships (2015, 2017). In the 2017 final: 24 pts/18 reb/5 ast. Senior: 23.5 PPG/10.4 RPG/7.5 APG. Two-time Gatorade POY. Minnesota Mr. Basketball. McDonald’s All-American. #1 PG in the 2018 class. USA Basketball U-16 gold (American record: 19 steals). The shrine told you where he was going.
bio.duke:
Tre arrived in 2018 alongside Zion Williamson, RJ Barrett, and Cam Reddish. The most hyped freshman class in history. The spotlight burned on Zion and RJ. Tre was the one who made it work.

Freshman: started all 36 games. 9.4 PPG, 5.3 APG, 1.9 SPG. Set the Duke single-season record for A/TO ratio (3.62) — also an ACC freshman record. Duke went 32–6, reached the Elite Eight. Coach K: ‘They are big-time moment players.’ Tre was the steadier, the one who held superstars together while everyone else got the highlights.

Then the decision that separated him from his brother’s path. Tyus left after one year. Tre came back. He returned for his sophomore season determined to lead his own team — not Zion’s team, not RJ’s team, but Tre’s team.

Sophomore: 16.2 PPG, 6.4 APG, 4.2 RPG, 1.8 SPG. Career-high 31 vs Georgia State. On February 8, 2020, at Chapel Hill: hit a buzzer-beating jumper to tie the game against North Carolina, sending it to overtime. Duke won 98–96. Named ACC Player of the Year AND ACC Defensive Player of the Year — the first player to win both. Duke career record holder in A/TO ratio (2.88). 5.8 APG (3rd in Duke history). 1.8 SPG (5th).

Then COVID cancelled March Madness. No tournament. No championship chase. The season ended in silence. Tre declared for the draft.
bio.after:
Drafted 41st overall by San Antonio in 2020 — a second-round pick, lower than his talent warranted. G League first: named to the 2020–21 All-G League Team. Earned minutes gradually as Dejounte Murray’s backup, then as a starter when Murray was traded in 2022.

As a starter: 13.5 PPG on 48.8% shooting, 7.5 APG, just 1.1 turnovers — the family DNA. April 2, 2023: first career triple-double (17/11/10) in overtime vs Sacramento. It came sixteen days after Tyus recorded his own first triple-double — against Tre’s Spurs.

Traded to Chicago in February 2025. Signed a 3-year, $24M contract in June 2025. Five years in, Tre Jones is a reliable starting-caliber point guard — not a star, but a professional, the kind of player teams trust with the ball.
bio.now:
Tre is 25. Plays for the Chicago Bulls. Married Maddy Torres in 2023. Three daughters. Plans to study kinesiology, inspired by brother Jadee, who studied it in college and now trains players and coaches at Apple Valley High and Minnehaha Academy.

When their mother was diagnosed with breast cancer in 2019, Tre was a sophomore at Duke. He and Tyus came together around her fight. After she beat the disease, they turned it into a cause. Tre partnered with the Susan G. Komen Foundation. Donated proceeds from pink sneakers. Promoted the More Than Pink Walk in San Antonio. He and Tyus collaborated on family advocacy videos. The brothers raised by a single mother found a way to fight the disease that tried to take her.

He was born four years after Tyus, into the same family, the same town, the same gymnasium. He built a Duke shrine in his bedroom at ten. Heard ‘How does your game compare?’ at every turn and became the first player in ACC history to win Player of the Year and Defensive Player of the Year in the same season. Came back for a second year when his brother left after one — not because he wasn’t good enough to go, but because he wanted to lead his own team. Hit a buzzer-beater at Chapel Hill. COVID stole his March. Second-round pick who earned a starting job and a $24M contract. Got his first triple-double sixteen days after his brother got his.

The Jones family produced two NBA point guards from a North Dakota point guard who raised them alone. Tyus was the trailblazer. Tre was the one who had to walk the trail his brother blazed and make it his own. He did. The shrine told you where he was going. The ACC trophies told you he’d arrived. And the pink sneakers told you what mattered most.

highlights.note: Started 78 games; 9.2/2.8/5.6 — became reliable starting PG
15.0/4.1/7.3 in last 10 games with Bulls

nba.postCareer: Chicago Bulls; 3-year/$24M contract; Susan G. Komen Foundation advocate

The third piece of Zion/RJ/Cam — three top-10 picks from one class. Game-winner at FSU. Drafted 10th. Five NBA teams. Lithuania. Now the G-League. The heartbeat doesn’t stop.
