nba.postCareer: Still active — San Diego Clippers (G-League, 2025-26); Reddish Family Foundation (0K food drive for Norristown, 2021)

bio.road:
Cameron Elijah Reddish was born on September 1, 1999, in Norristown, Pennsylvania, a working-class suburb northwest of Philadelphia. His father, Robert Reddish, played college basketball at Virginia Commonwealth University. His mother, Zanthia, raised Cam and his younger brother Aaron in a household where basketball was the heartbeat. Robert knew his son would play in the NBA when Cam was four years old, doing things with a basketball that eight-year-olds couldn’t do.

Cam played varsity basketball as an eighth-grader and freshman at the Haverford School on Philadelphia’s Main Line, then transferred to Westtown School in West Chester, Pennsylvania. Westtown was a 600-acre Quaker boarding school with rigorous academics and a basketball coach named Seth Berger — the founder of And1, the iconic streetball shoe and apparel company. Robert Reddish knew Berger because he had coached Berger’s son T.J. in AAU. At Westtown, Cam teamed with two other future NBA players: 6’11 center Mohamed Bamba and 6’6 shooting guard Brandon Randolph. Together they won back-to-back Friends’ School League championships and Pennsylvania Independent Schools state titles.

By his senior year, Reddish averaged 22.6 points and 5.6 rebounds per game, was named Mr. Pennsylvania Basketball, and was selected for the McDonald’s All-American Game, the Jordan Brand Classic, and the Nike Hoop Summit. On the Nike EYBL circuit the previous summer, he had averaged 23.8 points, 7.6 rebounds, and 3.1 assists for Team Final. Kevin Durant, watching Reddish’s highlights on his YouTube channel, said simply: I like this kid, man. He’s going to be a star.

Reddish was the third-ranked recruit in the Class of 2018 by both ESPN and 247Sports. He chose Duke over Connecticut, Kentucky, Villanova, and UCLA. I knew from the jump I wanted to go there, he said. His mother, Zanthia, described the process: We visited all the schools together. We had countless conversations. Lots of prayer. When he decided it was Duke, we said Duke it is. At Duke, he would join fellow top-three recruits RJ Barrett and Zion Williamson to form the most hyped freshman class in college basketball history.
bio.duke:
Reddish’s freshman season at Duke in 2018–19 was defined by being the third star on a team that had two transcendent ones. Zion Williamson was the most exciting player in college basketball. RJ Barrett was the most prolific scorer. Reddish was the silky 6’8 wing who was supposed to be the third piece of the puzzle — the versatile scorer who could stretch the floor and create his own shot.

He debuted with 22 points in Duke’s 118–84 demolition of Kentucky at the Champions Classic. He scored 25 against Army, 24 against Boston College, and 22 in a comeback win over Louisville. On January 12, 2019, he hit a game-winning three-pointer in the final seconds as Duke stunned Florida State in Tallahassee. Over 36 games, he averaged 13.5 points, 3.7 rebounds, and 1.9 assists.

The shooting was inconsistent — 33 percent from three, 35.6 percent from the field overall — and Reddish would later acknowledge that playing alongside two ball-dominant stars limited his rhythm. People mistake the smoothness of my game for a lack of motor, he said. I went to Duke to grow and prepare for the NBA, and I did that. Duke reached the Elite Eight before falling to Michigan State when Zion’s shoe exploded in a loss to North Carolina that became one of the most replayed moments in college basketball history.

On draft night — June 20, 2019 — Duke made history for only the second time ever: three players from one school taken in the top ten of a single draft. Zion went first overall to New Orleans. Barrett went third to New York. Reddish went tenth to Atlanta. The last time it had happened was Florida in 2007. The Zion-RJ-Cam class remains the gold standard for one-and-done recruiting.
bio.after:
The Atlanta Hawks selected Reddish with the tenth overall pick. His rookie year showed flashes — he averaged 10.5 points per game and showed the defensive versatility that scouts had loved — but injuries began to accumulate. A core muscle injury, a right Achilles strain, and an abdominal issue limited his availability. In January 2022, the Hawks traded him to the New York Knicks.

In New York, the promise seemed to renew itself briefly. But the Knicks traded him to the Portland Trail Blazers, who waived him. He signed with the Los Angeles Lakers for the 2023–24 season under JJ Redick — a fellow Duke alumnus — and appeared in 58 games, averaging 4.4 points. In the summer of 2025, Reddish signed with Siauliai of the Lithuanian Basketball League, his first stint overseas. He left the team in December by mutual agreement for personal reasons and returned to the United States.

In January 2026, Reddish was assigned to the Austin Spurs of the NBA G-League, then traded to the San Diego Clippers. At twenty-six years old, the tenth overall pick in the 2019 draft — the kid Kevin Durant had called a star, the third member of Duke’s most celebrated freshman class — is playing in the G-League, fighting to get back to the NBA.
bio.now:
Cam Reddish is currently playing for the San Diego Clippers of the NBA G-League. He has earned approximately $30 million in his NBA career across five teams and five seasons. He has never stopped working. He has never stopped believing that the talent that made Kevin Durant call him a future star, that made him the third-ranked recruit in America, that put him in the top ten of the NBA Draft alongside Zion and RJ, is still inside him.

Back in Norristown, Reddish has never forgotten where he came from. In 2021, while playing for the Atlanta Hawks, he and the Reddish Family Foundation hosted a food drive and donated $10,000 to the municipality of Norristown. He calls basketball his heartbeat. His favorite player growing up was Allen Iverson — the Philadelphia legend, the Norristown connection, the player who played with a chip on his shoulder and an answer for everyone who doubted him.

Cam Reddish was born in Norristown, Pennsylvania, the son of a VCU basketball player who knew his boy would be an NBA player when Cam was four years old. He played at Westtown School for the founder of And1, winning back-to-back state championships alongside Mohamed Bamba. Kevin Durant watched his highlights and said he was going to be a star. He chose Duke and joined Zion Williamson and RJ Barrett to form the most celebrated freshman class in college basketball history. He hit a game-winning three at Florida State. He was drafted tenth overall — the third top-ten pick from one school in one draft, something that had happened only once before in the history of the NBA. He has played for five NBA teams. He went to Lithuania and came home. He is now in the G-League, twenty-six years old, fighting to get back. His father knew at four. Kevin Durant said it on YouTube. The smoothness was always there. The motor was always there. People mistook one for the absence of the other. Cam Reddish is still playing basketball, because basketball is his heartbeat, and a heartbeat does not stop just because the arena gets smaller.

charity.name: Norristown Recreation Center
charity.note: Home of the Cam Reddish Room. Cam donated $10,000 to create a gaming and community space for Norristown youth. Non-profit youth recreation center offering basketball leagues, camps, and community programs.

players.name: Jeremy Roach
players.tagline: Picked up a basketball at six months old. Tore his ACL in high school, came back, committed to Duke. Played four years when everyone else played one. COVID season. Coach K’s farewell Final Four. Scheyer’s first ACC title. Two-time captain. 1,469 points. Then transferred to Baylor and faced his Brotherhood in the NCAA Tournament. The bridge between two eras.
players.now: Guard, Baylor University (5th-year transfer, 2024-25); faced Duke in 2025 NCAA Tournament 2nd round
players.drafted: Undeclared — used COVID extra year at Baylor for 2024-25
players.stat: 4 Duke seasons • 130 games • 108 starts • 1,469 pts • 14.0/3.3 senior • 42.9% 3PT • 2x captain

sources.label: GoDuke.com: Jeremy Roach player bio
Arlington Catholic Herald: Meet the diocesan high schooler headed to Duke (2020)
Hoops HQ: Jeremy Roach to face his former team (2025)
Duke Chronicle: Jeremy Roach commits to Baylor (2024)
CBS Sports: Jeremy Roach enters transfer portal (2024)

nba.postCareer: 5th-year guard at Baylor University (2024-25); faced Duke in 2025 NCAA Tournament 2nd round; two USA Basketball gold medals (FIBA U-16 Americas 2017, FIBA U-17 World Cup 2018)

bio.road:
Jeremy Roach was born on November 1, 2001, in Washington, D.C., and raised in Leesburg, Virginia. His mother, Carole, says the basketball story began when Jeremy was six months old: he sat down, picked up a full-size basketball, and started throwing it in the air. He just had to have a ball, she said. He was playing organized basketball by age four with the Leesburg Basketball Club, and by the time he hit the AAU circuit with Team Takeover on the Nike EYBL, he was one of the most coveted young point guards in the country.

Roach attended St. Paul VI Catholic High School in Fairfax, Virginia — a 45-minute commute each way from Leesburg that he made every day without complaint. He became the first boys’ basketball player in school history to sign with Duke. He won a state championship as a sophomore, maintained at least a 3.1 GPA all four years, and represented the United States at the FIBA Americas U-16 Championship and the FIBA U-17 World Cup, winning gold medals both times. He was the starting point guard for the U-17 team. He was the Virginia Gatorade Player of the Year as a senior.

Then, in November 2018, during his junior year, Roach tore the anterior cruciate ligament in his right knee. It was a normal basketball move — a high ball screen, a step left, a step right, and his knee collapsed underneath him. He knew immediately what it was. The Washington Post, USA Today, and Bleacher Report all covered the injury of one of the nation’s top recruits. He came back. On May 8, 2019, he became the first five-star recruit in the Class of 2020 to announce his commitment, choosing Duke over Kentucky, North Carolina, and Villanova.
bio.duke:
Jeremy Roach played four seasons at Duke, 2020–21 through 2023–24, and lived through nearly every scenario a college basketball player can experience. His career is a time capsule of the modern era — the COVID season, the coaching transition, the one-and-done carousel, the transfer portal, and the question of what it means to stay.

Freshman year, 2020–21: the COVID season. Duke went 13–11 and missed the NCAA Tournament for the first time since 1995. For a program that measures itself by March, it was humbling. Roach averaged 8.7 points and 3.2 assists as the starting point guard.

Sophomore year, 2021–22: Coach K’s final season, and Roach was the steady hand at point guard alongside Paolo Banchero, AJ Griffin, Mark Williams, and Wendell Moore Jr. Duke went 32–7, won the ACC regular-season title, and reached the Final Four before losing to North Carolina in Coach K’s last game. Roach had 15 points in the semifinal victory over Arkansas. It was rumored that he might enter the transfer portal after the season. He stayed.

Junior year, 2022–23: Jon Scheyer’s first season as head coach. Roach was named team captain and earned the team’s MVP award. He scored a career-high 23 points in the ACC championship game win over Virginia, pouring in 19 second-half points, and matched that 23 against Oral Roberts in the NCAA Tournament. He averaged 13.6 points per game. He stayed again.

Senior year, 2023–24: captain again, the veteran anchor on a young roster. Roach averaged 14.0 points, 3.3 assists, and shot a career-best 42.9 percent from three. He surpassed 1,000 career points — the 68th player in Duke history to reach that milestone. He scored 14 second-half points against top-seeded Houston in the Sweet Sixteen. Duke reached the Elite Eight before falling to NC State. In four years, Roach had played 130 games, started 108, and compiled a record that included a Final Four, an ACC Tournament championship, an ACC regular-season title, and an Elite Eight. His senior class finished with a 65–2 record at home.

He was part of 121 victories — tied for sixth in ACC history and fifth at Duke. He played for two head coaches. He was twice named captain. He lived through the worst Duke season in a generation and one of the best coaching farewells in the history of the sport. In an era when everyone leaves after one year, Jeremy Roach stayed four.
bio.after: After four years, Roach entered the transfer portal in April 2024, using his extra COVID year. He committed to Baylor. Scheyer: “We’ve been through a lot together” — handled with love and honesty. Roach: “I am forever grateful to be part of the Brotherhood. I’ll always be a Blue Devil.”
bio.now:
After four years at Duke, Roach entered the transfer portal in April 2024, using his extra COVID year of eligibility. He committed to Baylor. Jon Scheyer said the departure was handled with love and honesty. We’ve been through a lot together, Scheyer said. On Roach’s side: I am forever grateful to be part of the Brotherhood. I’ll always be a Blue Devil.

In March 2025, the draw produced the moment everyone had been waiting for: ninth-seeded Baylor faced top-seeded Duke in the second round of the NCAA Tournament. Jeremy Roach, wearing a Baylor jersey, faced the program he had given four years of his life to. All those guys over there are my brothers, Roach said before the game. I’ll just kind of imagine that it’s some of the pickup runs we had in the summer when we were playing against each other. Duke won, advancing to the Sweet Sixteen in what would become a run to the Final Four. But Roach had played the game with the same toughness and poise he had shown for four years in Durham.

His brother Jordan played basketball at Christopher Newport University. His sister Chloe played at Eastern Mennonite University. Basketball was a family language in the Roach household, and Jeremy spoke it the loudest.

Jeremy Roach was born in Washington, D.C., and raised in Leesburg, Virginia. He picked up a basketball at six months old and never put it down. He won two gold medals with USA Basketball before he was old enough to drive. He tore his ACL as a high school junior, came back, and became the first five-star in his class to commit to Duke. He played four years when everyone else played one. He lived through the COVID season, Coach K’s farewell, and Scheyer’s beginning. He was captain twice. He scored 1,469 points. He hit the biggest shots in the biggest games — 19 second-half points in the ACC championship, 14 in the second half against Houston in the Sweet Sixteen. He stayed when staying was harder than leaving. Then he left with grace, transferred to Baylor, and faced his Brotherhood on the tournament stage. Scheyer said they had been through a lot together. Roach said he would always be a Blue Devil. In an era defined by departures, Jeremy Roach is the player who proved that four years at Duke still means something. He was the bridge between two eras, and bridges are built to last.

players.name: Paolo Banchero
players.tagline: #1 pick. Coach K’s last lottery star.
players.now: Franchise player, Orlando Magic; 2x All-Star; NBA ROY 2023; Italian & U.S. dual citizen
players.stat: 1 Duke season • 17.2 PPG/7.8 RPG/3.2 APG • ACC Freshman of Year • Final Four • NBA ROY

bio.road:
There was a photograph hanging in the basement of the Banchero family home in Seattle. A USA Basketball team photo from the 1990s. One of the players was Paolo’s mother. His bedroom was in the basement. He saw it every day. ‘That was literally one of my biggest dreams as a kid, to play for USA.’

Paolo Banchero was born November 12, 2002, in Seattle. His mother, Rhonda Smith-Banchero, played college basketball at Washington, where she left as the all-time leading scorer with 2,948 points. She was the first Washington player drafted by the WNBA (3rd round, Sacramento Monarchs, 2000). Played professionally in the ABL and overseas. Husky Hall of Fame 2004. Became the basketball coach at Holy Names Academy in Seattle.

His father, Mario, played tight end at Washington. Mario’s Italian ancestors immigrated from northern Italy in the early 1900s and settled in Black Diamond, Washington — a small mining town in the Cascade foothills — working as farmers and coal miners. Rhonda and Mario met at Washington, married July 31, 1999. Three children: Paolo, daughter Mia (soccer player), youngest son Giulio (signed for Eastern Washington football). With a father named Mario, an uncle named Angelo, and a brother named Giulio: ‘I like having a unique name. When you think of Paolo, you think of me.’

Rhonda made him practice with the girls’ team at Holy Names to keep him humble. ‘She’s always been the one to humble me. Never too high, never too low.’ He never saw her play — she’d retired three years before he was born — but watched enough tape to recognize her game in his own. They watched WNBA games together.

In February 2020, he received Italian citizenship. Italy offered him a spot on their senior national team before college. He’d been cut from USA Basketball youth teams, which left him frustrated. But the photo in the basement kept pulling him back. He visited Italy for the first time in 2023: ‘You know how you get butterflies? It was like that. I was so proud.’

At O’Dea High School (his father’s school): backup QB on the state championship football team as a freshman; led basketball to the 3A state title as a sophomore. Washington Gatorade POY. Most expected him to stay home at Washington. Instead he committed to Duke, 2,341 miles away.
bio.duke:
Banchero arrived for Coach K’s final season, 2021–22 — the farewell tour. He was its centerpiece. College debut: 22 points vs Kentucky. Then 28 vs The Citadel, 21 vs Gonzaga, 23 vs Virginia Tech. Averaged 17.2 PPG, 7.8 RPG, 3.2 APG. ACC Freshman of the Year. Third-Team All-American. Physical, skilled, poised — a 6-10 forward with point-guard passing and a scorer’s mentality.

Duke went 32–7 and reached the Final Four. The semifinal against North Carolina — Duke’s greatest rival — was supposed to be the storybook ending for Krzyzewski. It wasn’t. Carolina won 81–77. Banchero had 20 points and 10 rebounds in his final college game. Coach K’s career ended one round short of the championship.
bio.after:
Draft night, June 23, 2022: every mock predicted the Magic would take Jabari Smith Jr. Instead they called Banchero’s name. The surprise was visible on his face. He made doubters look foolish immediately: 27 points, 9 rebounds, 5 assists in his debut vs Detroit — the first player since LeBron to post 25/5/5 in an NBA debut. Scored 20+ forty times as a rookie. NBA Rookie of the Year 2022–23 — the first Magic player to win since Shaquille O’Neal in 1993.

Summer 2023: chose the USA photo over the Italian passport. Represented Team USA at the FIBA World Cup. Led the team with 21 points in 19 minutes vs New Zealand. Americans lost to Germany in the semifinals by two points.

By his second season: All-Star. By his third: the franchise. The Magic built around him alongside Franz Wagner and Jalen Suggs. Averaged 28+ early in 2024–25 before an oblique injury. Everything in Orlando runs through Paolo.
bio.now:
Paolo Banchero is 22. Franchise player of the Orlando Magic. In November 2025, he joined the ownership group of the Seattle Seawolves rugby team — a hometown investment. He also holds a stake in AC Milan, bridging his two identities across an ocean. He carries an Italian passport and wears the American flag. He told his father Italy needs to be a regular family trip.

Rhonda still coaches girls’ basketball at Holy Names Academy and works at a nonprofit serving homeless people. Mario has served on Seattle district councils. Mia plays soccer. Giulio signed for Eastern Washington football. The Banchero family is what happens when Italian immigrants who mined coal in Black Diamond marry into a lineage of African American athletes from Franklin High School — and their children grow up bilingual in competition, humble enough to practice with the girls’ team and proud enough to carry two passports.

He was born in Seattle. His mother was the greatest scorer in Huskies women’s history. His great-grandparents dug coal out of the Cascades. He was three feet tall at fifteen months. His mother made him practice with the girls’ team. He got Italian citizenship at seventeen and almost played for Italy because USA Basketball had cut him. But there was a photograph in the basement where he slept, and it became the biggest dream of his childhood.

He chose Duke because Coach K was the greatest. He was the centerpiece of Krzyzewski’s final season. Lost to Carolina in the Final Four. Surprise #1 pick. Scored 27/9/5 in his debut. Rookie of the Year. Chose the red, white, and blue over the green, white, and red because of a photograph in a basement. He is 22 years old and he is the future of the Orlando Magic — the kid with two countries, two passports, two parents who played Division I sports at the same university, and one photograph that told him exactly who he was going to become.

highlights.note: NBA Rookie of the Year; 20.0/6.9/3.7
NBA All-Star; 28.8/8.8/5.4 in 34 games before torn oblique
23.9/8.0/5.8 in last 10 games; franchise cornerstone

nba.postCareer: Franchise player, Orlando Magic; 2x All-Star; NBA ROY 2023; Italian & U.S. dual citizen

players.name: AJ Griffin
players.tagline: Born into an NBA family. Shot on regulation hoops at age two while NBA players stopped to watch. Part of Coach K’s final season. 16th pick. Game-winning alley-oops as a rookie with his father coaching on the opposing bench. Then retired at 21 to follow Jesus. The Brotherhood’s most unexpected story.
players.now: Retired (Sept 2024, age 21); pursuing full-time Christian ministry; youngest voluntary retirement by an NBA first-round pick
players.stat: 1 Duke season • 10.4 PPG • .493/.447 shooting • ACC All-Freshman • 16th pick • 92 NBA games • Retired at 21

sources.label: CBS Sports: AJ Griffin explains why he gave up basketball to follow Jesus (2024)
BET: AJ Griffin Quits the NBA to Follow Jesus (2024)
EssentiallySports: Who Is AJ Griffin? (2024)
GoDuke.com: AJ Griffin player bio
Wikipedia: AJ Griffin (basketball)

teams.note: Rookie: 72 games, 8.9 PPG, 39% 3PT; two game-winning alley-oops
Traded June 2024; retired before playing a game

highlights.note: Game-winning alley-oop vs Toronto (Nov 19) with father coaching opposing bench
Game-winning alley-oop vs Chicago (Dec 11)
Retired September 29, 2024, at age 21 to pursue full-time Christian ministry

nba.postCareer: Retired at 21 (Sept 2024); pursuing full-time Christian ministry; youngest voluntary retirement by an NBA first-round pick

bio.road:
Adrian Darnell Griffin Jr. was born August 25, 2003, in Dallas, Texas. His father Adrian Griffin Sr. played ten NBA seasons after going undrafted from Seton Hall in 1996 — a journeyman wing who started in the 2006 NBA Finals for the Mavericks. His mother Aubrey Sterling was an All-American track athlete at Seton Hall. His brother Alan played at Illinois and Syracuse. His sister Aubrey played at UConn. Basketball and athletic excellence ran through every branch.

At age two, while other kids played with kiddie hoops, AJ took an NBA regulation basketball, cocked it behind his head, and launched it at a regulation net. The first time it went in, everyone thought it was cute. Then he kept doing it. The whole team stopped to watch. “His strength at that age was unbelievable,” his father said. “He just loved basketball.”

At Archbishop Stepinac in White Plains, NY, he played alongside his brother Alan as a freshman, winning the school’s first CHSAA title since 1984. As a sophomore: 20.9/10.9/3.9/3.5 blocks, forming one of the nation’s top backcourts with R.J. Davis. In 7th and 8th grade at Ossining, he played varsity alongside future NBA player Obi Toppin. Five-star recruit, projected top-five pick.

He committed to Duke in November 2019 over Kentucky and Villanova. But in 2020, during COVID lockdowns, AJ gave his life to Christ. “My values started to change. My heart started to change.” The transformation that would eventually lead him away from basketball had already begun.
bio.duke:
Griffin’s one season at Duke, 2021–22, was Coach K’s final year. He was part of the farewell team that included Paolo Banchero, Mark Williams, Wendell Moore Jr., Trevor Keels, and Jeremy Roach. Griffin started 25 of 39 games, averaging 10.4 points on 49.3 percent from the field and 44.7 percent from three — the fifth-highest three-point percentage in Duke history and second-best by a Duke freshman. He scored 12.6 points per game in ACC play on 48.9 percent shooting.

Duke went 32–7, won the ACC regular-season and tournament championships, and reached the Final Four in Coach K’s final season before falling to North Carolina in one of the most emotionally devastating games in program history. Griffin was named to the ACC All-Freshman Team and earned Honorable Mention All-ACC honors. He declared for the 2022 NBA Draft.
bio.after:
The Atlanta Hawks selected Griffin with the 16th overall pick in the 2022 NBA Draft — part of a record-setting draft that saw five Duke players selected. His rookie season was promising: 72 games, 8.9 points per game, 39 percent from three in 20 minutes per contest. He hit game-winning alley-oops in consecutive November and December games against Toronto and Chicago — the Toronto game-winner coming with his father, then a Raptors assistant coach, on the opposing bench. The image of the father watching his son hit a game-winner against his own team became one of the season’s iconic moments.

His father’s career took its own dramatic turn: Adrian Griffin Sr. was named head coach of the Milwaukee Bucks in May 2023, then fired just 34 games into the season despite a 30–13 record, replaced by Doc Rivers. AJ’s second NBA season was limited — just 20 games with the Hawks before being traded to the Houston Rockets in June 2024 for the 43rd pick in the draft.

Then, on September 29, 2024, AJ Griffin shocked the basketball world. At twenty-one years old, with a guaranteed NBA contract and a career that had barely begun, he announced his retirement. I gave up basketball to follow Jesus, he said in a video posted to social media. He explained that he wanted to pursue full-time Christian ministry. You can have all the riches in the world, but the only thing that truly matters is your relationship with Jesus. He’s the only one who can completely satisfy you. He’s the only one who can give you a purpose.

He traced the decision back to 2020, when he gave his life to Christ during the pandemic. Basketball had been his reason for living — the son of an NBA player, the brother of college athletes, the kid who shot on regulation hoops at age two. But faith had given him something basketball could not. The decision was met with surprise, respect, and in some corners, disbelief. No first-round pick had ever voluntarily retired this young.
bio.now:
AJ Griffin is pursuing full-time Christian ministry. He is twenty-one years old. He earned approximately $8 million in his two NBA seasons. He plans to share the Gospel through social media and in-person ministry work. Thank you to everyone who has been a part of this journey, he wrote. As I step out of one season of my life, I’m excited to step into a new one with my Lord and Savior.

AJ Griffin was born into an NBA family — father a ten-year player and head coach, mother an All-American, brother at Illinois and Syracuse, sister at UConn. He shot on regulation hoops at age two while NBA players stopped to watch. He played varsity as a seventh-grader alongside Obi Toppin. He committed to Duke and was part of Coach K’s final season, hitting threes at a rate that ranks among the best in Duke history. He was drafted 16th overall and hit game-winning alley-oops as a rookie with his father coaching on the opposing bench. He had every reason to keep playing. The money, the fame, the bloodline, the talent — all of it pointed toward a long NBA career. And then, at twenty-one, he walked away. Not because of injury. Not because of failure. Because he found something he wanted more than basketball. The Brotherhood includes players who gave everything to the game. AJ Griffin is the first who gave the game back to give everything to something else. He found me, Griffin said, smiling. It was the best decision of my life.

players.name: Gary Trent Jr.
players.tagline: Raised in NBA locker rooms. Trained at 3 a.m. by his father. A second-round pick who became a $54 million man.
players.now: Guard, Milwaukee Bucks
players.stat: 14.5 ppg at Duke (40.2% 3PT); 7 NBA seasons, $70M+ career earnings

bio.road:
Gary Dajuan Trent Jr. was born in Columbus, Ohio, on January 18, 1999, into a basketball family that had already lived the entire arc of the American hoops dream. His father, Gary Trent Sr., grew up in grinding poverty in Columbus — his mother dropping him off for his first job at 3:17 a.m. by the clock on her Cadillac. The elder Trent fought his way to Ohio University, where he earned the nickname “The Shaq of the MAC,” was named conference Player of the Year three times, and was drafted 11th overall by the Milwaukee Bucks in 1995. He played nine NBA seasons, averaging 8.6 points and 4.5 rebounds, with stops in Milwaukee, Dallas, Indiana, Toronto, and Minnesota. His mother Roxanne Holt and his stepmother Natalia both shaped his upbringing. His aunt, Tia Trent, was a two-time All-American sprinter at Indiana.

Gary Jr. grew up in NBA locker rooms. He was around Kevin Garnett as a child. His father began training him at age five, asking the boy if he was serious about basketball. When Gary said yes, the regimen started. At age six, he dribbled a basketball for a mile around a track while doing push-ups and sit-ups at each corner. At seven, he was scoring 30 points per game in youth league. By the time he reached high school, he was waking his father at 1 a.m. to go shoot in the gym — after already completing two or three workouts that day. The Trents’ social media was full of posts from 3 a.m. gym sessions.

At Apple Valley High School in Minnesota, Trent Jr. paired with Tre Jones — the younger brother of Tyus Jones, who had just led Duke to the 2015 national championship. Together, Trent and Tre Jones led Apple Valley to the Minnesota Class 4A state championship in 2015. By his junior year, Trent was averaging 26.4 points and 5.8 rebounds and was named Minnesota Player of the Year.

He transferred to Prolific Prep in Napa, California, for his senior year, where he averaged 31.8 points and 6.4 rebounds while shooting 39.3% from three. He was ranked #8 nationally by ESPN and the #1 shooting guard in the 2017 class. He committed to Duke on November 10, 2016, choosing the Blue Devils over Kentucky, UCLA, and Michigan State. His father called it “the greatest decision of his life so far.”
bio.duke:
Trent arrived at Duke for the 2017-18 season as part of a loaded freshman class that included Marvin Bagley III, Wendell Carter Jr., and Trevon Duval. The team was ranked #1 in the preseason and started four freshmen. In that lineup, Trent was the marksman — the player whose gravity from three-point range opened up driving lanes for everyone else.

He set a Duke freshman record with 97 made three-pointers, shooting 40.2% from beyond the arc — the best percentage in the ACC that season. He also led the team from the free-throw line at 87.6%, a rare combination of efficiency for an 18-year-old. His 14.5 points per game ranked third on the team behind Bagley and Grayson Allen, and he was one of three Blue Devils to start all 37 games.

The highlights were vivid. On December 9, 2017, he scored 25 points against Boston College, tying the Duke freshman record with six three-pointers. On January 15, 2018, he scored a career-high 30 points in a comeback victory over Miami, hitting shot after shot in the second half. On January 29, he posted 22 points and 10 rebounds against Notre Dame — showing he was more than just a shooter.

Duke entered the NCAA Tournament as the #2 seed in the Midwest. Trent was named to the All-Region team as the Blue Devils beat Iona, Rhode Island, and Syracuse to reach the Elite Eight. But Kansas, led by Devont’e Graham and Svi Mykhailiuk, ended Duke’s season in overtime. Afterward, all five Duke starters declared for the NBA Draft.

Krzyzewski had said during recruitment: “He’s strong and can score the ball. But he can also defend. That’s the thing that impressed me — his willingness to cover the best perimeter player on another team while still putting up points.” The Prolific Prep founder called him “the epitome of what you want an all-around leader to be.”
bio.after:
The Sacramento Kings selected Trent 37th overall in the 2018 NBA Draft and immediately traded him to the Portland Trail Blazers. Being a second-round pick meant no guaranteed contract and no safety net. His father’s words echoed: “This 15-year internship just got you into the country club. Now you’ve got to work 100 times harder to stay.”

Trent took the advice literally. After a quiet rookie season (15 games, 2.7 ppg), he broke out in year two, shooting 41.8% from three — emerging as one of the best young perimeter shooters in the league. By his third year in Portland, he was averaging 15.0 points and establishing himself as a legitimate NBA rotation player.

In March 2021, Toronto acquired Trent in a trade for Norman Powell. The move was a gamble for the Raptors — and it paid off spectacularly. In his first full season in Toronto, Trent went on a historic scoring run: five consecutive 30-point games (joining DeMar DeRozan as the only Raptor to achieve that), a season-high 42 points against Houston, and nine three-pointers against Phoenix. He scored 44 points on 17-of-19 shooting against Cleveland — one of the most efficient scoring performances in NBA history. The Raptors signed him to a three-year, $54 million deal.

After three seasons in Toronto, Trent signed with the Milwaukee Bucks in July 2024. In the 2025 NBA Playoffs, he erupted for 37 points in Game 3 of the first round, hitting nine three-pointers to tie a Bucks playoff record. Milwaukee re-signed him to a two-year deal in July 2025. He continues to be one of the league’s most dangerous perimeter scorers.
bio.now:
Gary Trent Jr. is 27 years old and in his eighth NBA season, playing for the Milwaukee Bucks alongside Giannis Antetokounmpo and Damian Lillard. He has earned over $70 million in career salary. He has played in 400+ NBA games and established himself as one of the premier three-point shooters of his generation.

His story is the basketball version of a family business. A father who grew up with nothing, made the NBA, and then poured every ounce of what he learned into his son. A kid who was dribbling for miles at age six and shooting at 3 a.m. as a teenager. A second-round pick who outworked his draft position and turned himself into a $54 million player.

The Duke connection runs deep: Trent played high school ball with Tre Jones, who followed him to Durham a year later. They won a state championship together in Minnesota and both became NBA players. His father’s advice before the Duke commitment endures as one of the great pieces of basketball wisdom a parent has ever given a child: “First, go to a basketball school. Secondly, don’t make it a basketball decision. This is a business decision.”

Gary Trent Sr. once said: “You cannot find a child in this draft who has logged more gym hours, more film hours, and more basketball conversation hours.” Seven years into his NBA career, the investment is still paying dividends.

highlights: 37 pts, 9 threes in 2025 playoff game (Bucks record)
2nd-highest plus-minus in NBA history (+54) vs Golden State
nba.postCareer: Active — Milwaukee Bucks guard

players.name: Jalen Johnson
players.tagline: Thirteen games. $150 million. The Duke career that almost wasn’t — and the NBA career that proved everyone wrong.
players.now: Starting forward, Atlanta Hawks; 2026 NBA All-Star; $150M contract
players.stat: 22.9 ppg, 10.5 rpg, 7.9 apg (2025-26); 2026 All-Star; 13 games at Duke

bio.road:
Jalen Tyrese Johnson was born in Wausau, Wisconsin, and raised in Sun Prairie — a Milwaukee suburb about as far from the AAU circuit spotlight as you can get. But basketball was in the blood. His father Roderick played at UW-Milwaukee and Southeast Missouri State before spending two years as a professional in Poland. His mother Stacy also played college basketball at UW-Milwaukee. His brother Rod played at Chattanooga. The Johnsons were a basketball family, and Roderick became Jalen’s primary coach from childhood.

At Sun Prairie High School, Johnson was immediately dominant — averaging 15.2 points, 6.2 rebounds, and 2.1 assists as a freshman. By his junior year, after transferring to Nicolet High School in Glendale, Wisconsin, he was the state’s best player: Wisconsin AP Player of the Year, Wisconsin Gatorade Player of the Year, and a consensus five-star recruit ranked 13th nationally in the 2020 class. He led Nicolet to the school’s first-ever state basketball championship in 2019.

Then the path got complicated. Johnson transferred to IMG Academy in Bradenton, Florida, for his senior season — the finishing school for elite recruits. But he left IMG mid-year without explanation, never playing a game for the academy. The move raised eyebrows. When he committed to Duke on July 4, 2019, he was still ranked as a top-five prospect nationally, but the narrative around him had shifted. Was he a can’t-miss talent? Or was there a pattern of not finishing what he started?

He chose Duke anyway, joining a 2020 freshman class that arrived under the strangest possible circumstances: a global pandemic, no fans in Cameron Indoor, and a program about to have its worst season in decades.
bio.duke:
Johnson’s Duke career lasted exactly 13 games. In any other context, that would be a footnote. But those 13 games were enough to show that his talent was real — and that something about the situation wasn’t working.

His debut against Coppin State on November 28, 2020, was staggering: 19 points, 19 rebounds, five assists, and four blocked shots. He became only the third Duke freshman ever to record a double-double in his first game. He earned ACC Freshman of the Week honors twice. On January 19, 2021, he dropped 24 points, 16 rebounds, and 7 assists against Pittsburgh — the kind of stat line that screams future All-Star.

But the 2020-21 Blue Devils were struggling. The team went 13-11 overall and 9-9 in the ACC — the worst season of Krzyzewski’s career. COVID protocols had emptied Cameron Indoor. The chemistry was off. Johnson’s minutes fluctuated: he played just eight minutes in his final game against NC State before the announcement came.

On February 15, 2021, Johnson announced he was leaving Duke mid-season to prepare for the NBA Draft. It was the first time a Duke player had ever opted out in the middle of a season. The reaction was polarized: some saw a smart business decision by a player whose draft stock was set. Others saw a pattern — IMG, now Duke — of a supremely talented player who walked away when things got hard.

His 13-game Duke stat line was tantalizing: 11.2 points, 6.1 rebounds, 2.2 assists on 52.3% shooting and a surprising 44.4% from three (on limited attempts). A per-40-minutes projection of 21 points, 11 rebounds, and 4 assists. The talent was never in question. The commitment was.

Duke Basketball Report captured the tension perfectly before the draft: “Talent is not what people are questioning about Johnson. He barely played in his senior year of high school and again at Duke. Can he guard Luka? Kawhi? Embiid? How much heart does he have? What’s going to happen when 30-year-old men knock him on his ass and tell him they’re about to do it again?”
bio.after:
The Atlanta Hawks selected Johnson 20th overall in the 2021 NBA Draft — lower than his talent warranted, higher than his track record suggested. His early years in Atlanta required patience. He appeared in just 22 games as a rookie, splitting time between the NBA roster and the College Park Skyhawks in the G League. He underwent non-surgical knee procedures. He averaged 2.4 points in limited minutes. The doubters felt vindicated.

In 2022-23, Johnson earned a more regular bench role, averaging 5.6 points and 4.0 rebounds in 70 games. Progress, but modest. Then came 2023-24 — and the breakout everyone had either predicted or given up on. Starting 52 of 56 games, Johnson averaged 16.0 points, 8.7 rebounds, and 3.6 assists with 51.1% shooting. He was one of the leading candidates for Most Improved Player before a torn labrum in his left shoulder ended his season in January 2025.

Before the injury, the Hawks had seen enough. In October 2024, Atlanta signed Johnson to a five-year, $150 million contract extension. The kid who quit Duke after 13 games was now worth $30 million a year.
bio.now:
The 2025-26 season has established Jalen Johnson as one of the best young players in basketball. He is averaging 22.9 points, 10.5 rebounds, and 7.9 assists per game — numbers that put him in the conversation with the league’s elite do-everything forwards. He has recorded 39 double-doubles and 8 triple-doubles, setting the Hawks franchise record for triple-doubles in a single season. In February 2026, he was named to his first NBA All-Star team as an Eastern Conference reserve.

On November 13, 2025, Johnson recorded one of the most remarkable stat lines in NBA history: 31 points, 18 rebounds, 14 assists, and 7 steals against the Utah Jazz. On December 5, he posted the fastest triple-double in Hawks history and the second-fastest in NBA history against Denver: 21 points, 18 rebounds, 16 assists. On December 18 — his 24th birthday — he scored a career-high 43 points against Charlotte.

He is 24 years old, locked into $150 million through 2030, and playing alongside Trae Young in one of the most exciting two-man games in the NBA. He has founded the Jalen Johnson Foundation, a nonprofit dedicated to youth mentorship and education. His brother Kobe also plays for the Hawks.

Jalen Johnson’s Brotherhood story is unlike anyone else’s. Thirteen games at Duke — the shortest meaningful career in the project. A mid-season departure that was criticized, questioned, and debated. And then: a $150 million contract, an All-Star selection, and a stat line that suggests he’s only beginning to scratch the surface.

The question Duke Basketball Report posed before the draft has been answered. What happens when 30-year-old men knock him on his ass? He gets up, scores 43 points, and signs for $150 million. The heart was never the problem. The 13 games at Duke just weren’t enough time to show it.

highlights: Hawks franchise record: most triple-doubles in a season (8+)
Fastest triple-double in Hawks history vs Denver
nba.postCareer: Active — Atlanta Hawks starter; Jalen Johnson Foundation (youth mentorship)

players.name: Spencer Hubbard
players.tagline: The smallest Blue Devil with the biggest heart.
players.now: Duke graduate (African American Studies); pursuing career in basketball/sports
players.stat: 28 games, 0.7 ppg, 40% from three; viral MSG three-pointer

bio.road:
Spencer Hubbard grew up in Los Angeles, the son of Michael and Beth Semans Hubbard. His mother was a Duke alumna. His brother Ryan would become a close companion. His uncle, Joe Hubbard, spent time on the football coaching staffs at USC, UNLV, and the Los Angeles Rams — so the family understood what it meant to be around elite athletics. But Spencer was not built like an elite athlete. He was 5-foot-8 and weighed about 157 pounds. What he had was something harder to measure.

At Harvard-Westlake School — one of the most prestigious private schools in Los Angeles, known for producing actors, tech founders, and the occasional Division I athlete — Hubbard was a four-year varsity basketball player and a three-year starter. He earned Open Division All-CIF honors as a senior and was a three-time All-Mission League selection, named honorable mention as a sophomore and second-team as both a junior and senior. He averaged 13.0 points and 4.0 assists per game as a senior, with career averages of 11.6 points and 3.0 assists. He helped lead Harvard-Westlake to Mission League titles as both a junior and senior and won a CIF Division I championship as a freshman.

He was not unrecruited. There was some Division I interest. He could have been a scholarship player at a smaller school and gotten real minutes, real stats, a real college basketball career by any conventional measure. But Spencer Hubbard did not want a conventional career. He wanted Duke. His mother had gone there. He had grown up watching the Blue Devils. And so he made a choice that most people in his position would never make: he applied to Duke as a regular student, with no athletic scholarship, no walk-on guarantee, and no promise of ever wearing the uniform.

“I wasn’t getting a ton of Division I looks but there was some interest,” he later said. “I really just loved the game and it’s been great these last five years.”
bio.duke:
Hubbard arrived at Duke in the fall of 2020, in the middle of the COVID-19 pandemic, and joined the basketball program as a member of the practice squad. Not a walk-on. Not on the roster. A practice player — the lowest rung of the ladder, the person who shows up every day to get scored on by future NBA lottery picks so they can prepare for real opponents. There are no cameras, no crowd, no box score entries. Just work.

By his sophomore year, 2021–22 — Coach K’s final season, the year of Paolo Banchero, AJ Griffin, and Trevor Keels — Hubbard had earned a walk-on spot. He appeared in six games across his first two roster seasons, totaling one point, two rebounds, and one steal in 15 minutes. The numbers were irrelevant. What mattered was that he was there, in the locker room in the depths of Cameron Indoor Stadium, part of the team.

His real contribution was in practice, where his 5-foot-8 frame, low center of gravity, and deceptive quickness made him a legitimate problem for Duke’s scholarship guards. Isaiah Evans, the five-star freshman, admitted it plainly: “You will look at him and think he’s a small guy, he can’t score — that’s the last thing you need to think. I promise you, if we would release practice footage of him cooking us on scout team, he would probably get a scholarship somewhere, I’ll be honest.”

In September 2023, three years after arriving on campus and paying his own way at one of the most expensive universities in America, Jon Scheyer called the team together in the practice facility. He began talking about Hubbard’s competitive desire, his daily commitment, the way he made everyone around him better. Then Scheyer said the words: “I just want you guys to know he’s not going to be a walk-on anymore.” Before he could finish the sentence, the room erupted. Hubbard’s teammates mobbed him. Duke posted the video to social media. It went viral. Spencer Hubbard, the 5-foot-8 practice squad player from Harvard-Westlake, was now a full scholarship athlete at Duke University.

“He’s shown up every day, for years at a time,” Scheyer told the team. “And he’s as competitive as anybody. He pisses you guys off more, going against him in practice.”

After graduating with a degree in African American Studies, Hubbard came back for a fifth-year graduate season in 2024–25 — the year of Cooper Flagg, Kon Knueppel, and Khaman Maluach. The team that would reach the Final Four. And it was during this final season that Spencer Hubbard had his moments.

On February 22, 2025, at Madison Square Garden, with Duke routing Illinois 110–67, Hubbard checked in to a standing ovation. He got the ball at the top of the three-point line, set his feet, and let it fly. It went in. The bench exploded. Cooper Flagg flexed, grabbed Tyrese Proctor around the waist, and found Khaman Maluach for a chest bump. Kon Knueppel jumped up and down like he was on a trampoline. Sion James turned to the ceiling and roared. The Bleacher Report video got over 500,000 likes on TikTok.

“It was surreal,” Hubbard said. “It makes it even more memorable in the context of MSG. I mean, all of the history in that place when it comes to the musicians that have played there and the basketball part of it.”

A week later, in Cameron Indoor against Florida State, he topped it: a fast-break layup followed by a three-pointer for a career-high five points. The Cameron Crazies — who were known to boo if Hubbard wasn’t dressed to play — went berserk. Scheyer, asked about it afterward, said simply: “The thing is, Spencer can really play. He can really play. So at the end, I’m not surprised at all when he makes those plays.”

Then came March. The NCAA Tournament. Hubbard saw action against Mount St. Mary’s in the first round, scoring two points and logging an assist as the crowd chanted his name. It was his second straight year appearing in the tournament — he’d also played against Vermont and JMU in the 2024 bracket.

Sion James, asked what Hubbard meant to the team, called him “a blessing.” Mason Gillis, the Purdue transfer in his first year at Duke, said: “He’s a selfless person. He’s always thinking of other people in front of himself. So whenever he gets a little moment, you know, we want him to be successful.”

His career stat line reads: 28 games, 19 total points, 7 rebounds, 6 assists, shooting 40% from three. It tells you almost nothing about what he actually was.
bio.after:
Spencer Hubbard was not drafted. He was never going to be. His five years at Duke were not a path to the NBA — they were the thing itself. He arrived as a freshman practice squad player during a pandemic, worked his way onto the roster, earned a scholarship, became a fan favorite, hit a three-pointer at Madison Square Garden that went viral, played in the NCAA Tournament, and was part of a Final Four team.

He graduated from Duke with a degree in African American Studies. He told reporters during the 2025 tournament that his plans were to work in basketball or another sport after college. Scheyer, when asked whether Hubbard might follow the Duke tradition of former players returning as assistant coaches, smiled and said: “Let’s go one step at a time with Spence. Let’s see about winning tomorrow, and then we’ll go from there.”
bio.now:
Spencer Hubbard is 24 years old. He completed his five-year Duke career in the spring of 2025 as part of a team that reached the Final Four. He is pursuing a career in basketball or sports, leveraging the connections, discipline, and reputation he built during his half-decade in Durham.

His story is not about points scored or games started. It is about what it means to choose something hard when easier options exist, to show up every day when nobody is watching, and to be ready when the moment comes. At a program defined by one-and-done lottery picks and future Hall of Famers, Spencer Hubbard — all five-foot-eight of him — might be the most Duke story of them all.

As Coach Scheyer put it: “To see a guy that’s been here for five years — blood, sweat, and tears, practice every day, growing up — that’s what college is all about. That’s what this program has been about.”

nba.postCareer: Pursuing career in basketball/sports

players.name: Kyle Filipowski
players.tagline: Two years in the post-K era.
players.now: Utah Jazz; 4-year/$12M contract; Scheyer era’s first cornerstone
players.stat: 2 Duke seasons • 70 games • Fr: 15.1 PPG/9.0 RPG (16 double-doubles) • ACC ROY • ACC Tourney MVP • 1st-Team All-ACC

bio.road:
Kyle Jarred Filipowski was born November 7, 2003, in Middletown, New York. His family was built on basketball the way some families are built on farming — the language spoken at the dinner table, the reason the car was always pointed toward a gymnasium.

His mother, Rebecca Hagerdon Filipowski, was a 6-4 force at Warwick Valley High School who scored 2,164 career points and was a 1982 Parade All-American. She played at Long Beach State. Knee injuries ended her career; she became a coach and teacher. Her sister Beverly played at Dartmouth. Her brother Randall at Boston College. His father David played at Slippery Rock University. David and Rebecca met at Warwick Valley. Four sons: Daniel, Taylor, and twins Kyle and Matthew.

Rebecca was Kyle’s first coach. She taught fundamentals, drove to AAU tournaments, eventually left teaching to invest in her sons’ development. David retired early to attend games. The family organized around basketball like a military family around deployments.

Kyle and twin Matt were inseparable on the court, playing together at Wilbraham & Monson Academy. Matt, also 6-11, committed to Harvard. Three high schools in five years: Minisink Valley (freshman), Fordham Prep (sophomore), Wilbraham & Monson (junior/senior). Each move elevated competition. In 99 prep games: 1,712 points, 973 rebounds. 2021 Gatorade Massachusetts POY. Naismith All-America first team. Also played baseball and water polo. Magna Cum Laude academic award. Won gold at the 2021 FIBA 3x3 U18 World Cup.

Committed to Duke July 29, 2021 — the first year of Jon Scheyer’s era. Filipowski would be the new coach’s first cornerstone.
bio.duke:
Filipowski arrived for Scheyer’s first season, 2022–23. The legend had retired. The banners hung in Cameron but the man who put them there was gone. Filipowski was asked to bridge the eras.

Freshman: 15.1 PPG, 9.0 RPG. Led all D-I freshmen with 16 double-doubles. 28 points/15 rebounds vs Pittsburgh. 22/13 vs North Carolina. In the ACC Tournament final vs Virginia: 20 and 10 — named Tournament MVP. ACC Rookie of the Year. A throwback big with modern versatility: post scoring, three-point shooting, high-post passing.

Despite being projected first round in the 2023 draft, Filipowski came back. A bet on himself and the program. In an era when the best freshmen almost always leave, he returned. Scheyer’s first cornerstone was also his most loyal.

Sophomore: 16.4 PPG, 8.3 RPG, 2.9 APG. First-Team All-ACC. Consensus second-team All-American. Career-high 30 vs Georgia Tech. Duke went 27–9.

On February 24, 2024, after Wake Forest upset Duke, the crowd stormed the court. Filipowski was struck by a rushing fan and suffered an apparent knee injury. The collision ignited a national conversation about court-storming safety. Scheyer called for a ban. Filipowski played four days later. The toughness was real.
bio.after:
Filipowski declared for the 2024 draft as a projected first-round pick. He received a green room invitation. He sat through the entire first round. His name was never called.

Reports connected the slide to concerns about his personal life — his relationship with his fiancée, Caitlin Hutchison, who is several years older, and his estrangement from the family that had organized its entire life around his basketball career. The personal swallowed the professional on draft night.

The Utah Jazz selected him 32nd overall. They stated they’d done a thorough background check and were excited regardless of the noise. He signed a 4-year, $12M contract on August 12, 2024. Rookie season: splitting time between the Jazz and their G League affiliate, the Salt Lake City Stars. Early returns: 6.3 PPG, 4.9 RPG, 1.9 APG with Utah. The tools are there. The development is ongoing.
bio.now:
Kyle Filipowski is 21 years old. He plays for the Utah Jazz. His twin brother Matt plays basketball at Harvard. His mother Rebecca, who scored 2,164 points at Warwick Valley and was his first coach, is no longer part of his daily life the way she once was. The family that organized itself around his career has fractured. The details belong to the Filipowskis. What is public is the pain.

What is also public: two seasons at Duke. 70 games. Sixteen double-doubles as a freshman — more than any freshman in D-I. ACC ROY. ACC Tournament MVP. Came back when the money said leave. First-Team All-ACC. Consensus All-American. Knocked down by a fan during a court-storming and played four days later. The first cornerstone of Scheyer’s Duke.

He was born in Middletown. His mother was a Parade All-American who taught him the game. His aunt at Dartmouth, his uncle at Boston College. He and his twin played side by side. Three high schools. Magna Cum Laude. Gold medal with USA Basketball. Chose Duke and became the best freshman in the country. Came back when he could have left. Got knocked down and got back up. Sat in the green room and watched thirty-one names called before his. Now he’s in Utah, twenty-one, building the career his family spent two decades preparing him for — navigating the distance between where he ended up and where he started. The cost of ambition is sometimes measured in that distance. Kyle Filipowski is still calculating.

highlights.note: Sophomore showing flashes as dynamic offensive threat from all three levels

nba.postCareer: Utah Jazz; 4-year/$12M contract; Scheyer era’s first cornerstone

players.name: Dereck Lively II
players.tagline: Born in Philadelphia. Raised in Bellefonte. Coached by his mother. Lost her two months before the Finals.
players.now: Dallas Mavericks; NBA Finals 2024; recovering from foot surgery
players.stat: 1 Duke season • 34 games • 5.2 PPG/5.4 RPG/2.4 BPG • 65.8% FG • 82 blocks (led all D-I freshmen)

bio.road:
Dereck Jerome Lively II was born February 12, 2004, in Philadelphia. His mother, Kathy Phillips Drysdale, was a 6-4 standout at Penn State (1988–92): 1,295 points, 717 rebounds, 89 blocks, part of the Lady Lions team that reached #1 in the AP poll in 1991. She spent 13 years with the Philadelphia 76ers in game operations. His father, Dereck Sr., was a Philadelphia-born chef — boisterous, funny, full of life. But he battled drug addiction.

In 2011, Kathy moved the family to Bellefonte, PA, near State College, for a marketing role at Penn State. On January 5, 2012, five weeks before Dereck’s eighth birthday, his father died of an overdose. The boy walked to the stairs and peered down. He saw his father on the floor, black and blue, lifeless.

Not long after, Kathy told her nine-year-old she had Hodgkin’s lymphoma. She would fight it for eleven years — through chemotherapy, a stem cell transplant, a brief remission — while coaching her son in AAU ball, driving to every tournament, and working full-time at Penn State.

A HoopGroup event gave Dereck his first exposure. Team Final (Nike EYBL) recruited him. Kathy found a documentary called We Town about Westtown School’s basketball program (Mo Bamba, Cam Reddish) and made him watch it. He agreed to attend the boarding school three hours from home. An ankle injury cost him freshman year. COVID sent him back to Bellefonte, where he worked out daily at the local YMCA with his mother, adding muscle and an outside shot. When he returned, Kathy didn’t recognize him.

Senior year: #1 recruit nationally (ESPN 100). 14 PPG/14 RPG/4.5 BPG. PAISAA state championship. McDonald’s All-American. PA Gatorade POY. Chose Duke over Kentucky, Michigan, and his mother’s Penn State.
bio.duke:
Lively arrived at Duke for the 2022–23 season as the headliner of a number-one nationally ranked recruiting class. The expectations were enormous. And then the season started, and the stat line looked nothing like what people expected from the top recruit in America. He averaged 5.2 points per game. He started 24 of 34 games. The box score was quiet. But the impact was deafening.

What Lively did at Duke was not about scoring. It was about everything else. He led all Division I freshmen with 82 blocks. He shot 65.8 percent from the field — second-best on the team — because every shot he took was at the rim, and he finished with the touch his mother had taught him on those post-move drills in Altoona. He averaged 5.4 rebounds and 1.1 assists. He was named to the ACC All-Freshman Team and the ACC All-Defensive Team. His 7-foot-7 wingspan made him a nightmare for anyone trying to attack the basket.

Coach Jon Scheyer saw what the box score couldn’t capture. Lively was the defensive anchor who allowed Duke’s perimeter players to gamble. He was the lob threat who made pick-and-roll coverage impossible. He was the communicator who organized the back line of the defense. In the ACC Tournament, Lively helped Duke win the championship. During March Madness, his averages rose to 5.8 points, 7.4 rebounds, 3.0 blocks, and 1.6 assists on 76.5 percent shooting. The deeper the tournament went, the bigger he played. Scheyer would later tell CBS Sports the summary of what Lively meant: he was the most impactful defensive player in the country by the end of the season.

Lively declared for the 2023 NBA Draft after one season. He had come to Duke to prepare for the professional game, and the preparation was complete. The raw numbers didn’t scream stardom. But every coach and scout who watched the tape understood what they were looking at: a 7-foot-1 center with a 7-foot-7 wingspan, elite rim protection, a 65 percent shooting touch, and the kind of basketball IQ that comes from being coached by a Penn State point-scorer since the sixth grade.
bio.after:
The Dallas Mavericks selected Dereck Lively II with the 12th overall pick in the 2023 NBA Draft. The pick itself was the product of an organizational gamble: Dallas had tanked the final games of the previous season to protect a first-round pick they owed New York, drawing a $750,000 fine from the league. The payoff was Lively. Luka Doncic, who had been overruled when he wanted to keep playing, quickly fell in love with his new center. The smile that lit up Doncic’s face whenever he talked about Lively told the story. After years of searching, the perennial MVP candidate finally had his lob threat and rim protector.

Lively’s rookie season: 55 games, 42 starts, 8.8 points, 6.9 rebounds, 1.4 blocks per game on a staggering 74.7 percent shooting — which would have led the entire NBA had he qualified for the league leaders. He was named to the All-Rookie Second Team. Tyson Chandler, the former Mavericks champion and Defensive Player of the Year, worked with Lively at practices throughout the season and marveled at how quickly the rookie grasped the dirty-work details that had taken Chandler years to learn. Dallas’s defensive rating jumped from 21st to 6th in the NBA. Lively was a primary reason why.

His mother was there. Kathy Drysdale had left her position at Penn State in November 2023 and moved to Dallas to be with her son during his first NBA season. She sat behind the bench for the early games. She attended community functions. She was doing what she had always done — being present, being his biggest fan and his toughest critic. But the Hodgkin’s lymphoma that she had battled for eleven years, through chemotherapy and a stem cell transplant and a brief remission in 2022, was winning.

On April 12, 2024, Kathy Phillips Drysdale died in Dallas. She was 53 years old. She was surrounded by her son, her two sisters, and members of the Mavericks organization. She had fought the disease for eleven years — long enough to see her son walk to the edge of the stairs in Bellefonte, lose his father, learn the game in a YMCA gym, bloom at Westtown, commit to Duke, play in the ACC Tournament championship, hear his name called on draft night, and step onto an NBA court. Before she died, she left her son a message: “Dear Dereck, you made it. Persevered, but you are not done yet. You’re not even close to being done yet. I love you.”

Dereck wrote the tribute on Instagram that evening. Then he went to the arena. Jason Kidd said Lively had come to shootaround that morning to address his teammates — his second family — about his mother’s declining health, before she passed that afternoon. The Mavericks held a moment of silence. The crowd roared its support. Derrick Jones Jr. called him “lil bro” and said the team would go to bat for him any day, even though Lively was a lot taller than everyone else.

Two months later, Dereck Lively II was playing in the NBA Finals. Dallas had fought through the Western Conference playoffs — Clippers, Thunder, Timberwolves — and Lively had been magnificent. He averaged 11.5 points and 11.3 rebounds in four consecutive wins bridging the Western Conference semifinals and finals. Against Oklahoma City in the deciding Game 6, the Mavericks outscored the Thunder by 26 points when Lively was on the floor. He was the hero of that closeout game.

In the Finals against Boston, down 3–0, Dallas faced elimination in Game 4 at home. Midway through the second quarter, Luka Doncic drove into the paint, drew Jayson Tatum, and found Lively alone in the right corner. Lively hadn’t attempted a three-pointer the entire postseason. He hadn’t attempted one since November. He had taken only 13 in his entire Duke career. He caught the pass, dipped it below his knees, rose, and launched a gorgeous arching moonshot that splashed through nothing but net. The arena erupted. Dallas never trailed again, winning 122–84 in the third-biggest blowout in NBA Finals history. Lively finished with 11 points and 12 rebounds — his second straight double-double in the Finals, making him the first rookie to accomplish that since Magic Johnson in 1980.

After the game, a reporter asked about the three-pointer. Lively’s answer was simple. His mother helped him make it. She was going to help him make a lot more. She helped him make the free throws. She helped him make the reads. She was above him, and she was guiding the ball.

Boston won the series in five games. But Lively’s rookie postseason — 21 games, 7.9 points, 7.4 rebounds, played with a poise and soul of someone much older — announced that Dallas had found its center for the next decade.
bio.now:
Dereck Lively II is 21 years old. He plays for the Dallas Mavericks. In his second NBA season, 2024–25, he started 25 of 32 games and averaged 9.1 points, 7.8 rebounds, and 2.6 assists before a stress fracture in his right ankle in January 2025 shut him down for several months. In December 2025, he underwent foot surgery and was ruled out for the remainder of the season. The injuries are the kind of setback that tests a young big man’s patience. Lively has been tested before.

He is an only child. His father died when he was seven. His mother was diagnosed with cancer when he was nine. She fought it through his entire childhood, his high school career, his year at Duke, and his first months in the NBA. She moved to Dallas to watch him play, and she died two months before he played in the NBA Finals. He wore a jersey with her name on it. The Mavericks honored her with a moment of silence. Luka Doncic posted a tribute. Kyrie Irving shared the video. The whole organization embraced the kid from Bellefonte whose mother had scored 1,295 points at Penn State and coached him in a YMCA gym while undergoing chemotherapy.

Kathy Drysdale is survived by her son, her mother Judy Phillips, and her sisters Lisa Parker and Pattie Gardner. She left Penn State in November 2023 after more than a decade of service — marketing manager for women’s volleyball, wrestling, and softball, Director of Fan Experience, a presence who left her mark on nearly every sport in the department. Her former coach at Penn State, Carolyn Kieger, called her one of the legends who put sacrifice and energy into making the program what it is today. Her friends said she approached cancer the way she approached a game: black and white, game plan, no doubt she was going to win. For eleven years, she did.

Dereck Lively II grew up in Bellefonte, Pennsylvania, a town most people have never heard of. He was born in Philadelphia. He lost his father at seven. He lost his mother at twenty. He was coached by the toughest coach he ever had — and the first. He watched a documentary about Westtown School on Amazon because his mother told him to sit down and watch it. He went to the YMCA every day during COVID because she said three-dimensional players work on their outside shot. He chose Duke over her Penn State because the Brotherhood was the right fit, and she understood. He blocked 82 shots as a freshman. He was drafted 12th. He made the NBA Finals as a rookie. He hit a three-pointer he had no business taking because he felt his mother guide it through the rim.

The Mavericks’ official statement after Kathy’s death said it best: so much of Kathy — her strength, tenacity, and joy — is reflected in her son. Every game Dereck Lively II plays is a game played for two. His mother knew he’d made it. She told him so. She also told him he wasn’t even close to being done yet. He’s not. He’s twenty-one years old, and there are so many more three-pointers to guide through the rim.

players.name: Jared McCain
players.tagline: Three shots on a ten-foot hoop. TikTok star. Painted nails. Pluto energy. The kid who wouldn’t choose between basketball and everything else.
players.now: Oklahoma City Thunder (traded from 76ers Feb 2026); defending NBA champions; ~5M TikTok followers
players.stat: 1 Duke season • 36 games • 14.3 PPG • 41.4% 3PT • 35 pts vs FSU (tied Zion’s freshman record) • ACC All-Rookie • Elite Eight

bio.road:
Jared Dane McCain was born February 20, 2004, and grew up in Corona, California — a sprawling city in the Inland Empire, east of Los Angeles. He is the son of Lance and Jina McCain. His father is African American. His mother has White and partial Filipino ancestry. Jared grew up navigating multiple cultural identities in a household that valued hard work, faith, and competition.

Lance McCain played college basketball at Midland College in Texas alongside Spud Webb — the 5-7 guard who would win the 1986 NBA Slam Dunk Contest and play twelve NBA seasons. After college, Lance did not pursue professional basketball. He became a flight attendant at Southwest Airlines. Jina was a track and field athlete at Sacramento State. They were not basketball royalty. They were a working family from the Inland Empire who happened to raise a basketball prodigy.

Jared’s first dream was baseball. He has a childhood poster that reads ‘I want to be an MLB player.’ That dream ended at age four, when he was playing up against older kids without a protective cup and took a hit that sent him home in tears. He made what he later called a business decision. He dropped baseball. He also played soccer — left defender, naturally left-footed. He is ambidextrous: eats and writes left-handed. But he shoots a basketball right-handed, because he broke his left thumb as a child and started shooting with his right out of necessity. The injury that could have derailed him instead gave him his shooting hand.

His older brother Jayce, four years his senior, played at Folsom High School and Cal State San Marcos, where he started 31 games as a junior and earned All-Conference honors. Jared idolized Jayce. From age three, his single goal was to play on his brother’s team. He watched Jayce’s games and begged his parents to let him practice. Jina set the terms: ‘Once you can make it in a ten-foot hoop three times in a row, we will put you on a team.’ Jared went outside, shot relentlessly, and made his first, second, and third shots. He was on a team that year.

Jayce showed no mercy in one-on-one. The tough love turned Jared into a competitive dog. But Jayce’s career was interrupted by health issues that prevented him from going professional. The brother who made the younger one became the brother who couldn’t follow him to the highest level.

At Centennial High School in Corona: junior year 16.9 PPG, Gatorade California POY. Appeared in three seasons of Believe, a YouTube docuseries. Senior year: McDonald’s All-American. Five-star recruit. Committed to Duke March 18, 2022, over Gonzaga, Houston, Kansas, and Louisville.

But basketball was only half the phenomenon. By the time he arrived at Duke, he had millions of TikTok followers — dance videos, lip-syncs, vlogs, trending challenges. He painted his nails before games as a ritual of self-expression. He was a Disney superfan who identified with Pluto because of Pluto’s happy personality and positive energy. He had his own T-shirt line with his face on it. He was the first Duke basketball player equally famous as a social media personality and as a player. Some loved it. Some said he should focus on the game. Jared McCain didn’t see a contradiction.
bio.duke:
McCain started all 36 games as a freshman at Duke, 2023–24. 14.3 PPG, 3.0 RPG, 1.9 APG, shooting 44.7% from the field and 41.4% from three. ACC All-Rookie Team. On February 17, 2024, he scored a career-high 35 points against Florida State — tying the Duke freshman single-game scoring record held by Zion Williamson.

Duke went 27–9 and reached the Elite Eight before falling to NC State. McCain earned All-Tournament honors at the ACC Tournament and the NCAA East Regional. His shooting was his calling card — 41.4% from three as a freshman is elite at any level. But what surprised coaches and scouts was his poise. He played with the maturity of someone who had been performing in front of millions of people since high school, because he had. The TikTok fame, far from being a distraction, had given him a comfort with pressure that most nineteen-year-olds don’t possess.

He declared for the draft after one season.
bio.after:
Philadelphia selected McCain 16th overall in the 2024 draft. Instant fan favorite — the TikTok presence, the painted nails, the personality impossible to ignore. In 23 rookie games: 15.3 PPG, 2.6 APG, 2.4 RPG, 46% FG. Career-high 34 points and 10 assists vs Cleveland. Eastern Conference Rookie of the Month for November — first Sixer since Ben Simmons in 2018.

December 14, 2024: lateral meniscus tear, left knee. Season over. Twenty-three games of brilliance, then silence.

The 2025–26 season brought more adversity. UCL tear in his right thumb before the season, surgery, delayed return until late October. In 37 games with Philadelphia: 6.6 PPG in 16.8 minutes. The rhythm was gone. On February 4, 2026, the Sixers traded McCain to the Oklahoma City Thunder for a first-round pick and three future second-rounders. The kid who had been Rookie of the Month was traded for draft capital before his twenty-second birthday.

He landed in the best possible situation. The Thunder had won the 2025 championship and had the league’s best record. They needed guard depth with SGA sidelined. McCain was reunited with Chip Engelland, the Brotherhood’s legendary shooting coach, now on Oklahoma City’s staff. It took one game for Thunder fans to embrace him.
bio.now:
Jared McCain is 21 years old. He plays for the Oklahoma City Thunder, the defending champions. He has nearly five million TikTok followers. He still paints his nails. He still dances. Jason Williams has publicly questioned whether McCain wants to be ‘a hooper or a TikToker.’ The criticism represents a generational fault line in basketball culture: the old guard that demands singular focus, and the new generation that sees no contradiction between self-expression and competition.

McCain’s answer has always been the same. He doesn’t choose. He is a basketball player who scored 15.3 points per game as a rookie and a content creator with millions of followers. He is a Disney fan who identifies with Pluto and a competitor who tied Zion Williamson’s freshman scoring record. He paints his nails and drains threes. The people who insist he must pick one don’t understand that the refusal to pick is the point.

His father Lance plays pickup and flies for Southwest Airlines. His mother Jina set the bar — three shots on a ten-foot hoop — and watched her son clear it on the first try. His brother Jayce, whose career was cut short, watches from a distance that is both proud and bittersweet. The McCains are not a basketball dynasty. They are a family that gave their son a challenge, and he has been answering it ever since.

He was born in Corona. His father played with Spud Webb. His first dream was baseball until a four-year-old made a business decision. He is naturally left-handed but shoots right because of a broken thumb. His mother told him to make three shots before she’d let him play. He made them immediately. His brother beat him one-on-one without mercy. He became a TikTok star and a McDonald’s All-American. He tied Zion’s record at Duke. He was drafted 16th and averaged 15.3 as a rookie before a torn meniscus ended his season. He was traded to the defending champions. He identifies with Pluto. He paints his nails. He won’t apologize for being himself. Jared McCain is twenty-one, and the people who think he needs to choose between basketball and everything else have not been paying attention. He’s never chosen. That’s why he’s interesting.
