players.name: Cooper Flagg
players.tagline: Born in Newport, Maine. Raised by headlights. The #1 pick.
players.now: Dallas Mavericks; #1 overall pick 2025 NBA Draft; 2nd-youngest #1 pick in history
players.stat: 1 Duke season • 37 games • 19.2 PPG/7.5 RPG/4.2 APG/1.4 SPG/1.3 BPG • Naismith + Wooden Awards

bio.road:
In a house three miles from the one where she would raise him, Kelly Bowman fell in love with basketball at age seven. Her father Dan affixed a hoop to a shed in their backyard in Newport, Maine — population 3,200. They played so often the grass faded into dirt. When it got dark, he’d park a car and turn on the headlights so they could keep shooting.

Kelly starred at Nokomis Regional High School, then earned a D-I scholarship to the University of Maine (team captain, three-time conference champion). Ralph Flagg also attended Nokomis, played at Eastern Maine Community College. They met at a community center basketball game. He couldn’t stop her up-and-under move. As a wedding present, they received a crabapple tree and planted it next to the driveway hoop.

In 2004, Kelly went into premature labor at 24 weeks. Twins Hunter and Ryder were born at Maine Medical Center. Hunter: 1 lb 10 oz. Ryder: two ounces lighter. Ryder died two days later. Hunter spent 109 days in the NICU. Kelly refused to leave Portland. The Ronald McDonald House became home.

Cooper and Ace Flagg were born premature on December 21, 2006, but healthy enough to go home for Christmas. The household was organized around basketball. Kelly coached Nokomis girls’ varsity; the boys grew up in the gym. Driveway games ended when someone ran inside bleeding. Cooper played in a 4th-grade rec league as a 2nd-grader.

In March 2022, all three Flagg brothers — Hunter a senior, Cooper and Ace freshmen — led Nokomis to its first state championship. Cooper: 22 pts/16 reb in the title game. First freshman named Maine Gatorade POY (20.5/10/6.2/3.7/3.7). Then Montverde Academy. FIBA U17 gold (17 reb vs Spain in the final, USA Basketball Male Athlete of the Year at 15). Reclassified from 2025 to 2024, skipping junior year.

Planned to announce his Duke commitment October 27, 2023. Delayed three days after the Lewiston shootings — 18 killed in his home state. Committed October 30. Signed with New Balance because two factories are near Newport. First men’s college basketball player sponsored by Gatorade. Invited to join USA Select Team at 17, scored up to 17 pts against LeBron James, Curry, and Durant. KD: ‘He’s 17 years old playing like a veteran.’
bio.duke:
College debut November 4, 2024: 18 pts/7 reb/5 ast against the University of Maine — his mother’s alma mater.

19.2 PPG, 7.5 RPG, 4.2 APG, 1.4 SPG, 1.3 BPG. Led Duke in all five major categories — the only freshman in Wooden Award history to do so. 48.3% FG, 37.4% 3PT, 83.6% FT. 709 points (20th in Duke single-season history). ACC Rookie of the Week a record 12 times.

January 11, 2025: 42 points vs Notre Dame — Duke and ACC freshman single-game scoring record (11-14 FG, 4-6 3PT, 16-17 FT, 6 reb, 7 ast). First ACC player in 25 years with 500+ pts, 100+ ast, 30+ blk in a regular season.

Duke 29-4, 19-1 ACC (regular season + tournament champions). NCAA Tournament: 14/18/30/16 pts through four rounds. Final Four vs Houston: 27 pts/7 reb/4 ast/3 blk/2 stl — first player since 1986 to lead his team in all five categories in a Final Four game. Duke lost 70-67.

Naismith Award. Wooden Award. AP POY. USBWA POY. NABC POY. ACC POY + ROY (4th to win both, joining Zion, Bagley, Okafor). Consensus 1st-Team All-American. One of the five best one-and-done seasons in history.
bio.after:
Selected #1 overall by Dallas Mavericks in the 2025 NBA Draft. Age 18 years, 186 days — second-youngest #1 pick in history, 8 days older than LeBron James in 2003.

Before the draft, he didn’t fly to LA for workouts. He went home to Maine. Worked out at a D-III gym at Colby College with his twin brother. Drove to Newport to watch his classmates graduate from Nokomis — the ceremony that should have been his if he hadn’t reclassified.

In December 2024, the Flagg family donated $10,000 to the Ronald McDonald House in Durham. Kelly cried telling the story of Hunter and Ryder. The twins born premature in Portland — one who survived, one who didn’t, 109 days in the NICU — had grown into a young man giving back to the place that gave his family a home during the worst days of their lives.
bio.now:
Cooper Flagg is 18. He plays for the Dallas Mavericks. Twin brother Ace plays at the University of Maine. Hunter is studying sports management. Ryder, who lived for two days, is remembered every time the family walks through the doors of a Ronald McDonald House.

Kelly Bowman Flagg, who shot baskets by headlight in a town of 3,200 people, who captained Maine, who planted a crabapple tree next to the driveway hoop — watched her son become the #1 pick. Ralph watched beside her.

He signed with New Balance because of Maine. He delayed his commitment because of Lewiston. He went home before the draft to watch his classmates graduate. He scored 42 against Notre Dame. He led his team in every category in a Final Four game. He won every major national award. He is eighteen.

The story begins with headlights. A father parks his car and turns on the high beams so his seven-year-old daughter can keep shooting after dark. Forty years later, that girl’s son is the first pick in the NBA Draft. The headlights are still on. Cooper Flagg is just getting started.

players.name: Kon Knueppel
players.tagline: Five brothers. Five trophies. A Nintendo Wii taught him to love basketball. Milwaukee’s finest shooter.
players.now: Charlotte Hornets; 4th overall pick 2025 NBA Draft; Summer League Champion + MVP; ROY frontrunner
players.stat: 1 Duke season • 39 starts • 14.4 PPG/4.0 RPG/2.7 APG • 40.6% 3PT • 91.4% FT • ACC Tournament MVP

bio.road:
Kon Knueppel II was born August 3, 2005, in Milwaukee, Wisconsin, into a house with six basketball hoops scattered around the property. His father, also named Kon, had scored 2,064 career points at Wisconsin Lutheran College — the school’s all-time leading scorer — and was a four-time All-Lake Michigan Conference selection. His mother, Chari Nordgaard Knueppel, had scored 1,964 career points at the University of Wisconsin-Green Bay — the program’s all-time leading scorer. She was an All-Horizon League member every season, played in two NCAA Tournaments, played professionally in Greece, and was inducted into UW-Green Bay’s Hall of Fame in 2004. Her number 33 was retired in January 2025 during a ceremony she flew in for between her son’s Duke games.

The basketball bloodlines ran deeper than even Kon and Chari. Chari’s brother, Jeff Nordgaard, starred at UW-Green Bay before being drafted by the Milwaukee Bucks in the second round in 1996. He played 13 NBA games and spent thirteen years playing professionally overseas. Chari’s maternal grandfather, Arthur “Bud” Rose, played basketball at North Carolina State in 1931–32 and earned Honorable Mention All-American honors — the first All-American in NC State history. The Knueppel-Nordgaard family tree reads like a basketball encyclopedia stretching back nearly a century.

On Kon’s father’s side, there were the uncles: Klint, Klay, and Kole. They competed alongside Kon Sr. in the Gus Macker 3-on-3 Basketball Tournament as a team called the “Flying Knueppel Brothers” — having rejected the alternative name, “Free the Knueppel.” Uncle Kole became a college football referee in the Big Ten.

Kon is the oldest of five boys. His brothers, in order: Kager, Kinston, Kash, and Kidman. All five play basketball. When Kon committed to Duke in November 2023, he posted a photo on Instagram of himself and his four younger brothers standing inside the Duke locker room. Each boy was holding one of Duke’s five national championship trophies. Five brothers. Five trophies.

But here is the part nobody expects. When Kon was five and six, he was not into sports at all. His uncle Jeff Nordgaard, the former NBA player, remembers being surprised. Little Kon was bookish, using vocabulary beyond his age. The family was starting to think he might be a non-athlete — and then his parents bought a Nintendo Wii. It was the family’s first video game console. Kon played Wii Sports basketball obsessively. The virtual game awakened something. His parents enrolled him in a kindergarten league. He didn’t score a single point in his first ten games. He ducked when teammates passed him the ball. The all-time leading scorers at Wisconsin Lutheran College and UW-Green Bay had produced a child who couldn’t catch a bounce pass.

Kon Sr. ran a men’s league in Milwaukee — had been running it for twenty years. In eighth grade, Kon II started playing in it. Among the regulars: Ben Brust, a former Wisconsin Badger and the program’s all-time three-point leader. Brust kept beating Kon on backdoor cuts. By senior year, Kon was the one doing the cutting. The men’s league taught him what no AAU circuit could.

Chari ran a daycare from the family home since 2006. Kon Sr. worked as a school counselor in Milwaukee public schools. Six hoops on the property. Five boys. Two parents who were all-time leading scorers. Blue-collar Milwaukee.

At Wisconsin Lutheran High School: 10.8 PPG as a freshman, 19.7 as a junior, 26.4 as a senior. Perfect 30–0 record, WIAA Division 2 State Championship. Wisconsin Mr. Basketball. Gatorade Wisconsin POY. Also played baritone in the school band. He chose Duke because: “I want to play against the best players every day in practice, because that will make me better.” Enrolled June 2024. Roommate: Cooper Flagg.
bio.duke:
Knueppel started all 39 games as a freshman — tying Duke’s single-season record for starts by a freshman. 14.4 PPG, 4.0 RPG, 2.7 APG. Shot 47.9% from the field, 40.6% from three, and 91.4% from the free-throw line — highest FT% in the ACC, second-highest by a Duke freshman in history (only J.J. Redick shot better). Made 38 consecutive free throws from February through March.

Cooper Flagg was the headliner. Knueppel was the other half. Flagg was impossible to keep out of the lane. Knueppel was a lethal shooter who made the high-IQ plays he’d learned from years in his father’s men’s league. Scheyer: “Their personalities were perfect for each other — competitive, unselfish, and smart.”

One preseason practice, Flagg didn’t have his best stuff. The guy who called him out? Knueppel. He told the future number-one pick to get his head in the game. Scheyer’s staff still points to that moment.

Then the ACC Tournament. Flagg sprained his ankle in the quarterfinal and missed the rest. Knueppel stepped into the spotlight: 21.0 PPG, 5.7 RPG, 4.7 APG across the tournament. 18 points and 8 rebounds in the title game against Louisville. ACC Tournament MVP. Nobody cheered harder for him than Flagg on the sideline.

The same week, back in Wisconsin, brother Kager scored 10 points in the WIAA Division 1 state title game. Wisconsin Lutheran won back-to-back championships in a higher division. Mother Chari flew between Raleigh and Madison, refusing to miss a game from any son. She attended every Duke game in person that season. Every one.

NCAA Tournament: 20.5 PPG in the Sweet 16 and Elite Eight. 16 points in the Final Four against Houston. East Region All-Tournament Team. Season line: 84 three-pointers, 117-of-128 free throws, and the quiet, lethal efficiency that made every scout say: this kid is going to play in the NBA for a long time.
bio.after:
Charlotte selected Knueppel 4th overall in the 2025 NBA Draft. On draft night, Cooper Flagg was mid-interview on NBA TV when the pick was announced. Flagg shot out of his seat — the most animated he’d been all evening, including when his own name was called first. The former roommates, the freshman duo who pushed each other every day, were both top-five picks.

Knueppel led the Hornets to the 2025 NBA Summer League championship, scoring 21 in the title game. Summer League Championship MVP. Regular-season debut October 22: 11 points and 5 rebounds vs Brooklyn. By early November: a 30-point game against Miami, Eastern Conference Player of the Week nomination. On November 14, he scored 32 in an overtime loss against Milwaukee — his hometown team, the franchise his uncle Jeff had once played for — hitting two late threes to force OT. Averaging 19.2 PPG, 5.5 RPG, 3.4 APG. Alongside Flagg, the two former roommates are frontrunners for NBA Rookie of the Year.
bio.now:
Kon Knueppel is 19 years old. He plays for the Charlotte Hornets. His former roommate Cooper Flagg plays for the Dallas Mavericks. The two are the top candidates for NBA Rookie of the Year.

Back in Milwaukee, the Knueppel machine keeps running. Kager, a 6-8 sophomore, has D-I offers and led Wisconsin Lutheran to a second consecutive state title. Kinston is generating recruiting buzz. Kash and Kidman won the national Lutheran grade-school basketball tournament in Fort Wayne, Indiana. Chari is retiring from the daycare to focus full-time on five sons and their schedules. Kon Sr. still runs the men’s league. The six hoops are still on the property.

He was born in Milwaukee into a house with six basketball hoops. His father scored 2,064 points at Wisconsin Lutheran College. His mother scored 1,964 at UW-Green Bay. His uncle was drafted by the Bucks. His great-grandfather was NC State’s first All-American in 1932. His father’s brothers called themselves the Flying Knueppel Brothers. He didn’t score a point in his first ten basketball games because a Nintendo Wii had to teach him to love the sport first. He learned to play off the ball from a former Badger in his father’s men’s league. He played baritone in the high school band. He led Wisconsin Lutheran to a perfect season. He chose Duke because he wanted to practice against the best. He was Cooper Flagg’s roommate. He told the future number-one pick to get his head in the game. He won ACC Tournament MVP when Flagg went down. He was the fourth pick. He scored 32 against his hometown Bucks. And when he committed to Duke, he posted a photo: five brothers, each holding one of the program’s five national championship trophies. Five brothers. Five trophies. One family. The Knueppels don’t just play basketball. They are basketball.

players.name: Isaiah Evans
players.tagline: Showtime Slim. JV as a freshman. Didn’t make his seventh-grade team. Bought his own cones. Single mother believed in the slow build. 48 points in the state quarterfinal — 21 in a row.
players.now: Duke Blue Devils sophomore; 14.7 PPG; projected 1st-round 2026 NBA Draft; jersey #0 retired at North Mecklenburg
players.stat: HS: 2x NC Mr. Basketball • McDonald’s AA • 4A State Champion • 27.5 PPG senior year • 48 pts state QF • Duke So: 14.7 PPG

bio.road:
Isaiah Demonte Evans was born December 6, 2005, in Fayetteville, North Carolina, and grew up in the orbit of Charlotte — close enough to feel the pull of one of the South’s great basketball cities, far enough away that nobody was looking for him. He was raised by his mother, Marieke Lemon, a single mother who worked long hours to provide for Isaiah and his younger brother. His grandmother stepped in when Marieke needed help. The household was built on sacrifice, structure, and the quiet understanding that nothing would be given.

Isaiah played everything as a kid — soccer, football, basketball. His mother supported every sport. He stopped playing football around sixth grade. Moved on from soccer when he was nearly ten. Basketball stuck. He started on local rec teams, and the love grew from there.

But here is the part nobody believes: Isaiah Evans did not make his seventh-grade team. A scheduling mix-up kept him off the roster. The following year, as a freshman at North Mecklenburg High School in Huntersville, he was placed on the junior varsity team. JV. Future five-star recruits do not play JV. Future McDonald’s All-Americans do not play JV. Isaiah Evans played JV because he was not yet the player the world would come to know, and his mother and coaches believed in a slow build. They were right.

The summer after freshman year, something activated. He spent the entire off-season grinding — not at a fancy facility, not with a personal trainer, not on an elite AAU team. He bought his own cones, ladders, and training equipment with money he had saved. He worked out three times a day: before school, after practice, late at night. Nobody was watching. Nobody was recruiting. He was a JV player from Huntersville teaching himself to be great with equipment he’d bought himself.

Sophomore year: varsity, nearly 20 PPG. Junior year: 26.0 PPG, 6.4 RPG, North Carolina Mr. Basketball. Senior year: 27.5 PPG, 6.3 RPG, 3.0 APG, shooting 53% from the field and 43% from three. Undefeated season. Class 4A state championship. In the state quarterfinal against nationally ranked Myers Park, the defending champions, he scored 48 points — including 21 in a row during the second half. SLAM Magazine called it a 48-piece, no fries. His nickname — Showtime Slim, for his slender 175-pound frame and his tendency to deliver under the brightest lights — was cemented.

North Mecklenburg retired his jersey. Number zero. First and last to wear it. ‘A lot of people don’t know this, but I’m going to be the first and last person to wear number zero. It really meant a lot to me.’

One more detail: Isaiah Evans grew up a Tar Heel fan. In April 2023, a Carolina kid committed to Duke — over Kansas, NC State, Tennessee, Texas, Auburn, and Florida State. His mother’s reaction to the whole journey: ‘You’ll hear that a lot. They’re like, Oh, he came out of nowhere.’ He didn’t come out of nowhere. He came out of Fayetteville, where his mother worked long hours and his grandmother helped, where he bought his own cones and worked out three times a day, where nobody was watching until everybody was.
bio.duke:
Evans’s freshman season at Duke, 2024–25, was a lesson in patience. He played behind Cooper Flagg, Kon Knueppel, and a roster loaded with future NBA picks. He averaged 6.8 PPG in limited minutes. But when he was hot, the building knew. Against Auburn in December, he hit six three-pointers in the first half alone — 18 points on 6-for-8 from deep — and Cameron Indoor erupted. Teammate Khaman Maluach: ‘He gave us a lot of energy.’

When Flagg went down with an ankle injury in the ACC Tournament quarterfinal, Evans stepped into the starting lineup and helped Duke win the conference championship. He shot 41.6% from three for the season. Duke reached the Final Four before falling to Houston. 35–4 record, three points from a championship game.

He tested the waters of the 2025 draft but returned to Duke for his sophomore year. The decision signaled he understood the developmental arc his mother had always believed in — the slow build, the patient climb.

Sophomore year, 2025–26: promoted to the starting lineup alongside Cameron and Cayden Boozer. Through 29 games: 14.7 PPG, 2.9 RPG, 1.3 APG, 43% from the field, 36.3% from three, 88.8% from the free-throw line. Hit a game-winning three against Florida. He was no longer the sixth man hoping for hot streaks. He was a starter on a 27–2 team sitting atop the ACC. At last, the player his mother always saw in the driveway.
bio.after:
Isaiah Evans’s Duke career is still unfolding. He is projected as a first-round pick in the 2026 NBA Draft. His combination of size (6-6), shooting range, and developing playmaking ability makes him attractive to scouts who value wings that stretch the floor. The path from JV freshman to first-round pick would be one of the most unlikely developmental arcs in modern basketball.

His mother Marieke will be in the stands. She has been in the stands since the rec leagues in Fayetteville, since the JV games at North Meck, since the state championship, since Cameron Indoor and the Final Four. She has never missed the show, even when the show was a fifteen-year-old playing junior varsity in front of forty people. That is the part of the Isaiah Evans story no stat line can capture: the mother who believed in the slow build when everyone else wanted the fast track.
bio.now:
Isaiah Evans is 20. He is a sophomore at Duke, starting alongside Cameron and Cayden Boozer on a team that is 27–2. He is nicknamed Showtime Slim. He hosts basketball camps for kids in Concord, North Carolina, through the nonprofit Destined 4 Greatness Hoops. His message to campers: consistent work and keeping your head down is what’s going to put you in the same position as me.

He was born in Fayetteville. His mother raised him alone, working long hours, with her mother helping. He played every sport before basketball chose him. He didn’t make his seventh-grade team. He played JV as a freshman. He bought his own cones and ladders and trained three times a day when nobody was watching. He became the best player in North Carolina. He scored 48 in a state quarterfinal — 21 in a row. He was a Tar Heel fan who committed to Duke. His jersey was retired at North Mecklenburg: number zero, first and last. He sat behind Cooper Flagg as a freshman and hit six threes in one half against Auburn. He came back because he believed in the slow build. He is averaging 14.7 and hit a game-winner against Florida.

His mother said it best. People say he came out of nowhere. He didn’t come out of nowhere. He came out of Fayetteville, where a single mother worked long hours and a kid bought his own cones with saved money and trained in the dark until the world had no choice but to notice. That’s not nowhere. That’s everywhere. That’s every kid who was told he wasn’t good enough, who played JV when he should have been varsity, who missed the seventh-grade team because of a scheduling mix-up. The difference is Isaiah Evans kept going. Showtime Slim doesn’t come out of nowhere. He comes out of the work.

nba.postCareer: Current Duke freshman (2025-26)

players.name: Cameron Boozer
players.tagline: Born via IVF to save his brother’s life. Four state titles. First since LeBron to win Mr. Basketball USA twice. Carlos Boozer’s son — but now Carlos is known as Cameron’s dad.
players.now: Duke Blue Devils freshman; projected top pick 2026 NBA Draft; 2x Gatorade National POY; 2x Mr. Basketball USA
players.stat: HS: 4 consecutive FL state titles • 2025 Chipotle Nationals Champions • 2x Gatorade National POY • 2x Mr. Basketball USA (first since LeBron) • FIBA U16 + U17 MVP

bio.road:
The story of Cameron Boozer begins before he was born — and it begins with someone else’s survival.

On May 31, 2006, Carlos Boozer and his wife CeCe received the diagnosis that would reshape their family. Their firstborn son, Carmani, had sickle cell disease. Carlos had just signed a six-year, $70 million contract with the Utah Jazz. He was a two-time NBA All-Star, a Duke championship hero, a man who had grown up in Juneau, Alaska. None of it mattered. His son was sick.

Sickle cell disease is a genetic blood disorder in which red blood cells become rigid and crescent-shaped, blocking blood flow and causing episodes of extreme pain, organ damage, and potentially death. Both Carlos and CeCe carried the sickle cell trait. Their best hope was a bone marrow transplant from a sibling’s umbilical cord blood. But they couldn’t simply conceive and hope — if their next child also carried the disease, it would mean two sick children. They needed a child who was both free of sickle cell and a perfect bone marrow match.

Carlos and CeCe chose in-vitro fertilization. Doctors harvested 34 eggs from CeCe. Ten became viable embryos. Only two were both sickle-cell-free and perfect bone marrow matches. Both were implanted. Both survived. On July 18, 2007, in Salt Lake City, Cameron Sikandar Boozer and his fraternal twin brother Cayden were born. They began their lives saving the life of their brother.

Three weeks later, stem cells from the twins’ umbilical cords were transplanted into Carmani at Miami Children’s Hospital. Forty nights in the hospital. It worked. Carmani was cured. ESPN documented the story in 2008 and returned in 2024 for Blood Brothers.

Cameron grew up in Miami. His parents divorced in 2015 but both remained present. CeCe had met Carlos when she was at NC State and Carlos was at Duke. She was 5-10, a D-I volleyball player. Carlos was 6-9, a two-time All-Star, Olympic gold medalist. At fourteen, the twins looked like they’d been built in a basketball lab. Carlos said the light bulb went on in junior high. CeCe said she knew by the eighth grade.

At Christopher Columbus High School: Cameron was the 6-9 power forward, Cayden the 6-4 point guard who knew where his brother wanted the ball before Cameron did. Sophomore year: 21.1 PPG/11.3 RPG/4.2 APG/2.0 BPG, state championship. Four consecutive Florida state titles. 2025 Chipotle Nationals crown. Cameron was named Gatorade National POY as a sophomore (2023) and again as a senior (2025). Mr. Basketball USA in 2023 and 2025 — the first player to win it twice since LeBron James. FIBA U16 Americas MVP (2023, leading USA to gold by 82 over Canada). FIBA U17 World Cup MVP (2024). The most fundamentally sound player in high school basketball.

The commitment came October 11, 2024. Cameron and Cayden chose Duke together, over Miami and Florida. The video featured Carmani — the brother whose life they’d saved. Carlos told ESPN: ‘I’m proud of them. They’ve worked their butts off.’ Then he shared what defines how far the family has come: ‘Hey, Mr. Boozer, you’re Cameron and Cayden’s dad? I get that all the time now.’ The two-time All-Star is now recognized for what his children are becoming. And he loves it.
bio.duke:
Cameron enrolled at Duke for the 2025–26 season alongside Cayden. They are the centerpiece of Scheyer’s next recruiting class — the follow-up to Flagg and Knueppel. Cameron was the #2 overall recruit in the 2025 class.

His freshman season is being written. What he brings to Durham is already clear: a 6-9 power forward with elite scoring touch, a rebounder who attacks the glass like his father, a passer with unusual vision for his size, and a winner who has never known a season without a championship. Four state titles. Two national MVPs. Two FIBA gold medals. He is eighteen, and his resume already reads like a career.

What makes Cameron different from his father at the same age is the modern game. Carlos at Duke was a low-post scorer, a physical rebounder built for the paint. Cameron has the same frame but plays facing the basket. He can shoot from the perimeter, handle in transition, run pick-and-roll as screener or initiator. The NBA he’s preparing for is different from the one his father played in.

Cayden is beside him — the same chemistry they’ve shared since they were toddlers wearing Bulls jerseys at Heat games. The connection is almost telepathic. Cayden doesn’t have to look. He knows where Cameron is.
bio.after:
Cameron Boozer’s Duke career is just beginning. He is projected as a top pick in the 2026 NBA Draft — mock drafts have placed him in the top three since he was a sophomore in high school. His combination of size, skill, basketball intelligence, and pedigree makes him one of the most valuable prospects in amateur basketball.

The NBA story will come. What matters now is the Duke story — and the family story. Cameron will play in Cameron Indoor Stadium, the same court where his father scored, rebounded, and won a national championship in 2001. He will wear Duke blue as the son of a Brotherhood legend, alongside his twin brother, the one conceived through the same act of medical science and parental love, delivered into the world with the same purpose: to save Carmani’s life and then to build lives of their own.
bio.now:
Cameron Boozer is 18 years old. He is a freshman at Duke. His twin Cayden is his teammate. His older brother Carmani — cured of sickle cell disease by stem cells from the twins’ umbilical cords — is 19, a freshman pitcher at the University of Fort Lauderdale, living a full life that exists because of a decision his parents made in 2006. Carlos works as an ACC Network analyst and recently joined the Utah Jazz front office. CeCe remains deeply involved.

The parents who drove a 1985 Dodge van from Washington, D.C., to Juneau, Alaska, because a six-year-old boy was shot on a basketball court — Carlos Sr. and Renee — moved back to Juneau. The family born from fear produced an NBA All-Star, an Olympic gold medalist, a medical miracle, and two of the most celebrated high school basketball players in the country.

Cameron was born in Salt Lake City on July 18, 2007. His umbilical cord blood contained the stem cells that saved his brother’s life. He was three weeks old when they were transplanted into Carmani. He didn’t know. He couldn’t know. He was a newborn. Seventeen years later, he is a Duke Blue Devil, a four-time state champion, a two-time national player of the year, a two-time Mr. Basketball USA, and the son of a man who now gets recognized as ‘Cameron and Cayden’s dad.’ The story of Cameron Boozer is a story about basketball, but it is first and forever a story about blood. The blood that was sick. The blood that healed it. And the brotherhood — the real one, between three brothers in Miami — that made all of it possible.

players.name: Cayden Boozer
players.tagline: The loud twin. The passer in a family of scorers. My brother’s keeper — since the day he was born.
players.now: Duke Blue Devils freshman; projected 1st-round 2026 NBA Draft; best passer in high school basketball
players.stat: HS: 4 FL state titles • 3 Nike EYBL titles • Jr: 12.0 PPG/7.6 APG • Led FIBA U17 World Cup in assists • 2x USA Gold

bio.road:
Every twin story has two sides, and in the Boozer family, the sides could not be more different. Cameron is stoic and serious. Cayden is loud and rambunctious. Their mother CeCe put it plainly: without Cayden, Cameron would have never gone to movies or done regular kid things. Cameron is the introvert with a basketball and a plan. Cayden is the extrovert who makes sure his brother — and everyone around him — actually lives.

Cayden Boozer was born July 18, 2007, in Salt Lake City, three weeks before he and Cameron performed the most important assist of their lives. Stem cells from their umbilical cords were transplanted into their older brother Carmani, who had sickle cell disease. The transplant worked. Carmani was cured. The story of how Cameron and Cayden were conceived through IVF — 34 eggs, 10 viable embryos, only 2 that were both sickle-cell-free and perfect bone marrow matches — is told in full in Cameron’s profile and in their father Carlos’s. It is the foundational story of the Boozer family. Cayden carries it with the knowledge that his life began as an act of love designed to save someone else’s.

CeCe described the family mantra as ‘I am my brother’s keeper.’ That phrase defines Cayden more than any stat line. He is the keeper. The one who watches out. The one who sees the floor — on the court and off it. Even as children, Cayden was the social one who dragged Cameron to birthday parties and movies. Cameron, left to his own devices, would have spent every waking hour in the gym. Cayden made sure his brother had a childhood alongside the basketball.

The twins grew up in Miami after Carlos’s NBA career wound down. CeCe and Carlos divorced in 2015 but both remained present. The boys wore Bulls jerseys to Heat playoff games as children, chanting ‘Let’s go Heat!’ At Columbus High School, Cameron was the scorer. Cayden was the conductor. At 6-5 and 210 pounds, Cayden had the size of a small forward but the vision of a floor general. He knew where Cameron wanted the ball before Cameron did — because they’d been playing together since before they could form sentences.

Junior year at Columbus: 12.0 PPG, 7.6 APG, 2.7 RPG, 1.8 SPG. Scouts called him the best passer in high school basketball. Assist-to-turnover ratio consistently better than 3:1. Nike EYBL: 6.5 APG leading the league. Peach Jam: 6.9 APG, including 11 in a semifinal. FIBA U17 World Cup: led the tournament in assists (6.4 APG) despite averaging just 18 minutes. He could score — 22 against Montverde, 22 against Link Academy — but he chose to pass.

ESPN compared him to Tre Jones. Others saw Andre Miller. The common thread: reliability. Cayden Boozer is the point guard who never panics, never forces, never turns it over when it matters. CeCe called him ‘trustworthy.’ For a point guard, there is no higher compliment. Committed to Duke October 11, 2024, alongside Cameron. Duke’s first five-star point guard since Jeremy Roach in 2020.
bio.duke:
Cayden arrived at Duke for 2025–26 alongside Cameron. His freshman year is underway. What he brings to Durham is what he has always brought: the ability to make everyone around him better. He organizes the offense, sets the tempo, finds the open man, and turns Cameron’s raw talent into easy baskets. He is the connector.

The basketball world will focus on Cameron, the projected top pick in the 2026 draft. But the people who watch Duke every game will know what Cayden knows, what CeCe knows, what every Columbus teammate knew for four years: Cameron is great because of Cameron, but Cameron is greater because of Cayden. The stoic one needs the loud one. The scorer needs the passer. The introvert needs the extrovert who drags him to the movies. That architecture has produced four state titles, three EYBL championships, two FIBA gold medals, and a national championship. It works because Cayden makes it work.
bio.after:
Cayden is projected as a first-round pick in the 2026 draft alongside Cameron. He may not be drafted as high — point guards who pass first are historically undervalued compared to dominant scorers — but his impact could be equally profound. Teams that value winning over individual statistics will see what Columbus and Duke have seen: Cayden Boozer makes every team he’s on better.

The question every Boozer fan asks is whether the twins will end up on the same NBA team. The odds are long, but the possibility is tantalizing: two brothers, conceived through IVF to save their sibling, playing professional basketball together the way they have since elementary school.
bio.now:
Cayden Boozer is 18. He is a freshman at Duke playing point guard alongside Cameron. Carmani — cured of sickle cell by the twins’ stem cells — is 19, a freshman pitcher at the University of Fort Lauderdale. Carlos works as an ACC Network analyst. CeCe remains deeply involved.

Cayden is the loud one, the rambunctious one, the one who makes sure his brothers live outside the gym. He is the passer in a family of scorers. He is the keeper of his brothers — both the one he saved at birth and the one he saves every game with a perfectly timed entry pass. He has spent his entire life making other people better: Carmani by being born, Cameron by being his point guard, his Columbus teammates by finding them in their spots, his USA Basketball squads by leading tournaments in assists while playing eighteen minutes a game.

He was born in Salt Lake City on July 18, 2007. Three weeks later, the stem cells from his umbilical cord helped cure his brother’s disease. He grew up in Miami wearing Bulls jerseys to Heat games. He is the fraternal twin of the most fundamentally sound player in high school basketball, and he chose to be the one who delivers the ball rather than the one who scores it. He averaged 7.6 assists because the game, for Cayden, has always been about making the people around him better. ESPN compared him to Tre Jones. His mother compared him to a brother’s keeper. She was right. Cayden Boozer has been keeping his brothers since the day he was born. He is the connector, the facilitator, the loud twin who makes the quiet twin whole. I am my brother’s keeper. That is the Boozer family mantra, and nobody has lived it more completely than Cayden.

players.name: Mark Mitchell
players.tagline: Scheyer’s first signature recruit. McDonald’s All-American. ACC Tournament champion. A story still being written.
players.now: Senior forward, Missouri Tigers; 2026 NBA Draft prospect
players.stat: 68 games at Duke (10.3 ppg, 67 starts); All-SEC at Missouri (13.9 ppg); 2023 ACC Tourney champ

bio.road:
Mark Mitchell Jr. grew up in Kansas City, Kansas — not exactly a Duke basketball pipeline state. He attended Bishop Miege High School, a Catholic school in Shawnee, where he was dominant from the start: 21.6 points, 8.7 rebounds, 2.7 assists, and 1.8 blocks per game as a sophomore. He won the DiRenna Award as the top player in the Kansas City area after leading Bishop Miege to the Kansas Class 4A state championship.

For his senior year, Mitchell transferred to Sunrise Christian Academy in Bel Aire, Kansas — one of the nation’s elite prep programs that has produced a string of Division I and NBA talent. At Sunrise Christian, he averaged 17.6 points and 6.3 rebounds and was selected to play in the 2022 McDonald’s All-American Game, where he tied for the game’s leading scorer with 19 points on 8-of-13 shooting. He was rated a five-star recruit by Rivals and 247Sports and ranked as high as #13 nationally.

Mitchell committed to Duke on December 10, 2021, choosing the Blue Devils over UCLA and Missouri in a ceremony broadcast live on CBS Sports HQ. When he made his announcement, he used four words that carried enormous weight: “I’m joining the Brotherhood.” It was one of Jon Scheyer’s first major recruiting wins as the heir apparent to Krzyzewski. Mitchell was the fifth ESPN 100 recruit in Duke’s 2022 class, a group that gave Scheyer the consensus #1 recruiting class in the country before he’d even coached his first game.

He arrived in Durham in the fall of 2022 as part of Scheyer’s inaugural class alongside Dereck Lively II, Dariq Whitehead, Kyle Filipowski, and Tyrese Proctor. It was the most scrutinized freshman class in Duke history — the first without Coach K on the bench — and Mitchell was the steady, physical presence that held it together.
bio.duke:
Mitchell exceeded expectations as a freshman. Recruited primarily as an inside player, Scheyer used him as a combo forward — and the results surprised everyone. He shot 35% from three-point range, particularly effective spotting up in the corner, while also finishing at the rim at a 68% clip. He started 32 of 33 games, averaging 8.7 points and 4.9 rebounds. His defensive impact was immediate: Duke’s opponents shot under 45% when Mitchell was on the court and over 48% when he sat.

The signature moment of his freshman year came in March 2023. Duke entered the ACC Tournament as an underdog, but Scheyer’s young team ran the table — beating Pittsburgh, Miami, and Virginia to win the championship. It was Scheyer’s first ACC Tournament title as head coach and the first for a first-year Duke coach since Vic Bubas in 1964. Mitchell was a steady force throughout the run, defending the opposition’s best forward in every game. The image of Scheyer’s first team cutting down the nets in Greensboro — with Mitchell among the starters — validated the entire post-K transition.

As a sophomore in 2023-24, Mitchell’s role shifted. His three-point shooting regressed, but his interior game exploded: 60% of his field goal attempts came at the rim, and he converted 79% of them. He averaged 11.8 points and 5.4 rebounds while starting 35 of 35 games. Duke earned a #4 seed in the NCAA Tournament and marched to the Elite Eight, beating Vermont, James Madison, and Houston before falling to NC State. Mitchell was the team’s defensive anchor throughout, guarding the opposition’s toughest matchup every night.

Over two seasons at Duke, Mitchell started 67 of 68 games and scored 700 points. He helped Scheyer to 54 wins, 29 ACC victories, one ACC Tournament championship, and two NCAA Tournament appearances. For a program navigating its most significant coaching transition in 42 years, Mitchell was the bridge — the recruit who bet on Scheyer before anyone knew what the Scheyer era would look like, and who delivered immediately.
bio.after:
In April 2024, Mitchell entered the transfer portal. The decision surprised some — he had been a two-year starter on a team that reached the Elite Eight — but Mitchell was chasing the offensive role he needed to prove himself as an NBA prospect. At Duke, he was the fourth or fifth option offensively. He needed to be the first.

Missouri won the recruitment, reuniting Mitchell with his childhood friend Tamar Bates and bringing him back to the SEC, close to his Kansas City roots. The fit was immediate. Under Dennis Gates, Mitchell averaged 13.9 points and 4.7 rebounds in 2024-25, shooting 50.5% from the field while becoming one of the most prolific free-throw generators in the conference. He scored 25 points against Oklahoma to reach 1,000 career points. He dropped 21 on #4 Alabama on 11-of-15 shooting. He earned All-SEC Second Team honors from the media and Third Team from the coaches — becoming just the third Tiger to earn All-SEC honors in his first season on campus.

He was one of only 14 players in the nation to total 450 points, 150 rebounds, 60 assists, 30 steals, and 25 blocks in a single season. His defensive versatility — the ability to guard multiple positions that Duke had cultivated — translated seamlessly to the SEC.

Mitchell returned to Missouri for his senior season in 2025-26 and is currently projected as a second-round pick in the 2026 NBA Draft. At 6-foot-9, 230 pounds, with plus athleticism and defensive versatility, his NBA archetype is clear: a 3-and-D combo forward who can switch across positions. The shooting needs to improve — his 26% three-point mark at Missouri is a concern — but the physical tools and defensive pedigree make him a legitimate prospect.
bio.now:
Mark Mitchell is 22 years old and in his fourth college season at Missouri. He is a 2026 NBA Draft prospect playing in the SEC, the toughest conference in college basketball. His career has spanned two blue-blood programs, an ACC Tournament championship, an Elite Eight, and an All-SEC selection. He has scored over 1,000 points across Duke and Missouri combined.

His Brotherhood story is one of transition. He was Scheyer’s first signature recruit — the McDonald’s All-American from Kansas who said “I’m joining the Brotherhood” on national television. He delivered two years of steady, physical, winning basketball at Duke, then made the difficult decision to leave in pursuit of a bigger role. It wasn’t a rejection of Duke; it was a bet on himself.

The parallel to Semi Ojeleye is striking: another Kansas kid, another Duke forward who needed a different stage to show his full game, another transfer who blossomed elsewhere. But where Ojeleye’s departure was painful and contentious, Mitchell’s was clean — a product of the modern transfer era where moving is strategy, not scandal.

Whether Mitchell hears his name called on draft night in June 2026 or finds his path another way, his Duke chapter is already written. He was there for the first ACC Tournament title of the Scheyer era. He was there for the Elite Eight run. He started 67 games. And he did it all as the first recruit to say yes to a program that was reinventing itself without the greatest coach in college basketball history. That’s a Brotherhood story worth telling, even if the ending hasn’t been written yet.

players.name: Caleb Foster
players.tagline: Three high schools. Two broken feet. One unwavering commitment to Duke. The quiet kid from Harrisburg who became the Brotherhood’s elder statesman at 20.
players.now: Active — Junior guard, #1 Duke Blue Devils (recovering from right foot surgery, Mar 2026)
players.stat: 96 career games (6.8 ppg, 38.5% 3PT); 2025-26: 8.5 ppg, 40.2% 3PT; 2x All-ACC Academic

bio.road:
Caleb Foster grew up in Harrisburg, North Carolina — a suburb of Charlotte, close enough to Duke that the program’s shadow loomed over every pickup game and AAU tournament of his childhood. His father Michael was a North Carolina state trooper who later retired to follow Caleb across the country during his basketball journey. His mother sacrificed alongside them. When asked what motivated him, Foster kept it simple: "My family — my dad and how hard he works, my mom what she’s sacrificed for me to travel and play basketball my whole life basically."

Foster attended Hickory Ridge High School in Harrisburg through his sophomore year before transferring to Oak Hill Academy in Mouth of Wilson, Virginia — the legendary prep school that has produced over 40 NBA players, including Carmelo Anthony and Rajon Rondo. At Oak Hill, Foster played under the legendary coach Steve Smith, who called him "the best point guard nationally in the 2023 class." He led the Warriors to a 32-8 record while averaging 11.9 points, 3.8 rebounds, and 4.5 assists on 53.2% shooting.

When Smith retired after Foster’s junior year, Foster transferred again — this time across the country to Notre Dame High School in Sherman Oaks, California. The move was about finding a regular high school experience after two years at a boarding school. He exploded as a senior, averaging 21.4 points, 6.8 rebounds, and 3.9 assists, earning MaxPreps California Player of the Year honors. On the Nike EYBL circuit that summer, he was devastating: 28.3 points, 5.7 rebounds, and 4.7 assists per game in the final session in Kansas City, and 25.2 points while shooting 44.4% from three at the prestigious Peach Jam.

None of it changed his mind. Foster had committed to Duke on September 16, 2021 — as a sophomore, before he’d even arrived at Oak Hill. He was the first player in the 2023 recruiting class to commit to the Blue Devils, pledging to Jon Scheyer before Scheyer had coached his first game. He chose Duke over Auburn, Louisville, Tennessee, Virginia, Clemson, Stanford, Illinois, and Wake Forest. When asked about his commitment as he bounced between schools and states, Foster never wavered. The Brotherhood called early, and he answered.
bio.duke:
Foster arrived at Duke in the fall of 2023 as part of a loaded freshman class that included Jared McCain, TJ Power (who later transferred), and Caleb Howard. From the very first game, Foster showed he belonged. In the Champions Classic against Michigan State, he erupted for 18 points, connecting on four three-pointers — including back-to-back daggers late in the game that iced the victory. It was the kind of moment that makes you lean forward in your seat. The kid from Harrisburg was not intimidated by the bright lights.

He settled into a key reserve role, starting some games and coming off the bench in others depending on matchups. He averaged 7.7 points, 2.4 rebounds, and 2.1 assists over 27 games, shooting solidly from three. The numbers don’t capture what he brought: defensive intensity, poise with the ball, an ability to play within the flow of a game rather than forcing it. Scheyer trusted him in big moments.

Then March arrived — and took him with it.

In February 2024, Foster suffered a stress fracture in his right ankle. He missed the final nine games of his freshman season, including the entire ACC Tournament and NCAA Tournament. He sat helplessly in a walking boot as Duke made its run to the Elite Eight, where they fell to NC State. The loss was crushing. Being unable to help was worse.

His sophomore year in 2024-25 was a study in perseverance. The roster had been completely remade around Cooper Flagg, Kon Knueppel, and Khaman Maluach — the most hyped freshman class since the Zion Williamson era. Foster started the first several games but was moved to the bench in early December when Scheyer promoted Sion James to the starting five. His minutes fluctuated. His role shrank. In a March blowout of Wake Forest, he didn’t play at all. It was, by any measure, the hardest stretch of his basketball life.

But Foster didn’t quit. He didn’t transfer. He didn’t sulk. He kept working, kept listening, kept finding ways to contribute. His play improved down the stretch, and in Duke’s postseason run to the Elite Eight, he provided key minutes off the bench. Scheyer was emotional about it afterward: "I couldn’t be more proud of him. That’s part of what makes this thing special too — the relationship to be able to hang tough when things aren’t going as well."

The 2025-26 season has been Foster’s vindication. With Flagg, Knueppel, Proctor, and McCain all gone to the NBA, Duke’s roster turned over again. Foster was now the elder statesman — the only player with meaningful Duke experience besides Maliq Brown. He stepped into the starting lineup alongside Cameron Boozer, Cayden Boozer, Isaiah Evans, and Patrick Ngongba II, and he thrived.

Through 31 games, Foster averaged 8.5 points, 3.6 rebounds, and 2.8 assists while shooting a career-best 40.2% from three-point range. He started 30 of 31 games. He posted a career-high 20 points at Louisville in January, going 9-of-11 from the field. He dished a career-high 8 assists against Arkansas. He pulled down a career-high 8 rebounds at Pittsburgh. The numbers across the board were career bests.

But the numbers only tell part of the story. Foster became the connective tissue of the #1-ranked team in America. He defended the opponent’s best perimeter player. He avoided turnovers — 25 assists to just 2 turnovers in Duke’s last six regular season games. He cashed open threes when Cameron Boozer drew double-teams. He was the steady veteran in a room full of freshmen, the quiet leader who held the lineups together.

He also hosted "The Brotherhood Podcast," a show that helped Duke’s massive freshman class bond. Nearly every new player cited Foster as the teammate who made them feel at home. The kid who’d attended three high schools in three states understood, better than anyone, what it meant to arrive somewhere new and need to feel like you belonged.

Duke went 29-2, won the ACC regular season at 17-1, and claimed the #1 overall ranking for a record 150th week in AP poll history. Cameron Boozer won ACC Player of the Year. But everyone in Durham knew: Caleb Foster was the glue.
bio.after:
Then came March 8, 2026 — the regular season finale against North Carolina at Cameron Indoor Stadium.

With about five minutes left in the first half, Foster was defending Seth Trimble on a drive when his right foot landed awkwardly — replays showed him stepping on Trimble’s foot, causing an ugly twist. He immediately stopped, crouched near midcourt, and waved to the bench. He walked gingerly to the locker room before halftime, his face in his hands. He returned to the bench in the second half wearing a walking boot on his right foot. Duke won 76-61 without him, but the victory felt hollow.

On Sunday morning, Foster underwent surgery for a fractured right foot — the same foot that had ended his freshman season with a stress fracture two years earlier. Coach Scheyer confirmed on Tuesday, March 10, that Foster would be out for the "foreseeable" future. He would miss the ACC Tournament entirely. His NCAA Tournament availability is uncertain at best.

"In his mind, he’s going to be back tomorrow," Scheyer said. "For me, I’m with him in the sense of doing something that hasn’t been done. Let’s chase this, let’s keep winning, let’s give him the opportunity to join us."

The math is cruel: Scheyer estimated Foster would need at least two weeks of rest before even beginning a comeback, meaning a return before the Final Four — if Duke gets there — is the best-case scenario. As of today, it is not certain that Foster will play another game this season.

For a player who has now lost two March Madness runs to right foot injuries, the heartbreak is almost unbearable. He missed the 2024 Elite Eight as a freshman. He watched from a reduced role as Duke reached the 2025 Elite Eight as a sophomore. Now, as a junior, with the #1 team in the country and a legitimate national championship within reach, March has taken him again.
bio.now:
Caleb Foster is 20 years old, sitting in a walking boot, watching his teammates prepare for the ACC Tournament without him. He left Cameron Indoor Stadium after the UNC game on a knee scooter, his family beside him. He is recovering from surgery on his right foot and racing the calendar toward a possible return in late March or early April.

His career stats read: 96 games, 6.8 points, 2.5 rebounds, 2.1 assists, 38.5% from three across three seasons. The numbers are modest for a five-star recruit. They don’t capture what he means. Foster is the longest-tenured commitment in the Scheyer era — the player who said yes to Duke before anyone else in the 2023 class, before Scheyer had even coached a game. He is the quiet kid who attended three high schools in three states and never once wavered on his commitment. He is the player who lost his freshman March to injury, lost his sophomore role to roster construction, and responded by becoming the steadiest veteran on the #1 team in America.

His father Michael retired from his career as a state trooper to follow Caleb across the country. His mother sacrificed in ways that never make the box score. The Foster family’s commitment to this dream has been total.

Duke is 29-2, the #1 overall seed, and the favorite to win the 2026 national championship. Whether Caleb Foster plays another game this season remains uncertain. What is not uncertain is what he has given this program: three years of loyalty in an era of transfers, two All-ACC Academic Team selections in a sport that doesn’t always value the classroom, and the connective tissue that held together a roster of ten freshmen and made them feel like they’d been playing together for years.

The Brotherhood Podcast host. The first commitment of the Scheyer era. The kid from Harrisburg who dreamed of winning a national championship at Duke.

March keeps trying to take that dream from him. He keeps fighting to get it back.

[Profile current as of March 11, 2026. Foster underwent right foot surgery on March 9. His return timeline is uncertain; Scheyer has indicated a Final Four return is the earliest realistic possibility.]

nba.postCareer: Active college player — Duke (2023-present); junior guard on #1-ranked Blue Devils

players.name: Khaman Maluach
players.tagline: From war-torn South Sudan to a Ugandan refugee community to his first basketball game in Crocs — the most improbable journey in Brotherhood history.

bio.road:
Khaman Maluach was born on September 14, 2006, in Rumbek, a town in Lakes State, South Sudan — a region that had been devastated by decades of civil conflict. He does not remember the year his family left. “I actually don’t know the year I left South Sudan,” he told ESPN. “I don’t remember anything, moving to Uganda. All I know is that I grew up in Uganda.”

His family fled to Kawempe, a crowded suburb on the outskirts of Kampala, Uganda’s capital. His father, Madit Maluach, stayed behind in South Sudan for work. His mother — whose name has not been publicly shared — raised seven children alone in a community where opportunities were scarce and school fees were a burden the family could not consistently afford. Khaman went two years without attending school because his family could not pay the tuition.

He grew up loving soccer and supported Manchester United. Basketball was not in the picture. He had never touched a basketball until he was thirteen years old.

The discovery came in the most improbable way. Walking home from school one day in late 2019, a man on a boda boda — a motorcycle taxi — stopped in front of the tall teenager. “You should start playing basketball,” the man told him. “I can get you shoes, I can get you the ball.” Maluach took the offer. The nearest court was an hour’s walk from his home, often packed with players, and he played his first game wearing a pair of Crocs.

But local coaches Wal Deng and Aketch Wuoi Garang saw something in the raw, gangly kid almost immediately. “The first time I saw Khaman, I saw so much potential,” Deng told BBC Sport Africa. Garang arranged for Maluach to attend Bethel Covenant College in Bwebajja on a scholarship, solving the school fees problem that had kept him out of education. “I trusted Akech because he was a South Sudanese coach,” Maluach later explained. “He gave me a scholarship to go study at school. By then, school tuition was hard, so I took the scholarship and it motivated me. That’s how I got into the game.”

At home, Maluach and his brother devised their own training facility. They stacked two or three large tires on top of each other to create a rim at the proper height, and Khaman practiced his shots every day — one basketball, one tire rim, and limitless ambition. At night, the brothers ran what they called the “night shift”: at midnight, when Ugandan telecommunications companies dropped their mobile data prices, they would stay up watching YouTube clips of Giannis Antetokounmpo and Joel Embiid. “I used to see Giannis’ jab step, and then with Joel I learned the shimmy,” Maluach said. “If they can make it there, I can make it there too.”

In late 2019, Maluach attended a basketball camp organized by the Luol Deng Foundation. Deng — the former Duke star and NBA All-Star who is also of South Sudanese heritage — had founded the organization to use basketball as a tool for empowerment across Africa. For Maluach, the camp was the gateway. His talent and the positive reference from Deng earned him a spot at the NBA Academy Africa in Saly, Senegal, in 2021. He was the youngest player ever enrolled at the academy. He was fourteen years old, approximately 4,700 miles from home, and had been playing organized basketball for less than two years.

The academy combined world-class coaching with education and exposure to international competition. Maluach thrived but the separation from his family was immense. He hasn’t seen them in years, choosing to focus on basketball and education. “I left home at a very young age so I had to learn a lot of stuff,” he told CBS Sports. “I had to learn how to be a man off the basketball court. The academy helped me through all that.”

His rise was stunningly fast. In 2022, at fifteen, he played for South Sudan’s professional team Cobra Sport in the Basketball Africa League as part of the BAL Elevate program. He was named MVP of the 2023 Basketball Without Borders Africa camp in Johannesburg. In August 2023, at sixteen, he was selected for the South Sudan national team roster for the 2023 FIBA Basketball World Cup, becoming the third-youngest player ever to appear in a World Cup game when he debuted in a victory over China. South Sudan, led by Maluach and a roster of players who had grown up in refugee camps, finished as the best-ranked African team and qualified for the 2024 Paris Olympics.

In 2024, playing for the Ugandan club City Oilers in the BAL season, Maluach averaged 17.5 points, a league-leading 13.5 rebounds, and 2.8 blocks per game. He set the BAL single-game record with 7 blocks. At the 2024 Paris Olympics, he was the youngest player in the entire tournament. In a pre-Olympic exhibition game against Team USA, South Sudan — 43-point underdogs — led by one point with ten seconds remaining before LeBron James sealed it with a late layup. Maluach was seventeen years old, standing on a court against the greatest basketball players on earth, representing a nation younger than he was.

On March 6, 2024, Maluach committed to Duke over UCLA, Kentucky, and Kansas. He chose Duke in part because of the impression Zion Williamson had made on him watching from Africa. He wore #9 at Duke to honor Luol Deng — the man whose foundation camp had changed the trajectory of his life.
bio.duke:
Maluach arrived in Durham in the fall of 2024 as the #3 overall recruit in the nation and the top-ranked center in the class, joining a Duke roster that included fellow five-star freshmen Cooper Flagg (#1 overall) and Kon Knueppel (#7 overall) — the consensus #1 recruiting class in the country.

For someone who had been playing organized basketball for roughly five years, his adjustment to the ACC was remarkable. Maluach averaged 8.6 points, 6.6 rebounds, and 1.3 blocks in 21.3 minutes per game. He shot 71.2% from the field — an extraordinary efficiency mark driven by his rim-running, lob-finishing, and offensive rebounding. He also hit 77% of his free throws, suggesting the potential for an expanded shooting range as his career develops.

His defensive impact transcended statistics. At 7-foot-2 with a 7-foot-7 wingspan and 9-foot-6 reach, Maluach altered countless shots without recording blocks. He was the anchor of Duke’s interior defense, and when he was on the floor, opponents thought twice about attacking the rim. He earned ACC All-Freshman Team honors and received votes for ACC Defensive Player of the Year as a freshman.

Jon Scheyer, who had sent staffers to Africa to recruit Maluach personally, was effusive in his praise. “I wish you guys could see him every day in practice,” Scheyer told reporters. “He’s completely about the right things. He’s completely about the preparation, attention to detail. For only playing the game for four or five years, his feel is incredible to me.” He called Maluach “one of the best human beings” and “one-of-one” off the court.

Cooper Flagg, the projected #1 pick in the 2025 NBA Draft and Maluach’s teammate, described him in terms that had nothing to do with basketball: “Everything about him is so pure. Some people you can just tell their intentions and whether they are pure. Khaman is one of those people. He always has a positive aura about him and has a way of putting smiles on people’s faces.”

The nickname among teammates was “Man Man.” He spoke four languages. He prayed daily. He studied film obsessively. And in the NCAA Tournament, he elevated.

In five tournament games, Maluach shot 84% from the field (21-of-25), an absurd number for a freshman center in March. In the Sweet Sixteen win over Arizona, he had 4 blocks and helped form a defensive fortress alongside Flagg that held the Wildcats’ offense in check. Duke reached the Final Four — the program’s 18th — where Maluach had 14 points in an 85-65 win over Alabama. The run ended with a loss to Houston in the national semifinal, but Maluach had announced himself as one of the most promising young centers in the world.

Duke finished 35-4. Maluach, Flagg (#1 pick), and Knueppel (#4 pick) all declared for the 2025 NBA Draft — three top-ten picks from the same freshman class.
bio.after:
On June 25, 2025, Khaman Maluach was selected 10th overall by the Houston Rockets in the 2025 NBA Draft. He was immediately traded to the Phoenix Suns as part of the blockbuster Kevin Durant deal that sent Durant to Houston in exchange for Jalen Green, Dillon Brooks, the #10 pick, and additional draft capital. The trade was finalized on July 6, 2025.

Maluach was the third Duke player taken in the top ten of the 2025 draft — after Cooper Flagg (#1, Dallas Mavericks) and Kon Knueppel (#4, Charlotte Hornets). Three teammates in the top ten. One had been playing basketball for five years.

The Suns view Maluach as their center of the future. They also acquired fellow Duke alum Mark Williams from Charlotte on draft night, providing a veteran presence while Maluach develops. Scouting reports praise his defensive potential — his size, length, lateral quickness, and rim protection are all considered elite tools — while noting that his offensive game, currently limited to dunks, lobs, putbacks, and dunker-spot finishes, has significant room for growth.

In December 2025, Maluach was assigned to the Suns’ G League affiliate, the Valley Suns, for development. Through his first NBA season, he has appeared in a handful of games for the parent club while spending most of his time developing in the G League — a patient approach consistent with his age (he turned 19 in September 2025) and the fact that he is still, remarkably, in his sixth year of playing organized basketball.
bio.now:
Khaman Maluach is a rookie with the Phoenix Suns, splitting time between the NBA roster and the Valley Suns of the G League. He is nineteen years old.

His agent is Josh Hairston of Klutch Sports, working alongside Rich Paul. His brother Majok remains in Uganda and watches Khaman’s games on YouTube because watching on live television makes him too emotional. His mother still lives in Kawempe. His father is still in South Sudan.

Maluach’s connection to Luol Deng has come full circle. Deng’s foundation camp in Uganda in 2019 was the moment that changed everything. Maluach wore Deng’s #9 at Duke. Deng, who is profiled elsewhere in this project, is now the president of the South Sudan Basketball Federation — the same federation that put Maluach on a World Cup roster at sixteen and an Olympic roster at seventeen. The Brotherhood, in this case, is literal: two South Sudanese men, separated by a generation, connected by Duke, connected by basketball, connected by the belief that sport can transform lives.

“Basketball means a big thing to me,” Maluach told BBC Sport Africa. “I believe it’s a gift God gave me to impact other people’s lives and to change my family’s life.”

“The orange ball has taken me so far.”

He speaks four languages. He prays every day. He practiced his first shots through stacked tires in a Ugandan suburb. He watched Giannis on midnight data plans. He played his first organized game in Crocs. He represented a nation younger than himself at the Olympics. He wore #9 to honor the man who showed him the door.

He is nineteen years old, and he is just beginning.

players.name: Patrick Ngongba II
players.tagline: Son of a Central African Republic immigrant and a Hurricane Hugo survivor who played in the WNBA — both parents played at GWU, and their son became Duke’s starting center.

bio.road:
The basketball DNA in Patrick Ngongba II’s family reads like a novel that spans three continents, a Category 4 hurricane, a Korean basketball league, and the WNBA — all before he ever picked up a ball himself.

His father, Patrick Ngongba Sr., was born in 1978 in Bangui, the capital of the Central African Republic. He moved to the United States in December 1993, a fifteen-year-old from a landlocked African nation arriving in the mid-Atlantic with a basketball player’s frame and ambition. He attended Calvert Hall College High School in Maryland, then played four seasons of college basketball at George Washington University from 1997 to 2001, a 6-foot-8 power forward and center. After graduating, the elder Ngongba played professionally overseas — first in South Korea’s KBL with the Wonju DB Promy during the 2001–02 season, and later for ASS Rabat in Morocco’s top division.

His mother’s story is equally extraordinary. Tajama Abraham was born on September 27, 1975, on the island of St. Croix in the United States Virgin Islands. She lived there until the age of fourteen, when Category 4 Hurricane Hugo struck on September 17 and 18, 1989. To survive, Tajama took shelter in a commercial refrigerator measuring eight feet by twelve feet, crammed inside alongside her four siblings, her mother, her father, and her grandmother — eight people in a walk-in cooler while the winds outside raged at 140 miles per hour. The family survived. That experience forged something in Tajama that would define the rest of her life.

She went on to play basketball at George Washington University from 1993 to 1997, where she retired as the program’s all-time leading scorer with 2,134 career points — a record that still stands — and was inducted into the GWU Basketball Hall of Fame. She was selected 31st overall in the 1997 WNBA Draft, playing for the Sacramento Monarchs in 1997 and the Detroit Shock in 1998. After her playing career, Tajama built a coaching career that spanned five different schools over two decades: assistant roles at Richmond, VCU, George Washington, and George Mason, plus a five-year stint as head coach at Radford from 2008 to 2013 where she won Big South Conference Coach of the Year honors. She later served as an assistant at Georgetown before returning to George Washington as a coach.

Patrick and Tajama met while playing basketball at GWU. They married in 2003. Basketball was not just the family business — it was the family language.

The extended family tree deepens the legacy further. Tajama’s brother, Faisal Abraham, played at Marquette University, where he ranked among the program’s all-time leading shot blockers at 6-foot-7. Patrick II’s older sister, Naja Ngongba, played college basketball at Radford, Mars Hill University, and Elizabeth City State University, and has represented the U.S. Virgin Islands internationally. His cousin Isaiah Abraham currently plays for Georgetown.

Patrick Ngongba II was born on October 30, 2005, in Manassas, Virginia, and grew up in the Northern Virginia basketball corridor. He began his prep career at Highland High School before transferring to St. Paul VI Catholic High School in Fairfax — a program with a direct pipeline to Duke that had already produced Jeremy Roach and Trevor Keels. At Paul VI, Ngongba developed into one of the premier centers in the country, a physically imposing post player with soft touch around the rim, underrated passing instincts, and the kind of basketball IQ that comes from growing up in a household where both parents had played at the collegiate and professional levels.

On the Nike EYBL circuit with Team Takeover, Ngongba averaged 12.0 points and 7.4 rebounds per game, shooting nearly 72% inside the arc. A breakout 33-point, 18-rebound, 5-assist performance against Phenom University in late May 2023 opened eyes across the country. He was rated a five-star recruit by ESPN, ranked the #19 overall player nationally and the #3 center in the 2024 class, and was named the best player in the state of Virginia.

Then came the setback that would test the family’s resilience. Ngongba suffered a significant foot injury that required surgery and forced him to miss his entire senior season at Paul VI. For a player whose stock was ascending rapidly, the timing was devastating. But Ngongba’s mother knew something about surviving storms. The family’s approach was patient, disciplined recovery.

After healing, Ngongba tried out for and made the USA Basketball U18 National Team, helping Team USA capture gold at the 2024 FIBA Men’s U18 AmeriCup, where he averaged 4.5 points, 4.8 rebounds, and 1.2 blocks per game while shooting 66.7% from the field.

On November 4, 2023, Ngongba committed to Duke over Kansas State, Kentucky, Michigan, UConn, and North Texas. Kansas State had recruited him the longest due to his strong ties to coach Jerome Tang, and North Texas had given him his first scholarship offer. But Duke — and the chance to play alongside Cooper Flagg, Kon Knueppel, and Khaman Maluach in what became the consensus #1 recruiting class in America — was the call. Darren Harris, his Paul VI teammate who had already committed to Duke, helped recruit him. Ngongba spoke with Flagg on his visit about forming a dominant frontcourt together.

“As parents, we were looking for coaches who would push Pat outside of his comfort zone,” Tajama told ESPN. “Pat is a composed player who has a steadiness about him and tries to do the little things well.”
bio.duke:
Ngongba arrived at Duke in the fall of 2024 still working his way back to full strength after the foot surgery that had cost him his senior season. A minor injury in preseason delayed his debut by two weeks. He made his first appearance on November 16, 2024, against Wofford, posting 2 points, 6 rebounds, and 2 assists.

As a freshman, Ngongba played behind Khaman Maluach, the starting center and eventual #10 NBA Draft pick. In 30 games, he averaged 3.9 points, 2.7 rebounds, and 0.5 blocks in just 10.6 minutes per game. The numbers were modest, but the growth was visible to anyone paying attention. Jon Scheyer called him “a quiet competitor” and praised his composure: “There’s not really a moment that’s too big for him. He’s really, really smart and he understands the game well, but he’s physical and competitive. You trust him with the ball. He’s just been such a solid guy for us.”

Maliq Brown, the veteran transfer from Syracuse, put it more simply in November: “Pat is different.”

The moments came when they needed to come. In the Sweet Sixteen of the 2025 NCAA Tournament — a 100-93 win over Arizona — Ngongba delivered 8 points, 2 rebounds, and an assist in 12 minutes off the bench. Duke reached the Final Four, finished 35-4, and sent three players into the top ten of the NBA Draft: Flagg (#1), Knueppel (#4), and Maluach (#10). Five Blue Devils total went to the league. Ngongba watched, learned, and waited for his turn.

His sophomore season has been the payoff. With Maluach in the NBA, Ngongba stepped into the starting center role and has been one of the most improved players in the ACC. Through 29 games (28 starts), he is averaging 10.7 points, 6.0 rebounds, 1.9 assists, and 1.1 blocks per game while shooting 60.2% from the field. He leads Duke with 32 blocks on the season — doubling his career total from freshman year in a single campaign.

The passing has been the revelation. From the high post, Ngongba reads cutters and finds three-point shooters with a precision that belies his size. Draft analysts have called his playmaking his standout skill — a 6-foot-11 center who functions as a connector in Duke’s offense, making reads that recall Wendell Carter Jr.’s time in Durham. He scored a career-high 17 points against Niagara while also draining his first career three-pointer. Against Florida in a 67-66 nail-biter, he posted 11 points, 5 rebounds, 5 assists, and 5 blocks — the kind of line that fills up every column.

Alongside freshman Cameron Boozer, who leads the team in points, rebounds, and assists, Ngongba has formed one of the most formidable frontcourts in the nation. Duke entered March 2026 at 29-2, ranked #1 in the country, and the overwhelming favorite for the #1 overall seed in the NCAA Tournament. Ngongba has been integral to that — the steady, physical, high-IQ center who anchors the defense and makes everyone around him better.

There has been one complication. The foot that cost him his senior year of high school has flared again. Ngongba missed the regular-season finale against North Carolina with foot soreness and has been ruled out of the ACC Tournament. Scheyer has prioritized getting him healthy for the NCAA Tournament: “To be honest, health going into the NCAA Tournament is the priority. It just is.” Duke won convincingly without him — Boozer had 26/15/5 and Brown dominated defensively in a 76-61 rout of UNC — but the Blue Devils’ title hopes rest in significant part on Ngongba’s return to the lineup.
bio.now:
Patrick Ngongba II is a sophomore starting center for the #1-ranked Duke Blue Devils. He is twenty years old, recovering from foot soreness, and expected to return for the opening round of the 2026 NCAA Tournament.

He is projected as a late first-round pick in the 2026 NBA Draft. Bleacher Report’s Jonathan Wasserman has mocked him to the Denver Nuggets at #25, noting that certain NBA teams will be drawn to his passing and ability to facilitate from inside the defense. Draft evaluators compare him to Wendell Carter Jr. — another former Blue Devil — citing similar physical profiles, playmaking instincts, and defensive steadiness. His scouting report highlights his bruising strength, rim efficiency, and passing IQ, while flagging his foot injury history and the need to develop a more consistent perimeter shot and pick-and-roll game for the NBA.

His mother coaches basketball at George Washington, the same university where both his parents played, where his mother set the all-time scoring record, and where his parents met. His sister played college ball at three schools. His uncle blocked shots at Marquette. His cousin plays at Georgetown. The family that survived a hurricane in a refrigerator, that crossed an ocean from the Central African Republic, that bridged continents through basketball — they all watch Patrick wear #21 for Duke.

There is a version of this story that is simply about basketball lineage, about genetics and training and advantage. But the full version is richer. It is about a father who left Bangui at fifteen and found his way to an American university through sport. It is about a mother who huddled with her family in a commercial cooler while a hurricane tore apart her island, then became one of the greatest players in the history of her college program, then played in the WNBA, then coached for twenty years across five schools. It is about their son, who inherited not just their height and their touch but their steadiness — the quality his mother identified when she told ESPN that Pat “tries to do the little things well.”

He missed his senior year. He backed up an NBA lottery pick as a freshman. He stepped into the starting lineup as a sophomore and became one of the most efficient and intelligent centers in the ACC. He is dealing with a foot injury heading into March Madness. He is twenty years old, and his family has survived worse.

“Pat is a quiet competitor,” Scheyer has said. “There’s not really a moment that’s too big for him.”

The son of a man from Bangui and a woman from St. Croix, raised in Virginia, playing for Duke. The Brotherhood runs deep — and in the Ngongba family, it runs through bloodlines.
