players.name: Dame Sarr
players.tagline: Born in small-town Italy to Senegalese immigrants, played for FC Barcelona at 16, defied the club to attend the Nike Hoop Summit, and landed at Duke as a EuroLeague veteran.

bio.road:
Elhadji Dame Sarr was born on June 4, 2006, in Oderzo, a small town of roughly 20,000 people in the Treviso province of northeastern Italy’s Veneto region. His parents were both born in Senegal. His father had moved to Italy in 1992; his mother followed in 2004. They built a life in Oderzo, raising four children — Dame’s older sister, born in 2005, Dame himself, and two younger brothers, born in 2010 and 2017. The family’s names have not been publicly shared, but their influence runs through every chapter of Dame’s story: the discipline, the maturity, the willingness to leave home at thirteen and cross oceans for basketball.

Sarr started playing basketball at six years old, joining the youth ranks of his local team Basket Oderzo in 2012. He also played soccer, as most Italian kids do, but by the time he was thirteen, basketball had won. In 2019, he left home to join Orange1 Basket Bassano, an academy in Bassano del Grappa, about an hour from Oderzo. For a thirteen-year-old, it was a significant step — leaving his family to live and train at an academy, betting on a sport that barely registers in Italy’s national consciousness compared to soccer.

The bet paid off in the summer of 2022. FC Barcelona — one of the premier basketball clubs in the world, playing in both Spain’s Liga ACB and the EuroLeague — spotted the sixteen-year-old Sarr while he was playing for Bassano at a local tournament. Barcelona signed him immediately. A kid from a town of 20,000 in Veneto was now property of one of the most iconic sports institutions on earth.

Sarr spent the 2022–23 season playing for Barcelona’s Junior and B teams in the Liga EBA. But in January 2023, still only sixteen years old, he was called up to the first team for a Liga ACB match against Bilbao Basket. Playing in the final seconds of a victory, he became the second-youngest player ever to appear in an official game for FC Barcelona’s basketball program. He was sixteen years and seven months old, playing in one of the top professional leagues in the world.

The 2023–24 season continued the pattern: primarily Barcelona B, with occasional call-ups to the first team. He made his EuroLeague debut in November 2023, entering a game against Panathinaikos — the second-best basketball league on the planet, after the NBA. He was seventeen.

For the 2024–25 season, Sarr was officially promoted to Barcelona’s first-team roster. His teammates included veterans and former NBA players like Jabari Parker — the former Duke star who had been the #2 pick in the 2014 NBA Draft. Sarr was now sharing a locker room with a Blue Devil legend, an experience that would shape his next decision profoundly. In Liga ACB play, Sarr appeared in 12 games, averaging 5.8 points and 1.7 rebounds in 12.6 minutes per game, shooting an impressive 52.1% from the field and 42.9% from three. In the EuroLeague, he appeared in 15 games, including two starts. In March 2025, he erupted for 21 points and 3 assists in a Liga ACB win over CB Breogán — the kind of performance that announced him as a legitimate professional.

And then came the moment that changed everything.

In April 2025, Sarr was invited to the Nike Hoop Summit in Portland, Oregon — an annual showcase featuring the best young international talent against elite American high school players. Barcelona, which had initially agreed to let him attend, reversed course as Sarr became an important rotation player. They denied his request. Sarr went anyway. He boarded a plane, flew across the Atlantic, and played in the Hoop Summit without his club’s permission. He scored 17 points, grabbed 4 rebounds, and dazzled NBA scouts in 25 minutes of action — playing against future Duke teammates Cameron and Cayden Boozer.

Barcelona released a statement calling Sarr’s actions a breach of his obligations. On April 17, 2025, the two sides agreed to part ways. His amateur contract was terminated. The kid from Oderzo had defied one of the biggest clubs in world basketball, bet on himself, and won.

College programs lined up immediately. Oregon, Illinois, and Kansas all recruited hard. Kansas appeared to be the front-runner. But Duke — the program where Jabari Parker had played, the program Sarr had watched obsessively during the 2025 NCAA Tournament — made a late push. On May 22, 2025, Sarr committed to Duke.

“Duke was my dream school,” he told ESPN. “I played with Jabari Parker this season, who went to Duke. When I had the chance to go there, I had to take it. My ultimate goal is to play in the NBA. There’s no better place to prepare you for that than Duke.”

His sister, he said, had been part of his journey from the beginning — attending games, recording them with their father. His younger brothers, now fourteen and eight, looked up to him. He was leaving all of them, crossing the Atlantic, to play college basketball in a country he had never lived in.

Alongside his club career, Sarr represented Italy at every youth level: the 2022 U16 European Championship, the 2023 U18 European Championship, and the 2024 U18 EuroBasket in Finland, where he led Italy in scoring (13.4 ppg) and assists (4.7 apg) with 6.3 rebounds and 3.1 steals per game. On November 25, 2024 — while still at Barcelona — he made his debut for Italy’s senior national team in a EuroBasket 2025 qualifier against Iceland. He was eighteen years old, a full international, a EuroLeague rotation player, and a few months from becoming a Blue Devil.
bio.duke:
Sarr arrived at Duke in the summer of 2025 as a five-star recruit according to 247Sports, carrying three years of professional experience on a roster full of freshmen who had never played above the high school level. Jon Scheyer recognized both the advantage and the challenge: Sarr’s European pedigree meant he understood spacing, tempo, and defensive schemes at a level most American freshmen simply do not, but the pace and physicality of the ACC would demand adjustments of their own.

He earned the start in his Duke debut on November 4, 2025, against Texas in Charlotte, posting 8 points, 6 rebounds, 3 steals, and an assist. A week later, he exploded for 19 points at Army West Point — 13 of them in the first eight minutes of action — going 6-for-8 from the field including 3-for-5 from three-point range. The game, played at West Point in honor of former Duke coach Mike Krzyzewski, felt like an announcement: the kid from Oderzo, via Barcelona, belonged.

Through 31 games and 23 starts in the 2025–26 season, Sarr has averaged 6.2 points, 3.6 rebounds, 1.0 assists, and 1.0 steals in 21.9 minutes per game, shooting 40.2% from the field and 33.3% from three-point range. The raw numbers are modest — the fifth option on a team loaded with NBA talent — but they dramatically understate his impact, particularly on the defensive end.

Defense has been Sarr’s calling card. At 6-foot-8 with a 7-foot wingspan and a twitchy, disruptive style honed by three years of professional competition, he has become Duke’s best perimeter defender. His on-ball pressure has consistently neutralized opponents’ best scorers: in a January 2026 win at Stanford, Sarr helped hold Ebuka Okorie to 9 points on a night the Cardinal star was averaging 23.1. He earned ACC All-Defensive Team honors as a freshman — the recognition that best captures what he has meant to a Duke team that has been elite on that end of the floor.

The offensive game has come in flashes. Against Florida State, he shot 4-for-5 from the field including 3-for-4 from deep, scoring 13 points. Against SMU, he matched his career high with 4 steals while knocking down two critical three-pointers. Against Texas Tech, he drained three first-half triples. Against Niagara, he posted 5 points, 5 boards, 4 steals, 3 assists, and a block — a stat line that touches everything. When the shot is falling, Sarr looks like the lottery pick the preseason projections promised. When it’s not, his defense and versatility still keep him on the floor.

Duke entered March 2026 at 29-2 and ranked #1 in the country. Sarr has been one of five starters alongside Cameron Boozer, Patrick Ngongba, Isaiah Evans, and Caleb Foster — a lineup where four of the five players stand 6-foot-5 or taller. The positional size and defensive versatility that Scheyer has built into his roster construction owes much to Sarr’s presence on the wing. He is the connector, the defender, the European professional who brings a maturity and composure that a roster of American freshmen desperately needs.

“I can play for my teammates, create for myself and for my teammates,” Sarr said on the Brotherhood Podcast after committing. “I’m very adaptable.”

He has been exactly that. He has also been, quietly, one of the most fascinating players in college basketball — a Senegalese-Italian who played in the EuroLeague at seventeen, defied FC Barcelona at eighteen, and is now defending the best guards in the ACC at nineteen.
bio.now:
Dame Sarr is a freshman starter for the #1-ranked Duke Blue Devils. He is nineteen years old, an ACC All-Defensive Team selection, and a projected first-round pick in the 2026 NBA Draft.

ESPN’s Jonathan Givony has projected him as high as the #9 pick; other outlets have him in the late lottery or early twenties, with mock destinations ranging from the Oklahoma City Thunder to the Memphis Grizzlies to the Denver Nuggets. Scouting reports praise his length, defensive versatility, and professional poise, while noting he needs to add strength, improve his shot creation, and become more consistent as a scorer. His player comparison from the Duke Chronicle: Shai Gilgeous-Alexander — another long, twitchy wing who compensated for a smaller frame with lightning-fast decision-making and relentless defensive energy.

His parents remain in Oderzo. His sister, who recorded his childhood games with their father, still follows every minute. His younger brothers — fourteen and eight — are watching from Italy. The family that came from Senegal to a small town in Veneto, that raised a boy who started playing basketball at six and left home at thirteen, that watched him sign with Barcelona at sixteen and defy them at eighteen — they are watching their son play for the #1 team in America.

Sarr’s path to Duke is unlike any in the program’s history. He is not the first international player to wear Duke blue — Luol Deng came from England via Sudan, and Khaman Maluach came from Uganda via South Sudan and Senegal. But Sarr is the first to arrive with professional experience in one of the world’s top leagues. He played in the EuroLeague before he played in the ACC. He guarded professionals before he guarded collegians. He shared a locker room with Jabari Parker — the 2014 Duke star — before sharing one with Cameron Boozer. The Brotherhood, for Sarr, is not just metaphorical. It is a living chain that connected Barcelona to Durham through a player who wore Duke blue fifteen years before Sarr arrived.

“Duke was my dream school,” he said. “There’s no better place to prepare you for the NBA than Duke.”

The son of Senegalese immigrants, raised in a small Italian town, trained in Spain, tested against the best players in Europe, bold enough to defy one of the biggest clubs in the world to chase his dream. He crossed the Atlantic and landed in Durham. He wears #7 for the Blue Devils. He is nineteen years old, an ACC All-Defensive freshman, and he is just getting started.

players.name: Nikolas Khamenia
players.tagline: Both parents emigrated from Belarus for basketball. He watched Space Jam every morning before pre-school, won three USA gold medals, and chose Duke over the school ten miles from his house.

bio.road:
Nikolas Khamenia was born on December 27, 2006, in North Hollywood, California, into a family where basketball was not just a passion but a migration story. Both of his parents were born in Belarus and came to the United States as young adults to play college basketball. His father, Valery \u201CVal\u201D Khamenia, played at George Washington University before returning to Belarus to compete in the professional leagues there. He eventually settled in Los Angeles, where he became an assistant basketball coach at Los Angeles Valley College. His mother also played basketball. They married and raised four children \u2014 Nikolas, an older sister, a younger sister, and a younger brother.

Basketball was the family language long before Nikolas could articulate it. Every morning before pre-school, he watched the 1996 movie \u201CSpace Jam\u201D \u2014 the one with Michael Jordan and Bugs Bunny \u2014 a ritual he later described on Duke\u2019s Brotherhood Podcast as his earliest basketball memory. He started playing organized ball at six years old, and by middle school it was clear he was something more than a coach\u2019s son with good fundamentals. He had three siblings who all gravitated toward the sport, but it was Nikolas who took it furthest, fastest.

Growing up a San Antonio Spurs fan in Los Angeles, Khamenia modeled his game after an unusual constellation of influences: Kawhi Leonard\u2019s two-way dominance, Tim Duncan\u2019s post craft, Tony Parker\u2019s court vision, Manu Ginobili\u2019s creativity. As he matured, he added Kevin Durant\u2019s versatility and the cerebral playmaking of Nikola Jokic and Luka Doncic to his study list. The result was a player who processed the game faster than he could run it \u2014 a 6-foot-8 forward with guard skills, extraordinary passing instincts, and an almost preternatural sense of where teammates were going to be before they got there.

Khamenia attended Harvard-Westlake School in Studio City, one of the premier academic and athletic prep schools in the country and the same school that had recently produced Duke guard Spencer Hubbard. Under coach Dave Rebibo, Khamenia developed into one of the most complete players in California \u2014 averaging 14.0 points, 7.2 rebounds, and 4.0 assists per game as a junior. He helped lead Harvard-Westlake to back-to-back California state championships in 2023 and 2024, with overall records of 33-2 and 33-3 respectively. His senior year added a third outstanding campaign at 31-3.

\u201CHe has a blue-collar approach to basketball while having a tremendous amount of skill, vision, IQ and toughness,\u201D Rebibo said. \u201CHe is the ultimate winner.\u201D

On the AAU circuit, Khamenia played for the Basketball Training Institute on the Puma Pro16 circuit, further expanding his national profile. But it was his USA Basketball career that elevated him into elite company. Khamenia represented the United States three times in FIBA competition, winning gold each time:

At the 2024 FIBA U18 AmeriCup \u2014 alongside Duke teammate Patrick Ngongba \u2014 he started all six games and averaged 7.7 points, 6.3 rebounds, and 3.0 assists as Team USA won gold. At the 2024 FIBA 3x3 U18 World Cup in Hungary, he was named tournament MVP as the U.S. captured another gold medal. And at the 2025 FIBA U19 Men\u2019s World Cup in Switzerland, he averaged 9.7 points, 3.3 rebounds, and 2.1 assists in just 15.2 minutes per game, shooting 44% from three-point range. In one game, he posted 14 points, 7 rebounds, 5 assists, and 5 steals. Three tournaments, three golds, one MVP.

He was named a McDonald\u2019s All-American and played in the 2025 Nike Hoop Summit, scoring 5 points and grabbing 2 rebounds off the bench for the U.S. team. He was rated a consensus top-20 recruit nationally and the best player in California.

On October 22, 2024, Khamenia committed to Duke over UCLA and Gonzaga. UCLA had been the presumptive leader \u2014 a Los Angeles kid at the hometown school \u2014 but Duke\u2019s late push, powered by Scheyer\u2019s relationship with Khamenia and the pull of an incoming class that already included Cameron and Cayden Boozer, proved decisive. Spencer Hubbard, his Harvard-Westlake predecessor at Duke, helped with the connection: \u201CSpencer Hubbard used to be at Harvard-Westlake and I have a great relationship with him,\u201D Khamenia said.

\u201CDuke was a place I visited and felt it was special from the moment I left campus,\u201D he told ESPN. \u201CCoach Scheyer believes in me and my abilities. Duke is a special place that has put out a lot of great players and ultimately helped them develop.\u201D

The 2,500-mile move from Los Angeles to Durham didn\u2019t faze him. His parents had made a longer one. \u201CMy parents being from Belarus, they came to the country to attend college and play basketball,\u201D Khamenia said after his exhibition debut. \u201CSo, for me going across the country \u2014 same country, just different state, chasing my dream \u2014 I\u2019m not really homesick just because I have goals in mind and things I want to achieve.\u201D
bio.duke:
Khamenia arrived in Durham as part of the consensus #1 recruiting class in the country \u2014 headlined by Cameron Boozer (#3 national recruit), his twin brother Cayden, Dame Sarr from FC Barcelona, and Sebastian Wilkins from Brewster Academy, joining returning players like Patrick Ngongba, Isaiah Evans, Caleb Foster, and Maliq Brown.

Scheyer\u2019s assessment of Khamenia was pointed and specific. \u201CThat was the thing recruiting Nik: the guys that have been the most successful here have been the best competitors, and we watched him in high school, and we felt there wasn\u2019t a better competitor in the country,\u201D Scheyer said. \u201CHe\u2019s fearless. He has a way of just cleaning things up on both ends. He gets loose balls. He makes connecting plays, passing, getting guys open. And his size allows him to guard, really, one through five.\u201D

In Duke\u2019s exhibition opener against UCF, Khamenia started and led the team with 32 minutes, posting 14 points, 8 rebounds, a steal, and a block. He looked every bit the starter Scheyer had envisioned. But when Dame Sarr returned to full health and claimed the starting small forward spot, Khamenia transitioned to the bench without complaint \u2014 a role that has ultimately showcased exactly the qualities Scheyer recruited him for.

Through 30 games in the 2025\u201326 season, Khamenia has averaged 5.5 points, 3.3 rebounds, and 1.1 assists in 16.8 minutes per game, shooting 46.3% from the field and 34.2% from three-point range. He has started eight games and come off the bench in twenty-two others. The numbers do not capture his impact.

Khamenia is the connective tissue of Duke\u2019s offense. His passing \u2014 the skill scouts have called his best natural attribute \u2014 manifests in quick decisions, cross-court darts, and reads that create open shots for teammates before defenses can rotate. He functions less as a scorer and more as a facilitator who happens to be 6-foot-8 with a reliable three-point shot. When Cameron Boozer draws double teams in the post, it is often Khamenia who finds the open shooter. When Duke needs a lineup that can switch everything defensively, Khamenia\u2019s ability to guard positions one through five makes him invaluable.

247Sports\u2019 scouting report called his basketball IQ \u201Cterrific\u201D and noted he \u201Cprocesses the game incredibly quickly, allowing him to know exactly what to do with the ball as soon as he touches it.\u201D The Duke Chronicle compared him to Gordon Hayward \u2014 a similarly sized, fundamentally sound forward whose value exceeded his scoring average because of his playmaking and defensive versatility.

The season has featured several vintage Khamenia performances. He scored in double figures multiple times, shot efficiently from three when called upon, and consistently delivered the kind of unglamorous, high-IQ basketball that wins games without making highlight reels. His best games have been the ones where the stat sheet looks modest but the game felt different when he was on the floor \u2014 the extra pass, the deflection, the offensive rebound that extended a possession.

Duke is 29-2 and the #1 overall seed in the upcoming NCAA Tournament. Khamenia\u2019s role as the team\u2019s primary bench wing gives Duke a depth advantage that few programs can match. When starters rest, the quality does not drop. That is Khamenia\u2019s gift to this team \u2014 and it is the same gift he gave to Harvard-Westlake when they won two straight state titles, and to Team USA when they won three straight gold medals.
bio.now:
Nikolas Khamenia is a freshman guard/forward for the #1-ranked Duke Blue Devils. He is eighteen years old, a key rotation player on a team expected to compete for the national championship, and a projected second-round pick in the 2026 NBA Draft.

His scouting profile emphasizes his passing, basketball IQ, and defensive versatility while acknowledging that his athleticism and shot creation need development for the NBA level. He is the kind of player who will likely get better in his second year if he returns to Duke \u2014 a player whose value increases as the talent around him increases, because his primary skill is making everyone else better.

His father still coaches at Los Angeles Valley College, still teaches the game to young players who are chasing the same dream that brought him from Belarus to America three decades ago. His mother, who also made that transatlantic journey for basketball, watches from California. His three siblings \u2014 the older sister, the younger sister, the younger brother who plays basketball with him at home \u2014 are growing up in a household where the sport is not just recreation but heritage, a thread that connects Minsk to North Hollywood to Durham.

The Harvard-Westlake pipeline to Duke now runs two deep: Spencer Hubbard blazed the trail, and Khamenia followed. They are different players \u2014 Hubbard was a gritty guard, Khamenia is a cerebral wing \u2014 but the through-line is the same: intelligence, competitiveness, and a willingness to do whatever the team needs rather than whatever looks best on a highlight reel.

\u201CI feel like I bring to this team just being a guy that does whatever we need to win,\u201D Khamenia said in an interview during his junior year at Harvard-Westlake. That was his self-assessment at sixteen. At eighteen, wearing #14 for Duke, it remains the most accurate description of who he is as a player.

He watched Space Jam every morning before pre-school. His parents crossed an ocean for basketball. He won three gold medals for his country before he played a college game. He chose Duke over the school ten miles from his house because he wanted to be pushed. He moved into a bench role without protest because the team needed it. He is a coach\u2019s son who plays like a coach\u2019s dream.

\u201CThere wasn\u2019t a better competitor in the country,\u201D Jon Scheyer said.

The son of Belarus, raised in Hollywood, playing for Duke. #TrustYourWork.
